Venture capitalist Arthur Rock raised $2.5 million in two days, mostly from calls to people who trusted Noyce's name. The company was called Intel, short for Integrated Electronics.

The founding team understood silicon physics better than almost anyone alive. Moore brought deep technical knowledge. Noyce brought credibility and vision. Andy Grove, employee number three, brought operational discipline that bordered on paranoia. This combination — vision, credibility, execution — would define Intel for decades.

Their first product, the 3101 Schottky bipolar memory chip, shipped in April 1969. It worked but faced competition. The breakthrough came in 1970 with the 1103 DRAM chip, the first commercially viable dynamic random-access memory. By 1972, it was the best-selling semiconductor in the world. Intel had created the memory chip industry.

But the real pivot happened almost by accident. In 1969, a Japanese calculator company called Busicom asked Intel to design twelve custom chips. Ted Hoff, an Intel engineer, proposed something radical: instead of custom chips, build one general-purpose chip that could be programmed. The 4004, released in 1971, was the first commercial microprocessor. Intel bought back the rights from Busicom for $60,000.

For years, Intel didn't understand what they had. Microprocessors were a side business. Memory chips generated the revenue. The 8080 processor, released in 1974, found its way into early personal computers like the Altair, but Intel saw this as a niche market. The company was a memory company that happened to make processors.

The crisis came from an unexpected direction. In the early 1980s, Japanese manufacturers — backed by government support and massive capital investment — flooded the memory market with high-quality, low-cost DRAM chips. Intel's margins collapsed. By 1984, the company was losing money on memory for the first time. The business that had defined Intel was becoming unsustainable.

Grove and Moore faced the decision that defines many startup paths: what are we actually building? In Grove's retelling, Moore asked him: "If we got kicked out and the board brought in a new CEO, what do you think he would do?" Grove answered immediately: "He would get us out of memories." Moore stared at him and said, "Why shouldn't you and I walk out the door, come back, and do it ourselves?"

They did. Intel exited memory — laying off thousands, closing factories, abandoning the market they had created. It was wrenching. But by 1986, they were solely a microprocessor company. The 386 chip, released that year, established Intel's dominance in PC processors. The IBM PC architecture had created a standard. Intel owned the brain.

The next fifteen years were conquest. Intel didn't just make chips; they defined the pace of computing. Moore's Law became a marketing tool and a forcing function. Every eighteen to twenty-four months, a faster chip. "Intel Inside" stickers turned a component into a brand. By the mid-1990s, Intel had over 80% of the PC processor market and margins that made them one of the most profitable companies on Earth.

But success created rigidity. Intel optimized everything around high-performance PC and server processors. When the market shifted toward mobile in the 2000s, Intel stumbled. The ARM architecture — low-power, modular, designed for phones — was everything Intel's x86 wasn't. Intel tried to adapt with Atom processors, but the company's culture and economics were built for high-margin PC chips, not the lower-margin, power-efficient mobile world.

Apple's decision to use Intel processors in Macs (2006-2020) looked like validation but proved temporary. When Apple shifted to their own ARM-based chips in 2020, it exposed Intel's vulnerability. The company that had defined processor performance was losing on multiple fronts: Apple designing their own chips, AMD resurgent with better architecture, TSMC and Samsung outpacing Intel's manufacturing.

Intel's manufacturing advantage — once unassailable — eroded through a series of delays. The 10-nanometer process, expected around 2015, shipped in volume only in 2019. The 7-nanometer process (later renamed "Intel 4") faced similar delays. Companies that had relied on Intel's fabrication prowess — the "fabs" that made Moore's Law real — looked elsewhere.

By 2021, Intel was in a position almost unthinkable two decades earlier: catching up. New CEO Pat Gelsinger, an Intel veteran, announced an ambitious plan to rebuild Intel's manufacturing leadership and become a foundry for other companies' chip designs. The U.S. government, suddenly aware that advanced chip manufacturing had moved to Asia, backed this with billions in subsidies through the CHIPS Act.

Intel today is not failing, but it is no longer defining the future. It remains massive, profitable, essential to data centers and PCs. But the company that invented the microprocessor, that made Moore's Law a self-fulfilling prophecy, that turned silicon into the most important material of the information age, is now fighting to catch up to the cadence it once set. The path from dominance to challenger wasn't sudden collapse. It was the slow accumulation of decisions optimized for yesterday's market, the weight of success making it harder to see what comes next, the discovery that what made you a giant can make you slow.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Xerox PARC
entries.descriptor: The lab that invented the future and gave it away
entries.content:
# Xerox PARC

***The lab that invented the future and gave it away***

> **Years:** 1970-present

In 1970, Xerox Corporation was printing money. The company's copier monopoly generated such reliable profits that executives worried about antitrust action. They decided to hedge against an uncertain future by building a research lab in Palo Alto, three thousand miles from headquarters in Rochester, New York. The distance was intentional. They wanted brilliant people doing unfettered research into "the office of the future." They got more than they bargained for.

The Palo Alto Research Center — PARC — assembled an extraordinary collection of talent. Bob Taylor, who had managed ARPANET's development at the Pentagon, led the computer science lab. He recruited refugees from the defense establishment and academia who shared a vision: computers should be personal, graphical, and networked. At the time, computing meant timesharing on mainframes. PARC's researchers thought this was backwards.

Within three years, they had invented the modern world.

The Alto, completed in 1973, was the first personal computer with a graphical user interface. It had a mouse, windows, icons, and a bitmap display. You pointed and clicked. You could see what you were creating. Engineers at PARC used Altos to write code, scientists used them to write papers, and everyone used them to send email across the first Ethernet network. They developed the first laser printer. They created object-oriented programming. They built the foundations of the internet as we know it.

And Xerox executives, back in Rochester, had no idea what to do with any of it.

The disconnect was profound. PARC researchers were building tools for knowledge workers in a digital future. Xerox executives sold machines that made copies of paper. The Alto cost $40,000 to build — more than most cars — and required technical expertise to maintain. How do you sell that to offices that still used carbon paper? When PARC researchers demonstrated their creations, executives nodded politely and asked when they'd develop a better copier.

The tension exploded in the late 1970s. PARC had built revolutionary technology but Xerox couldn't commercialize it. Engineers watched as outsiders recognized what they'd created. In 1979, Steve Jobs visited PARC after Xerox invested in Apple. The deal gave Apple access to PARC's innovations in exchange for pre-IPO shares. Jobs spent hours watching demonstrations of the Alto's interface. He saw immediately what it meant.

"Why aren't you doing anything with this?" he kept asking. "This is revolutionary."

PARC researchers gave him the full tour. They showed him everything: overlapping windows, pop-up menus, icons, the mouse. Jobs absorbed it all. Later, he would call it "the best thing I'd ever seen." Apple's engineers immediately began building what would become the Lisa and Macintosh. They didn't copy PARC's work directly — they reimagined it, refined it, made it commercial. But the inspiration was unmistakable.

Xerox did eventually try to commercialize PARC's innovations. The Xerox Star, released in 1981, was a fully-realized graphical workstation — elegant, sophisticated, and doomed. It cost $16,595, required extensive training, and ran proprietary software. Only 25,000 units sold. Meanwhile, the IBM PC, released the same year with a command-line interface and open architecture, became the industry standard.

The conventional narrative calls this "Xerox's fumble" — a tale of corporate blindness and missed opportunity. But the reality was more complex. PARC's innovations required an entire ecosystem that didn't exist in the early 1980s. Cheap microprocessors, affordable memory, mass production of peripherals, software developers, user comfort with computers — none of this was ready. Xerox had the technology a decade before the market could absorb it.

What Xerox did fail at was strategy. They could have licensed the technology, spun out companies, partnered with smaller firms that understood the emerging PC market. Instead, they tried to control everything themselves and succeeded at nothing. Engineers left in frustration. Some joined Apple. Others started companies that would define Silicon Valley: Adobe, 3Com, Electronic Arts. They took PARC's culture and innovations with them.

By the mid-1980s, the damage was clear. Apple's Macintosh brought graphical computing to consumers. Microsoft's Windows brought it to businesses. Both built on foundations laid at PARC. Xerox's stock price stagnated while the computer industry exploded into a trillion-dollar market. The company that had funded the future was left selling copiers.

PARC itself survived, continuing to do influential research in materials science, ubiquitous computing, and digital services. In 2002, Xerox spun it out as an independent subsidiary. It still exists today, still innovating, still inventing technologies that others will commercialize. But its legend is frozen in those years between 1970 and 1980, when a room full of researchers in Palo Alto built the future and watched it walk out the door.

The story reveals something fundamental about innovation. Vision without execution is archaeology — interesting artifacts with no impact. PARC's researchers were right about everything. They saw clearly where computing was going. But seeing the future and profiting from it are entirely different skills. Xerox built the future. Apple sold it. Microsoft scaled it. And PARC became the most famous cautionary tale in technology: the lab that had it all and kept nothing.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Atari
entries.section: The video game pioneer that lost everything
entries.descriptor: The pioneer that invented the industry and then lost it
entries.content:
# Atari

***The pioneer that invented the industry and then lost it***

> **Years:** 1972-1996 (as independent entity)

---

Nolan Bushnell and Ted Dabney started Atari in 1972 with $500, a name from the Japanese board game Go, and a simple conviction: video games could be more than novelties in university labs. They had already built Computer Space, the first commercial arcade game, for another company. It flopped — too complicated for bar patrons. The lesson stuck. Their next game would be simple enough for anyone.

Pong was that game. A white dot bouncing between two paddles on a black screen. No instructions needed. Bushnell installed the first prototype in a Sunnyvale bar called Andy Capp's Tavern in September 1972. Within days, the machine stopped working. The problem: it was so popular the coin box had overflowed, jamming the mechanism. By 1973, Atari was shipping Pong machines as fast as they could build them. Competitors flooded in with clones, but Atari had won the race to define what an arcade game was. They had created an industry.

The home console followed inevitably. If people paid quarters to play in bars, they would pay hundreds to play at home. Atari released the home Pong console for Christmas 1975 through a Sears exclusive. It sold 150,000 units. The company was growing so fast Bushnell could barely manage it. Money flowed in, but also out — on R&D, manufacturing, ambitious bets. In 1976, needing capital and structure, Bushnell sold Atari to Warner Communications for $28 million.

The sale gave Atari resources to attempt something audacious: a programmable home console. The Atari 2600, released in 1977, didn't ship with a fixed set of games. It played cartridges. New games meant new sales, forever. The business model was brilliant. The execution was troubled. The 2600 launched slow. Games were scarce, graphics crude, price high. For two years it looked like an expensive mistake.

Then Space Invaders arrived. Atari licensed the arcade hit for the 2600 in 1980. Sales exploded. Suddenly every household wanted one. The 2600 became the center of living rooms. Parents who had never touched a joystick found themselves playing. By 1982, Atari was generating $2 billion in revenue, more than the entire film industry's box office. It was the fastest-growing company in American history.

But the foundation was cracking. Warner treated Atari like a content business, greenlighting everything. Quality collapsed under volume. The company released hundreds of games, most forgettable or broken. Retailers stocked shelves with trash alongside treasures. Consumers lost the ability to distinguish. Third-party developers flooded in, churned out shovelware, and eroded trust in the platform. Atari's own games grew sloppy. They rushed E.T. The Extra-Terrestrial to market in five weeks for Christmas 1982. It was almost unplayable. They manufactured more cartridges than consoles in existence. Most ended up in landfills.

Meanwhile, competitors arrived with superior hardware. Coleco and Mattel released consoles that made the 2600 look ancient. Personal computers — Commodore, Apple — offered more power and flexibility. Atari's next-generation machines, the 5200 and later the 7800, launched poorly and late. The 5200's controllers broke constantly. The 7800 sat in warehouses for two years during corporate turmoil.

The crash came in 1983. Retailers, burned by overstock and returns, slashed orders. Atari's revenue fell from $2 billion to $338 million in a year. Warner took a $536 million loss. The company laid off thousands. The press declared video games a fad that had ended. Parents agreed. The unsold inventory — pallets of E.T. cartridges and other failures — was buried in a New Mexico landfill, a literal grave for the industry Atari had created.

Warner split Atari in two in 1984. The home console and computer division was sold to Jack Tramiel, the hard-edged founder of Commodore. The arcade division stayed with Warner briefly before also being sold. Tramiel ran Atari as a lean, almost vindictive operation, slashing costs and pushing new computers — the ST line — that were excellent machines sold into a market that no longer trusted the name. Atari limped through the late 1980s, releasing the Lynx handheld and the Jaguar console, both technically impressive and commercial disasters. By then, Nintendo and Sega owned what Atari had built.

The Jaguar, released in 1993 as the first 64-bit console, was Atari's last stand. It launched with a handful of games and a controller with more buttons than sense. Developers ignored it. Consumers didn't care. In 1996, Atari Corporation merged with JTS, a disk drive manufacturer. The video game business was over. The name would be bought and sold for years by companies hoping nostalgia had value. It did, but only as a memory.

Atari invented the video game industry, proved it could be bigger than Hollywood, and then disintegrated through mismanagement, greed, and failure to adapt. They had built the market and the platform, but treated both as if they would last forever without care. By the time they realized quality mattered, trust was gone. Nintendo learned the lesson — strict licensing, quality control, first-party excellence — and built an empire on Atari's grave.

---

*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Microsoft
entries.descriptor: From garage to monopoly to reinvention
entries.content:
# Microsoft

***From garage to monopoly to reinvention***

> **Years:** 1975-present

In December 1974, Paul Allen walked past a newsstand in Harvard Square and saw the cover of Popular Electronics: the Altair 8800, the first commercially available personal computer. He bought the magazine and ran to find his friend Bill Gates. They both understood immediately. The computer industry was about to explode, and it would need software. They called MITS, the Altair's manufacturer, and claimed they had written a BASIC interpreter for the machine. They hadn't. They didn't even have an Altair. But MITS was interested, so they built the interpreter in eight weeks, using a simulator Allen wrote, and tested it for the first time when Allen flew to Albuquerque to demonstrate it. It worked.

Gates dropped out of Harvard. They founded Micro-Soft in Albuquerque in 1975, hyphen later dropped. The early years were pure execution: writing BASIC interpreters for every microcomputer that emerged. Their insight was simple but not obvious: software would be more valuable than hardware. The machine would become commodity. The programs would be the prize.

The turn came in 1980. IBM, late to personal computing and desperate to catch up, needed an operating system for its PC. They went first to Digital Research, whose CP/M dominated microcomputers. The meeting went badly — founder Gary Kildall allegedly flew his plane instead of meeting IBM. IBM asked Microsoft if they could provide an operating system. Gates said yes, though Microsoft didn't have one. He bought QDOS ("Quick and Dirty Operating System") from Seattle Computer Products for $75,000, modified it, and licensed it to IBM as PC-DOS. Critically, Gates retained the right to license the OS to other manufacturers as MS-DOS.

When IBM launched the PC in 1981, it became the standard. And when clones emerged — Compaq, Dell, dozens of others — they all needed MS-DOS. Microsoft had positioned itself as the tollbooth on the industry's main road. By 1983, thirty percent of computers ran Microsoft software. By 1985, that number was seventy percent.

Windows, released in 1985, was the next expansion. It was initially terrible — a graphical shell over DOS that crashed constantly. Apple, which had pioneered the graphical interface with the Mac in 1984, sued for copyright infringement. But Microsoft iterated, and Windows 3.0 in 1990 finally worked well enough to matter. By then, the installed base of DOS machines meant Windows could spread like fire through dry grass. Apple's closed ecosystem — beautiful, integrated, expensive — couldn't compete with Windows' chaotic ubiquity.

The 1990s were dominance. Windows 95 was a cultural event. Office became the standard for productivity software, leveraging Windows' market position. Internet Explorer, bundled free with Windows, destroyed Netscape's browser business. Microsoft's strategy was ruthlessly effective: embrace, extend, extinguish. Identify a successful product category, create a competing version, bundle it with Windows, iterate until the competitor died.

The problem was that this looked exactly like monopoly behavior. The Department of Justice filed antitrust charges in 1998. The trial was a disaster for Microsoft. Internal emails revealed executives discussing strategies to "cut off Netscape's air supply." Gates, deposed for days, was evasive and combative. In 2000, Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson ruled that Microsoft was a monopoly that had abused its power and ordered the company split in two. The decision was overturned on appeal in 2001, but Microsoft accepted a consent decree limiting its behavior.

The ruling came just as Microsoft was missing the future. Google launched in 1998 and became the gateway to the internet that Explorer never was. The iPod arrived in 2001, the iPhone in 2007, and Microsoft's mobile strategy — Windows Mobile, then Windows Phone — failed completely. Social media emerged with Facebook in 2004, and Microsoft's attempts (MSN, Windows Live) went nowhere. Cloud computing began its rise with Amazon Web Services in 2006. Microsoft's response, Windows Azure, was late and initially incoherent.

Gates stepped down as CEO in 2000, though he remained chairman. His successor, Steve Ballmer, was a brilliant operator of the existing business but struggled with new paradigms. Revenue grew — Windows and Office printed money — but the company felt slow, defensive, consumed by internal politics. By 2013, when Ballmer announced his retirement, Microsoft's stock had been essentially flat for a decade while Apple and Google soared.

Satya Nadella, who became CEO in 2014, inherited a company in crisis of relevance. His response was philosophical and practical. He declared Microsoft a "mobile-first, cloud-first" company, even though Microsoft had no mobile platform worth mentioning. He meant the world was mobile-first, and Microsoft needed to serve that world. Office came to iPad and iPhone. Windows became less central to Microsoft's identity. Most dramatically, Nadella embraced open source — once called "a cancer" by Ballmer — and focused the company on Azure, the cloud platform.

It worked. Azure became the second-largest cloud platform after AWS. Office 365's subscription model created predictable recurring revenue. Microsoft bought LinkedIn for $26 billion in 2016 and GitHub for $7.5 billion in 2018, integrating them without destroying their value. The company's market capitalization, which had stagnated around $250 billion for years, reached $3 trillion by 2024, briefly making Microsoft the world's most valuable company.

The arc is unusual. Most companies that achieve Microsoft's dominance in the 1990s either collapse (Nokia, BlackBerry) or calcify (IBM, Oracle). Microsoft nearly calcified. The consent decree constrained its monopoly tactics just as the industry shifted to platforms it didn't control. But where other aging giants clung to past glories, Nadella forced a pivot. Microsoft remains massive, profitable, and relevant fifty years after its founding — not because it won every battle, but because it survived enough defeats to learn humility.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Apple
entries.descriptor: From garage to empire: The improbable resurrection that redefined consumer technology
entries.content:
# Apple

***From garage to empire: The improbable resurrection that redefined consumer technology***

> **Years:** 1976-present

In 1976, Steve Wozniak built a computer for himself. He showed it to his friend Steve Jobs at the Homebrew Computer Club in Palo Alto. Jobs saw something Wozniak didn't: people would pay for this. Not hobbyists soldering their own machines — regular people who wanted a computer that just worked. They sold Wozniak's VW van and Jobs's calculator to raise $1,300, built fifty units in Jobs's parents' garage, and created Apple Computer Company.

The Apple I was a circuit board. The Apple II, released in 1977, was the insight made real: a complete computer in a plastic case with a keyboard, color graphics, and eight expansion slots. It came assembled. You plugged it in. For the first time, non-engineers could own computers. Schools bought them. Families bought them. VisiCalc, the first spreadsheet program, ran on it and turned the Apple II into a business tool. By 1980, Apple was printing money.

Then the world shifted. IBM entered the personal computer market in 1981 with an open architecture that let other manufacturers build compatible machines. Microsoft licensed its operating system to anyone. Suddenly there were dozens of PC makers, all competing on price. Apple kept its hardware and software closed, controlling everything, charging premium prices. The market chose abundance and affordability over elegance and control.

Jobs had already moved on to the Macintosh, convinced that the future was graphical interfaces and mice, not command lines. He was right about the destination but wrong about the path. The Mac launched in 1984 with a legendary Super Bowl ad positioning Apple as the rebel against IBM's conformity. The computer itself was beautiful, revolutionary, and fatally constrained — underpowered, unexpandable, overpriced. Developers stayed with the PC. Businesses stayed with the PC. The Mac became a niche product for designers and educators.

Jobs's intensity had created Apple's early success. Now it was destroying the company. He fought with CEO John Sculley, whom he'd recruited from Pepsi with the line, "Do you want to sell sugar water for the rest of your life, or do you want to come with me and change the world?" In 1985, the board sided with Sculley. Jobs was stripped of power, then left entirely.

Apple spent the next eleven years searching for an identity. It licensed Mac OS to other manufacturers, then reversed course. It tried portable devices, digital cameras, gaming consoles — products that presaged the future but arrived too early or executed too poorly. Microsoft Windows improved with each version, borrowing the Mac's graphical interface ideas while running on cheaper hardware. Apple's market share cratered. By 1997, the company was ninety days from bankruptcy.

Microsoft, ironically, needed Apple alive. A monopoly investigation loomed, and Microsoft could point to Apple as proof of competition. Bill Gates invested $150 million in Apple in exchange for patent cross-licensing and a promise that Microsoft Office would continue on the Mac. It was humiliating and necessary.

Apple needed a new operating system. Its own efforts had failed. The company agreed to buy NeXT, the computer company Jobs had founded after leaving Apple. The acquisition was as much about Jobs as the technology. He returned as advisor, then interim CEO, then CEO.

Jobs cut immediately. He killed dozens of products, reducing Apple's lines to four: consumer and professional, desktop and portable. He convinced Jony Ive, a designer ready to quit, to stay. Together they created the iMac — a translucent, colorful all-in-one computer that looked like nothing else in the beige world of PCs. It was unapologetically different. It sold. Apple stopped bleeding.

But selling Macs to loyalists wouldn't build a company. Jobs saw something others missed: people wanted their music everywhere, and the MP3 players on the market were terrible. In 2001, Apple released the iPod. It held 1,000 songs, synced with iTunes on your Mac, and fit in your pocket. "1,000 songs in your pocket" was the entire pitch. Within two years, the iPod was selling faster than Macs.

Then Jobs broke iPod's platform. In 2003, Apple launched iTunes for Windows. Mac purists revolted, but Jobs understood: iPod's market wasn't Mac owners, it was everyone. Letting Windows users buy iPods and music from iTunes turned Apple into a music company that happened to make computers. By 2006, iPod was half of Apple's revenue.

Jobs took the insight further. The iPod was a phone without the phone. What if you added the phone? Carriers controlled mobile phones, dictating features and design. Jobs convinced AT&T to let Apple control the entire experience in exchange for exclusivity. The iPhone launched in 2007: a glass slab with no keyboard, no stylus, just a touchscreen that responded to fingers. It was iPod, phone, and internet device in one. Developers initially had no access. When Apple opened the App Store in 2008, tens of thousands of apps appeared in months. The iPhone became the computer in everyone's pocket.

The iPad followed in 2010. Critics called it a big iPhone. Jobs called it the future of computing. Both were right. Apple Watch, AirPods, services — each extended the ecosystem. Every product integrated with every other product, creating switching costs and reinforcing loyalty. The closed system that nearly killed Apple in the 1980s became its moat in the 2000s.

Jobs died in 2011. Tim Cook, his successor, lacked Jobs's product vision but excelled at operations and scale. Apple became the first trillion-dollar company, then the first two-trillion-dollar company. It prints money from services — App Store fees, subscriptions, advertising. It has more cash than most countries' GDP.

The company that almost died became the most valuable company on Earth by doing what Jobs insisted on from the start: controlling the entire experience, charging premium prices, and making products so good that people accept the constraints. The difference between 1985 and 2025 isn't the strategy. It's that the market finally valued what Apple was selling.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Oracle
entries.descriptor: The database company that became an empire by selling to enterprises that had no choice
entries.content:
# Oracle

***The database company that became an empire by selling to enterprises that had no choice***

> **Years:** 1977-present

In 1977, Larry Ellison was a 32-year-old programmer who had never finished college and had bounced between jobs. He had worked at Ampex, where he helped build a database for the CIA codenamed "Oracle." When IBM published a research paper describing a "relational database" — a new way to organize and query data — Ellison saw something others missed: IBM had published the blueprint but wasn't selling the product.

Ellison founded Software Development Laboratories with Bob Miner and Ed Oates. They had $2,000. Their bet was simple: build what IBM described, get it to market first, and sell it to the corporations who desperately needed it. Data was exploding. Companies were drowning in information they couldn't access. The existing systems were nightmares of rigid hierarchies and custom code.

They built Oracle Version 1 but never released it — too buggy. Version 2 shipped in 1979, the first commercial relational database, though it was really the first version. The numbering was marketing. It worked well enough. More importantly, it worked on different kinds of computers. IBM's systems locked you into IBM hardware. Oracle ran anywhere. For corporate IT departments trying to escape vendor lock-in, this mattered.

The early customers were intelligence agencies and defense contractors. Organizations with massive data problems and budgets to match. Oracle was expensive and difficult, but the alternatives were worse. The company grew fast — too fast. Ellison hired armies of salespeople and paid them commissions on signed contracts, not collected revenue. Sales exploded. So did accounting chaos.

Through the 1980s, Oracle became the standard enterprise database. Not because it was elegant — it wasn't. Not because it was easy — it was notoriously complex. But because in the enterprise, "nobody ever got fired for buying Oracle." The switching costs were enormous. Once your data lived in Oracle, migration was a multi-year project. Oracle knew this. Their pricing reflected it.

Ellison ran the company like a sales organization that happened to write software. The culture was aggressive, competitive, ruthless. Salespeople were ranked publicly. The weak were culled. The strong became millionaires. Product quality mattered less than landing the next Fortune 500 contract. Oracle's software was powerful but brittle. Implementations took years and cost millions. An entire consulting industry grew up around making Oracle actually work.

By 1990, Oracle was a giant. Then it nearly collapsed. The aggressive accounting caught up with them. They had booked revenue on deals that weren't real, contracts that wouldn't be paid, implementations that would never complete. In 1990, the company announced its first-ever quarterly loss. The stock crashed. Lawsuits piled up. Layoffs gutted the workforce. Ellison brought in professional management to clean up the mess.

The turnaround worked because the underlying business was sound. Customers were locked in. New customers had nowhere else to go. Microsoft was trying to compete from below with cheaper databases. IBM was there with older technology. But Oracle owned the high end — the massive enterprise deployments where cost wasn't the primary concern. Reliability, support, and fear of switching were what mattered.

The company rebuilt itself around its dominance. Oracle Database became the foundation of an empire. Through the 1990s and 2000s, Ellison went on an acquisition spree, buying enterprise software companies: PeopleSoft, Siebel, BEA Systems, Sun Microsystems. Each acquisition was controversial. Each expanded Oracle's grip. They sold not just databases but entire enterprise stacks — HR systems, customer management, hardware, operating systems.

The strategy was vertical integration for the enterprise. Sell everything a large company needs to run its technology. Make it work together — mostly. Charge for everything. The sales pitch was simplicity: one vendor, one throat to choke. The reality was complexity: sprawling software portfolios that overlapped and competed internally. But enterprises bought it because the alternative was managing dozens of vendors themselves.

The open-source movement threatened this model. MySQL and PostgreSQL offered free databases that worked well enough for many use cases. Cloud computing threatened it more. Amazon Web Services launched database services that were easier to use and cheaper to run. Oracle was late to cloud, dismissive of it, then panicked. Ellison called cloud computing "complete gibberish" in 2008. By 2012, Oracle was pivoting desperately toward cloud services.

The transition was rocky. Oracle's business model was built on massive perpetual licenses and maintenance fees. Cloud meant subscriptions and lower margins. Their first cloud offerings were clunky — often just their old software running in data centers they now called "the cloud." But they had advantages: existing customers with sunk costs, enterprise relationships, and a sales force that knew how to sell fear.

Today, Oracle is a $200 billion company. Not because it innovates fastest or builds the best technology. Because it captured enterprises decades ago and never let go. The database is still at the core, still expensive, still hard to escape. The cloud business is growing — more slowly than competitors but growing. Ellison is one of the richest people alive, famous for his yachts and his fighter jets and his Hawaiian island.

The Oracle story is about lock-in as moat, aggression as strategy, and timing the market before product perfection. They weren't first to databases, but first to market with the right technology. They weren't the best engineers, but the best at selling to people who couldn't afford to be wrong. The path was dominance through indispensability, maintained through decades of acquisitions and aggressive defense of territory.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: 3Com
entries.descriptor: The networking pioneer that won too early and lost the next war
entries.content:
# 3Com

***The networking pioneer that won too early and lost the next war***

> **Years:** 1979-2010

In 1979, Robert Metcalfe had a problem. He'd invented Ethernet at Xerox PARC — a way to connect computers over local networks using coaxial cable — and watched Xerox fumble the commercialization. The personal computer was arriving. Every company would need networks. And the inventor of the core technology was sitting on the sidelines.

Metcalfe left Xerox and founded 3Com with Howard Charney and others. The name stood for "Computer Communication Compatibility." The pitch was simple: we will connect all these computers that are suddenly appearing on desks. We will make Ethernet the standard. We will build the networking industry.

The timing was perfect. IBM had just launched the PC. Companies were buying them by the dozen. But PCs sitting alone on desks were just expensive typewriters. Connected together, sharing printers and files, they became infrastructure. 3Com sold Ethernet adapter cards that slotted into PCs. By 1984, they were public. By the late 1980s, Ethernet had defeated Token Ring and other competing standards. 3Com had won.

The victory came from strategic patience. Metcalfe made Ethernet an open standard — anyone could build Ethernet products. This seemed counterintuitive. Why not lock it up? But openness meant widespread adoption. It meant Ethernet became the default. And 3Com, with its head start and expertise, could sell premium products into a growing market. They didn't need to own the standard. They needed the standard to win.

Through the 1980s, 3Com expanded. They acquired companies. They built modems, routers, switches — the full stack of networking gear. When companies needed to connect their offices, they called 3Com. The company became synonymous with networking itself.

Then came the hubs. In the early 1990s, 3Com bought several companies making network hubs and switches. These were the devices that sat in server rooms and connected dozens or hundreds of computers. Higher margins than adapter cards. Stickier relationships with IT departments. 3Com's revenue climbed past a billion dollars. They seemed unstoppable.

But they were fighting the last war. A new kind of networking was emerging: enterprise-scale switching and routing, the infrastructure that connected entire corporate campuses and, soon, the internet itself. This required different technology — faster, more sophisticated, more expensive. It required selling to a different buyer: not the PC user or small business, but the CIO of a Fortune 500 company.

Cisco saw this future. They built routers for this new scale. They hired enterprise sales teams. They positioned themselves not as a PC networking company but as the infrastructure provider for the internet age. By the mid-1990s, Cisco was eating 3Com's lunch in the high end.

3Com tried to respond. In 1995, they spun out Palm Computing, the handheld device maker they'd acquired. Palm became a phenomenon — the PalmPilot defined the PDA category. For a moment, 3Com looked visionary. They'd moved beyond networking into computing itself. The stock soared.

But Palm was a distraction from the core problem. 3Com's networking business was being commoditized from below and out-innovated from above. Cheap Asian manufacturers flooded the market with Ethernet cards and hubs. Cisco owned the high-end routing and switching market. 3Com was squeezed in the middle.

In 2000, 3Com spun out Palm to shareholders. It was a tacit admission: the future wasn't in 3Com's networking business. The split left 3Com as a pure networking play just as the dot-com bubble burst. Revenue collapsed. The company that had defined networking in the 1980s was now struggling to matter in the 2000s.

They tried everything. They pivoted to wireless. They tried to build enterprise switches to compete with Cisco. They acquired companies and divested divisions. Nothing stuck. The problem was fundamental: 3Com had won the first networking war so decisively that they couldn't see the second war was different. They kept optimizing for adapter cards and small business hubs while Cisco built the infrastructure of the internet.

There was a brief resurgence. In 2003, 3Com formed a joint venture with Huawei, the Chinese telecom giant. The partnership gave 3Com access to low-cost manufacturing and new products. For a few years, it worked. Revenue stabilized. The company looked like it might survive.

But by 2007, it was clear 3Com was a shell. The brand still had value — nostalgia, installed base, enterprise relationships. But the technology was increasingly irrelevant. Hewlett-Packard tried to buy 3Com for $2.7 billion but the deal collapsed over regulatory concerns about the Huawei connection. In 2009, Huawei sold its stake. A few months later, HP tried again. This time, the deal closed for $2.7 billion — a fraction of 3Com's peak market cap.

HP wanted the enterprise networking assets to compete with Cisco. They rebranded 3Com's products under the HP name. By 2010, the 3Com brand was retired. The company that invented Ethernet, that won the first networking war, that seemed destined to own computer communication for decades — it simply ceased to exist.

What killed 3Com wasn't failure. It was success. They won so completely in the 1980s that they couldn't adapt when the rules changed in the 1990s. They kept selling to small businesses when the real money moved to enterprises. They kept optimizing products when the game became platforms. They kept thinking of networking as a PC problem when it became an internet problem.

Cisco didn't beat 3Com with better technology. They beat them by understanding that the market had shifted. The companies that survived weren't the ones who won the early battles. They were the ones who recognized when the war had changed.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Adobe
entries.descriptor: From desktop publishing revolution to creative cloud empire
entries.content:
# Adobe

***From desktop publishing revolution to creative cloud empire***

> **Years:** 1982-present

In December 1982, John Warnock and Charles Geschke left Xerox PARC with a technology their former employer had no interest in commercializing. They had developed PostScript, a programming language that could describe the precise layout of text and graphics on a printed page. Xerox saw it as a solution without a market. Warnock and Geschke saw it as the foundation of a new industry.

They named their company Adobe, after the creek that ran behind Warnock's house. The first challenge was survival. PostScript was elegant technology, but in 1982, personal computers couldn't do much with documents beyond displaying green text on black screens. The founders needed someone to build hardware that could bring PostScript to life.

Steve Jobs provided the lifeline. Apple was developing the LaserWriter, a printer that would cost $7,000 but produce output that looked professionally typeset. Jobs licensed PostScript for the LaserWriter, giving Adobe $2.5 million in advance payments and a per-unit royalty. When the LaserWriter shipped in 1985 alongside the Macintosh and Aldus PageMaker, desktop publishing was born. What had required $100,000 in typesetting equipment could now be done with $10,000 in computer hardware.

Adobe went public in 1986. Revenue came almost entirely from PostScript licensing deals with printer manufacturers. It was a good business, but Warnock and Geschke understood the trap: they were dependent on hardware companies who might eventually decide to build their own page description languages. They needed to control more of the value chain.

The answer was software. In 1987, Adobe released Illustrator, a drawing program for the Macintosh. It let designers create the kind of graphics that PostScript could describe. Then came Photoshop, acquired from brothers Thomas and John Knoll in 1988 for what would prove to be an absurdly low price. Adobe didn't invent Photoshop—Thomas Knoll wrote the first version as a graduate student project—but Adobe saw what it could become.

Through the 1990s, Adobe assembled what would become the Creative Suite: Premiere for video editing, After Effects for motion graphics, InDesign to finally kill QuarkXPress's dominance in page layout. Each acquisition or development extended Adobe's reach into creative workflows. The strategy was ecosystem lock-in through workflow integration. A designer who learned Photoshop would want Illustrator. A design shop using Illustrator would want InDesign. The tools talked to each other in ways that competitors' products didn't.

But the business model was showing cracks. Adobe sold perpetual licenses—pay once, own forever. Customers would skip versions, sometimes several in a row, waiting for features that mattered to them. Revenue was cyclical, spiking with major releases and slumping between. Wall Street hated the unpredictability. Piracy was rampant; Creative Suite cost thousands of dollars, putting it out of reach for students and small studios who simply copied it instead.

In 2011, Adobe announced a subscription model. Creative Suite would become Creative Cloud—$50 per month for access to all Adobe's creative tools, always updated, cloud storage included. The reaction was volcanic. Petitions gathered hundreds of thousands of signatures. Designers felt betrayed. They didn't want to rent software. They wanted to own it.

Adobe didn't blink. In 2013, they stopped selling perpetual licenses entirely. Creative Cloud or nothing.

The financial transformation was dramatic. By 2015, Adobe had more Creative Cloud subscribers than they'd ever had Creative Suite customers. Revenue became predictable, recurring, growing. The subscription model did something perpetual licenses never could: it made Adobe indispensable rather than just useful. Stop paying, lose access to years of work files. The switching cost became infinite.

The pivot to cloud did something else—it democratized access. At $50 per month, students and small studios could actually afford legitimate Adobe software. The piracy problem evaporated. A generation of designers who might have used pirated copies or switched to cheaper alternatives became paying customers instead.

But Adobe's dominance created complacency. Feature development slowed. Bugs persisted across versions. Competitors emerged—Sketch for interface design, Figma for collaborative design, Affinity for photo editing—all faster, cheaper, or more innovative. Adobe responded not with better products but with acquisition attempts. In 2022, they announced a $20 billion deal to buy Figma, the collaborative design tool that had emerged as the first genuine threat to Adobe's design monopoly in decades.

Regulators in the U.S. and EU saw a monopolist trying to buy its way out of competition. By 2024, facing certain regulatory rejection, Adobe abandoned the acquisition and paid Figma a $1 billion termination fee.

Today Adobe is worth over $200 billion. Creative Cloud has 32 million subscribers generating $20 billion in annual revenue. The tools that desktop publishers used in 1985—PostScript, Illustrator, Photoshop—remain industry standards forty years later. But the company faces an uncomfortable reality: it succeeded by making itself essential, then locked customers in with subscriptions. Now it must innovate to keep them, and innovation was never what Adobe did best. What Adobe did best was consolidate.

The path from PostScript to Creative Cloud wasn't inevitable. It required seeing that the real business wasn't printers or software licenses but creative workflows themselves. Adobe built the tools, then built the trap, then made the trap so comfortable that customers stayed willingly. Until they don't.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Sun Microsystems
entries.descriptor: "The workstation that built the internet, then missed its successor"
entries.content:
# Sun Microsystems

***"The workstation that built the internet, then missed its successor"***

> **Years:** 1982-2010

In 1982, a Stanford graduate student named Andy Bechtolsheim designed a computer workstation so powerful it could run CAD software and handle complex calculations that required mainframes. He built it for himself because nothing on the market could do what he needed. Three fellow Stanford affiliates—Vinod Khosla, Scott McNealy, and Bill Joy—saw what he'd made and understood its market. They founded Sun Microsystems in February 1982. The name stood for Stanford University Network.

The timing was perfect. Engineers and researchers needed computing power but couldn't afford minicomputers from DEC or Data General. Sun's approach was radically different: use off-the-shelf components, run Unix instead of proprietary operating systems, and price aggressively. The first Sun-1 workstation shipped in 1982. Within two years, Sun had revenue over $8 million. By 1986, they were public with a market cap over $200 million.

Bill Joy, a Berkeley Unix developer who'd helped create BSD, became Sun's chief scientist and the company's philosophical center. He believed in open systems—computers that talked to each other regardless of manufacturer. In 1984, Sun introduced NFS (Network File System), which let different computers share files seamlessly. They published the specification openly. Competitors could adopt it. Most did. It became an industry standard, and suddenly Sun workstations were essential infrastructure.

The real victory came with SPARC, Sun's microprocessor design introduced in 1987. Unlike competitors who used Intel or Motorola chips, Sun designed its own and licensed the architecture openly. Anyone could build SPARC chips. The openness was strategic—it prevented vendor lock-in and attracted partners. But Sun kept the highest-performing versions for itself. SPARC servers became the backbone of emerging internet infrastructure.

By the mid-1990s, Sun owned technical computing. Their servers ran Wall Street trading floors, telecom switches, and the databases behind early e-commerce. The tagline was "The Network Is The Computer"—prescient when few saw computing becoming fundamentally distributed. Scott McNealy, CEO since 1984, was Sun's public face: combative, confident, deeply anti-Microsoft. He testified against Microsoft in antitrust hearings. He mocked Windows as unstable. Sun's customers were engineers and IT managers who shared his disdain for desktop computing compromises.

Then came Java. In 1995, Sun released a programming language designed by James Gosling that could run on any platform—write once, run anywhere. Java arrived exactly as the web exploded. It powered early web applications, enterprise systems, and eventually billions of mobile phones. Sun gave it away. No licensing fees for developers. The strategy was to use Java to sell high-margin servers and services. It worked spectacularly for server sales. Java became ubiquitous. But Sun never figured out how to capture the value it created.

The dot-com boom was Sun's zenith. Every startup needed servers. Sun's high-end systems cost $1 million each. Revenue hit $18.3 billion in 2001. Market cap peaked near $200 billion. McNealy was fortune's favorite. But the economics were already shifting. Companies like Google were building server farms from cheap Intel-based machines running Linux. They bought thousands of commodity servers instead of dozens of Sun's premium systems. The unit economics favored horizontal scaling over vertical.

When the dot-com bubble burst in 2000, Sun's revenue collapsed. Startups vanished. Enterprise customers cut spending. Sun's stock fell 90%. The company that rode the internet's rise suddenly looked overexposed to its decline. Revenue dropped to $11 billion by 2003. Layoffs came in waves. McNealy remained optimistic—the network would rebound, Sun's technology was superior, competitors would stumble.

But the market had moved. Linux on commodity Intel servers wasn't just cheaper—it was good enough. Open source had become the real open system, not Sun's controlled openness. Amazon launched AWS in 2006, and suddenly startups didn't buy servers at all. Java remained everywhere, generating zero revenue. Sun acquired MySQL for $1 billion in 2008, trying to embrace open source after fighting it for years. It was too late and too expensive.

The 2008 financial crisis finished what structural shifts began. Sun reported a $2.2 billion loss in fiscal 2009. IBM offered $7 billion to acquire them in 2009, then walked away over antitrust concerns and price disagreements. Oracle emerged as the buyer, paying $7.4 billion in January 2010.

Oracle wanted Java and Solaris. Larry Ellison closed the acquisition in April 2010 and began dismantling Sun. The hardware business was gutted. SPARC development slowed to maintenance mode. The workforce was cut by half. The Sun campus in Menlo Park was sold. The brand disappeared. Oracle extracted what it needed—database integration, middleware, Java licensing leverage against Google—and discarded the rest.

Sun's technology still runs critical systems. Java powers Android and enterprise applications worldwide. Solaris lives on in specialized niches. ZFS influenced modern file systems. But as a company, Sun demonstrated how building essential infrastructure doesn't guarantee capturing its value. They created open systems that let customers avoid vendor lock-in, then discovered their customers took the lesson seriously. They dominated a market—technical workstations—that became obsolete. They saw the network future clearly but built it on premium hardware when commodity systems would win.

The founders became legends elsewhere. Khosla became a prominent VC. Joy advised startups. Bechtolsheim's check to Google—literally the company's first funding—became Silicon Valley lore. McNealy remained Sun's defender, arguing Oracle got a bargain, insisting Sun's vision was correct even if its execution stumbled. He wasn't entirely wrong. The network did become the computer. Sun just didn't survive to see it completely realized, or couldn't adapt when it arrived differently than imagined.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Compaq
entries.descriptor: The clone that became the original
entries.content:
# Compaq

***The clone that became the original***

> **Years:** 1982-2002

In 1981, three senior managers from Texas Instruments met for lunch at a Houston pie shop. Rod Canion, Jim Harris, and Bill Murto weren't looking to start a revolution. They were looking for a business opportunity in the sudden gold rush around the IBM PC, announced that August. By the time coffee arrived, Canion had sketched a portable computer on a paper placemat. The sketch looked like a sewing machine with a handle. It would become the fastest company to reach $1 billion in sales.

The timing was everything. IBM had done something unprecedented: published the technical specifications of the PC. They assumed the money was in mainframes. The PC was almost an experiment. That openness created a window, and Canion's team saw it immediately. They weren't going to make a cheaper IBM PC clone — dozens of companies would try that and fail. They would make something IBM couldn't: a portable PC that ran all the same software. Take your work home. Take it on a plane. The value proposition was obvious to anyone who'd ever worked late.

They needed a name. Someone suggested "Gateway," but a company already used it. They tried "Compaq" — short for compatibility and compact. It sounded technical. It sounded serious. More importantly, it was available.

The first meeting with venture capitalists in January 1982 was supposed to be a preliminary conversation. Canion brought the placemat sketch and a business plan. Ben Rosen, a former analyst who'd become one of tech's most respected VCs, stopped him midway through the pitch. "How much do you need?" 

Canion had planned to ask for $1.5 million in a first round, maybe $3 million total. Rosen offered $2.5 million immediately. By the end of the week, they had commitments for $30 million. The portable PC market didn't exist yet, but Rosen understood what IBM's openness meant. The question wasn't whether PC clones would succeed, but which ones would win.

Compaq shipped its first portable in March 1983. The machine weighed 28 pounds — "portable" was generous — but it worked. More importantly, it ran every piece of IBM PC software perfectly. This was harder than it looked. IBM's BIOS — the basic input/output system that let software talk to hardware — was copyrighted. Compaq couldn't just copy it. They used "clean room" development: one team analyzed IBM's BIOS and wrote specifications, another team wrote new code from those specs, never seeing the original. The result was legally distinct but functionally identical.

The Compaq Portable sold for $2,995, more than an IBM PC. It didn't matter. First-year sales hit $111 million. The following year: $329 million. The year after: $504 million. Fastest company to reach $100 million. Fastest to $200 million. Fastest to $500 million. They went public in 1983. The stock soared.

But the portable market was a beachhead, not a destination. By 1985, Compaq was making desktop PCs that competed directly with IBM — and winning. They were faster. They used newer Intel chips before IBM did. They had better engineering. When Intel released the 386 chip in 1986, IBM waited. Compaq shipped a 386 machine in 1986. For the first time, a clone beat IBM to market with IBM-compatible technology. The student had become the teacher.

The shift was tectonic. For decades, IBM set standards and others followed. Now Compaq led and IBM scrambled to catch up. The PC market fractured. Dozens of clone makers competed on price, but Compaq competed on innovation. They were more expensive than generic clones but better than IBM. It was a viable middle position — for a while.

By the early 1990s, the market had changed. Dell was selling directly to customers, cutting out distributors and retailers, passing savings to buyers. Compaq was stuck in the traditional retail channel, with traditional margins. Dell's prices kept dropping. Compaq's sales slowed. The board pushed out Rod Canion in 1991. His replacement, Eckhard Pfeiffer, launched a price war. If Dell wanted to compete on cost, Compaq would compete on cost and scale. They bought Tandem Computers in 1997 for $3 billion. They bought Digital Equipment Corporation in 1998 for $9.6 billion.

The acquisitions were supposed to move Compaq beyond PCs into enterprise computing — servers, services, consulting. Instead, they created an unwieldy giant. Too many product lines. Too many overlapping technologies. Too much complexity. The PC business was commoditizing. The enterprise business was getting hammered by Sun, HP, and IBM. Pfeiffer was forced out in 1999.

The dot-com crash devastated demand. Dell kept winning on price. HP, under Carly Fiorina, was hungry for scale. In 2001, HP proposed a merger. The deal was controversial — Walter Hewlett, son of HP co-founder Bill Hewlett, led a public battle against it — but it closed in 2002. Compaq ceased to exist as an independent company. The brand lingered on some HP products for a few years, then disappeared.

Twenty years from paper placemat to Fortune 500 to gone. Compaq proved that speed and execution could beat incumbent giants. Then it proved that beating IBM didn't mean beating the market. The PC became a commodity. The companies that thrived sold on price or pivoted to services. Compaq tried both too late. The same openness that created the opportunity — IBM's published specs — made differentiation impossible. Eventually, every PC was the same. Compaq had been the best at making something that, in the end, couldn't be best at anything.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Dell
entries.descriptor: The direct model that bypassed retail and built an empire
entries.content:
# Dell

***The direct model that bypassed retail and built an empire***

> **Years:** 1984–present

In 1983, Michael Dell was a pre-med freshman at the University of Texas. He was also buying IBM PC parts, upgrading them in his dorm room, and selling them to local businesses at a markup. The insight wasn't complicated: computers were being sold through retail stores by salespeople who knew nothing about them, at prices inflated by middleman margins. Dell could build better machines, sell them cheaper, and still make money — if he cut out the retailers entirely.

He started PC's Limited in 1984 with $1,000. The model was simple: build to order, sell direct. A customer would call, specify what they wanted, and Dell would assemble it and ship it. No inventory sitting in warehouses. No retail markup. No guessing what configurations the market wanted. The customer told you, then you built it.

This wasn't just cheaper. It was faster. In 1985, while Compaq and IBM were forecasting demand six months out and building inventory accordingly, Dell was building machines after the customer paid. When component prices dropped — which they did constantly in the PC market — Dell's costs dropped immediately. Compaq was stuck selling inventory bought at last quarter's prices.

By 1986, the company was doing $60 million in revenue. Dell dropped out of school. The company went public in 1988 at a $30 million valuation. Within five years, it was worth over $2 billion.

The direct model became a case study taught in business schools. But the brilliance wasn't just the distribution strategy. It was how the entire company was organized around velocity. Dell didn't need to predict the future. It responded to the present in real time. Inventory turned over every six days. When Intel released a new chip, Dell had machines with that chip shipping within weeks. Compaq took months.

By the mid-1990s, Dell was the cost leader in PCs. It was also becoming the quality leader. The direct relationship with customers created a feedback loop. When something broke, Dell knew immediately. When customers wanted a new feature, Dell heard it first. The company wasn't designing for a hypothetical market. It was designing for actual buyers.

In 1996, Dell launched dell.com. Customers could configure and order online. Within two years, the website was doing $30 million in sales per day. The internet didn't just complement the direct model — it perfected it. The friction of phone orders disappeared. The cost of customer acquisition dropped. The flywheel spun faster.

By 2001, Dell was the largest PC maker in the world. It had surpassed Compaq, which had bought the leading direct seller, Gateway, in a panic and couldn't make the model work. Compaq got acquired by HP. Dell kept growing.

But the direct model had limits. It worked brilliantly for businesses buying in volume and tech-savvy consumers who knew what they wanted. It worked less well for mainstream consumers who wanted to touch a machine before buying or needed hand-holding. And it worked not at all for retail environments where impulse purchases and brand visibility mattered.

When laptops became consumer products — fashion statements as much as tools — Dell struggled. Apple was selling through gleaming stores where you could feel the hardware. Best Buy had endcaps and shelf space. Dell had a website and a call center. When smartphones and tablets began cannibalizing PC sales, Dell's core market shrank.

The company tried to diversify. It bought Perot Systems for $3.9 billion to expand into IT services. It acquired storage companies, software companies, anything to become more than a PC maker. The acquisitions were competent but not transformative. Dell was trying to pivot from a high-velocity hardware business to a slower-moving services business, and the cultures didn't mesh.

By 2013, the PC market was in decline. Dell's stock was underperforming. Michael Dell, who had returned as CEO in 2007, decided the company needed to transform without the scrutiny of public markets. He took Dell private in a $24.9 billion leveraged buyout — the largest tech LBO in history.

Private, Dell could make long-term bets. It acquired EMC in 2016 for $67 billion, the largest tech acquisition ever. EMC brought data storage, cloud infrastructure, and VMware. Dell was no longer a PC company. It was an enterprise infrastructure conglomerate.

The transformation worked, but expensively. The debt from the EMC deal was staggering. In 2018, Dell re-listed publicly through a complex transaction involving VMware. The company that had pioneered lean, fast operations was now a sprawling giant carrying tens of billions in debt, competing in cloud infrastructure against Amazon, Microsoft, and Google.

The direct model still runs inside Dell. Servers and enterprise gear are built to order, shipped fast, costs managed tightly. But the company Michael Dell built in his dorm room — the one that moved faster than everyone else because it didn't hold inventory — has become something different. It survived by becoming what it once disrupted: big, diversified, and slow.

The model that made Dell dominant in PCs turned out to be a perfect fit for one moment in technology history. When that moment passed, the company had to choose between remaining pure and remaining relevant. It chose survival.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Cisco
entries.descriptor: The acquirers who built an empire by buying the future
entries.content:
# Cisco

***The acquirers who built an empire by buying the future***

> **Years:** 1984-present

In December 1984, a husband and wife who worked at Stanford University wanted to send each other emails across campus. The problem was that Stanford's computer networks — like most networks everywhere — couldn't talk to each other. Leonard Bosack ran the computer science department's network. Sandy Lerner ran the business school's. They were separated by a few hundred yards and an insurmountable technical barrier.

They solved it in Bosack's living room. Using technology Bosack had helped develop at Stanford, they built a device that could route data packets between incompatible networks. It was called a router, and it made the internet possible. They called their company Cisco, after San Francisco, and funded it with credit cards.

Stanford wanted the technology. Bosack and Lerner wanted to commercialize it. The university's technology licensing office moved slowly. The couple moved fast. They began selling routers from their living room to other universities and research institutions that had the same problem. By 1986, they were doing $250,000 a month in sales. Stanford eventually licensed them the technology, taking an equity stake and royalties.

The timing was perfect in a way the founders couldn't have fully anticipated. In the late 1980s, the internet was transitioning from a research project to infrastructure. Organizations needed to connect networks. Cisco's routers were the only products that worked reliably at scale. The company grew 100% year-over-year. By 1990, they went public with $70 million in revenue.

Then the founders lost control. In 1990, the board — led by Sequoia Capital's Don Valentine, who had invested in 1987 — forced out Sandy Lerner over management style conflicts. Bosack, furious at his wife's firing, quit in protest. They sold their shares for $170 million and left. Valentine brought in John Morgridge as CEO. Morgridge brought in John Chambers from Wang Laboratories in 1991. In 1995, Chambers became CEO.

What happened next defined Cisco more than the router did. Chambers understood that the internet was going to explode and that Cisco couldn't build everything fast enough. So Cisco would buy everything. Between 1993 and 2000, Cisco acquired 71 companies. Not for their revenue or customer bases, but for their technology and engineering talent.

The acquisition machine had a method. Cisco targeted companies with 50-100 employees building complementary technologies — switching, security, wireless, optical networking. They paid premiums but moved fast. They kept founders and engineers, integrated them rapidly, and maintained velocity. The model worked because Cisco had the distribution. Small companies built innovation; Cisco gave them scale instantly.

The strategy matched the market perfectly. The internet was growing exponentially. Demand for networking equipment was insatiable. Companies needed routers, switches, security appliances — everything required to build the infrastructure of the digital economy. Cisco became the arms dealer of the dot-com boom. By 2000, Cisco was the most valuable company in the world, worth $555 billion.

Then the crash came. The dot-com bubble burst. The companies buying Cisco's equipment vanished overnight. Revenue fell 30%. The stock fell 86% from its peak. The acquisition spree stopped. Cisco laid off 18,000 employees.

But Cisco didn't die. The infrastructure it built remained essential. The internet kept growing, just more slowly. Chambers refocused on enterprise customers — banks, hospitals, corporations building internal networks. Cisco maintained gross margins above 60%, an almost unheard of level for hardware. The routers were expensive, but switching costs were enormous. Once a company built its network on Cisco equipment, replacing it meant replacing everything.

Cisco resumed acquiring, but differently. Larger deals now. In 2003, Linksys for home networking. In 2006, Scientific-Atlanta for video. In 2012, NDS for video software. Some worked; many didn't. The model that worked brilliantly in the 1990s — buying 50-person startups building point technologies — worked less well when Cisco tried to enter new markets entirely.

The 2010s brought new challenges. The cloud meant customers needed different architectures. Software-defined networking threatened to commoditize hardware. Competitors like Arista built faster, cheaper switches. Amazon, Google, and Microsoft built their own networking equipment. The internet Cisco built was being rebuilt without Cisco at the center.

Chambers stepped down in 2015. His successor, Chuck Robbins, pivoted toward software and subscriptions. More acquisitions: AppDynamics for $3.7 billion in 2017. Duo Security for $2.35 billion in 2018. Splunk for $28 billion in 2024 — Cisco's largest acquisition ever, a bet that security and observability are the future of networking.

Today, Cisco is worth $240 billion. Still massive, still profitable, still essential to how the internet works. But no longer the inevitable winner. The story of the next decade is whether Cisco can transform fast enough — from hardware to software, from products to platforms, from selling boxes to selling outcomes.

The company that made the internet possible by routing packets between incompatible networks must now figure out how to route itself between incompatible eras. The living room startup that became the most valuable company in the world did it by acquiring the future faster than anyone else. The question now is whether the future can still be bought.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: BlackBerry
entries.section: The smartphone that wasn't smart enough
entries.descriptor: The fortress that couldn't evolve
entries.content:
# BlackBerry

***The fortress that couldn't evolve***

> **Years:** 1984-2016 (as independent company)

In 1999, if you were important, you had a BlackBerry. By 2009, if you were important, you still had a BlackBerry — and probably an iPhone hidden in your other pocket. By 2016, BlackBerry had stopped making the devices that once defined mobile email, corporate security, and executive status. The company that owned enterprise mobility so completely that its devices were called "CrackBerries" couldn't survive the shift from communication tool to computing platform.

Research In Motion started in 1984 in Waterloo, Ontario, founded by Mike Lazaridis, an engineering prodigy who'd dropped out of university to build wireless technology. For over a decade, RIM was an engineering services company, building wireless modems and displaying point-of-sale terminals. Competent but unremarkable. The insight that changed everything came from observing the emerging pager market: people desperately wanted mobile communication, but pagers were one-way. Email was exploding in offices but remained tethered to desks. The combination seemed obvious — once someone thought of it.

The first BlackBerry device launched in 1999 as a two-way pager with email capability. Ugly, utilitarian, with a tiny screen and a keyboard that seemed designed for thumbs. Which it was — Lazaridis obsessed over the physical interface. The device pushed email instantly to the user rather than requiring them to check. For executives who lived and died by email, this was transformative. More important: RIM built the infrastructure to make it work securely through corporate firewalls. BlackBerry Enterprise Server became the moat. IT departments loved it. It was secure, manageable, controllable. Everything could be encrypted and remotely wiped.

The device spread through financial services and government like wildfire. By 2003, BlackBerry was an executive status symbol. By 2007, it had 10 million users. The physical keyboard made email fast — faster than any touchscreen could manage, everyone agreed. The devices were tools, not toys. They did one thing supremely well: secure, instant, mobile communication for professionals who needed to stay connected.

Then Steve Jobs walked onto a stage in January 2007 with a device that did everything BlackBerry didn't. The iPhone was beautiful where BlackBerry was functional, open where BlackBerry was controlled, a computer where BlackBerry was a communications device. It had a real web browser, real apps, real media. RIM's co-CEOs, Lazaridis and Jim Balsillie, famously dismissed it. The battery life was terrible. The keyboard was missing. It wasn't secure. Corporate IT would never approve it. They were right about all of this. They were also doomed.

The iPhone didn't beat BlackBerry in enterprise — at first. It beat BlackBerry in culture. Employees wanted iPhones. They bought them personally and demanded IT support them. "Bring Your Own Device" policies emerged not from strategic planning but from executives who wanted iPhones and had the authority to demand exceptions. The consumerization of IT inverted everything RIM had built its business on. Security and control, once advantages, became inconveniences to route around.

RIM's response was confusion masquerading as confidence. They released the BlackBerry Storm in 2008, a touchscreen device that clicked when you pressed it — a physical response to touch, as if the problem with touchscreens was that users needed mechanical feedback. It was terrible. They released the PlayBook tablet in 2011 without native email support. A BlackBerry tablet that couldn't do email without tethering to a BlackBerry phone. The company that had made mobile email ubiquitous shipped a tablet that treated email as an afterthought.

The deeper problem was architectural. BlackBerry 10, released in 2013 as the company's salvation, was a genuinely good operating system — modern, gesture-based, elegant. It arrived six years after the iPhone and three years after Android had flooded the market. Developers had already chosen iOS and Android. Apps were the new moat, and BlackBerry had none of the apps that mattered. The company tried everything: allowing Android apps to run on BlackBerry, paying developers to port apps, building their own versions of popular services. Nothing worked. Platform effects are brutal: users go where apps are, developers go where users are, and late entrants can't break the cycle without some massive advantage. BlackBerry's advantage — security, keyboard, corporate integration — no longer mattered enough.

The decline was swift once it started. Market share collapsed from 20% in 2009 to under 1% by 2016. The company that once had 80 million users was losing them at a rate of millions per quarter. Balsillie left in 2012. Lazaridis left shortly after. New CEO John Chen tried to pivot the company toward software and services — the enterprise security business still existed, still mattered. In 2016, BlackBerry announced it would stop designing its own hardware, licensing the brand to other manufacturers. The devices would continue, but the company behind them had moved on.

What killed BlackBerry wasn't that they built bad products. The Bold 9900, released in 2011, was perhaps the best physical keyboard phone ever made. What killed them was building for a world that had already ended. They optimized for corporate IT departments in an era when users had seized control. They perfected secure email in an era when apps had become the interface. They built a fortress when everyone else was building an ecosystem.

The company still exists, profitably, selling security software and services to enterprises and governments. The brand persists. But the path from dominance to irrelevance took less than a decade. BlackBerry was never disrupted by a better BlackBerry. It was disrupted by a different definition of what a mobile device should be.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Arm Holdings
entries.section: The chip designer in every phone
entries.descriptor: The architecture that conquered mobile by giving up control
entries.content:
# Arm Holdings

***The architecture that conquered mobile by giving up control***

> **Years:** 1990-present

In 1990, twelve engineers sat in a barn in Cambridge, England, with a problem that seemed trivial compared to the computing giants of the era. Apple needed a chip for its Newton handheld device. Acorn Computers, a British firm that had built respectable machines for schools, spun out a joint venture with Apple and VLSI Technology to design it. They called it Advanced RISC Machines — ARM.

The twelve engineers had no fabrication plants. No sales force worth mentioning. What they had was a processor architecture called RISC — Reduced Instruction Set Computing — that used fewer, simpler instructions than the complex chips Intel was building. RISC wasn't new. Academic researchers had explored it for years. But Acorn's implementation was unusually elegant. More importantly, it was unusually power-efficient.

The Newton failed. Apple's handheld was too expensive, too early, too clumsy. But the ARM chip inside it was good. Really good. It sipped power while competitors guzzled it. For devices that ran on batteries, this mattered enormously. The twelve engineers in the barn had accidentally built the architecture the mobile future would need.

In 1998, ARM made a decision that would define everything that followed: they would not manufacture chips. Ever. Intel made chips. Motorola made chips. Every serious semiconductor company made chips. ARM would design processor architectures and license them. Companies could license the design, modify it, manufacture it themselves, and ARM would take a royalty on every chip sold.

The decision came partly from necessity — fabrication plants cost billions — but it transformed necessity into strategy. By licensing rather than manufacturing, ARM eliminated the conflict that kept Intel from serving everyone. Intel competed with potential customers. ARM didn't. A company could license ARM's design and not worry that ARM would undercut them next quarter with a better chip.

The model seemed questionable. Royalties were small — pennies per chip. Volume would have to be massive. In the late 1990s, the PC was king, Intel was dominant, and mobile phones were simple devices making calls. Where would the volume come from?
