It came from phones. Nokia licensed ARM. Then Ericsson. Then everyone. As phones became smarter, they needed more processing power but couldn't sacrifice battery life. ARM's architecture delivered both. By the early 2000s, ARM processors were in nearly every mobile phone made. Not ARM chips — ARM designs, manufactured by dozens of companies under license.

When the iPhone launched in 2007, it ran on an ARM processor. So did Android phones. So did iPads and Kindles and fitness trackers and smart thermostats. The more devices became mobile or embedded, the more they needed ARM. The company that didn't manufacture anything became the foundation of mobile computing.

The licensing model created a flywheel. Every company that licensed ARM became invested in the ecosystem. Software written for ARM-based devices worked across platforms. Developers learned ARM. Tools improved. The architecture became standard not through monopoly but through ubiquity. Companies could compete fiercely on chip implementation while sharing the underlying architecture.

ARM went public in 1998, grew quietly through the 2000s, then exploded as smartphones devoured the world. By 2015, ARM-based chips were shipping at a rate of 15 billion per year. The company still didn't manufacture a single one.

Then came the acquisitions. In 2016, SoftBank bought ARM for $32 billion — a massive bet that ARM would power not just phones but the Internet of Things, autonomous vehicles, data centers. SoftBank's vision was intoxicating but vague. ARM continued growing, but the pressure to justify the price intensified. The licensing model remained, but strategic questions multiplied. Should ARM push into servers where Intel dominated? Should it hold tighter control over designs? SoftBank needed returns.

In 2020, Nvidia announced plans to acquire ARM for $40 billion. The deal promised to unite the dominant chip designer with the dominant GPU maker. It also threatened the open licensing model that made ARM valuable. Customers revolted. Regulators balked. China, the U.S., and Europe all raised competition concerns. In 2022, Nvidia abandoned the acquisition.

ARM went public again in 2023, this time on Nasdaq, raising $5 billion in the largest IPO of the year. The company SoftBank had bought for $32 billion was now valued at $54 billion. But the triumphant return masked deeper tensions. Apple, once ARM's partner, was designing its own ARM-based chips with less reliance on ARM's latest designs. Qualcomm faced legal battles with ARM over licensing terms. China's semiconductor ambitions complicated everything.

The fundamental model hasn't changed. ARM still doesn't manufacture chips. It still licenses designs. But the world it created is more complicated now. Data centers are experimenting with ARM-based servers, threatening Intel's last stronghold. RISC-V, an open-source architecture, offers an alternative that costs nothing to license. The geopolitical fracturing of the semiconductor industry means ARM's position as neutral Switzerland is harder to maintain.

The company that conquered mobile by refusing to control manufacturing now faces questions it could once ignore. As chips become more strategic, more geopolitical, more contested, can a licensor remain above the fray? The model that seemed weak in 1990 became dominant by 2010. Whether it remains dominant through 2030 depends on whether the forces that made it powerful — openness, neutrality, ubiquity — can survive forces that want chips to be weapons, platforms, moats.

Arm Holdings proved you could win by designing what others build. The question now is whether you can keep winning that way.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Palm
entries.section: The PDA that paved the way
entries.descriptor: The research lab that built the future of mobile computing, sold it too early, and watched others reap the reward
entries.content:
# Palm

***The research lab that built the future of mobile computing, sold it too early, and watched others reap the reward***

> **Years:** 1992-2010

In 1992, Jeff Hawkins was convinced that personal computers would eventually fit in your pocket. This was not a widely shared belief. Laptops weighed ten pounds. The idea of computing power shrinking to palm-size seemed like science fiction, or at best, a niche market for tech enthusiasts with too much money.

Hawkins had been designing portable electronics at GRiD Systems, where he'd watched the company's early pen-based computers fail to find a market. The technology was ahead of the interface. Users didn't need computers that tried to replace desktops. They needed something different: a device that did a few things exceptionally well. Hawkins spent weekends at a diner with a block of wood carved to the size he imagined a pocket computer should be, testing whether it felt right to carry, to hold, to use.

He founded Palm Computing with Donna Dubinsky and Ed Colligan in 1992. Their first product, the Zoomer, launched in partnership with Tandy and Casio, was a disaster. It tried to do everything and succeeded at nothing. The handwriting recognition didn't work. The interface was cluttered. It was too expensive. The company nearly died.

Hawkins went back to basics. He invented Graffiti, a simplified handwriting system that required users to learn new letter forms but worked reliably. He designed the interface around four core functions: calendar, contacts, tasks, and notes. Everything else was stripped away. The result was the Pilot 1000, launched in 1996.

It was a sensation. The Pilot cost $299, synced with desktop computers via a cradle, and did exactly what busy professionals needed without trying to replace their PCs. Palm sold more units in eighteen months than the entire pen computing industry had sold in its history. The company couldn't manufacture them fast enough. Computing magazines called it the most successful consumer electronics launch ever.

But Palm's corporate structure was a mess. The company had sold itself to U.S. Robotics in 1995 for survival capital. When 3Com acquired U.S. Robotics in 1997, Palm became a subsidiary of a subsidiary, buried inside a networking hardware company that didn't understand consumer electronics. Hawkins and Dubinsky chafed under 3Com's management. Decision-making slowed. Product development languished.

In 1998, Hawkins and Dubinsky left to found Handspring, licensing the Palm OS to compete with their former company. They launched the Visor, a Palm-compatible device with an expansion slot that let users add capabilities. It worked. Handspring captured market share while Palm, still inside 3Com, struggled to innovate.

Meanwhile, Microsoft saw the market Palm had created and wanted it. They launched Windows CE devices, spending hundreds of millions to evangelize their platform. The PocketPC grabbed headlines and developer attention. Palm's advantage was its installed base and simplicity, but the OS was aging. Written in the early 1990s, it couldn't handle modern demands: color screens, multimedia, wireless networking, multitasking.

3Com spun Palm out in an IPO in March 2000, at the peak of the dot-com bubble. The stock opened at $38 and closed the first day at $95, valuing Palm at $53 billion—more than the parent company that still owned most of it. It was absurd. Palm had revenue but unclear path to sustaining growth against Microsoft's assault.

The split left Palm divided. The hardware division remained Palm Inc. The software platform became PalmSource. This separation weakened both. Hardware couldn't control its destiny without owning the OS. Software couldn't ensure its platform evolved to serve users without controlling hardware innovation.

Palm tried to modernize. They acquired Handspring in 2003, reuniting the founders. They launched the Treo, a smartphone that combined the Palm OS with phone capabilities and a keyboard. It found an audience among business users—lawyers, doctors, executives who needed email on the go. But the Treo was expensive, and the OS underneath still couldn't compete with what was coming.

In 2007, Apple launched the iPhone. It wasn't just better than Palm; it was a different category. The touchscreen was responsive. The interface was intuitive. The App Store created an ecosystem. Palm's advantage—simplicity and installed base—evaporated. Suddenly, Palm devices felt ancient.

Palm tried one last reinvention. They hired Jon Rubinstein, who had led hardware development for the iPod. He greenlit Palm Pre, a new device with a new OS called webOS. It was beautiful. The interface was gesture-based, smooth, years ahead in multitasking design. It launched in June 2009 to rapturous reviews.

It failed. Sprint was the exclusive carrier, limiting reach. The app ecosystem was tiny compared to iPhone and the surging Android. Hardware quality was inconsistent—the Pre's slider mechanism broke. Most importantly, the market had moved. Consumers didn't want another platform. They wanted apps, and apps were being built for iOS and Android.

Palm burned through cash trying to compete. In April 2010, HP acquired Palm for $1.2 billion, mostly for the webOS intellectual property. HP launched one webOS tablet, the TouchPad, then killed the entire division six weeks later. WebOS was sold to LG, where it powers smart TVs—a footnote.

Jeff Hawkins had been right in 1992. Computers would fit in pockets. Palm proved the market existed and showed how it should work. They created the category, defined its interface conventions, and demonstrated its commercial viability. Then they watched Microsoft, Apple, and Google harvest what they'd planted. The company that invented the smartphone died just as smartphones conquered the world.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Silicon Graphics
entries.descriptor: The workstation maker that gave Hollywood its magic and then bet everything on a future that never came
entries.content:
# Silicon Graphics

***The workstation maker that gave Hollywood its magic and then bet everything on a future that never came***

> **Years:** 1981–2009

In 1981, Jim Clark left Stanford's computer science department carrying an idea that seemed impossibly ambitious: build computers that could render three-dimensional graphics in real time. Clark had spent years working on geometry engines — specialized hardware that could process the mathematical operations needed to turn wireframe models into solid, shaded objects on screen. The computers of the early 1980s couldn't do this. They were built for spreadsheets and word processing, not for manipulating virtual worlds.

Clark recruited a team of Stanford graduate students and engineers, raised $800,000 from venture capitalists who barely understood what he was trying to build, and founded Silicon Graphics Inc. The first product, the IRIS 1000, shipped in 1984. It cost $75,000 and could do something no other desktop machine could: rotate a 3D model smoothly while calculating how light would fall across its surfaces. For engineers designing cars or aircraft, for researchers visualizing molecular structures, it was revelatory.

The company found its market among technical users who needed to see their data in three dimensions. Oil companies bought SGI workstations to visualize seismic surveys. Pharmaceutical companies used them to model drug interactions. But the real breakthrough came from an unexpected direction: Hollywood.

In 1991, James Cameron used SGI workstations to create the liquid metal Terminator in *Terminator 2: Judgment Day*. The effect was seamless enough that audiences believed they were watching something physically real. Two years later, *Jurassic Park* used SGI machines to render dinosaurs that moved with weight and presence. The company's logo appeared in the credits of nearly every major effects-driven film of the 1990s. SGI hadn't just enabled a new kind of moviemaking — it had made computer-generated imagery credible.

The business model was elegant: sell expensive, specialized hardware that ran proprietary software on a variant of Unix called IRIX. SGI controlled the entire stack. Customers paid premium prices because nothing else could do what SGI machines did. By 1997, the company had $3.7 billion in revenue and employed 11,000 people. Its headquarters in Mountain View — two distinctive buildings designed to look like microchips — became a symbol of Silicon Valley ambition.

But the foundation was already cracking. Microsoft and Intel were improving commodity PC hardware at a ferocious pace. By the mid-1990s, a high-end PC with a specialized graphics card could render 3D at a fraction of an SGI workstation's cost. The performance gap — once a chasm — was narrowing to a crack. Jim Clark saw it coming. In 1994, he left to start Netscape, telling colleagues that SGI's business model was doomed.

SGI's response was to bet on the next computing paradigm. The company acquired Cray Research in 1996 for $740 million, trying to dominate high-end supercomputing. It invested heavily in servers, believing the future was in data centers, not workstations. Management predicted that the internet boom would create massive demand for SGI servers powering web applications and streaming media.

The prediction was half-right. The internet did explode. But the servers running it were cheap Linux boxes, not expensive SGI machines. Meanwhile, the workstation business collapsed faster than anyone anticipated. Hollywood switched to commodity hardware running Windows and Linux. Engineering firms followed. The proprietary Unix workstation — SGI's entire reason for existing — became a relic in less than five years.

Revenue peaked in 1997 and then fell off a cliff. By 2000, the company was losing hundreds of millions annually. Layoffs came in waves. The Cray acquisition, instead of opening new markets, had saddled SGI with an expensive business it didn't know how to run. The company cycled through CEOs, each with a new strategy: focus on visualization, pivot to storage, sell supercomputers to governments.

In 2006, SGI filed for bankruptcy protection. It emerged in 2006 as a smaller company focused on high-performance computing for technical markets. But the market had moved on. In 2009, Rackable Systems acquired what remained of Silicon Graphics for $25 million — less than the company had spent on a single quarter's R&D during its peak. The name persisted for a while in Rackable's product lines, then quietly disappeared.

The pattern was visible in retrospect: a company built around proprietary hardware in a world moving toward commodity components, unable to adapt because adaptation meant abandoning the source of its margins. SGI's engineers had solved genuinely hard problems — real-time 3D rendering, advanced graphics architectures, scalable visualization. But solving hard technical problems doesn't guarantee survival when the economic ground shifts beneath you. The technology that made SGI dominant in 1995 was available on a $2,000 Dell by 2000. The company that had defined computer graphics became a case study in disruption from below.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Wang Laboratories
entries.section: The word processor empire that vanished
entries.descriptor: The word processor king who couldn't see the PC revolution
entries.content:
# Wang Laboratories

***The word processor king who couldn't see the PC revolution***

> **Years:** 1951-1992

An Wang arrived in America from China in 1945 with forty dollars and a scholarship to Harvard. By 1948, he had invented magnetic core memory — the technology that would make modern computing possible. IBM paid him half a million dollars for the patent. In 1951, at age thirty-one, Wang opened a small laboratory in Boston to build specialized calculators. He had no grand vision of computing empires. He simply wanted independence.

For two decades, Wang Laboratories grew steadily in the shadows of the mainframe giants. Wang made desktop calculators, then moved into word processing in the early 1970s. The timing was perfect. Secretaries typed on IBM Selectrics. Corrections meant retyping entire pages. Wang's dedicated word processors — terminals connected to minicomputers — let users edit on screen before printing. Law firms, government offices, and corporations bought thousands of units. The machines were expensive, proprietary, and incredibly profitable.

By 1978, Wang Laboratories was doing $200 million in revenue. An Wang had built a vertically integrated kingdom — his own processors, his own operating system, his own sales force, his own service network. The company went public that year. The stock soared. Wang became a billionaire, and Boston became a tech hub rivaling Silicon Valley. He bought the Boston Garden and put his name on it.

The early 1980s were the peak. Wang's word processing systems dominated offices across America. The company expanded into minicomputers, competing directly with Digital Equipment Corporation and Data General. Revenue hit $3 billion by 1988. Wang employed 33,000 people. An Wang appeared on magazine covers as a visionary immigrant entrepreneur. The company built a massive headquarters complex in Lowell, Massachusetts — twin towers that symbolized arrival.

But the PC was already eating the foundation. Apple II and then IBM PC with WordPerfect and Lotus 1-2-3 could do word processing for a fraction of Wang's price. The machines were open, modular, upgradeable. Wang's systems were closed, proprietary, and increasingly obsolete. Companies that had spent millions on Wang infrastructure faced a choice: keep paying Wang's premium prices or rip everything out and start over with PCs.

An Wang saw the threat but couldn't respond. His entire business model depended on proprietary lock-in. Moving to open standards meant cannibalizing his own revenue. His word processors were still profitable. The sales force resisted change — their commissions came from big system sales, not cheap PCs. Wang tried introducing PC-compatible products, but half-heartedly. The company culture was built around An Wang's engineering philosophy: integrated systems under central control. PCs represented the opposite.

The financial structure made pivoting impossible. Wang had gone public but maintained control through dual-class shares. An Wang made all major decisions. He promoted his son Fred to president in 1986 despite limited experience and internal opposition. The move demoralized senior executives. Several left. Fred Wang struggled with the scope of the crisis. An Wang, now in his sixties and in declining health, couldn't let go.

The collapse came fast. Revenue peaked in 1988 at $3 billion, then fell off a cliff. Customers abandoned Wang systems en masse. The minicomputer business evaporated as PC networks took over. By 1989, the company was losing money. By 1990, it was hemorrhaging cash. An Wang finally removed Fred and brought in outside management, but it was too late. The debt load from the boom years was crushing.

An Wang died of cancer in March 1990, at age seventy. He had built one of America's great technology companies and watched it disintegrate in his final years. The company limped forward under new management, trying to salvage something from the wreckage. They sold the towers. They laid off tens of thousands. They tried to reposition as a software and services company.

In August 1992, Wang Laboratories filed for bankruptcy. The company emerged from bankruptcy in 1993 as a shadow of itself, then was eventually acquired and absorbed. The brand disappeared. The Lowell towers stood empty for years before being converted to condos.

Wang's story is the story of disruptive innovation in miniature. An Wang built a phenomenally successful business solving a real problem — editing documents before printing. He built deep expertise and infrastructure. He created a self-reinforcing system that was highly profitable precisely because it was closed and proprietary. When a new technology made that system obsolete, he couldn't pivot. The very things that made Wang Laboratories successful — vertical integration, proprietary control, premium pricing — became fatal liabilities.

The company's death wasn't inevitable in 1980 or even 1985. But by 1988, when management finally acknowledged the crisis, the window had closed. The installed base was already switching. The debt was already unsustainable. An Wang's refusal to cede control — and his decision to promote his son — poisoned any chance of transformation.

What survives is An Wang's early innovation. Magnetic core memory made the computer age possible. That contribution outlived the company by decades. But Wang Laboratories itself became a cautionary tale: the giant that couldn't adapt, the founder who held on too long, the company that mistook a temporary advantage for permanent dominance.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: AOL
entries.descriptor: The dial-up giant that conquered the internet, then lost it
entries.content:
# AOL

***The dial-up giant that conquered the internet, then lost it***

> **Years:** 1985-present

Steve Case wasn't supposed to build an internet empire. In 1983, he was a marketing executive at Pizza Hut, testing whether people would order pizza through their television sets. The experiment failed, but Case saw something: people wanted to connect through screens. When he joined a struggling online service called Quantum Computer Services in 1985, most Americans had never heard of email. By 2000, his company — renamed America Online — would be worth more than Time Warner, Disney, and News Corp combined.

Quantum started as a service for Commodore 64 owners. The idea was simple: dial into a computer network and access news, weather, games. But the execution was clunky, and the market was tiny. Case became CEO in 1991 and made a bet that would define the company: AOL wouldn't be for computer enthusiasts. It would be for everyone.

This was radical. In the early 1990s, getting online meant knowing Unix commands and navigating text-based interfaces. CompuServe and Prodigy served educated professionals. Case wanted to reach middle America — people who had never touched a computer. AOL's interface used buttons, icons, and the friendly greeting "You've Got Mail." It was the Fisher-Price version of the internet, and critics mocked it. Tech-savvy users called it "America On Hold" and dismissed its chat rooms as vapid. Case didn't care. He wasn't building for them.

The growth strategy was as aggressive as the product was simple. AOL carpet-bombed America with free trial CDs. They came in cereal boxes, magazine inserts, shrink-wrapped with electronics. AOL sent out 250 million CDs in one year alone. The marginal cost was nothing; the customer lifetime value was enormous. People tried it, got hooked on email and chat rooms, and stayed. By 1996, AOL had 5 million subscribers. By 1998, 15 million. By 2000, 25 million — more than half of all Americans with internet access.

The business model was beautiful. Subscribers paid $19.95 a month for unlimited access. AOL didn't just provide the connection — it *was* the internet for most users. People went online by opening AOL. They read news through AOL. They shopped through AOL's partnerships with Amazon and Barnes & Noble. AOL collected a cut of every transaction. The company became a gateway, a platform, and a tollbooth all at once.

But the unlimited access model nearly destroyed them. In 1996, AOL switched from hourly billing to unlimited monthly plans. Usage exploded. The network couldn't handle it. Subscribers got busy signals for hours. Lawsuits piled up. State attorneys general investigated. Case's bet almost killed the company, but AOL upgraded its infrastructure frantically, and the crisis passed. By 1997, they were back on track — and heading toward the deal that would define them.

The Time Warner merger, announced in January 2000, was the largest in history. AOL, a 15-year-old company, would acquire Time Warner, a media conglomerate with HBO, CNN, Warner Bros, and Time Magazine. The price: $165 billion. Steve Case sold it as the future: AOL's internet platform combined with Time Warner's content empire. "We're in the business of building brands," Case said. Wall Street believed him. Time Warner shareholders approved.

It was a disaster from the start. The cultures clashed immediately. AOL's executives were brash dot-com cowboys who wore khakis and thought in quarters. Time Warner's were old-media executives who wore suits and thought in decades. AOL's ad revenue — built on banner ads during the bubble — collapsed after 2000. The promised synergies never materialized. Time Warner executives discovered that AOL had been inflating revenue through questionable ad deals. By 2002, the company posted a $99 billion loss — the largest in corporate history.

The internet itself moved past AOL. Broadband replaced dial-up, and broadband users didn't need AOL's walled garden. Google became the gateway to the web. Facebook became the place for connection. AOL's subscriber base peaked in 2002 and declined every quarter after. The company that had defined the internet for 20 million households became obsolete within five years.

Time Warner spun off AOL in 2009, valuing it at $3.4 billion — 98% less than the merger price. AOL stumbled through various reinventions: a content farm strategy, an advertising technology play, acquisitions of TechCrunch and The Huffington Post. None of it worked. Verizon bought AOL in 2015 for $4.4 billion, mostly for its ad tech. In 2021, Verizon sold it to Apollo Global Management for $5 billion. Today AOL exists as a portfolio of declining media properties and legacy dial-up subscribers — yes, hundreds of thousands still pay for AOL dial-up.

Steve Case's original insight was correct: the internet would be for everyone, and the company that made it easy would win. AOL won that battle completely. But winning the battle for dial-up meant nothing when the war moved to broadband, search, and social. The company that brought America online became the symbol of what the internet left behind.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: GeoCities
entries.descriptor: The city that taught a generation to build on the web
entries.content:
# GeoCities

***The city that taught a generation to build on the web***

> **Years:** 1994-2009

In 1994, the web was a void. Most people couldn't imagine what to do with it. David Bohnett and John Rezner saw something different: not a library of documents, but a place where ordinary people could stake a claim. They called it Beverly Hills Internet, then quickly renamed it GeoCities — digital neighborhoods where anyone could build a home.

The metaphor was literal. Users didn't just get web hosting. They joined themed "neighborhoods." Technology enthusiasts went to SiliconValley. Artists claimed SoHo. Personal pages filled Heartland. Each neighborhood had its own aesthetic, its own culture. The taxonomy was crude but powerful — it gave structure to chaos, community to isolation.

The product was radical in its simplicity. You didn't need to know HTML, though the tool would teach you if you wanted to learn. You didn't need money — the basic service was free, supported by banner ads. You just needed something to say. GeoCities provided 2MB of server space and a WYSIWYG editor. Millions of people, most of whom had never considered themselves publishers, suddenly were.

What they built was glorious and terrible. Flashing text. Tiled backgrounds. Guestbooks. Hit counters. MIDI files playing unbidden. GIF animations of flames, stars, dancing babies. Every rule of design violated simultaneously. Every page a different experiment in what the web could be.

It looked like chaos. It was chaos. But it was generative chaos. GeoCities pages were where people learned HTML by viewing source and copying code. Where teenagers built shrines to their favorite bands. Where hobbyists documented their obsessions. Where small businesses got their first online presence. The barrier to publishing had been a printing press, a broadcast license, a record deal. Now it was nothing.

The growth was exponential. By 1997, GeoCities was the fifth most visited site on the web. By 1998, it had 3.5 million users — "homesteaders" — publishing 2 million pages. Venture capital arrived. The company went public in August 1998, in the first flush of dot-com mania. The IPO raised $66 million. The stock price tripled on the first day.

Then came the first crisis. In June 1999, GeoCities changed its terms of service. The new language claimed ownership over everything users posted — text, photos, code. The reaction was instant and furious. Homesteaders organized. They built protest sites on GeoCities' own servers. They coordinated boycotts. GeoCities backed down within days, apologizing and reverting the terms. But the damage was done. Trust, once broken, doesn't fully repair.

A month later, Yahoo came calling. The deal was $3.57 billion in stock — the third-largest acquisition in internet history at that point. Yahoo saw GeoCities as a content engine, a community play, a traffic generator. They had 19 million users posting content that brought in visitors that saw ads. The math seemed obvious.

But Yahoo didn't understand what they'd bought. GeoCities wasn't a product — it was a culture. The neighborhoods mattered. The creative anarchy mattered. Yahoo saw inefficiency and ugliness. They saw wasted server space on pages with twelve visitors. They saw brand risk in user-generated chaos.

Slowly, Yahoo began to optimize. They changed the URLs, breaking millions of links across the web. They imposed stricter rules. They pushed users toward Yahoo-branded templates. They turned neighborhoods into directories. The homesteaders began to leave. Not in a flood — it was too slow for that — but in a steady, quiet exodus. They went to Blogger, to LiveJournal, to self-hosted WordPress blogs. Anywhere that felt less corporate, less controlled.

By the mid-2000s, GeoCities was a ghost town. The pages remained, but few were updated. New users stopped coming. Yahoo kept the lights on, but without investment or attention. The web had moved on to Web 2.0 — to platforms with real-time updates, social graphs, algorithmic feeds. Static HTML pages in themed neighborhoods felt ancient.

In April 2009, Yahoo announced they would shut down GeoCities. The closure came in stages — Japan's version survived until 2019 — but for most of the world, it ended on October 26, 2009. Preservationists raced to archive what they could. The Archive Team pulled down terabytes of data. Individual users scrambled to save their pages. Much was lost anyway.

The numbers were staggering. At its peak, GeoCities hosted 38 million pages. It was the third most-visited site on the web. And then it vanished, taking with it a vast, messy archive of late-90s internet culture — the first draft of what people thought the web could be.

GeoCities failed because Yahoo treated it as a product when it was a platform, as content when it was expression, as real estate when it was community. But it succeeded first, magnificently, because it gave millions of people their first experience of building something on the internet. It was training wheels for a generation of web creators.

The aesthetic died. Flashing text and tiled backgrounds became punchlines. But the idea survived, mutated, spread. GeoCities taught people that the web wasn't something you consumed — it was something you made. That lesson outlived the platform by decades.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Netscape
entries.descriptor: The browser that won the web and lost the war
entries.content:
# Netscape

***The browser that won the web and lost the war***

> **Years:** 1994-2003

In early 1994, Marc Andreessen was twenty-two years old and recently arrived in Silicon Valley. He had spent the previous year at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications at the University of Illinois, where he and a small team built Mosaic, the first web browser that made the internet visual and accessible. Mosaic spread like wildfire through universities and research labs, but Andreessen saw nothing from it—NCSA owned the code, and he'd moved on.

Jim Clark saw something different. Clark had founded Silicon Graphics and made a fortune, but he'd been pushed out by his own board. He was looking for the next big thing, and when he met Andreessen, he found it. The web was exploding. Mosaic had proven people wanted graphical browsers. But Mosaic was slow, buggy, and controlled by a university. Clark proposed they build a better browser and a company around it. They would move fast, hire the best engineers, and capture the internet before anyone else realized it was worth capturing.

They called it Mosaic Communications, then changed the name to Netscape after NCSA threatened to sue. Andreessen recruited most of his old Mosaic team. They worked in a cramped office in Mountain View, writing code around the clock. The goal was simple: build the best browser in the world and give it away for free. The business model would come later.

Netscape Navigator 1.0 launched in December 1994. It was faster than Mosaic, more stable, and infinitely more polished. Within weeks, it dominated. By early 1995, Netscape had 75% of the browser market. The company wasn't selling the browser—it was free for individuals, nominally paid for corporations—but the usage created a platform. Netscape sold server software to companies that wanted to build websites. The browser was the distribution channel. The servers were the revenue.

In August 1995, Netscape went public. It was one of the most anticipated IPOs in history. The company had been profitable for exactly one quarter. It was sixteen months old. On the first day of trading, the stock opened at $28, shot to $75, and closed at $58. Netscape's market cap hit $2.9 billion. Andreessen, still twenty-three, was worth $58 million. The IPO didn't just make Netscape rich—it ignited the dot-com boom. Suddenly, the internet wasn't a curiosity. It was a gold rush.

But Microsoft was watching. Bill Gates had dismissed the internet as a fad, but Netscape's success changed his mind. In December 1995, Gates issued his "Internet Tidal Wave" memo, redirecting the entire company toward the web. Microsoft would bundle a browser with Windows. They would give it away for free—actually free, not Netscape's honor-system free. They would crush Netscape.

Internet Explorer 1.0 was terrible. IE 2.0 was mediocre. But Microsoft had infinite resources and Windows on 90% of computers. With IE 3.0 in 1996, they closed the gap. Netscape's market share started to erode. The browser wars had begun.

Netscape fought back by betting on the future. If the browser was going to be free, the money had to come from somewhere else. They pivoted to enterprise software—web servers, intranet tools, commerce platforms. They tried to turn the browser into a platform itself, with Java and JavaScript making the web an application environment. The vision was right, but the execution was scattered. Netscape was trying to be ten companies at once.

Meanwhile, Microsoft ground them down. IE 4.0 launched in 1997, integrated into Windows so deeply that uninstalling it could break the operating system. PC manufacturers couldn't ship Netscape even if they wanted to—Microsoft wouldn't allow it. Netscape's market share dropped from 80% to 50% to 30%. Revenue growth stalled. The stock collapsed.

By 1998, Netscape was dying. The enterprise pivot hadn't worked. The browser market was lost. The company had no path to profitability. In November, AOL acquired Netscape for $4.2 billion in stock—a fortune, but a fraction of what it might have been. The acquisition was defensive. AOL wanted the engineers and the brand, but mostly it wanted to keep Netscape out of Microsoft's hands. Netscape's browser became AOL's browser. The enterprise products were folded into AOL's portfolio. The company dissolved.

Before it ended, Netscape made one final move that would outlive everything else. In January 1998, the company open-sourced the Netscape browser code, releasing it as the Mozilla project. It was a Hail Mary—if they couldn't win commercially, maybe they could build a community. The Mozilla code was a mess, and the project nearly died, but it survived. Years later, it became Firefox, the only browser that could challenge Microsoft's monopoly. Netscape lost, but it planted the seed for the next fight.

The Department of Justice sued Microsoft in 1998 for antitrust violations, citing the Netscape war as evidence. Microsoft lost. They were ordered to break up the company, though the ruling was overturned on appeal. But the damage was done. Microsoft's dominance was checked. The web stayed open.

AOL shut down Netscape's browser division in 2003. By then, IE had 95% market share. Netscape was a memory. But the company had already changed everything. It proved the internet could be commercial. It triggered the dot-com boom. It forced Microsoft to fight for the web instead of owning it. It turned the browser into the operating system of the future. Netscape lost the war, but it won the web.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Yahoo
entries.descriptor: The search engine that won the early web, then lost its way
entries.content:
# Yahoo

***The search engine that won the early web, then lost its way***

> **Years:** 1994-2017

In early 1994, two Stanford electrical engineering graduate students named Jerry Yang and David Filo were doing what graduate students do: procrastinating on their dissertations. They began compiling lists of interesting websites, organizing them into categories on a page they called "Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web." The web was still so small you could theoretically catalog it by hand. Their guide became addictive to early web users desperate for any form of navigation in the chaos of cyberspace.

By fall, their hobby had consumed their workstations, their trailer, and any pretense of academic work. They renamed it Yahoo — an acronym for "Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle," though the cowboy exclamation was the real appeal. Traffic exploded. By early 1995, Yahoo was serving a million page views per day on Stanford's network. The university politely suggested they take it elsewhere.

They incorporated in March 1995. Sequoia Capital, which had passed on many internet ideas, saw something different here: not technology risk but pure distribution play. Mike Moritz led a $2 million round. The bet was that organizing the web mattered more than searching it. Yahoo's human-curated directory felt trustworthy in ways algorithmic search didn't. You browsed Yahoo the way you browsed a library.

Yahoo went public in April 1996 at $13 per share. On day one it closed at $43. The company was barely two years old. Revenue was minimal. No one cared. Yahoo was the front door to the internet, and everyone wanted in.

What Yahoo understood before others was that being a portal mattered more than being a search engine. In 1997 and 1998, they bought everything: email, calendaring, shopping, news, sports, entertainment. If people did it online, Yahoo wanted to be where they did it. The strategy worked brilliantly. By 1999, Yahoo was the most visited website on earth, worth $120 billion at the peak. The directory that two students built to avoid dissertation work had become one of the most valuable companies in the world.

But Yahoo had a problem it couldn't see. Google launched in 1998 with a genuinely better search algorithm. Yahoo dismissed it as technology for technology's sake. Why would users want algorithmic search when human curation worked fine? In 2000, Yahoo even licensed Google's search technology to power its own results, not seeing the existential threat.

The dotcom crash hammered Yahoo like everyone else. The stock fell from $118 to $8. But Yahoo had real revenue from advertising, real traffic, real business. They survived when hundreds of others vaporized. By 2002, Yang and Filo stepped back from operations, bringing in Hollywood executive Terry Semel as CEO. The company needed adult supervision to become a real media business.

Semel pursued media deals aggressively. Yahoo bought Inktomi and Overture, getting its own search technology. But the company's focus remained scattered across dozens of properties. Google, meanwhile, did one thing superbly: search that worked better every day, monetized through advertising that was both more targeted and less intrusive than Yahoo's banner approach.

In 2006, Yahoo had a chance to change everything. Facebook, barely two years old, was available for $1 billion. Yahoo agreed, then lowered the offer to $850 million as its own stock price fell. Mark Zuckerberg walked. Yahoo also rejected a chance to buy Google in 2002 for $5 billion. The company kept choosing to build rather than buy the future.

By 2007, Yahoo's position was deteriorating. Google dominated search. Facebook was swallowing social. Yang returned as CEO but seemed paralyzed by sentimentality for what Yahoo had been. Microsoft offered $44.6 billion in 2008 — roughly $33 per share for a company trading at $19. The board rejected it. Yahoo's stock would never see that price again.

A parade of CEOs followed: Carol Bartz, fired over the phone by the board; Scott Thompson, gone after four months over a resume scandal; Ross Levinsohn, interim; Marissa Mayer, hired away from Google with great fanfare in 2012. Mayer tried everything: mobile focus, content acquisitions, the $1.1 billion purchase of Tumblr. Nothing arrested the decline. Yahoo had become a portfolio of assets slowly losing relevance, held together by momentum and a stake in Alibaba worth more than Yahoo itself.

The final chapter was fragmentation. In 2017, Verizon bought Yahoo's core business for $4.5 billion — less than a tenth of the Microsoft offer and a fraction of what Yahoo had once been worth. The Alibaba stake and Yahoo Japan were spun off separately. The company that had defined the early internet ended as spare parts.

Yahoo's failure wasn't singular or dramatic. It was death by a thousand missed turns. They had capital, talent, traffic, and time. What they lacked was focus and the ability to see that being everything to everyone on the internet was a strategy for the web's first decade, not its second. They won the portal era so decisively they couldn't imagine it would end.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Amazon
entries.descriptor: From online bookstore to everything store
entries.content:
# Amazon

***From online bookstore to everything store***

> **Years:** 1994-present

Jeff Bezos was a senior vice president at D.E. Shaw, a quantitative hedge fund in New York, when he noticed a number that changed his life: the internet was growing at 2,300 percent per year. It was 1994. He was thirty years old. He made a list of twenty products that could be sold online, ranked them by feasibility, and concluded that books made the most sense. The catalog was enormous — millions of titles in print — yet no physical bookstore could stock more than a fraction. An online store could offer selection that physical retail could never match. He called his wife MacKenzie, quit his job, and they drove west to Seattle while he typed a business plan on a laptop.

He named the company after the world's largest river. The domain Cadabra.com briefly existed but was abandoned when someone misheard it as "cadaver." Amazon.com launched from a garage in Bellevue in July 1995. Bezos held meetings at a Barnes & Noble cafe, using the competition's space to plan its disruption. The first employee was Shel Kaphan, a programmer who built the site. Early orders came from all fifty states and forty-five countries in the first thirty days. They rang a bell each time an order came in. Within weeks, the bell never stopped, so they retired it.

The site was crude but functional. It had reviews — user-generated content before that was a concept people named. It had recommendations based on what other customers bought. It had selection that physical stores couldn't touch. The warehouse was chaotic. Everyone packed books on hands and knees until someone suggested buying packing tables. Bezos installed a neon sign reading "Amazon.com" visible from the highway, annoying neighbors but announcing arrival.

Funding came from family, then angels, then Kleiner Perkins in 1996 — eight million dollars at a valuation that made early angels very rich. The company went public in May 1997 at $18 per share, raising $54 million. Skeptics abounded. Barron's ran a cover: "Amazon.bomb." The thesis: books were low-margin, shipping was expensive, Barnes & Noble would crush them online, the valuation was absurd. Bezos wrote an annual shareholder letter explaining that Amazon would focus on long-term growth over short-term profits, would make bold investments, and would fail often. It was a warning and a manifesto. Most ignored it.

The expansion began immediately. Music, then videos, then toys, then electronics. Critics called it unfocused. Bezos called it inevitable. The vision was never books. It was everything. Infrastructure investments were massive — warehouses, logistics, technology. Losses mounted. The stock collapsed during the dot-com crash, falling from $106 to $6 by 2001. Layoffs came. The press wrote obituaries. Bezos kept building.

Two insights saved and transformed the company. First: the infrastructure built to sell products could be rented to others. Amazon Web Services launched in 2006, offering cloud computing to startups and enterprises. It became more profitable than retail. Second: the platform could host third-party sellers, turning Amazon from a retailer into a marketplace. By 2023, third-party sales accounted for sixty percent of units sold. Amazon took a cut, provided logistics, and avoided inventory risk. The flywheel spun faster.

The Kindle arrived in 2007, sold out in six hours, and redefined book publishing. Amazon Prime launched in 2005 — unlimited two-day shipping for $79 annually — and seemed insane until it wasn't. Prime created lock-in. Members spent more, shopped more often, tolerated price increases. Video and music were added to justify the rising fee. By 2024, Prime had over 200 million members worldwide.

Whole Foods was acquired in 2017 for $13.7 billion, bringing physical retail and groceries into the empire. Amazon entered pharmacy, healthcare, logistics for third parties, advertising, satellite internet. Each move leveraged existing infrastructure. Each expansion triggered regulatory scrutiny and competitor panic.

The culture was notoriously intense. The "leadership principles" were real. Frugality was doctrine — door desks as symbol. Bezos read every customer complaint email forwarded with a "?" and expected answers. Meetings began with silent reading of six-page memos. PowerPoint was banned. Data ruled decisions. Employees burned out or thrived. Turnover was high. Performance was Darwinian.

Bezos stepped down as CEO in 2021, becoming executive chairman. Andy Jassy, who built AWS, took over. Bezos turned attention to Blue Origin, his space company, and The Washington Post, which he bought in 2013. By then Amazon employed 1.5 million people, generated over $500 billion in annual revenue, and held a market cap above $1.5 trillion.

The company faced antitrust investigations in the U.S. and Europe, accused of anti-competitive practices — favoring its own products, using seller data against them, squeezing suppliers. Unions organized in warehouses, citing brutal conditions and surveillance. The outcomes remained unresolved, but the scrutiny was constant.

Amazon's path was never one thing. It was books, then everything, then infrastructure, then marketplace, then media, then logistics provider. Each layer built on the last. Each expansion seemed like distraction until it became dominance. The original insight — infinite selection, customer obsession — never changed. The execution compounded. The critics who predicted failure in 2000 were correct about the risks and wrong about the will. Bezos believed scale would eventually overcome losses. He was patient when capital was cheap and ruthless when it mattered. The everything store became everything because it refused to stay a bookstore.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Craigslist
entries.descriptor: The classified ad that refused to grow
entries.content:
# Craigslist

***The classified ad that refused to grow***

> **Years:** 1995-present

In 1995, Craig Newmark was a software engineer at Charles Schwab in San Francisco, newly transplanted from the East Coast and trying to figure out how to meet people. He started sending emails to friends about interesting events around the city — art shows, tech meetups, things happening in the nascent web community. The list grew organically through forwards and word of mouth. People started asking if they could post their own events. Then apartment listings. Then job openings. By 1996, what had started as Craig's personal hobby had become craigslist — lowercase, informal, almost apologetic about existing.

Newmark ran it from his apartment. The site looked like a text file rendered in a browser because that's essentially what it was. No graphics. No branding. Just links and listings organized by category. When the volume got too high for him to handle alone, he incorporated in 1999 and brought on a single employee. Not to scale the business — to answer customer service emails.

The timing was accidentally perfect. eBay had shown that people would transact with strangers online. Newspapers were still printing classified ads at extortionate rates. The web was transitioning from novelty to utility. Craigslist offered what the internet promised and legacy media couldn't: a place to post anything, reach anyone, pay nothing.

By 2000, the site was in multiple cities. The dot-com boom was incinerating capital all around it. Startups with similar models — classifieds, marketplaces, local commerce — were raising tens of millions and hiring hundreds of employees. They had business development teams and sales forces and expensive ad campaigns. They had plans to dominate local commerce, to become destination sites, to capture network effects before anyone else. Craigslist had Craig Newmark and a handful of others answering support tickets.

The venture capitalists came calling. So did acquirers. Newmark said no, mostly. In 2004, he let eBay buy a 28% stake for an undisclosed sum — a hedge, a way to fund the operation without fundamentally changing it. But Newmark retained control. He stepped back from day-to-day operations and handed management to Jim Buckmaster, a programmer who shared his philosophy: keep it simple, keep it free, resist the obvious temptations.

The obvious temptations were enormous. Newspapers were bleeding classifieds revenue. Jobs, apartments, used cars — these were cash machines that moved online, and Craigslist owned the migration. The site charged fees for job listings in major cities and brokered apartment listings in New York, but almost everything else remained free. Competitors calculated what Craigslist could be worth if it acted like a normal business. The estimates ran into the billions.

But Craigslist didn't act like a normal business. It didn't optimize. It didn't redesign. It barely changed the interface. It didn't hire salespeople or build a mobile app until years after mobile became dominant. It didn't raise prices. It didn't expand into obvious adjacencies. When Airbnb and Uber showed how to monetize spare bedrooms and car rides, Craigslist continued letting people post both for free. When machine learning made it possible to detect and remove scams at scale, Craigslist relied mostly on user flags.

This created an opening. Dozens of startups launched to "unbundle" Craigslist — to take one category and do it properly, with venture funding and design polish and fraud protection. Apartment listings went to Zillow and Trulia. Job boards to Indeed and LinkedIn. Used goods to OfferUp and Facebook Marketplace. Personals to dating apps, then disappeared entirely when legislation made them legally risky. Each vertical that left took value with it.

But the site persisted. It remained the default for certain transactions — sublets in college towns, used furniture, gig work that didn't fit LinkedIn. The interface that looked hopelessly dated in 2005 looked exactly the same in 2025. The lack of features became a kind of feature. You could post quickly, search simply, deal directly. No algorithms deciding what you saw. No promoted listings. No upsells.

The business remained profitable — estimates suggested hundreds of millions in annual revenue with a staff of fewer than fifty people. The ratio was absurd. Instagram had been acquired for $1 billion with thirteen employees, but Instagram had growth. Craigslist had equilibrium. It printed money by refusing to chase more money.

eBay eventually sued in 2008, claiming Newmark and Buckmaster had diluted its stake and frozen it out. The court sided mostly with Craigslist. eBay sold its shares in 2015. The site remained private, controlled by Newmark and Buckmaster, who continued running it as they always had: minimal staff, minimal changes, maximum resistance to being anything other than what it was.

Newmark became a philanthropist, giving away hundreds of millions to journalism and veterans' causes. Buckmaster kept the servers running. The site's market share eroded year by year as better-funded competitors took pieces of what it had pioneered. But it never collapsed. It never sold. It never went public. It never hired thousands. It never expanded internationally with purpose. It just stayed there, doing its one thing, looking like 1999, refusing every path toward becoming something larger.

The outcome was both failure and success, depending on what you measured. If the goal was to maximize growth, capture markets, and build enterprise value, Craigslist left billions on the table. If the goal was to create something useful and keep it that way without letting incentives corrupt it, the site achieved exactly what its founders intended. They had built something the internet kept trying to replace, and thirty years later, it was still there, ugly and functional, the classified ad that wouldn't die because it never tried to become anything else.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: eBay
entries.descriptor: The marketplace that turned collectibles into commerce
entries.content:
# eBay

***The marketplace that turned collectibles into commerce***

> **Years:** 1995-present

On Labor Day weekend 1995, Pierre Omidyar sat at his computer in San Jose writing code for what he thought would be an experiment. He was a software engineer at General Magic, working on the side to build internet tools. One of them was a simple marketplace where people could list items for auction. He called it AuctionWeb, hosted on his personal site alongside pages about the Ebola virus. The legend says his girlfriend wanted to trade Pez dispensers. The truth is simpler: Omidyar believed in the efficient market, in letting buyers and sellers find their own prices.

The first item sold was a broken laser pointer. Omidyar contacted the winning bidder to make sure they understood it didn't work. The buyer replied: "I'm a collector of broken laser pointers." That's when Omidyar realized he'd stumbled onto something real. People wanted to buy things that had no traditional retail market. Strange things. Used things. Things nobody knew how to price.

Traffic grew. By February 1996, the site was generating enough revenue from listing fees — ten cents to twenty-five cents per auction — that Omidyar had to upgrade his ISP account. By June, he'd quit his job. AuctionWeb was pulling in $10,000 a month. Omidyar recruited his friend Jeff Skoll, a Stanford MBA, to write the business plan. They renamed it eBay — Omidyar's intended name for his consulting business, Echo Bay, shortened because the domain was taken.

The site had no inventory, no warehouses, no employees handling goods. It was pure infrastructure. Buyers and sellers met, transacted, and left feedback. The feedback system — Omidyar's innovation — solved the trust problem that should have killed an anonymous marketplace. Reputation became currency. Power sellers emerged, people running full-time businesses out of their garages. Collectibles were the engine: Beanie Babies, trading cards, memorabilia. Entire secondary markets materialized.

But eBay was chaotic. The site crashed constantly. Customer service was Omidyar and Skoll answering emails. They needed management. In 1997, they hired Meg Whitman, a Harvard MBA who'd worked at Hasbro and Disney. She was their fifth choice. The first four turned them down. Whitman thought the site was ugly, the business bizarre. But she saw the numbers: millions in revenue, almost pure profit, exponential growth. She took the job.

Whitman professionalized the operation. She brought in engineers to stabilize the platform, built a customer service team, introduced category managers. She also made a crucial strategic choice: eBay would grow through acquisition and international expansion, not by forcing a single business model. When collectors started selling cars, eBay bought Auto Trader. When people wanted fixed-price options, eBay added "Buy It Now." When competitors emerged abroad, eBay bought them or launched local sites.

The IPO in September 1998 was a phenomenon. The stock opened at $18, shot to $47, and closed at $54. Omidyar and Skoll became billionaires. Whitman became the face of the company. eBay was proof that the internet could generate real profits, not just promise. The market capitalization hit $20 billion by 1999.

But the auction model had limits. eBay worked for unique items — collectibles, used goods, one-offs. It worked less well for commodity goods where price discovery mattered less than convenience. Amazon was building a different model: fixed prices, fast shipping, predictable experience. eBay's growth began to slow. The platform attracted fraud. Sellers gamed feedback systems. Buyers complained about quality. The site's reputation shifted from quirky treasure hunt to sketchy flea market.

Whitman tried to evolve. eBay bought PayPal in 2002 for $1.5 billion, solving the payment problem and gaining a fintech subsidiary. It bought Skype in 2005 for $2.6 billion, hoping to integrate communication into commerce. That acquisition failed spectacularly. Skype didn't fit. eBay sold it four years later at a loss. The company also expanded into China, competing with Alibaba. That failed too. Alibaba understood local dynamics — seller relationships, payment systems, logistics — in ways eBay didn't.

By 2008, the core business was stagnant. Amazon had become the default for online shopping. Craigslist had captured local classified ads. Etsy was winning handmade goods. eBay was stuck between markets. Whitman stepped down. Her successor, John Donahoe, tried to fix the platform — simplifying the interface, lowering fees for high-volume sellers, pushing fixed-price listings. But the marketplace that had thrived on chaos couldn't easily become streamlined. Sellers who'd built businesses around the old system revolted.

eBay spun off PayPal in 2015, splitting the company into two. PayPal was the growth business; eBay was the legacy. The core auction platform limped along, still profitable, still massive, but no longer defining the future. Amazon was logistics and speed. Etsy was craft and community. Craigslist was local and free. eBay was the place you went when you didn't know where else to go.

What eBay proved: you could build a massive business by connecting buyers and sellers without owning anything. The platform model worked. But being first didn't mean staying dominant. eBay created a market, then watched others build better versions. The auction format — its original insight — became a limitation. Markets want efficiency, but they also want certainty. eBay offered price discovery when no one knew what things were worth. Once they did, auctions felt like friction.

The company still exists, still processes billions in transactions annually. But it occupies a strange space: too successful to die, too constrained to reinvent itself. It created the online marketplace and then became a monument to what marketplaces used to be.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Broadcast.com
entries.descriptor: The right idea at the exact right moment — then gone
entries.content:
# Broadcast.com

***The right idea at the exact right moment — then gone***

> **Years:** 1995-1999

In 1995, Mark Cuban was running MicroSolutions, a systems integration company he'd eventually sell for $6 million. Todd Wagner was a lawyer. They were friends from Indiana University who shared a problem: they wanted to listen to Indiana basketball games in Dallas. Radio stations didn't stream online. Nothing streamed online reliably. The internet was still mostly text and tiny images loading one painful line at a time.

Cuban and Wagner saw the gap between what the internet could theoretically do and what it actually did. Streaming audio seemed obvious — radio over the internet — but the technology barely worked. RealAudio had launched in 1995, making it technically possible to send audio over dial-up connections, but nobody was building the infrastructure to do it at scale. The broadcast industry wasn't interested. Radio stations saw the internet as a curiosity, not a distribution channel.

They started AudioNet in September 1995, operating out of Cuban's house with one server. The first "broadcast" was a baseball game between KLIF radio in Dallas and a computer in Cuban's spare bedroom. It worked. Barely. The audio quality was terrible, the buffering constant, but it worked. Within months they were streaming dozens of radio stations. By early 1996, they had their first employee and actual office space.

The timing was everything. The internet was exploding but still primitive. Anyone who could make media work online had a massive first-mover advantage. AudioNet wasn't technically sophisticated — they were essentially setting up servers and connecting radio stations who wanted to experiment. But in 1995, that was enough. Nobody else was doing it.

The real insight came in early 1997. The web was becoming commercial. Companies were starting to believe the internet mattered. AudioNet had been focused on sports and radio — niche content for enthusiasts. But what if you could broadcast anything? Corporate events, investor presentations, concerts, lectures. What if every company needed to stream something eventually?

They rebranded to Broadcast.com in March 1998 and expanded aggressively. They weren't just streaming radio anymore. They were streaming Victoria's Secret fashion shows, presidential speeches, concerts, sporting events. Anything that could be broadcast on traditional media, they would put online. The business model was thin — mostly advertising, some licensing fees — but the growth was spectacular. By late 1998, they were broadcasting over 420 radio stations, 60 television stations, and hundreds of live events. Traffic was doubling every few months.

The company went public in July 1998. The IPO was perfectly timed for the peak of internet mania. The stock opened at $18, hit $74 by the end of the first day, and closed at $62.75. Mark Cuban and Todd Wagner became very wealthy on paper. The company's market cap reached $1 billion despite revenues of only $13.5 million and mounting losses.

The valuation made no sense by traditional metrics, but that didn't matter in 1998. Investors were betting on potential, on owning the future of media distribution. Broadcast.com was the future. Or at least, it was positioned as the future. The technology was still clunky. Most people were on dial-up connections. Streaming was jerky and unreliable. But everyone could see where it was heading. Bandwidth would get cheaper. Connections would get faster. Broadcast.com would be there first, owning the infrastructure.

That's what Yahoo believed when they acquired Broadcast.com in April 1999 for $5.7 billion in stock. At the time, it was one of the largest internet acquisitions ever. Yahoo was building a portal empire, trying to be everything to everyone. Media streaming fit their strategy. Cuban and Wagner became even wealthier — Cuban's stake was worth around $1.4 billion.

The acquisition looked brilliant for about eighteen months. Then the bubble burst. Yahoo's stock collapsed. The grand vision of the internet portal died. Broadband was coming, but not as fast as predicted. The business model for streaming media never materialized. Advertising rates collapsed. The infrastructure costs were enormous.

Yahoo rebranded Broadcast.com as Yahoo Broadcasting, then eventually just absorbed it into Yahoo's media properties. The distinct entity disappeared. The technology was integrated into Yahoo's platform, then gradually became obsolete as YouTube, podcasting, and later streaming services built better infrastructure and discovered actual business models.

Mark Cuban took his Yahoo stock and sold it before the crash — a move that distinguished him from most dot-com winners who rode their paper wealth back down to nothing. He used the money to buy the Dallas Mavericks and build a second fortune in sports and media investing. Todd Wagner pursued philanthropy and film production. Neither spent much time defending Broadcast.com's legacy.

The story of Broadcast.com is the story of perfect timing meeting imperfect execution meeting an irrational market. The idea was correct — media would move online, streaming would become ubiquitous. They were simply too early, and built the wrong infrastructure for the wrong business model. But being too early didn't matter when they could exit to a greater fool. Yahoo paid billions for a company that would never be worth billions, but Cuban and Wagner sold at exactly the right moment, before anyone realized that first-mover advantage in dot-com infrastructure was often just first-loser disadvantage.

They built something real — streaming media was their innovation, however primitive. They timed the exit perfectly. The company died quietly inside Yahoo. But the founders won.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Webvan
entries.section: Too much, too fast, too early
entries.descriptor: The grocery delivery dream that arrived a decade too early
entries.content:
# Webvan

***The grocery delivery dream that arrived a decade too early***

> **Years:** 1996-2001

In 1996, Louis Borders stood in a supermarket watching people push carts through fluorescent aisles and thought: there has to be a better way. Borders had already revolutionized bookstores with his brother Tom, building Borders Books into a national chain through data-driven inventory management. Now he saw the same inefficiency in groceries — consumers wasting time, stores wasting space, the entire supply chain optimized for 1950s shopping habits.

His vision was total vertical integration. Webvan would build massive automated warehouses where goods moved on conveyor belts, picked by robots, packed in minutes, and delivered within a 30-minute window that customers could schedule online. No storefronts. No checkout lines. No parking lots. Just perfect logistics meeting perfect technology, reimagining grocery retail from first principles.

The timing seemed perfect. It was the height of the dot-com boom. Amazon had proven you could sell products online. Peapod had shown grocery delivery worked, albeit slowly and expensively. Borders believed he could leapfrog them all with superior automation and scale. Venture capitalists agreed. In November 1996, Webvan raised $10 million. By the time it went public in November 1999, it had raised $375 million more.

The business model was breathtaking in its ambition. Each automated warehouse would cost $35 million to build and cover 330,000 square feet — larger than five football fields. Inside, a mile of conveyor belts would route 8,000 products to packing stations. The system could theoretically handle 8,000 orders daily, far more than any conventional supermarket. Webvan would achieve profitability through pure operational efficiency, processing orders for less than it cost traditional grocers to stock shelves.

They launched in the San Francisco Bay Area in June 1999. The service was magical. You browsed groceries on a clean website — revolutionary at a time when online shopping meant clunky forms and broken images. You scheduled delivery down to the half-hour. A cheerful driver arrived in a Webvan-branded truck with your groceries in temperature-controlled totes. No tipping allowed. If something was out of stock, they called you. If they were late, they gave you a discount.

Customers loved it. The press adored it. Time magazine named Webvan one of the year's best ideas. Borders became a visionary, the man who would obsolete the supermarket. The stock opened at $15 and shot to $30 on the first day of trading. Webvan's market capitalization hit $7.9 billion — more than Safeway, a century-old chain with 1,500 stores.

But the unit economics never worked. Each order cost Webvan more to fulfill than customers paid. The company lost money on every delivery, a fact they planned to solve with scale. More orders per warehouse would drive down fixed costs. More warehouses would drive down distribution costs. Eventually, around 5,000 orders per day per warehouse, they would break even. Then profits would follow.

The problem was reaching that scale. Webvan expanded frantically, signing contracts for 26 automated warehouses across the country before the first one proved profitable. They entered Atlanta, Dallas, Chicago, Los Angeles, and more — each market requiring tens of millions in infrastructure before selling a single banana. They acquired HomeGrocer.com, a competitor, for $1.2 billion in stock, adding more unprofitable markets to their network.

The spending was staggering. Building warehouses. Buying trucks. Hiring drivers. Marketing to acquire customers. Customer acquisition costs ran as high as $210 per person, and each customer ordered infrequently enough that payback took years. In 2000, Webvan spent $525 million and generated $178.5 million in revenue. They were burning through $20 million per month.

Meanwhile, the assumptions underlying the model began to crack. Grocery margins were even thinner than anticipated — 2-3% at best. Customers loved the service but wouldn't pay premium prices for commodities. A gallon of milk cost the same at Webvan as at Safeway, so Webvan made no money on it. The automated warehouses, while impressive, required massive order volume to justify their cost. Without density, delivery trucks spent more time driving than delivering.

Then the dot-com bubble burst. In March 2000, the NASDAQ peaked and began its collapse. Suddenly capital markets closed to unprofitable companies. Webvan's stock, which had traded above $30, fell below $1. The $375 million raised pre-IPO and the $375 million from the IPO itself — three-quarters of a billion dollars — was running out. They needed more funding to reach profitability, but no one would invest.

In April 2001, Webvan laid off 2,000 employees. In May, they pulled out of Dallas and Atlanta. By July, it was over. The company filed for bankruptcy, shut down operations, and laid off its remaining 2,000 workers. The automated warehouses, marvels of engineering that cost $35 million each, sold for pennies on the dollar. Customers who had scheduled deliveries for that week found the website simply gone.

The autopsy was brutal. Webvan had raised $800 million and lost it all. Borders' vision of vertical integration meant every failure compounded — bad warehouses, bad logistics, bad marketing, bad expansion all at once. The company had optimized for a future that required tens of millions of customers to materialize immediately, when in reality behavior change takes decades. They built for scale before proving the model, and scale killed them.

A decade later, Instacart launched with the opposite approach. No warehouses. No trucks. No employees picking orders. Just software connecting customers to contract shoppers who bought groceries from existing stores. Instacart proved the demand Webvan had seen was real — people did want grocery delivery. But the path to serving that demand was not revolution. It was iteration, constraint, and patience.

Webvan arrived too early with too much capital and too grand a vision. The dream was correct. The execution was a cautionary tale about confusing inevitability with imminence.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: eToys
entries.descriptor: The $8 billion IPO that couldn't survive Christmas
entries.content:
# eToys

***The $8 billion IPO that couldn't survive Christmas***

> **Years:** 1997-2001

In 1997, Toby Lenk saw what everyone saw but few believed: parents would buy toys online. Disney veteran Lenk had watched retail transform through catalog shopping and understood the pain of toy stores — limited selection, seasonal madness, stressed parents dragging children through aisles of temptation. The internet promised something better: browse at midnight, ship to your door, avoid the crowds.

He wasn't alone in seeing it. Amazon had launched the year before. But Lenk believed toys deserved a specialist. Parents wanted expertise, gift recommendations, age-appropriate suggestions. Amazon sold everything. eToys would be the toy authority online.

The timing seemed perfect. Internet penetration was accelerating. E-commerce infrastructure was maturing. The first generation of children raised on computers were becoming parents themselves. And venture capital was flooding into anything with ".com" after its name.

Sequoia Capital led a $20 million Series A in 1998. By then, eToys had built an impressive warehouse operation in California, stocked deep inventory, and created a website that actually worked — no small feat in 1998. The company shipped fast, wrapped gifts, included batteries. Parents loved it. Revenue grew from $700,000 in 1997 to $30 million in 1998.

Then came the IPO. In May 1999, at the peak of dot-com euphoria, eToys went public at $20 per share. It opened at $78. By day's end, the company was valued at $8 billion — more than Toys "R" Us, a century-old retailer with 1,500 stores and $11 billion in revenue. eToys had $30 million in revenue and had never posted a profit.

The valuation was absurd, but eToys wasn't alone. In 1999, investors valued growth over profit, vision over fundamentals, potential over reality. eToys raised $164 million in the IPO. It had capital to dominate.

The company spent aggressively. A $20 million marketing deal with Warner Bros. A Super Bowl ad costing $2.5 million. Partnerships with AOL and Yahoo. The purple logo plastered across billboards and buses. Brand awareness soared. So did costs.

The business model had a problem, though it wasn't obvious during the boom. Toys are seasonal. Sixty percent of sales happened in Q4. eToys needed massive warehouse capacity for Christmas but that capacity sat mostly empty the rest of the year. Shipping was expensive — toys are bulky, low-margin items. The company was selling $30 toys and spending $15 to ship them, often with free shipping promotions to win customers.

Customer acquisition costs were brutal. In the land-grab mentality of 1999, everyone believed scale mattered more than unit economics. Get big fast, dominate the category, profitability would follow. eToys spent $100 to acquire customers buying $40 worth of toys.

Revenue grew to $151 million in 1999. Losses grew faster: $190 million. For every dollar of revenue, eToys lost $1.26.

The company knew it needed to reach $750 million in revenue to break even at scale. The path was clear: spend more, grow faster, reach profitability through volume. In dot-com logic, this made sense.

Then the market turned. In March 2000, the NASDAQ peaked and began its collapse. Capital that had flowed freely dried up. Investors who had valued growth above all suddenly wanted profitable businesses. The IPO window slammed shut. Companies burning cash faced a reckoning.

eToys stock, which had touched $86, fell below $1 by December 2000. The company still had $30 million in cash, but it wasn't enough for another year of losses. Christmas 2000 would make or break the company.

They went all in. Massive inventory purchases. Aggressive marketing. The warehouse was full, the website ready, the customer service staffed. Then the orders came — too many orders. The system was overwhelmed. Shipments were late. Packages were wrong. Customer service collapsed under the volume. The very success eToys had spent millions pursuing destroyed its ability to deliver.

Parents who had ordered toys in November received them in January, after Christmas. After the moment that mattered. The angry calls and emails were relentless. The brand, which eToys had spent $100 million building, was damaged in weeks.

Revenue for 2000 hit $237 million, up from $151 million. But losses were $244 million. The company had $18 million in cash and $274 million in debt. In February 2001, after failing to secure additional funding, eToys filed for bankruptcy.

KB Toys bought the eToys brand and customer list for $5.4 million — a company once valued at $8 billion sold for the price of a modest house. The 500,000-square-foot warehouse was liquidated. The purple logo disappeared from the internet.

Toby Lenk had been right about the market. People would buy toys online. Amazon's toy section thrives. Target and Walmart dominate online toy sales. The category exists and is massive.

But eToys' path required perfection: flawless execution at massive scale, sustained capital access, profitable unit economics, and time to reach break-even volume. They got the growth. They missed everything else. In the land grab, they grabbed land they couldn't hold.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: PayPal
entries.descriptor: The accidental payments giant born from a failed Palm Pilot money-beaming experiment
entries.content:
# PayPal

***The accidental payments giant born from a failed Palm Pilot money-beaming experiment***

> **Years:** 1998-present

In December 1998, Max Levchin and Peter Thiel founded Confinity in Palo Alto with $300,000 in funding and an idea that seemed clever at the time: beaming money between Palm Pilots using infrared technology. Levchin, a Ukrainian immigrant and cryptography expert who'd dropped out of the University of Illinois, had the technical chops. Thiel, a former securities lawyer and hedge fund manager with contrarian instincts, had the vision and connections. They thought they were building the future of digital wallets.

They were wrong about the Palm Pilot part.

The infrared demo worked — you could literally beam dollars from one device to another at trade shows — but nobody actually wanted to do this in real life.
