The gaming press mostly ignored it — too young, too crude, not a "real" game. That dismissal was protective. While journalists chased the next Halo, Roblox quietly accumulated millions of children who logged in after school to hang out in worlds their friends had made.

The monetization model was unusual. Users could build for free. Playing was free. But Roblox sold "Robux," a virtual currency, and let creators charge for access to their games or for virtual items within them. Initially, Roblox kept most of the revenue. But in 2013, they introduced the Developer Exchange program: creators could convert Robux back into real dollars. It was a slow payoff — you needed to accumulate substantial Robux, and Roblox's cut was steep — but it worked. Teenagers who had started building for fun began treating it like a job. Some made thousands of dollars. A few made millions.

This creator economy became the engine. By 2015, some of the most popular experiences on Roblox were built by teenagers or college students, not the company. *Adopt Me*, a pet simulation game created by two developers from the UK, would eventually reach billions of visits. *Jailbreak*, a cops-and-robbers game, was built by a college student. Roblox didn't need to make hit games. It needed to make the platform where hit games emerged.

But there were growing pains. Moderation was a nightmare. Kids built inappropriate content. Predators tried to use the chat. Roblox's moderation system was part algorithmic, part human, and perpetually overwhelmed. The company added filters, restricted chat for younger users, hired thousands of moderators. Critics argued it was never enough. Lawsuits cropped up. Regulation loomed. Roblox responded with layers of controls but never fully solved the problem — the cost of a platform where millions of kids interact daily.

Funding came slowly, then all at once. Roblox raised modest rounds in the early years, then $92 million in 2017, $150 million in 2020. By then, the pandemic had arrived. Lockdowns put kids at home with nothing to do. Roblox became their social life. Daily active users surged from 19 million in early 2020 to over 30 million by year's end. Hours played skyrocketed. Revenue doubled, then doubled again.

In March 2021, Roblox went public via direct listing. No IPO roadshow, no underwriters setting a price — they just let the market decide. It debuted at a valuation of $45 billion. Baszucki, who had spent fifteen years building a thing journalists called a kids' game, was suddenly a billionaire many times over. Wall Street analysts scrambled to understand what Roblox was. A gaming platform? A social network? A metaverse? Roblox called itself a "human co-experience platform." The market didn't care about the label. It cared about the numbers: tens of millions of daily users, billions in revenue, and a creator economy that was mostly self-sustaining.

The post-pandemic years tested the thesis. As kids went back to school, engagement plateaued. Revenue growth slowed. The stock fell from its peak. Critics argued Roblox had been a pandemic beneficiary, not a structural shift. But the users didn't leave. They aged up. Roblox began courting older audiences, partnerships with brands like Gucci and Nike, concerts by Lil Nas X. It leaned into the metaverse narrative just as Facebook rebranded to Meta. The terminology was trendy, but the substance was the same: Roblox was still a place where people built worlds and hung out.

By 2024, Roblox had over 70 million daily active users. The top creators were earning millions. The platform had paid out billions to developers. Baszucki talked about a future where Roblox was agnostic to device, reality, or age — a persistent space where human experience happened. Whether that vision materializes or whether Roblox becomes another platform kids outgrow remains to be seen. But the path is clear: from a physics simulator teachers used, to a game kids made, to a universe they inhabit.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Unity
entries.descriptor: The game engine that democratized an industry, then bet its future on monetization
entries.content:
# Unity

***The game engine that democratized an industry, then bet its future on monetization***

> **Years:** 2004-present

In 2004, three Danish developers — David Helgason, Joachim Ante, and Nicholas Francis — set out to build a game engine that would let small teams create the kinds of 3D games previously only possible at major studios. They called it Unity, and their initial target market was Mac game developers. This was an almost comically bad business decision. The Mac gaming market was tiny. The dominant engines — Unreal, CryEngine — served studios with budgets and engineering teams. Nobody was clamoring for democratization.

But that tiny market gave Unity focus. Without resources to compete on cutting-edge graphics, they competed on accessibility. Their editor ran in the browser. Their scripting used C#, familiar to many programmers, instead of the arcane languages of other engines. They made it possible to build once and deploy to multiple platforms. They priced it within reach of independent developers. In 2005, they released version 1.0 at Apple's Worldwide Developer Conference. It sold modestly. They survived.

The breakthrough came not from Mac games but from the iPhone. When Apple opened the App Store in 2008, Unity was one of the few engines ready for mobile deployment. Suddenly their multi-platform strategy mattered immensely. Indies building iPhone games needed exactly what Unity offered: fast iteration, cross-platform deployment, and affordable pricing. As the mobile gaming market exploded, Unity rode the wave. Angry Birds, Temple Run, Pokémon Go — all built in Unity. By 2012, Unity powered more than half of mobile games.

This success created a paradox. Unity had democratized game development. Millions of developers used it, most of them small teams or individuals. But free and cheap users don't build billion-dollar companies. Unity needed enterprise revenue. They began moving upmarket, courting AAA studios while maintaining their indie base. They added features for high-end graphics, acquired companies for specialized tools, and pivoted their business model repeatedly — from paid licenses to freemium to subscriptions.

In 2020, they went public at a $13.6 billion valuation. The pitch to investors was clear: Unity wasn't just a game engine anymore. It was a platform for all real-time 3D content — games, architecture, film, automotive design. They called it the "metaverse" before that term became poisoned. But underneath the growth narrative, tensions mounted. Developers loved Unity for what it had been: accessible, affordable, community-driven. The company needed it to become something else: a high-margin SaaS business with enterprise contracts and recurring revenue.

In 2022, Unity acquired ironSource, a mobile app monetization company, for $4.4 billion. The deal signaled a strategic bet: Unity's future wasn't just creation tools but monetization infrastructure. They would help developers make money from their games through ads and in-app purchases, taking a cut of the revenue. It made financial sense. It also quietly redefined what Unity was — no longer just an enabler of creators, but a intermediary extracting value from their success.

Then came September 2023. Unity announced a new "Runtime Fee" — a per-install charge for games that crossed certain revenue and download thresholds. The backlash was immediate and ferocious. Developers saw it as a betrayal. The fee applied retroactively to games already shipped. It penalized success. It created perverse incentives around install fraud. The community that had built Unity's dominance revolted. Major developers threatened to abandon the platform. Unity's stock plummeted.

The company backtracked within days, revising the fee structure, then effectively canceling it. John Riccitiello, the CEO who had overseen the pivot to monetization, resigned. The Runtime Fee debacle revealed something deeper than a pricing misstep. It exposed the fundamental tension in Unity's business model: a company built on empowering small creators trying to extract enterprise-level revenue from those same creators.

Today, Unity remains the dominant engine for mobile and indie games, with a massive installed base and network effects that make it hard to displace. But the trust is fractured. Competitors like Unreal and Godot (an open-source alternative) gained momentum during the crisis. Developers diversify their engine choices, hedging against future policy changes. Unity's revenue from "Create Solutions" (the actual engine) has stagnated while revenue from "Grow Solutions" (ads and monetization) has grown — a shift that reflects both the company's strategy and the community's wariness.

The path Unity took is a familiar one in tech: start by serving an underserved community, achieve dominance through their loyalty, then struggle to convert that dominance into margins investors demand. The founders' insight was correct — 3D creation was too hard, too expensive, too gatekept. They solved it. But solving it for millions of small creators created a business that didn't naturally generate billions in profit. The monetization pivot was logical. It was also, to the community that made Unity possible, a betrayal of the original mission.

Unity democratized game development. Then it had to monetize democracy. That tension remains unresolved, and the company's next chapter depends on whether it can find a business model that serves both creators and shareholders — or whether it will have to choose.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: ServiceNow
entries.descriptor: The enterprise software giant that emerged from one founder's rage against terrible IT tools
entries.content:
# ServiceNow

***The enterprise software giant that emerged from one founder's rage against terrible IT tools***

> **Years:** 2004-present

Fred Luddy had spent two decades building enterprise software when he founded ServiceNow in 2004. He'd worked at various software companies, seen countless IT departments struggle with clunky, expensive systems, and grown increasingly frustrated with how bad the tools were. At Peregrine Systems, he'd helped build asset management software. At Remedy, he'd seen help desk systems that required armies of consultants just to configure. The enterprise software industry had become a racket — vendors sold complex products that took years to implement, required constant customization, and locked customers into expensive maintenance contracts.

Luddy wanted to build something different: help desk software that ran entirely in a web browser, required no installation, and could be configured without an army of consultants. The model was simple — monthly subscriptions, automatic updates, and clean design. He called it Glidesoft initially, later renaming it ServiceNow.

The timing was terrible. In 2004, the dotcom crash was still fresh. Enterprise buyers were skeptical of hosted software. The dominant model was still on-premise installations. Salesforce had pioneered cloud CRM but remained controversial — many IT departments considered cloud applications insecure and unserious. The idea of running critical IT infrastructure through a web browser seemed reckless.

Luddy funded the first two years himself, working from a small office in San Diego. He built the initial product alone, writing code and talking to potential customers. The pitch was straightforward: your IT help desk doesn't need to be a nightmare of servers and consultants. It can be a web application that just works. Early customers were small IT departments willing to take a chance on something unproven because their existing tools were so painful.

The product solved a real problem. IT help desks at most companies ran on systems built in the 1990s — Remedy, Peregrine, HP Service Center. These required dedicated servers, complex installations, and specialized administrators. Making simple changes meant hiring consultants. The total cost of ownership was astronomical. ServiceNow offered the same functionality through a browser, with updates that happened automatically and configuration anyone could learn. The value proposition was obvious to anyone who'd suffered through traditional enterprise IT tools.

But selling to enterprise IT departments in 2006 meant overcoming deep skepticism. IT leaders had been burned by software vendors repeatedly. They'd invested millions in systems that under-delivered. They'd been through painful implementations. They didn't trust cloud applications with their critical workflows. ServiceNow's sales cycle was long and grinding.

The company raised $5 million in 2006 from JMI Equity. The funding allowed Luddy to hire a sales team and build out the product. By 2007, ServiceNow had gained traction among mid-sized companies. The recession of 2008-2009 could have killed them, but instead it helped. Companies desperate to cut IT costs looked at ServiceNow's subscription model and saw an escape from expensive maintenance contracts and consultant fees. The value proposition became impossible to ignore.

In 2009, ServiceNow raised $41.5 million in a Series C led by Greylock Partners. The company brought in Frank Slootman as CEO in 2011. Slootman had led Data Domain to a $2.4 billion acquisition by EMC and had a reputation for aggressive growth. Luddy, who preferred building product to running a large organization, stepped back to focus on technology as chief product officer.

Under Slootman, ServiceNow accelerated. The company had initially focused just on IT help desk software — tracking tickets, managing incidents, handling service requests. But Luddy had built the platform to be flexible. ServiceNow expanded into IT operations management, asset management, configuration management. The same platform that tracked help desk tickets could manage any workflow. That insight transformed the business.

ServiceNow went public in 2012 at $18 per share, raising $210 million. The market valued it at $2.4 billion — remarkable for a company with $150 million in revenue. Wall Street was betting on the platform play. If ServiceNow could become the system of record for IT operations, it could expand into other enterprise workflows.

That's exactly what happened. ServiceNow moved into HR service delivery, facilities management, security operations, customer service management. Every large organization had workflows that needed automation, tracking, and management. ServiceNow's platform could handle all of them. The value to customers was consolidation — instead of buying point solutions from dozens of vendors, they could build workflows on a single platform.

The growth was relentless. Revenue grew from $267 million in 2012 to over $7 billion by 2023. The market cap exceeded $140 billion. ServiceNow became one of the most valuable enterprise software companies in the world, competing with Oracle and SAP but with a modern cloud architecture they couldn't match.

The success came from Luddy's original insight: enterprise software didn't have to be painful. It could be elegant, cloud-native, and user-friendly. But it also came from recognizing that IT service management was just the entry point. The real opportunity was becoming the platform for all enterprise workflows. ServiceNow didn't just replace Remedy. It replaced dozens of enterprise applications by providing a flexible platform for any process that needed automation.

By 2024, ServiceNow served over 85% of the Fortune 500. The company Luddy started alone in San Diego had become enterprise infrastructure — the system that large organizations used to run themselves. The rage against terrible IT tools had produced one of the defining enterprise platforms of the cloud era.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Souq
entries.descriptor: The Amazon of the Middle East — until it became Amazon
entries.content:
# Souq

***The Amazon of the Middle East — until it became Amazon***

> **Years:** 2005-2017

In 2005, Ronaldo Mouchawar and Samih Toukan were trying to solve a problem that barely existed yet in the Arab world: online shopping. The internet was still dial-up in most of the region. Credit card penetration was abysmal. Logistics infrastructure was decades behind the West. E-commerce seemed like an idea imported from a different planet.

They started anyway. Mouchawar, a Syrian who'd worked at Yahoo, and Toukan, a Jordanian entrepreneur who'd already sold one company, launched Souq.com as an auction site — a Middle Eastern eBay. The model made sense: let sellers come to you, avoid the inventory problem, build the marketplace first. For the first few years, it limped along. Auctions never caught fire. The trust wasn't there. Neither was the payment infrastructure.

In 2009, they pivoted. Souq became a retailer. They bought inventory, warehoused it, sold it direct. The shift was existential — from marketplace operator to full-stack e-commerce company. It meant capital requirements. It meant logistics. It meant betting that the infrastructure they needed would materialize around them as they built.

The bet paid off slowly, then suddenly. Smartphones arrived in the Middle East like a flood. By 2011, mobile internet leapfrogged desktop in adoption. A generation skipped PCs entirely and went straight to apps. Souq was there. They launched a mobile app early. They built payment systems around cash-on-delivery because that's what customers trusted. When parcels arrived at doors, drivers collected crumpled bills. It was analog and expensive, but it worked.

The logistics problem was harder. Addresses in Dubai or Riyadh weren't like addresses in New York. Street names were suggestions. Building numbers were theoretical. Souq drivers learned neighborhoods like taxi drivers, navigating by landmarks and phone calls. "I'm at the building with the green awning near the mosque." The company built a delivery network from scratch because no one else would.

By 2013, Souq was the largest e-commerce platform in the Arab world. They operated across six countries — UAE, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman. Each market had its own quirks. Saudi Arabia, the biggest prize, required careful navigation. Cultural norms shaped product selection. Gender segregation affected logistics. But the market was enormous and oil-rich and internet-hungry.

Competitors appeared. Noon, backed by Emirati billionaire Mohamed Alabbar, launched with huge capital. Amazon was always rumored to be coming. Alibaba circled. But Souq had the advantage of incumbency and local knowledge. They understood Ramadan shopping spikes. They knew which payment methods worked in which neighborhoods. They had relationships with customs authorities in a dozen ports.

The company raised capital in waves. Tiger Global, Naspers, IFC all invested. By 2016, Souq was valued at over $1 billion. They had 45 million visitors a month. The business was growing 80% year over year. They employed 3,000 people. The path seemed clear: keep scaling, dominate the region, eventually IPO.

Then Amazon called. Talks began in late 2016. At first, it seemed like a standard acquisition dance — due diligence, valuation debates, structure negotiations. But for Mouchawar and his investors, the calculus was complex. Souq was winning. The market was growing faster than almost anywhere on earth. Mobile-first consumers were easier to acquire than desktop-trained Westerners. Why sell now?

The answer was competition. Amazon was coming to the Middle East either with Souq or without it. If without, they would bring infinite capital and a global brand. Noon was already spending lavishly. The market could afford one dominant player. Maybe two. Not three. And Amazon offered something no local investor could: integration into a global system. Souq's merchants could access Amazon's worldwide marketplace. The technology stack would leap forward overnight.

In March 2017, Amazon announced the acquisition for $580 million — later reported as closer to $650 million with earnouts. It was Amazon's largest-ever acquisition outside the United States. For Mouchawar, it was validation and surrender in one. He'd built the thing that proved the market existed. Now the giant would take it from here.

The transition was bumpy. Amazon rebranded Souq slowly, testing what to keep and what to change. Cash-on-delivery stayed. The name lasted until 2019, when Souq.com became Amazon.ae and Amazon.sa. Mouchawar stayed on to run the Middle East operation. He became a vice president in Seattle's empire, a regional governor reporting to distant powers.

By 2024, the transformation was complete. The Souq brand was memory. Amazon's Middle East operation was growing but not dominating as expected. Noon held its ground. The market fractured more than anticipated. The inevitable conquest looked messier in execution.

Souq proved e-commerce could work in the Middle East. They built payment rails and logistics networks in markets everyone said weren't ready. They showed a path from zero to billion-dollar valuation. And then they showed something else: that even winners get absorbed. The founder who builds the category often doesn't own its maturity. Mouchawar created Amazon's entry. Whether that was the best exit or the last exit depends on what he wanted the ending to be.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Workday
entries.section: The second act of PeopleSoft's founders
entries.descriptor: The enterprise software insurgency that refused to compromise
entries.content:
# Workday

***The enterprise software insurgency that refused to compromise***

> **Years:** 2005-present

In 2005, Dave Duffield had already built and sold PeopleSoft, a human resources and financial software company, to Oracle for $10.3 billion. He was 64 years old. He could have retired. Instead, Oracle's Larry Ellison made a critical miscalculation: he called Duffield's life's work "junk" and promised to stop developing PeopleSoft products. Duffield had been forced to sell in a hostile takeover. Now Ellison was insulting what he'd built. Duffield decided to do it again—and do it differently.

He partnered with Aneel Bhusri, PeopleSoft's former head of product strategy, who was 38 and working in venture capital. They saw an opening. The enterprise software model was broken. Companies like Oracle and SAP sold expensive licenses for software installed on-premises. Implementation took years. Upgrades were nightmares. Customers were locked in and miserable.

The cloud was emerging, but enterprise buyers didn't trust it for their most sensitive data—payroll, HR records, financial systems. Salesforce had proven cloud delivery could work for CRM, but those were sales records, not the company's core operational systems. HR and finance executives were conservative. They wanted proven technology, not experiments.

Duffield and Bhusri bet they could build cloud-native enterprise software that was actually good. Not just cheaper or more convenient—genuinely superior. They raised $15 million from Greylock Partners and started building. The name "Workday" came from their vision: software you could use every workday without hating it.

Their first target was human capital management—HR systems. They knew this space intimately from PeopleSoft. They understood what customers wanted and what the legacy systems couldn't deliver. But instead of replicating the old architecture in the cloud, they built something new. A single database for all HR data. An interface that didn't require a training manual. Automatic updates that wouldn't break customizations.

The early days were a grind. They launched in 2006 with no customers and a product that needed years of development before it could handle the complexity of large enterprises. They sold to mid-sized companies first—big enough to have real HR complexity, small enough to take a risk on an unproven vendor. Their first customer went live in 2008.

Then 2008 happened. The financial crisis froze enterprise software spending. Companies that might have considered a new system stopped signing contracts. Workday's pipeline evaporated. They had built for two years and were just beginning to gain traction when the market collapsed.

But the crisis also helped them. Oracle and SAP were so expensive, so slow to implement, that when budgets recovered, companies looked for alternatives. Workday's cloud model meant no hardware to buy, no data center to build. Faster deployment. Lower upfront costs. For companies emerging from the crisis looking to do more with less, it was compelling.

They started winning major accounts. First dozens of employees, then hundreds, then thousands. Chiquita Brands in 2009. Flextronics in 2010. They weren't just winning mid-market deals anymore—they were taking Fortune 500 accounts away from Oracle and SAP.

In 2012, they expanded into financial management—the other half of the enterprise software equation. This was riskier. HR systems touch everyone in a company, but finance is the nervous system. CFOs don't experiment. But Workday had proven they could execute. They built financial management with the same architecture: cloud-native, unified database, consumer-grade interface. And they sold it bundled with HR, creating a platform that could run core operations for an entire enterprise.

They went public in October 2012 at $28 per share, raising $637 million in the largest software IPO since VMware. The stock jumped 75% on the first day. Investors believed. By then, Workday had over 400 customers and revenue approaching $250 million annually.

The growth continued. They passed $1 billion in revenue in 2016. $3 billion in 2019. $5 billion in 2022. They kept winning deals against Oracle and SAP—not by being cheaper, but by being better. Faster to implement. Easier to use. More flexible. And because everything was in the cloud, they could update the software twice a year. Every customer got the improvements automatically. No more painful, expensive upgrades.

Oracle and SAP tried to respond. They built cloud versions of their products, but they were retrofits—legacy systems awkwardly adapted to the cloud. Workday's advantage was architectural. They were cloud-native from day one. The difference showed in win rates.

By 2024, Workday serves over 10,000 customers, including more than half the Fortune 500. Revenue exceeds $7 billion annually. The company is worth over $60 billion. Duffield, now in his eighties, remains chairman.

The path was improbable: a 64-year-old founder, starting over in a category dominated by entrenched giants, selling to conservative enterprise buyers, launching just before a global financial crisis. But Workday succeeded because they refused to compromise. They didn't build a cheaper version of the old model. They rebuilt enterprise software from scratch, cloud-native, at a time when that bet was far from obvious. They sold it to skeptical buyers who eventually realized the cloud wasn't just adequate—it was superior. And they executed relentlessly for nearly two decades, outlasting competitors who thought age and experience would be liabilities. Duffield proved otherwise.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Klarna
entries.section: Buy now, pay later, worry later
entries.descriptor: The buy now, pay later empire that made checkout frictionless and debt fashionable
entries.content:
# Klarna

***The buy now, pay later empire that made checkout frictionless and debt fashionable***

> **Years:** 2005-present

Sebastian Siemiatkowski, Niklas Adalberth, and Victor Jacobsson met at Stockholm School of Economics in 2005. They were in their early twenties. They watched friends abandon online shopping carts at checkout — too many steps, too much friction, credit card forms that felt like interrogations. E-commerce was growing, but trust remained fragile. Consumers feared fraud. Merchants lost sales to checkout anxiety.

The insight: flip the risk model. Instead of consumers trusting merchants with payment information, have a trusted intermediary assume the risk. Let people buy now, pay later. Make checkout a single click.

Sweden in 2005 was perfect terrain. High internet penetration, digitally literate population, strong credit infrastructure. The banking system was conservative — credit cards weren't ubiquitous. Invoicing was common. Klarna would digitize that cultural norm, making it instant.

They launched with merchants first. The pitch: we'll handle payment risk, you get higher conversion. Klarna guaranteed payment even if the customer defaulted. For consumers: buy without entering card details, pay within 14 or 30 days. No interest if paid on time. Simple.

Early traction came from fashion e-commerce. Young shoppers loved it. Try before you pay. Return what doesn't fit. Klarna absorbed the friction that killed impulse purchases. Merchants saw conversion rates jump 20-30%. Word spread.

The Swedish market was too small for the ambition they had. By 2010, they pushed into Norway, Finland, Denmark, Germany. Each country required new banking partnerships, credit models adjusted for local behavior. Germany was the prize — Europe's largest e-commerce market — but also the hardest. German consumers were debt-averse. Klarna positioned itself not as lending but as flexible payment. It worked.

Funding came steadily. Sequoia led a round in 2010 after watching Klarna's merchant retention numbers. When a platform has 80% repeat merchants and growing consumer habit, investors pay attention. By 2011, Klarna was processing millions of transactions monthly. The flywheel spun: more merchants attracted more consumers, more consumers attracted more merchants.

But competition arrived. PayPal launched installment products. Banks woke up. Afterpay emerged in Australia with a similar model but focused on younger demographics and smaller purchases. Klarna responded by going bigger and broader. Not just fashion — electronics, travel, anything. Not just invoices — split payments into installments. Not just online — partner with physical stores.

The U.S. expansion in 2015 was the bet that would define the next chapter. America had abundant credit but also credit card debt exhaustion. Millennials and Gen Z were skeptical of traditional credit. Klarna framed buy now, pay later as budgeting, not borrowing. No interest (if you paid on time), no hard credit checks, no revolving debt trap.

Partnerships accelerated everything. H&M, Zara, Macy's, Sephora. By 2019, Klarna had deals with 200,000 merchants globally. They launched a shopping app that aggregated deals and let users browse with Klarna-powered checkout embedded. The app wasn't just payments — it was discovery. Klarna became a destination.

The pandemic supercharged demand. E-commerce surged. Younger shoppers, stuck at home with stimulus checks, discovered buy now, pay later en masse. Klarna's valuation hit $45.6 billion in June 2021 — Europe's highest-valued private tech company. Siemiatkowski was everywhere — podcasts, profiles, the face of fintech's new wave.

Then the market turned. Interest rates rose. Tech valuations collapsed. Klarna's lending model — profitable when capital was cheap and defaults low — became expensive. Consumer debt started creeping up. Regulators in the U.K. and U.S. began scrutinizing buy now, pay later, asking if it encouraged overspending, particularly among young, financially precarious users.

Revenue was growing, but losses were too. Klarna had spent heavily on marketing, U.S. expansion, product development. In 2022, they raised at a $6.7 billion valuation — an 85% drop. Layoffs followed. Ten percent of staff in May, another round in November.

Siemiatkowski pivoted the narrative. Klarna wasn't just payments — it was AI-powered shopping. They leaned into machine learning for fraud detection, credit assessment, personalized recommendations. In 2024, Klarna announced its AI assistant handled two-thirds of customer service chats, replacing hundreds of human agents. Efficiency became the story when growth stalled.

But the core tension remained. Was buy now, pay later innovation or repackaged credit? Klarna argued it was both — and better. No compound interest, transparent terms, budgeting tools. Critics saw predatory lending with a millennial aesthetic. Studies showed users spent more with buy now, pay later. Defaults, while low compared to credit cards, were rising.

By 2024, Klarna was profitable in some markets, still burning cash in others. They filed confidentially for a U.S. IPO, targeting 2025. The narrative shifted again: not hyper-growth fintech darling but sustainable payments infrastructure. Slower, steadier, less glamorous.

The company that made checkout frictionless had revealed a deeper friction — between convenience and consequence. Klarna succeeded by removing the moment of hesitation before purchase. That was the product. Whether that moment existed for a reason — whether friction was feature or bug — remained unresolved.

Klarna became enormous by making spending easier. The outcome, still unfolding, will reveal whether easier was better.

---

*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Reddit
entries.section: The front page of the internet
entries.descriptor: The front page of the internet, built by accident
entries.content:
# Reddit

***The front page of the internet, built by accident***

> **Years:** 2005-present

Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian were fresh out of the University of Virginia in 2005 when they pitched Paul Graham on a different idea entirely. They wanted to build a service that let people order food from their phones — this was before smartphones, before apps, before any of that infrastructure existed. Graham rejected the idea but saw something in them. He told them to come to Boston anyway, to be part of the first Y Combinator batch, and to build something else.

They built Reddit in three weeks.

The concept was simple to the point of being obvious: a place where people could post links and vote them up or down. The best links rose to the top. The community decided what mattered. Digg already existed and was popular, but Huffman and Ohanian thought they could do it better — faster, cleaner, more focused on the community rather than the algorithm.

Reddit launched in June 2005. The early traffic was fake. Huffman and Ohanian created dozens of fake accounts and populated the site with links and discussions to make it look alive. This wasn't deception so much as necessity — nobody joins an empty party. They seeded conversations, argued with themselves, built the illusion of a community until a real community formed.

It worked. Users arrived, gradually at first, then steadily. The site grew through word of mouth among programmers, students, and the kind of people who read Slashdot and Fark. The interface was deliberately minimal — almost ugly — which became part of its identity. No ads cluttering the page, no manipulation, just links and votes and conversation in nested threads beneath.

By October 2006, just sixteen months after launch, Condé Nast acquired Reddit for somewhere between ten and twenty million dollars. For two founders barely a year out of college, it was a win. But the acquisition created problems that would define the next decade. Reddit became a division of a magazine publishing company that didn't understand it and didn't know what to do with it. Huffman left in 2009, burnt out and frustrated. Ohanian left the same year. The site they'd built kept growing, but without them.

Reddit's growth through the late 2000s and early 2010s was organic and chaotic. Subreddits — communities focused on specific topics — emerged as the core innovation. Anyone could create one. Some became enormous (r/AskReddit, r/funny, r/pics). Others were tiny and obsessive. The site became a sprawling federation of communities, each with its own culture, rules, and moderators. There was no master plan. The structure emerged from users doing what they wanted.

The community was fiercely loyal and fiercely critical. When Reddit tried to hire a "community manager," users revolted at the corporate framing. When the site went down, which it did often, users understood it as a scrappy operation held together with duct tape. That scrappiness was part of the appeal — Reddit felt like it belonged to its users, not to Condé Nast.

But scale brought problems. Subreddits dedicated to racism, sexism, and harassment flourished. Reddit's hands-off moderation philosophy — "we don't interfere unless it's illegal" — protected both the site's best communities and its worst. The company was slow to act, afraid of being seen as censors, unsure where to draw lines. When they finally did ban subreddits, it came late and inconsistently, satisfying no one.

In 2012, Condé Nast spun Reddit out into an independent company, still owned but operationally separate. Yishan Wong became CEO. He lasted two years before quitting abruptly. Ellen Pao took over in 2014 and immediately became the target of a user revolt — partly over banning harassment subreddits, partly over firing a beloved employee, partly over being a woman in charge. She resigned in 2015. The chaos was public and ugly.

That's when Huffman came back. He'd spent the intervening years building Hipmunk, a travel search engine that never broke through. Returning to Reddit meant returning to unfinished business. He instituted clearer rules, banned more hateful communities, and slowly dragged Reddit toward something resembling corporate adulthood. Users complained, but the site kept growing.

Reddit's business model was always a problem. The site was massive — hundreds of millions of users — but made little money. Ads were minimal and often ignored. Reddit Gold, a subscription service, brought in some revenue but not enough. The company raised venture capital, valuing itself in the billions, but the path to profitability remained unclear.

By the 2020s, Reddit had become culturally essential. It was where GameStop's stock surge organized, where r/WallStreetBets became a phenomenon, where niche communities thrived. It was valuable not because it made money but because it was where certain conversations happened. In 2021, Reddit filed confidentially for an IPO. In March 2024, it finally went public, pricing at $34 per share and valuing the company at over $6 billion.

The IPO wasn't triumphant. The stock popped briefly, then settled. Reddit still hadn't figured out how to monetize its users without alienating them. The company remained what it had always been: essential, chaotic, unprofitable, and impossible to replicate. It succeeded not through brilliant strategy but through survival — through building something people wanted and refusing to destroy it while trying to make it profitable. The front page of the internet was never designed. It emerged. And it's still here.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: UiPath
entries.section: The robots that do the boring work
entries.descriptor: The automation empire built from Bucharest
entries.content:
# UiPath

***The automation empire built from Bucharest***

> **Years:** 2005-present

In 2005, three Romanian engineers founded a company called DeskOver in Bucharest. Daniel Dines, Marius Tîrcă, and a small team began building automation tools for desktop applications — the kind of unsexy enterprise software that processes forms, moves data between systems, and handles repetitive digital tasks. They weren't thinking about venture capital or Silicon Valley. They were building software services for hire, the kind of work that kept the lights on in Eastern European tech hubs.

For years, they operated in obscurity. DeskOver built automation libraries and SDKs that other companies could license. The revenue was modest but steady. The team stayed lean. They sold to enterprises that needed to reduce the tedium of digital work — insurance companies processing claims, banks moving data between legacy systems, healthcare organizations managing patient records. The market existed, but it was fragmented, technical, and unsexy. No one was calling it a revolution.

By 2013, Dines recognized a pattern. Enterprises were asking for the same capabilities over and over: something that could watch what humans did on computers and replicate it. Not just APIs or scripts, but software that could interact with interfaces the way humans did — clicking buttons, reading screens, filling forms. He saw that what they'd been selling piecemeal could become a platform. He renamed the company UiPath and pivoted from services to product.

The timing was perfect, though it wasn't obvious yet. Enterprises were drowning in digital processes that couldn't be fully automated because they crossed too many systems. An insurance claim might touch five different applications, each with its own interface, each requiring human eyes and hands. Traditional enterprise software required expensive integration projects. UiPath offered something different: software robots that could be trained by watching employees work, then deployed to handle the repetitive parts.

They called it Robotic Process Automation — RPA. The term made it sound like industrial robotics for knowledge work, which was exactly the point. UiPath built a visual development environment where business analysts, not just programmers, could design automation workflows. The barrier to entry dropped. The sales cycle shortened. Enterprises that would spend months evaluating enterprise software could deploy a UiPath bot in weeks.

The company stayed in Bucharest, leveraging lower costs and a deep talent pool in a city that had quietly become one of Europe's engineering centers. But Dines knew the customers were elsewhere. In 2015, UiPath opened offices in London and New York. They began selling into the Fortune 500 in earnest.

Revenue grew fast. In 2017, they closed a $30 million Series A, their first institutional funding after twelve years of bootstrapping. Six months later, they raised $153 million. The venture world suddenly noticed. Here was a capital-efficient company with massive revenue growth, strong unit economics, and a market that seemed enormous. Every large enterprise had processes begging for automation.

By 2018, UiPath was the fastest-growing enterprise software company in history by some metrics. ARR went from $1 million to $100 million in under three years. Competitors emerged — Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism — and the RPA category became one of the hottest in enterprise tech. Analysts proclaimed it a revolution. UiPath raised $568 million at a $7 billion valuation, becoming Romania's first decacorn.

The growth came with challenges. The product was powerful but required skill to implement well. Enterprises would deploy dozens or hundreds of bots, then struggle to manage them. Some bots broke when underlying applications changed. The promise of business users building their own automation collided with the reality that complex workflows still required technical expertise. UiPath invested heavily in making the platform more robust, building governance tools, adding AI capabilities for reading documents and understanding unstructured data.

The company also faced the inherent tension in its business model. RPA was often a stepping stone. Enterprises used it to automate processes across legacy systems they couldn't easily replace. But over time, those legacy systems would be replaced or integrated properly. What happened to the RPA layer then? UiPath's answer was to become more than just screen-scraping robots — to add machine learning, process mining, and orchestration, to become the intelligent automation platform that coordinated work across an enterprise, not just mimicked human clicks.

In April 2021, UiPath went public at a $35 billion valuation, one of the largest software IPOs in years. Dines rang the opening bell at the NYSE. The company that had bootstrapped for over a decade, that had started in Bucharest far from the usual startup centers, had become one of the most valuable software companies in the world.

But the public markets were less forgiving than private ones. Revenue growth, while still strong, was slowing. The pandemic had accelerated digital transformation, but it had also pulled forward demand. The competitive landscape was shifting — Microsoft was building automation tools into its own platforms. The question of whether RPA was a permanent layer or a temporary bridge remained unanswered. The stock dropped 40% in the first year, then stabilized as the company demonstrated it could grow sustainably and expand beyond pure RPA.

By 2024, UiPath was still growing, still profitable, still headquartered in New York but with deep roots in Bucharest. The outcome wasn't a fairy tale exit or a spectacular crash. It was something more interesting: proof that you could build a massive enterprise software company far from traditional centers, that bootstrapping discipline could coexist with venture scale, and that the most valuable automation wasn't always the most visible.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: YouTube
entries.descriptor: The video platform that won by losing money at scale
entries.content:
# YouTube

***The video platform that won by losing money at scale***

> **Years:** 2005-present

In February 2005, three former PayPal employees registered the domain youtube.com. Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim had witnessed the infrastructure revolution at PayPal — how to move money at internet scale — and they saw another infrastructure problem waiting to be solved. The internet was choking on video.

The founding story has multiple versions. One involves a dinner party where guests couldn't easily share videos. Another centers on Janet Jackson's Super Bowl halftime show and the impossibility of finding clips online. The truth is simpler: video was broken on the web. Files were too large. Formats were incompatible. Hosting was expensive. Embedding was impossible. If you wanted to share a video, you attached it to an email and hoped.

The technical challenge was immense. Video requires bandwidth that would bankrupt most startups. Storage costs scale linearly with content. Transcoding — converting uploaded videos into formats that browsers could play — required serious computational power. Most sensible people would have concluded the unit economics made a video platform impossible.

YouTube's insight was that the unit economics didn't matter if the platform became essential. They would lose money on every video view and make it up in volume — or rather, in becoming the default infrastructure for internet video. They built for ease: one-click uploading, automatic transcoding, simple embedding. The technical complexity disappeared behind a red upload button.

The first video, "Me at the zoo," uploaded April 23, 2005, showed Jawed Karim at the San Diego Zoo talking about elephants. Eighteen seconds. Unremarkable. But it worked. You clicked, it played. No plugins, no downloads, no format confusion.

Growth came faster than anyone expected. By July 2006, users were uploading 65,000 videos daily. By October, 100 million videos viewed per day. The traffic was staggering. The bandwidth bills were terrifying. Sequoia Capital led a $3.5 million Series A in November 2005, followed by an $8 million Series B in April 2006. The money burned fast. Every viral video was a financial hit.

But virality was the product. A lonelygirl15 video would get a million views. An SNL sketch would spread before the network could issue a takedown. User-generated content exploded — bedroom musicians, makeup tutorials, gaming commentary, amateur comedy. YouTube wasn't hosting professional content; it was creating a new category of creator.

The copyright situation was legally catastrophic. Users uploaded TV shows, movies, music videos. Viacom sued for $1 billion. The major labels threatened. The DMCA's safe harbor provisions protected YouTube — barely — as long as they responded to takedown notices. They built ContentID, a system for identifying copyrighted material automatically. It was innovative and insufficient. The lawsuits piled up.

Google was watching. They had launched Google Video in 2005 and it was dying. YouTube was winning because it was simpler, faster, more viral. Google tried to compete, then realized they should acquire. In October 2006, eighteen months after YouTube's launch, Google paid $1.65 billion.

The tech press called it insane. YouTube had virtually no revenue. The copyright liability was enormous. The bandwidth costs were unsustainable. But Google saw infrastructure. They had the data centers, the bandwidth, the ad technology. YouTube had the network effects and the cultural dominance. Google could absorb the losses while building the monetization.

It took years. YouTube lost money consistently through 2010. Ad rates on user-generated content were terrible. Premium advertisers didn't want their brands next to amateur videos. The platform experimented — partnerships with professional content creators, YouTube TV, YouTube Premium, Super Chat for live streams. They built an entire creator economy: ad revenue sharing, sponsorships, merchandise integration.

The breakthrough was scale and patience. As broadband spread and mobile video exploded, YouTube became default infrastructure. Every phone had the app. Every website embedded YouTube videos. Musicians launched careers from bedroom studios. Brands built channels. Politicians campaigned. The presidential debates streamed live.

By 2020, YouTube generated $19.8 billion in ad revenue. The creator economy they spawned was worth tens of billions more. MrBeast built a production empire on YouTube revenue. Educational channels reached millions. The platform had become essential cultural infrastructure — the world's second-largest search engine, processing more queries than Bing, Yahoo, and Ask combined.

The unit economics eventually worked, but only at a scale that would have killed any normal startup. Google's willingness to lose billions while the platform matured was the hidden subsidy that made YouTube possible. No VC-backed startup could have survived long enough to reach profitability. The infrastructure play only worked because Google could treat it as infrastructure.

YouTube didn't solve the copyright problem; they outlasted it. ContentID processed hundreds of millions of claims. Rights holders learned they made more money monetizing viral clips than issuing takedowns. The system was imperfect but sustainable — controversial enough to anger creators and copyright holders in equal measure, but functional enough to keep operating.

The company the founders built was a bet that infrastructure would matter more than economics, that dominance would eventually generate revenue, that being essential was more valuable than being profitable. They were right, but only because Google could afford to be patient. The path from three former PayPal employees to the world's video platform required both vision and the kind of institutional patience that only a search monopoly's balance sheet could provide.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: 23andMe
entries.section: Spit in a tube, learn your fate
entries.descriptor: The privacy promise that became a data trap
entries.content:
# 23andMe

***The privacy promise that became a data trap***

> **Years:** 2006-present

In 2006, Anne Wojcicki saw a market inefficiency so obvious it seemed criminal. Healthcare was a trillion-dollar industry built on reactive treatment, yet the human genome — sequenced just three years earlier — offered the possibility of predictive, personalized medicine. The data existed. The technology was plummeting in cost. But patients couldn't access their own genetic information without a doctor's permission, and doctors had little incentive to order tests for healthy people.

Wojcicki wasn't a scientist. She was a Wall Street analyst who'd spent years studying healthcare companies and concluded the entire industry was designed to keep patients in the dark. Her co-founders brought the technical credibility: Linda Avey had worked in genomics, and Paul Cusenza in bioinformatics. They raised $3.9 million from venture firms and launched with a vision that merged consumer empowerment with scientific ambition: sell genetic tests directly to customers, build the world's largest genetic database, and use that data to revolutionize drug discovery.

The early product was expensive — $999 for a spit kit that told you about ancestry, disease risk, and traits like earwax type. But it hit the cultural moment perfectly. This was peak Web 2.0, when "democratizing information" was gospel and users happily traded data for services. Time Magazine named 23andMe's kit "Invention of the Year" in 2008. Wojcicki's marriage to Google co-founder Sergey Brin didn't hurt the buzz. Google Ventures invested $3.9 million in 2007.

The business model was elegant in theory. Phase one: sell tests, build database. Phase two: monetize the database through pharmaceutical partnerships. Customers would get cheap genetic insights. Drug companies would get unprecedented research data. 23andMe would sit in the middle, collecting revenue from both sides.

The FDA had other ideas. In 2013, after years of informal warnings, the agency ordered 23andMe to stop marketing health-related genetic tests. The company had been providing information about disease risk — including for serious conditions like breast cancer and Alzheimer's — without proving the tests were accurate or that customers could interpret them correctly. 23andMe kept selling ancestry tests but had to gut the health reports that justified the $99 price point.

The ban lasted four years. During that time, 23andMe made a strategic choice that would define its future: it pivoted hard into the pharmaceutical partnership model. In 2015, it launched a therapeutics division. By 2018, it had partnered with GlaxoSmithKline in a $300 million deal giving GSK access to 23andMe's genetic database — now containing data from over 5 million customers who'd consented to research participation.

The FDA gradually relented. Between 2017 and 2019, 23andMe won approval to offer certain health reports again. The company went public via SPAC in 2021 at a $3.5 billion valuation. Wojcicki framed the listing as vindication: they'd survived the regulatory gauntlet and built exactly what they'd promised — a genetic database powering drug discovery.

But the math had shifted underneath them. The cost of genetic sequencing kept falling, which meant tests kept getting cheaper — good for customers, bad for margins. Competition intensified from Ancestry.com and others. By 2021, most people who wanted a consumer genetics test had already bought one. The market was saturated.

Worse, the pharmaceutical partnerships weren't producing blockbuster returns. Drug discovery is slow and failure-prone even with perfect data. 23andMe's database, while large, had sampling problems: customers were overwhelmingly white, female, and affluent. The therapeutics division burned cash without bringing products to market.

Then came the part Wojcicki probably didn't anticipate in 2006: customers got scared. Initially, people had trusted 23andMe with their genetic data because the company promised it would never be shared without consent. But after the GSK deal, privacy advocates started asking harder questions. What happens to your data if 23andMe gets acquired? Goes bankrupt? Gets hacked? In 2023, a data breach exposed information on 6.9 million users — not their raw genetic data, but enough to spook the market.

The stock collapsed. By late 2024, shares that traded at $17.65 on SPAC debut were under $5. The company announced layoffs and shut down its therapeutics division. The board resigned en masse over disagreements about strategy. Wojcicki took the company private in a desperate attempt to avoid delisting.

The irony is sharp. 23andMe succeeded at its original mission: it built a massive genetic database and proved consumer genomics could work. Over 14 million people have used its service. The company generated real scientific insights about disease and human variation. But success at the mission didn't translate to a sustainable business.

The problem wasn't the science or the vision. It was the model itself. Consumer genetics turned out to be a one-time purchase in a market that reached saturation within a decade. The database — the supposed moat — was only valuable if customers trusted you with it, and trust is fragile when your business model requires monetizing their most intimate data. Drug companies would pay for access, but not enough to justify a multi-billion dollar valuation.

Wojcicki had promised to democratize genetic information. She did. But what she built was worth far less than what it cost to build. The data is still there, sitting on servers, representing millions of people who thought they were buying personalized health insights. What they actually bought was a seat on a platform whose business model required turning their DNA into someone else's product.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Spotify
entries.descriptor: The pirates who became the music industry
entries.content:
# Spotify

***The pirates who became the music industry***

> **Years:** 2006–present

In 2006, the music industry was at war with the internet. Napster had been crushed. LimeWire and BitTorrent flourished in the shadows. Record labels sued teenagers. Steve Jobs had convinced them to sell songs for 99 cents on iTunes, but piracy still dominated. The industry's strategy was legal threats and technological barriers. It wasn't working.

Daniel Ek saw something else. A Swedish programmer who'd made millions building advertising technology, Ek had grown up pirating music. He understood the appeal: instant access to everything. But he also saw piracy's friction — the hunt for files, the quality issues, the malware. He believed you could beat piracy not by making it illegal, but by making legal music better than pirated music. The insight seemed obvious. Convincing anyone it could work was another matter.

Ek partnered with Martin Lorentzon, another Swedish tech entrepreneur. They started Spotify in Stockholm, far from Silicon Valley and the music industry's power centers. That distance proved useful. The major labels were embattled and desperate. Ek offered them a deal: let him stream their entire catalogs, and he'd pay them a share of revenue — both from ads and subscriptions. The labels had rejected similar proposals before. But Ek had leverage. Piracy in Sweden was so rampant that any revenue seemed better than nothing. In 2008, Spotify launched in Sweden with unprecedented access: virtually all recorded music, instantly, legally.

The product was remarkable. No waiting for downloads. No managing files. Search for any song and it played within seconds. The experience felt magical because it was technically brutal. Spotify deployed peer-to-peer technology — the same infrastructure that powered piracy — to reduce server costs and latency. Users didn't just consume music; their devices helped distribute it. The irony was intentional.

Growth in Sweden was explosive. Within months, piracy dropped measurably. Ek had proven the concept: make legal easier than illegal, and people switch. But Sweden was small. The real prize was global expansion, and the labels weren't ready to give him that. They'd been burned before. They wanted to see sustained revenue. They wanted control. They wanted to hedge their bets by licensing to multiple services. Ek spent years negotiating country-by-country launches. It was slow, frustrating, and necessary. By 2011, Spotify was in seven European countries and finally, the United States.

The US launch came with complications. Free streaming enraged some artists. Thom Yorke called it "the last desperate fart of a dying corpse." Taylor Swift pulled her catalog. The criticism stung, but the economics were real: per-stream payouts were fractions of a cent. Spotify's defense was existential — without streaming, piracy would dominate and artists would get nothing. The labels, now dependent on Spotify's revenue, mostly agreed. The company kept growing.

The growth strategy was relentless user acquisition, funded by venture capital. Spotify raised hundreds of millions while remaining private far longer than typical tech unicorns. The delay was strategic. Going public meant transparency about the uncomfortable truth: Spotify paid roughly 70% of revenue to labels. The margins were terrible. Profitability seemed distant. But the user growth was undeniable. By 2015, Spotify had 75 million users, 20 million paying. By 2018, when it finally went public via direct listing, it had 170 million users, 75 million paying.

The public markets revealed what insiders knew: Spotify was a scale business with structural problems. The labels held leverage because they owned the content. Spotify couldn't differentiate on catalog — everyone had the same songs. It competed on experience, recommendations, and playlists. The algorithmic playlists — Discover Weekly, Release Radar — became cultural phenomena. They worked because Spotify had data no one else had: what millions of people listened to, when, and in what sequence. The company wasn't just streaming music; it was learning taste.

But learning taste didn't solve the margin problem. Spotify tried various strategies. It paid labels less per stream if users found music through Spotify playlists rather than artist pages — a move that infuriated musicians but improved economics. It invested heavily in podcasts, acquiring Gimlet, Anchor, and Parcast, then signing exclusive deals with Joe Rogan and others. Podcasts had better margins because Spotify could sell ads directly and own the content. The strategy was obvious: become less dependent on music labels.

The label problem persisted anyway. Even as Spotify's revenue grew into billions, profitability remained elusive. The company reported quarterly profits occasionally, then losses again. Investors tolerated it because the user growth continued and the vision remained compelling: become the audio platform for everything. Music, podcasts, audiobooks. Global reach, personalized delivery, data-driven curation.

By 2024, Spotify had over 600 million users, more than 200 million paying. It dominated music streaming globally. It had transformed how people consumed music, resurrected the industry's revenue, and made Ek a billionaire. But it still paid most of its revenue to labels. It still fought for margins. The company had won the format war but remained trapped in the economics it had accepted to launch.

The story of Spotify is the story of revolutionary user experience built on unrevolutionary business terms. Ek saw the future correctly: streaming would replace ownership, access would beat piracy, and personalization would define the experience. But seeing the future and controlling it are different things. Spotify needed the labels to exist. That need became permanent. The pirates became the industry, but the industry still owned the treasure.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Twitter/X
entries.descriptor: The town square that almost died, survived, and became a political weapon
entries.content:
# Twitter/X

***The town square that almost died, survived, and became a political weapon***

> **Years:** 2006–present

In March 2006, Jack Dorsey sent the first tweet: "just setting up my twttr." The platform emerged from Odeo, a failing podcasting startup whose founders—Dorsey, Evan Williams, and Biz Stone—needed a new idea after iTunes added podcasting support. They built a service where you could broadcast short status updates to your friends via SMS. The 140-character limit came from the constraints of text messaging. They called it obvious, simple, almost trivial.

For two years, Twitter was a curiosity. Tech insiders used it. Mainstream culture ignored it. The company had no business model, chronic technical failures, and a product that was difficult to explain. The "fail whale"—the error page showing a whale being lifted by birds—became Twitter's unofficial mascot because users saw it so often. The servers couldn't handle the traffic. The team couldn't decide what Twitter was for.

Then, in 2007, they set up screens at South by Southwest. Tweets from the conference scrolled past in real-time. Conference-goers started tweeting obsessively. Twitter usage tripled. The team won the festival's web award. More importantly, they discovered what Twitter was: a global conversation layer, a way to follow events as they happened through the eyes of people experiencing them.

The Iranian election protests of 2009 proved it. When the government blocked traditional media, protesters used Twitter to organize and broadcast to the world. The State Department asked Twitter to delay scheduled maintenance to keep the service running. Suddenly Twitter wasn't just a tech platform—it was infrastructure for global communication, for revolution, for collective attention.

The growth was explosive and chaotic. Celebrities joined. Media organizations built their strategies around it. The Arab Spring happened on Twitter. By 2013, the platform had 200 million active users and filed for an IPO. The company was valued at $31 billion on its first day of trading.

But Twitter never figured out how to make money at the scale its influence suggested it should. It sold ads, but users hated them and the targeting was weak. It tried promoted tweets, but engagement was low. Facebook printed money by knowing everything about its users. Twitter knew what you said publicly but couldn't monetize that into reliable revenue. Wall Street grew impatient. The stock price declined. User growth stalled around 300 million—massive but tiny compared to Facebook's billions.

Worse, the platform became toxic. Harassment campaigns proliferated. Coordinated abuse targeted women and minorities. Bots spread disinformation. State actors used Twitter to manipulate elections. The company's moderation was inconsistent and widely criticized from all sides. Twitter was simultaneously too censorious and not censorious enough, depending on who you asked. The global town square had become a shouting match and a weapon.

Leadership churned. Dorsey was ousted as CEO in 2008, returned in 2015. Dick Costolo led through the IPO years. Each regime promised to fix growth, fix revenue, fix toxicity. None succeeded completely. By 2020, activist investors were pushing for Dorsey's removal again. The board resisted, then didn't. In 2021, Dorsey stepped down and Parag Agrawal, the CTO, took over.

Four months later, Elon Musk bought 9% of the company. Then he offered to buy it all for $44 billion. Then he tried to back out, claiming Twitter had lied about the number of bots on the platform. Twitter sued to force the deal through. In October 2022, Musk completed the purchase, firing Agrawal and most of the executive team immediately.

What followed was unlike any acquisition in tech history. Musk fired roughly 80% of Twitter's employees, sometimes via late-night email. He reinstated banned accounts, including Donald Trump's. He charged for verification, destroying the system that distinguished real accounts from impersonators. He rebranded Twitter to "X," claiming it would become an "everything app." Advertisers fled. Revenue collapsed. The platform's reliability degraded. Musk tweeted conspiracy theories and attacked critics. Major brands left. Competitors like Threads and Bluesky emerged, founded by former Twitter employees.

Yet Twitter—X—persisted. Musk's changes drove some users away but activated others. The platform became more explicitly political, more chaotic, more central to certain communities and less relevant to others. Musk claimed X was worth less than half what he paid for it, but he also integrated it with his other companies and used it as a political weapon, openly supporting candidates and causes.

By 2024, X was unrecognizable from the Twitter of 2006, or even 2020. It had survived countless near-deaths: technical failures, revenue crises, leadership chaos, and an acquisition that would have destroyed most companies. It remained one of the most influential platforms in the world, shaping political discourse and breaking news, even as its cultural dominance fragmented and its business model remained unsolved.

The path Twitter took—from SMS experiment to revolution platform to cautionary tale to oligarch's megaphone—revealed something fundamental about social networks: they are not just products but public infrastructure, yet they are owned and controlled by private interests. Twitter's influence always exceeded its business success. Its outcome remains unwritten.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Shopify
entries.descriptor: The snowboard shop that became the infrastructure of commerce
entries.content:
# Shopify

***The snowboard shop that became the infrastructure of commerce***

> **Years:** 2004-present

In 2004, Tobias Lütke wanted to sell snowboards online. He was a programmer from Germany living in Ottawa, frustrated by how terrible the available e-commerce software was. The existing platforms—clunky, expensive, designed for enterprises—made simple tasks complicated. Lütke decided to build his own. The snowboard shop, Snowdevil, never became much of a business. The software he built to run it became Shopify.

Lütke's co-founders were Scott Lake, a college friend, and Daniel Weinand, who had worked with Lütke in Germany. They weren't trying to build a platform. They were trying to solve a problem they had themselves. The software was written in Ruby on Rails—a framework so new it barely had documentation. This choice would matter later. Rails made rapid development possible. It also attracted a certain kind of developer, the kind who valued craft and disliked bloat.

They launched Shopify in 2006 as a hosted platform. You paid a monthly fee, set up your store, and started selling. No servers to manage, no software to install. This was obvious in retrospect but radical at the time. E-commerce platforms were complicated beasts that required IT departments. Shopify was for people who just wanted to sell things.

The early growth was slow and organic. Small merchants, artists, makers—people who couldn't afford Magento or didn't want to deal with osCommerce. The platform was simple but extensible. Developers could build apps and themes. This wasn't planned as a strategic moat. It emerged from Lütke's engineering philosophy: build the core well, let others extend it.

The first real money came from venture capital in 2010—a $7 million Series A. By then, Shopify had tens of thousands of merchants. The business model was elegant: subscription revenue plus a cut of payment processing. The more merchants sold, the more Shopify made. Incentives aligned.

The competition in the early years was fragmented. BigCommerce, Volusion, Magento—each had a niche. Amazon was the giant, but Amazon didn't let you own your brand or your customer. Shopify did. This distinction—merchant empowerment versus platform dominance—became the core of Shopify's identity. They positioned themselves as the anti-Amazon, the infrastructure for independent commerce.

The real inflection came around 2013-2014. Mobile commerce was exploding. Shopify had invested heavily in mobile checkout, making it smooth when most platforms treated mobile as an afterthought. They also launched the Shopify App Store, turning the platform into an ecosystem. Developers built tools for email marketing, inventory management, shipping, analytics. Merchants could compose their own stack. Shopify was becoming infrastructure.

The IPO in 2015 raised $131 million at a $1.3 billion valuation. Lütke remained CEO—unusual for a founder at that scale, but Lütke was unusual. He was technical, product-focused, uninterested in the performance of being a tech CEO. He wrote code. He thought in systems. Shopify's culture reflected this: engineering-driven, deliberate, allergic to bullshit.

The platform kept expanding. Shopify Plus launched for enterprise merchants. Shopify Capital offered loans to merchants. Shopify Shipping negotiated rates with carriers. Shopify POS brought the platform into physical retail. Each move deepened the moat. Switching costs grew not through lock-in but through integration. A merchant using ten Shopify services had no reason to leave.

The pandemic was an accelerant. Physical retail collapsed overnight. Every business needed to sell online immediately. Shopify's sign-ups surged. Revenue doubled. The company's market cap briefly exceeded $150 billion, making it more valuable than any traditional retailer. Lütke became one of the richest people in Canada.

But the pandemic boom was also a trap. Shopify hired aggressively—headcount ballooned. They expanded into logistics, building fulfillment centers to compete directly with Amazon. This was a departure from their core model. Shopify had always been software. Fulfillment was physical, capital-intensive, a different business entirely.

The pullback was brutal. In 2022, as e-commerce growth normalized, Shopify laid off 10% of its workforce. Lütke took responsibility in a memo that was uncommonly direct: they had bet the pandemic surge would continue and been wrong. In 2023, they sold the logistics business to Flexport, admitting the mistake. The market cap had fallen by more than half from its peak.

Yet the core business remained. By 2024, Shopify powered over two million merchants, processing hundreds of billions in gross merchandise volume. It was the infrastructure of independent commerce—not the biggest platform, but the one that let anyone compete. The snowboard shop had become the picks and shovels of the internet economy.

The path Shopify took was unusual. They started from a real need, built software that solved it, and expanded concentrically. They avoided the temptation to become Amazon. When they tried—logistics, fulfillment—they retreated. The discipline to stay close to their core was rare. So was the founder who stayed technical, stayed deliberate, stayed CEO.

The outcome is still being written, but the pattern is visible: infrastructure wins by enabling others. Shopify didn't need to be the biggest merchant. They needed to be essential to all the merchants. That distinction—between being the store and being the system that powers stores—was the entire strategy.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: HubSpot
entries.descriptor: The bootstrap that became an empire by inventing its own category
entries.content:
# HubSpot

***The bootstrap that became an empire by inventing its own category***

> **Years:** 2006–present

In 2004, Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah were graduate students at MIT's Sloan School of Management. Halligan had spent years in sales and was frustrated by how broken the traditional marketing playbook had become. Cold calling yielded diminishing returns. Trade show booths gathered dust. Direct mail went straight to recycling. Meanwhile, buyers were doing their own research online, finding vendors through Google searches and blog posts, making decisions before ever talking to a salesperson.

The insight was simple but profound: the internet had inverted the buyer-seller relationship. Instead of interrupting people with ads and cold calls, companies needed to attract customers by being helpful. Create content that answers their questions. Optimize for search engines. Build trust before asking for the sale. Halligan called this "inbound marketing" — a term that didn't exist yet, which is precisely what made it powerful.

Shah had built a small software company as an undergraduate and sold it for modest returns. He understood code and product. Halligan understood go-to-market. In 2006, they each put in $250,000 — Shah's from his exit, Halligan's mostly borrowed — and started building. The first product was blogging software with built-in SEO tools and analytics. Not revolutionary technology, but it packaged what marketers needed to execute on this inbound philosophy.

The real genius wasn't the software. It was the evangelism. Halligan and Shah didn't just sell a product; they sold a worldview. They wrote a book, *Inbound Marketing*, that became a manifesto. They launched an annual conference, INBOUND, that became a pilgrimage. They created a certification program that trained thousands of marketers in their methodology — and made them advocates for the software that enabled it. They were building a movement, and movements don't have competitors; they have converts and heathens.

The early customers were mostly small businesses — the long tail of companies who couldn't afford Salesforce or Marketo but needed something better than spreadsheets and gut instinct. HubSpot charged monthly subscriptions starting at a few hundred dollars. Not enough to get rich quick, but enough to grow steadily. The unit economics worked because the product was self-serve and the customers sold themselves through the content ecosystem HubSpot had built.

By 2011, they had raised $65 million across several rounds and had thirty-three thousand customers. But they were also burning cash. The sales team was expensive. Customer acquisition costs were climbing. Churn was higher than investors wanted. Shah, as CTO, had built the product on a Ruby on Rails stack that was starting to buckle under scale. Technical debt was accumulating faster than they could pay it down.

The cultural blueprint was equally ambitious and brittle. HubSpot promoted radical transparency — publishing the company's investor pitch deck publicly, sharing metrics internally that most companies locked in vaults. They codified their culture in a 128-slide deck called the "Culture Code" that went viral and became a recruiting magnet. But the reality behind the manifesto was messier. When journalist Dan Lyons joined as a content marketer in 2013, he found a pressure-cooker environment of metrics obsession and youth worship. His subsequent book, *Disrupted*, painted HubSpot as a caricature of startup excess: ping-pong tables and beer kegs masking a ruthless focus on growth at any cost.

The controversy didn't slow them down. In 2014, HubSpot went public at a $880 million valuation. The IPO was well-timed — just before the late-2015 SaaS correction that would have made it much harder. They used the capital to expand beyond marketing software into sales tools, then customer service, then CMS. The strategy was to own the entire customer relationship stack for small and medium businesses. Not to compete with Salesforce for enterprise giants, but to be the all-in-one platform for companies who found Salesforce too complex and expensive.

The category creation strategy paid compounding dividends. "Inbound marketing" became industry vernacular.
