A customer was using their service to run a premium SMS scam, racking up charges that customers disputed. The chargebacks hit Twilio's merchant account. Within days, their payment processor threatened to freeze all operations. The company was weeks from running out of money, but the immediate threat was that legitimate customers would suddenly lose phone service because of one bad actor.

Lawson and the team worked frantically with payment processors, implemented fraud detection systems, and brought in compliance expertise. They survived, barely, but learned a brutal lesson: when you become infrastructure, you inherit all the downstream risk. They invested heavily in trust and safety systems, built compliance teams, and implemented sophisticated abuse detection. The playbook wasn't in any startup guide because no software company had operated at the intersection of telecommunications regulation and developer platform before.

The platform expanded methodically. SMS came next, then messaging. Each addition followed the same pattern: take something complex in telecommunications, expose it through clean APIs, let developers build. They added phone number purchasing, call recording, conference calling, SIP trunking. Each feature opened new use cases and brought new customers.

Going public in 2016 validated the model but also revealed its challenge. Revenue grew consistently — from $167 million in 2014 to over $400 million in 2016 — but profitability remained elusive. The infrastructure costs were real, the competition from both telecom incumbents and new API companies was intensifying, and customers expected prices to decrease over time even as reliability expectations increased.

The strategic response was expansion beyond core telephony. They acquired SendGrid for email APIs in 2018, a $3 billion bet that communications meant more than just voice and SMS. The acquisition doubled the customer base overnight and expanded the platform's utility. A customer could now handle all their communication needs — email, SMS, voice, video — through a single platform with one bill and one API paradigm.

The pandemic became an unexpected accelerator. As the world moved to remote everything, the demand for programmable communications exploded. Telemedicine needed video and SMS. Remote work needed better coordination tools. Contact tracing required automated calling and messaging at massive scale. Twilio's revenue jumped from $1.1 billion in 2019 to $2.8 billion in 2021. The stock price reached dizzying heights, briefly making the company worth over $70 billion.

But the post-pandemic adjustment was harsh. Growth slowed as pull-forward demand normalized. Competition intensified as every cloud provider launched competing services. The stock collapsed, falling 80% from its peak. In 2023, activist investors pushed for restructuring. Lawson, who had led the company from inception, faced pressure to demonstrate a path to sustained profitability, not just growth.

The company responded with layoffs — twice in six months — cutting roughly 25% of the workforce. They restructured around product-led growth and enterprise sales, acknowledging that the pure developer-first model had limits. The magic of the early days — developers discovering Twilio organically and scaling it themselves — still worked for small customers, but the big revenue came from enterprise deals that required sales teams, implementation support, and custom pricing.

Today, Twilio sits in an unusual position. It succeeded completely in its original mission: voice and SMS are now programmable through APIs that developers take for granted. But that success created a commodity market. The infrastructure works so reliably that it became invisible, and invisibility in technology often means price compression. The company processes billions of interactions, serves hundreds of thousands of developers, and remains the default choice for programmable communications. Yet it searches for what comes next — the adjacent market that's still complex enough to command platform pricing, still novel enough to avoid commodity competition.

The path from those first phone call APIs to a communications platform worth tens of billions revealed a pattern that other infrastructure companies would follow: find something hard, make it easy, scale it massively, then figure out how to defend margins in a market you created. Twilio proved telecommunications could be software. Now it has to prove software margins can survive in telecommunications.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: GitHub
entries.descriptor: The social network that made code collaborative
entries.content:
# GitHub

***The social network that made code collaborative***

> **Years:** 2008-2018 (acquired by Microsoft)

In 2007, open source software lived in a maze of disconnected repositories, mailing lists, and patch files. Contributing to a project meant navigating arcane command-line tools and begging maintainers to accept your changes. Three programmers in San Francisco — Tom Preston-Werner, Chris Wanstrath, and PJ Hyett — saw this friction and built something elegant: a website that made Git, the version control system, actually pleasant to use.

They called it GitHub. The insight wasn't the technology — Git already existed, created by Linus Torvalds for Linux development. The insight was social: treat code like a social network. Make repositories discoverable. Make forking frictionless. Make pull requests visual. Turn the solitary act of programming into something collaborative and public.

Preston-Werner wrote the founding vision in a blog post: "I want to make GitHub the best place to share code with friends, co-workers, classmates, and complete strangers." They built it nights and weekends while consulting. No business plan. No fundraising pitch. Just three developers scratching their own itch.

They launched in April 2008. Within a year, 46,000 repositories. The timing was accidental perfection. Ruby on Rails was exploding. jQuery was going mainstream. Node.js would arrive soon. Every open source project needed hosting, and SourceForge — the incumbent — was slow, ugly, and hostile to Git. GitHub won by being obviously better.

The business model came later, almost as an afterthought: free public repositories, paid private ones. Developers loved it. Then something unexpected happened: companies started using GitHub internally. Engineering teams at startups wanted the same workflow for private code. GitHub's biggest competitor became... installing Git servers yourself. They won by making the alternative look medieval.

By 2012, GitHub had raised $100 million at a $750 million valuation without ever needing the money. They were profitable. Revenue from companies buying private repositories dwarfed the free tier. Andreessen Horowitz invested not because GitHub needed capital but because being part of this company seemed wise. Every major technology company now depended on GitHub infrastructure. The platform had become essential.

But success brought tension. Preston-Werner, the visionary founder, became a liability. In 2014, his wife's harassment of a female employee led to internal revolt. The company hired an investigator. Preston-Werner resigned. For a company built on openness and collaboration, the irony was brutal. GitHub had exported its culture to millions of developers while failing to maintain it internally.

Wanstrath took over as CEO. He faced a company that had grown to cultural importance without ever quite becoming a traditional business. GitHub employed hundreds but still felt like a developer collective. It had revolutionized software but hadn't built much management structure. The product had evolved through what users demanded, not strategic planning. This worked until it didn't.

The platform's very success created problems. GitHub had become infrastructure — critical, expected, free for open source. But infrastructure requires reliability, security, and support at scales that hobbyist-turned-company DNA struggled to provide. Downtime meant thousands of projects blocked. Security issues meant backdoors into major software. GitHub needed to grow up.

Microsoft had been watching. For years, Microsoft was the enemy — proprietary software, closed ecosystems, corporate rigidity. Developers using GitHub often built software that competed with Microsoft products. But under Satya Nadella, Microsoft had transformed. The company open-sourced .NET. It embraced Linux. It moved to cloud-first strategy where developers were the customer, not the obstacle.

In 2018, Microsoft offered $7.5 billion. The developer community reacted with predictable horror. Microsoft buying GitHub felt like Empire acquiring the Rebellion. The company that represented closed-source corporate software buying the platform that represented open-source community values seemed like capitulation or corruption or both.

But Wanstrath knew something the critics didn't: GitHub needed resources it couldn't generate alone. The platform was buckling under its own importance. Microsoft could provide infrastructure, security expertise, and enterprise sales that GitHub lacked. More importantly, Microsoft needed GitHub more than GitHub needed to stay independent. Nadella's transformation required developer credibility, and GitHub was credibility incarnated.

The acquisition closed in October 2018. Microsoft kept GitHub largely autonomous — branding, culture, roadmap. They poured resources in: more employees, better infrastructure, free private repositories for everyone. The nightmare scenario where Microsoft ruined GitHub didn't materialize. Instead, GitHub got the support it needed while Microsoft got the developer relationship it craved.

The story reveals a particular path: build essential infrastructure, grow through utility not marketing, become too important to fail, get acquired by someone who needs what you represent. GitHub never planned this. They built a better mousetrap and discovered they'd built a city.

Ten years from founding to exit at $7.5 billion. Three developers who wanted to make Git easier ended up changing how software gets made. Every major company now uses GitHub or GitHub-like workflows. The pull request is standard. Code review is expected. Open source is default. The social network for code became the substrate for software itself.

The platform that made code collaborative got absorbed into the company that once symbolized the opposite. But perhaps that's the outcome that makes sense: GitHub changed Microsoft more than Microsoft changed GitHub. The acquisition wasn't defeat. It was proof that the revolution had won.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Shein
entries.descriptor: From factory-floor algorithm to fast-fashion empire built on data, speed, and deliberate opacity
entries.content:
# Shein

***From factory-floor algorithm to fast-fashion empire built on data, speed, and deliberate opacity***

> **Years:** 2008-present

In 2008, Chris Xu was a search engine optimization consultant in Nanjing when he noticed something the Western fashion industry hadn't: China's garment factories had enormous excess capacity, lightning-fast production cycles, and almost no connection to end consumers. Meanwhile, online shoppers were typing specific searches — "pink floral midi dress," "oversized denim jacket" — that revealed exactly what they wanted to buy. Xu saw the gap. He started Shein not as a fashion company but as a data operation that happened to sell clothes.

The model was radical in its simplicity. Shein would produce nothing until the data said to produce it. Third-party sellers listed designs on the platform. The system tracked which items got clicks, which got added to carts, which got purchased. Winners got reordered in batches of 100-200 units. Losers disappeared. A design could go from concept to website listing in three days. From trending on TikTok to shipping from a Guangzhou warehouse in a week. Traditional fashion brands planned collections eighteen months in advance. Shein's inventory turned over in weeks.

Xu kept the operation invisible. No brand story. No founder interviews. No flagship stores. Just an app that fed an algorithm. The website was a slot machine of cheap clothes — $8 crop tops, $12 jeans, $3 bikinis — with new items added by the thousands daily. The experience was overwhelming by design. Scarcity was artificial but effective: items showed "Only 2 left in stock!" to trigger impulse purchases. Models were generic. Product photos were formulaic. Nothing suggested craftsmanship or heritage. Everything suggested urgency.

The supply chain was the secret. Shein operated out of Guangzhou's garment district, where small factories competed desperately for orders. Xu's system gave them orders, but only if they agreed to brutal terms: accept any design, produce any quantity on demand, deliver within days, absorb the cost of unsold inventory. Traditional retailers canceled orders and bankrupted suppliers. Shein's model was worse: suppliers bore all risk, Shein captured all margin. But in a region with overcapacity and thin margins, factories took the deal.

By 2015, Shein was a stealth giant. Revenue hit $5 billion without a single article in the Western business press. The company spent nothing on brand marketing and everything on performance marketing: Instagram ads, Google Shopping, affiliate influencers. The algorithm optimized for customer acquisition cost, not brand perception. Xu hired engineers, not designers. The company tracked every click, every scroll, every abandoned cart. The data fed back into sourcing decisions hourly.

Then TikTok exploded, and Shein's model became unstoppable. The platform was built for "haul videos" — teenagers showing off piles of cheap clothes. Shein was the perfect product: photogenic, affordable, abundant. Influencers posted haul videos with Shein affiliate links. The videos went viral. The algorithm learned what was trending before traditional trend forecasters even noticed. Shein could design, produce, and ship a knockoff of a viral TikTok outfit faster than the original creator could capitalize on their moment.

The growth was exponential. By 2020, Shein was reportedly worth $15 billion. By 2022, $100 billion — larger than Zara and H&M combined. The company shipped to 150 countries. Its app was the most downloaded shopping app in the United States. It was fast fashion's endgame: clothing as disposable as content, trend cycles measured in days instead of seasons, inventory as infinite as the internet.

The model attracted scrutiny. Investigations found labor violations, environmental disasters, design theft on an industrial scale. Shein was accused of copying designs from independent creators, scraping images from Instagram, using forced labor in Xinjiang. Every few months, a new scandal: lead in children's clothing, workers earning pennies per garment, copyright infringement so blatant it became parody. Shein's response was always the same: vague promises to investigate, tweaks to supplier standards, silence.

None of it slowed the growth. Shein's customers were young, price-sensitive, and increasingly immune to outrage. The company's carbon footprint was catastrophic, but the app was fun. Labor practices were abysmal, but the dress was $9. Traditional brands tried to compete on speed and lost. Zara, once the benchmark for fast fashion, looked slow by comparison. Shein wasn't just faster. It had eliminated the concept of a fashion cycle entirely.

By 2023, the company faced a new problem: geopolitics. Shein's supply chain was entirely in China, its founder was Chinese, and its dominance triggered regulatory scrutiny. The U.S. was tightening rules on imports from Xinjiang. Europe was proposing regulations on ultra-fast fashion. Xu began preparing for an IPO, but going public meant transparency — audits, disclosures, accountability. Shein started relocating some production to Turkey, Brazil, Mexico. It hired lobbyists, PR firms, sustainability consultants. It tried to tell a brand story after fifteen years of having none.

The IPO stalled. Regulators asked questions the company didn't want to answer. Valuation dropped. But revenue kept climbing. Shein had cracked something fundamental: a production system faster than desire itself, a business model that required no brand loyalty because the customer never wore the same thing twice. It had turned clothing into data, fashion into an algorithm, and the global garment industry into a race to the bottom that it was winning.

Whether Shein could survive regulatory pressure, geopolitical tension, or its own reputation was unclear. What was clear: it had built the factory of the future. And the future, it turned out, was cheap, fast, and utterly disposable.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Uber
entries.descriptor: The aggressive scaling of a regulatory arbitrage play that rewrote urban transportation
entries.content:
# Uber

***The aggressive scaling of a regulatory arbitrage play that rewrote urban transportation***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In the winter of 2008, Travis Kalanick and Garrett Camp couldn't get a cab in Paris. Camp, fresh from selling StumbleUpon, sketched an idea: push a button, get a ride. The insight wasn't technological — GPS-enabled smartphones existed, and black car services operated in every major city. The insight was regulatory: existing taxi systems were protected monopolies created by artificial scarcity. Licensing systems in cities like New York and San Francisco limited the number of cabs through medallions that cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. This created profits for medallion owners and misery for everyone else — riders who couldn't find cabs, drivers locked out of the market, and a service quality floor set by regulatory capture rather than competition.

UberCab launched in San Francisco in May 2010 as a black car service. You opened an app, requested a ride, and a town car appeared. Payment happened automatically. No cash, no tipping ambiguity, no wondering if the driver would show. The first fleet was small — around a dozen cars. But the experience felt like magic in a way that concealed the operational complexity underneath: driver recruitment, real-time matching algorithms, payment processing, background checks, insurance.

Within months, the cease-and-desist letters arrived. San Francisco's regulators said UberCab was operating an unlicensed taxi service. Kalanick, who had fought legal battles with his previous company (the file-sharing service RedSweep, later Scour), saw this as validation. If regulators opposed it, there was a moat. The company dropped "Cab" from the name, reframed itself as a technology platform connecting riders and drivers rather than a transportation provider, and kept operating. The bet: grow faster than regulators can organize, make the service indispensable to users, and force the political conversation.

This became the template. Enter a city without permission. Recruit drivers. Acquire users through heavy subsidies — both riders and drivers got paid to use the service in the early days, with Uber covering the difference. Achieve liquidity in the marketplace where riders find cars quickly and drivers stay busy. Then dare regulators to shut down a service that thousands of voters now loved. The model worked because the product actually was better. Taxis were genuinely bad in most cities — dirty cars, rude drivers, broken credit card machines, mysterious routes, cash-only pressure. Uber was clean, trackable, and seamless.

The company raised $11 million in 2011, $37 million in 2012. Benchmark led the Series A, seeing the network effects: the more drivers on the platform, the faster pickups; the faster pickups, the more riders; the more riders, the more income for drivers. It was a two-sided marketplace with powerful dynamics once it reached density in a city. By 2013, Uber operated in 35 cities across the world.

Then came UberX. Instead of only black cars, Uber opened the platform to regular people driving their own vehicles. This dropped prices dramatically and exploded the supply side. Anyone with a car and a clean background check could become an Uber driver. The regulatory theory — that Uber was just a technology platform — suddenly became more plausible, or at least harder to attack. These weren't professional drivers or commercial vehicles. They were people with cars giving rides. The taxi industry screamed that this was exactly what taxi regulations were meant to prevent: unlicensed commercial drivers without proper insurance or oversight. Uber responded that this was the future of work: flexible, independent, enabled by technology.

The model was brutally capital-intensive. Each city required massive subsidies to reach liquidity. Drivers needed incentives to join before enough riders existed. Riders needed cheap fares to switch from taxis. Competitors like Lyft in the US and Didi in China forced bidding wars for drivers and riders. Uber raised $258 million in 2013, $1.2 billion in 2014, $1.6 billion in 2015. Kalanick's pitch was simple: winner-takes-most dynamics meant the company that achieved dominance in each city would own that market. Second place was nowhere.

The growth was matched by controversies. Uber's culture, shaped by Kalanick's aggressive style, became notorious. A "God View" tool let employees track riders in real time, including journalists and critics. Surge pricing during emergencies — a rational economic response to supply-demand imbalances — created PR disasters. Female employees reported systematic harassment and discrimination that HR ignored. Uber used a tool called "Greyball" to identify and evade regulators trying to catch drivers. The company's self-driving car program killed a pedestrian in Arizona. Kalanick was caught on camera berating a driver about fares.

By 2017, Uber was in crisis. A former engineer, Susan Fowler, published a blog post detailing the sexism and retaliation she had experienced. Investors who had tolerated Kalanick's aggression when growth was exponential began demanding change. In June 2017, he resigned as CEO.

Dara Khosrowshahi, former CEO of Expedia, took over. He was the opposite of Kalanick: diplomatic, process-oriented, focused on repairing relationships with regulators, drivers, and employees. He pulled Uber out of China and Russia, where the company was burning billions fighting Didi and Yandex, and took equity stakes instead. He pushed toward an IPO.

Uber went public in May 2019 at a $82 billion valuation, raising $8.1 billion. The stock dropped immediately. The company had never been profitable. The path to profitability was unclear. Autonomous vehicles, once pitched as the solution that would eliminate driver costs, had proven far harder than anticipated. The regulatory status of drivers remained contentious — were they contractors or employees? Different jurisdictions reached different conclusions. California's Proposition 22 in 2020, heavily funded by Uber and other gig economy companies, preserved contractor status, but the legal battles continued.

The COVID-19 pandemic cratered ride demand, but Uber's food delivery business, Uber Eats, surged. The company finally posted its first profitable quarter in 2023, fourteen years after founding. By then, it had spent over $31 billion in cumulative losses to build its position.

Uber transformed urban transportation. Taxis, once protected monopolies, became competitors or disappeared. Medallion values collapsed — a New York City taxi medallion worth $1.3 million in 2013 traded for $200,000 by 2020. Cities worldwide rewrote regulations. The company proved you could break regulated markets if you moved fast, spent enough, and made the product good enough that the public became your ally against incumbents.

The question that remains is whether Uber's path was building a business or buying a market. The company dominates ride-sharing, but at the cost of staggering capital expenditure and years of losses. The model worked because venture capital was abundant and patient. Whether it represents a sustainable business or a subsidy-dependent service that only functions while burning investor money is still being determined — every day the app continues to operate.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: WhatsApp
entries.descriptor: The acquisition that seemed absurd until it didn't
entries.content:
# WhatsApp

***The acquisition that seemed absurd until it didn't***

> **Years:** 2009-2014 (acquired)

Jan Koum arrived in Mountain View in 1992 as a sixteen-year-old immigrant from Ukraine. His family lived on food stamps. He taught himself programming. By 2007, he'd spent nearly a decade at Yahoo, watching the company's infrastructure creak under the weight of its own ambition. When Yahoo passed him over for a role he wanted, he quit. He applied to Facebook. They rejected him. He applied to Twitter. They rejected him too.

In early 2009, Koum bought an iPhone. He noticed something: the seven-month-old App Store was about to spawn an entire industry. More importantly, he noticed that Apple's new push notification system — announced in June — would allow apps to ping users even when closed. This wasn't just a feature. It was infrastructure for a new kind of communication.

Koum registered WhatsApp Inc. on his birthday, February 24, 2009. The name came from "What's Up" — a play on status updates. The first version crashed constantly. It was a simple app: you could update your status, and your contacts could see it. No one cared. Downloads were negligible. Koum nearly shut it down.

Then something happened. Early users started updating their statuses constantly — not to broadcast to everyone, but to ping specific people. "At the gym." "Call me." "Busy." They were using status updates as asynchronous communication. They were reinventing the away message as a chat app.

Koum saw it. In June 2009, he added messaging. WhatsApp 2.0 launched with push notifications as its core mechanic. When a message arrived, your phone buzzed. Instantly. Reliably. For free.

This was the insight that mattered: in 2009, SMS was still expensive. Carriers charged per message or capped monthly limits. International texts were extortionate. But WhatsApp ran over data. If you had Wi-Fi or a data plan, messages cost nothing. For immigrants, for people with family abroad, for anyone texting across borders, this wasn't a convenience. It was a revolution.

The app spread through networks — family to family, country to country. Koum didn't market it. He didn't need to. The value proposition was obvious to anyone who'd ever paid for SMS. WhatsApp grew through pure utility.

Brian Acton, Koum's friend and former Yahoo colleague, joined as co-founder in October 2009. Acton had also applied to Facebook. Also rejected. He brought discipline and infrastructure expertise. Together, they made architectural decisions that would define the company: no ads, no games, no gimmicks. Just messaging. Fast. Reliable. Private.

They charged $0.99 per year after the first year free. This was radical. Facebook was free. Twitter was free. Every social app was free, monetized through ads or data harvesting. Koum and Acton insisted on the opposite model. They'd both worked at Yahoo during the ad-tech boom. They'd seen how advertising corrupted product decisions. They wanted to build something different: software people paid for because it worked.

The model worked because WhatsApp worked. By February 2013, it had 200 million users. By October 2013, 350 million. The growth curve was nearly vertical. It was dominant in Europe, Latin America, and India — everywhere SMS pricing was punitive and smartphone adoption was accelerating.

What made WhatsApp extraordinary wasn't just growth. It was efficiency. In 2012, the company had 32 billion messages sent by 100 million users — and fifty employees. Fifty. Instagram, acquired by Facebook for $1 billion with 30 million users, had thirteen employees. WhatsApp was more efficient still. Their infrastructure was lean, their code disciplined, their overhead minimal. Koum's Yahoo experience showed: he built for scale from day one.

Facebook noticed. Mark Zuckerberg had tried to buy WhatsApp in 2012 for $500 million. Koum declined. By late 2013, Facebook tried again. This time, the number was different. In February 2014, Facebook acquired WhatsApp for $19 billion — $16 billion in stock and cash, $3 billion in restricted stock for employees.

The price seemed insane. The press called it a bubble. Analysts questioned Zuckerberg's judgment. WhatsApp had 450 million users but only $20 million in revenue. The math didn't math.

Except it did. Facebook wasn't buying revenue. It was buying the future. WhatsApp was growing faster than any communication platform in history. It was the dominant messaging app in over 100 countries. More importantly, it was winning the war Facebook was losing: mobile-first international growth, especially in markets where Facebook was just another social network but WhatsApp was essential infrastructure.

The deal included unusual terms. Koum joined Facebook's board. WhatsApp would remain autonomous. The encryption, the no-ads policy, the privacy commitments — all would stay.

They didn't. Not entirely. By 2018, both Koum and Acton had left Facebook over disagreements about encryption, data sharing, and monetization. The company they built to avoid Yahoo's mistakes got absorbed by a company making different mistakes.

But the product survived. By 2020, WhatsApp had two billion users. It is, by any measure, one of the most successful software products ever built. Families coordinate through it. Businesses run on it. Governments fear it. The app Koum almost abandoned in 2009 became the primary communication channel for a quarter of humanity.

What made WhatsApp work wasn't a clever growth hack or a viral loop. It was relentless focus on a problem that mattered: expensive, unreliable messaging. They solved it completely. They charged for it honestly. They scaled it ruthlessly. And when the tech world's dominant platform came calling with a price that seemed absurd, it turned out the only absurdity was thinking communication at that scale could ever be worth less.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Venmo
entries.descriptor: The social payment app that became the verb
entries.content:
# Venmo

***The social payment app that became the verb***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In 2009, Andrew Kortina and Iqram Magdon-Ismail were roommates at the University of Pennsylvania, working on a music startup that wasn't going anywhere. During a weekend visit to New York, Kortina forgot his wallet. Magdon-Ismail covered dinner. Later, the awkwardness of settling up — writing a check, finding an ATM, doing math on scraps of paper — felt absurd. They were building products for smartphones. Why did paying a friend still work like 1987?

They pivoted. The music startup died. Venmo was born as a way to text money to friends. The first version was literally SMS-based — you texted a dollar amount and recipient to a phone number, and Venmo handled the rest. It was clunky. Banks didn't make it easy. But the core insight was sound: payments between friends should feel like conversation, not commerce.

The early product had almost no users. Kortina and Magdon-Ismail seeded it themselves, splitting dinner tabs through the app and posting the transactions publicly. This wasn't just dogfooding — it was the product. Venmo's defining feature, the social feed showing who paid whom with emoji-laden captions, started as a way to make the product feel alive when almost nobody was using it. "Josh paid Sarah 🍕❤️" was content. It was proof of life.

The college demographic found it first. Splitting rent, bar tabs, Ubers before Uber had in-app splitting. Venmo spread through group houses and friend networks. The social feed became performative — inside jokes, emoji strings, deliberate vagueness. Paying someone back wasn't just a transaction; it was a minor publication. The feed was weird, but it worked. People wanted to be on the platform their friends were on. Network effects locked in fast.

Venmo's growth had one problem: it was burning money. Literally. Every transaction cost them more than they made. They charged nothing to send money between bank accounts and ate the interchange fees when users funded payments with credit cards. The business model was "get big, figure it out later." By 2012, they needed an exit or they'd die.

Braintree, a payment processor, bought Venmo for $26.2 million. It wasn't a victory lap. Braintree wanted the user base and the mobile-first DNA. For Kortina and Magdon-Ismail, it was survival. A year later, PayPal acquired Braintree for $800 million, largely because Venmo came with it. PayPal had missed mobile. Venmo was the way back in.

Under PayPal, Venmo exploded. The parent company had infrastructure, compliance expertise, and patience for losses while the platform scaled. By 2017, Venmo was processing $17.6 billion in payment volume annually. It became a verb. "Venmo me" entered the lexicon. PayPal finally monetized it — merchant acceptance, instant transfer fees, a debit card. The social feed remained, a fossil of the early insight that payments could be content.

But Venmo never fully escaped its original limitation: it only worked well between friends. Attempts to expand into commerce felt forced. Merchants didn't need another payment rail. Venmo's brand was casual, social, emoji-laden — not the gravitas of "real" transactions. Competitors like Zelle, launched by a consortium of banks, offered instant transfers without leaving your bank app. Zelle had no social feed, no brand cachet, and processed more volume than Venmo by 2018. Turns out most people just wanted to move money, not perform it.

Venmo's path reveals a recurring startup trap: building something people love that doesn't obviously make money. The social feed was brilliant product design and terrible business strategy. It created stickiness but not revenue. The peer-to-peer model had brutal unit economics. Venmo won by being delightful and then getting acquired by someone who could absorb the losses long enough to extract value.

Today, Venmo is profitable, massive, and culturally entrenched. PayPal reports it separately in earnings. It generated over $1 billion in revenue in 2023. But it's a feature of the PayPal empire, not an independent company. The founders left years ago. The outcome wasn't failure — it was subsumption. Venmo succeeded by becoming indispensable, then becoming owned.

The company that made splitting a pizza feel like a social act became a utility. The feed still exists, but nobody checks it anymore. The insight was right: payments should feel effortless. The business was right: scale first, monetize later. The path was right: get acquired before you run out of money. What Venmo couldn't do was become PayPal itself. It became the thing PayPal bought to stay relevant. That's a kind of victory, even if it's not the one the founders imagined in 2009, writing checks for dinner and thinking there had to be a better way.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Kickstarter
entries.descriptor: The platform that changed how things get made — then struggled with what it had become
entries.content:
# Kickstarter

***The platform that changed how things get made — then struggled with what it had become***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In 2002, Perry Chen stood outside a New Orleans jazz club, frustrated. He wanted to bring a musician to the city for a show, but he couldn't afford it unless he knew others would buy tickets. The idea lodged: what if you could fund something before it existed, pooling commitments from strangers who wanted it to be real?

Seven years later, Chen teamed with Yancey Strickler and Charles Adler in a small New York apartment. They'd all worked at the edges of the creative economy — music, arts, technology — and shared a conviction that the traditional funding model was broken. Artists had to beg institutions. Inventors had to convince manufacturers. The internet made global audiences possible, but no infrastructure existed to turn scattered enthusiasm into actual money.

Kickstarter launched in April 2009 with 28 projects. The mechanism was elegant: all-or-nothing funding. A creator sets a goal and deadline. Backers pledge money. If the goal is met, the creator gets funded and must deliver. If not, nobody pays. This wasn't pre-order. It wasn't investment. It was something new: patronage at scale, with accountability built in.

The timing was accidental brilliance. The 2008 financial crisis had frozen traditional capital. But it had also demolished faith in institutions. People wanted to fund things directly — to see their money create something tangible. Kickstarter offered that. The early projects were small: an indie album, a short film, a design object. But the platform revealed something nobody quite expected: the intensity of demand for things the market said didn't exist.

In 2010, a playing card company called Theory11 raised $250,000 — twenty times their goal. Not because playing cards were revolutionary, but because they'd found their people. This became the pattern. Kickstarter didn't just fund projects. It proved markets existed. It showed creators they weren't alone.

The breakthrough came in 2012 with Pebble. A smartwatch, before Apple made smartwatches. Pebble asked for $100,000. They raised $10.3 million from 69,000 backers, shattering every record. Suddenly Kickstarter wasn't just for artists. It was a product-launch platform. Hardware companies used it to validate demand before manufacturing. Game studios bypassed publishers. The platform became infrastructure for an entire mode of production: make the thing your audience actually wants, not what a gatekeeper thinks will sell.

By 2013, Kickstarter had funded $1 billion in projects. Oculus raised $2.4 million there, then sold to Facebook for $2 billion — causing fury among backers who'd helped birth it, only to see it absorbed by exactly the kind of corporation Kickstarter was supposed to circumvent. This tension — between empowering creators and feeding the traditional system — would never resolve.

The platform's problems multiplied with its success. Fraud became endemic. Projects raised millions, then delivered nothing or something terrible. Backers had no recourse. They weren't customers with consumer protection. They weren't investors with equity. They were patrons left holding the bag. The all-or-nothing model prevented obvious scams but couldn't stop incompetence or dishonesty after funding.

Worse, success warped incentives. Kickstarter became a marketing engine. Companies with capital used it to generate buzz and validate concepts, not because they needed the money. The platform designed for underdogs became a launch pad for the well-connected. Algorithms favored projects already succeeding, creating winner-take-all dynamics. The democracy of funding became an oligarchy of visibility.

Internally, Kickstarter struggled with its identity. In 2015, they became a Public Benefit Corporation — legally obligating themselves to balance profit with public good. In 2017, staff tried to unionize; management resisted, then accepted, becoming the first major tech company with a union. In 2020, they laid off workers amid the union's first contract negotiation. The company preaching creator empowerment was fighting its own staff over power.

The blockchain era brought fresh crisis. In 2021, Kickstarter announced it would rebuild on blockchain technology. The backlash was immediate and fierce. Creators and backers saw it as capitulation to crypto's extractive logic. After months of protest, the company quietly downplayed the initiative. It proceeded, but without conviction.

Meanwhile, the market Kickstarter created had been colonized. Indiegogo was already a competitor. Then came equity crowdfunding platforms like Seedrs and Crowdcube. Patreon took the creator-support model and made it recurring. GoFundMe turned crowdfunding into charity infrastructure. YouTube, Twitch, and OnlyFans integrated direct fan support into their platforms. Kickstarter's innovation — connecting creators with supporters — became table stakes.

The company never became a giant. It never IPO'd. It remained profitable but constrained by its principles: the 5% fee, the no-equity model, the commitment to not exploit users for maximum growth. These principles prevented Kickstarter from becoming extractive. They also prevented it from becoming dominant.

Today, Kickstarter occupies a strange position. It still funds thousands of projects annually. Graphic novels, tabletop games, and design objects thrive there. But the platform's cultural centrality has faded. It succeeded so thoroughly at proving the model that others could copy it, improve parts, and capture specific niches. Kickstarter showed that direct creator-supporter relationships could work at scale. Then scale fractured into a thousand specialized platforms.

The company built the infrastructure for a more direct economy. Then discovered that infrastructure, once built, belongs to everyone.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Pinterest
entries.descriptor: The visual search engine that arrived when social media was already crowded — and thrived by serving a different need
entries.content:
# Pinterest

***The visual search engine that arrived when social media was already crowded — and thrived by serving a different need***

> **Years:** 2010-present

Ben Silbermann grew up collecting insects. As an adult working at Google in customer support, he found himself still drawn to collecting — now design ideas, travel photos, things he wanted to remember. The web was full of bookmarks and links, but he wanted something more tactile. Something that felt like pinning images to a corkboard.

In 2009, he left Google with co-founders Paul Sciarra and Evan Sharp to build what would become Pinterest. The initial idea was broader — an app called Tote for catalog shopping on mobile. It didn't work. Nobody used the shopping features. But they noticed users kept sharing images of products they liked, creating collections. That behavior — the collecting itself — became the product.

They launched Pinterest in March 2010 as an invite-only site. Growth was agonizingly slow. By summer, the site had fewer than 3,000 users. Silbermann personally wrote to the first 5,000 users, asking for feedback and offering his phone number. He visited Pinterest meetups in stranger's homes. His mother recruited her friends in Iowa.

The timing looked terrible. Facebook had 500 million users. Twitter was exploding. Instagram launched the same year Pinterest did. Social media seemed solved, saturated. Investors saw a photo-sharing site arriving too late to a crowded party.

But Pinterest wasn't competing in the same space. Facebook was about people you knew. Instagram was about moments. Pinterest was about aspirations — recipes you wanted to cook, rooms you wanted to design, places you wanted to visit. It was future-oriented where others were present-oriented. A tool for planning and dreaming rather than sharing and broadcasting.

The user base was also different. While tech startups chased young males in San Francisco, Pinterest's early adopters were women in the Midwest planning weddings, organizing craft projects, collecting recipes. Demographics that Silicon Valley routinely underestimated. The tech press barely covered it. Valley investors didn't understand it. Meanwhile, usage was doubling every month and a half.

By 2011, the growth became impossible to ignore. The site hit 10 million monthly users faster than any standalone site in history. Andreessen Horowitz led a $27 million Series A. The next year, Japan's Rakuten invested $100 million at a $1.5 billion valuation. Pinterest was a rocket ship that had launched without anyone watching.

The challenge shifted from getting users to managing scale. The infrastructure buckled constantly. The team grew from 10 to 200 in two years. Silbermann, an introvert who had been writing personal emails to users, was suddenly running a major company. He had never managed anyone before Google, and at Google he'd been in support, not engineering or product.

The business model question loomed. Pinterest drove enormous traffic to retail sites — users clicked through to buy things they'd pinned. But Pinterest captured none of that value. In 2013, they experimented with "Promoted Pins" — paid placement that looked native. Retailers loved it. Pinterest had accidentally built one of the web's most powerful buying intent signals. People pinning wedding dresses were far more likely to buy wedding dresses than people searching for them.

Then came the accusations. Artists and photographers complained about copyright infringement — their images pinned without attribution or permission. Etsy sellers found their products on Pinterest with links stripped away. Pinterest responded by making it easier for sites to opt out, adding better attribution, and positioning themselves as driving traffic rather than stealing content. The controversy faded but revealed tensions in the model.

Going public in 2019 exposed another challenge. Pinterest had 300 million monthly users and $1.1 billion in revenue. Impressive, but slow growth. Facebook had billions. Snap was capturing young users. TikTok was exploding. Pinterest's IPO was lukewarm, pricing below range. The stock dropped further. Wall Street wanted hockey stick growth. Pinterest offered steady utility.

The pandemic changed everything. People stuck at home started cooking, reorganizing, planning renovations. Pinterest usage surged. Revenue jumped 58% in 2020. The stock quintupled. Then the pandemic ended and growth stalled. Users who'd come for sourdough recipes left. The stock crashed back down.

Leadership turbulence followed. In 2022, Silbermann stepped down as CEO, staying on as executive chairman. Bill Ready, formerly of Google Commerce, took over. The company faced pressure to monetize internationally — strong usage in Europe and Latin America but little revenue. They experimented with e-commerce features, trying to capture transactions instead of just driving them elsewhere. They added video to compete with TikTok and YouTube. They emphasized creator tools to compete for influencers.

Through it all, Pinterest remained useful in a way few social platforms could claim. It wasn't addictive like TikTok or anxiety-inducing like Twitter. It didn't require performance like Instagram or oversharing like Facebook. It was a tool people returned to when planning a wedding, remodeling a kitchen, looking for recipes. Boring utility — the hardest thing to build and the easiest to undervalue.

The company today sits in an odd position. It proved a massive overlooked market existed — visual search for inspiration and planning. It survived the crowded social media wars by not really being social media. It went public and stayed independent while peers were acquired or collapsed. But it never achieved the explosive growth or cultural dominance investors expected. It became infrastructure — important, useful, underestimated. The very qualities that made it succeed made it easy to take for granted.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Square/Block
entries.descriptor: The payments company that became a Bitcoin believer
entries.content:
# Square/Block

***The payments company that became a Bitcoin believer***

> **Years:** 2009-present

Jack Dorsey had already co-founded Twitter when he started thinking about his next company. The idea came from a personal frustration shared by his friend Jim McKelvey, a glassblower in St. Louis. McKelvey lost a sale because he couldn't accept credit cards. In 2009, this was a universal problem for small merchants — accepting card payments required expensive equipment, long approval processes, and a relationship with a payment processor that treated small businesses as barely worth the trouble.

The insight was simple: smartphones had everything needed to process payments except one thing. Dorsey and McKelvey built that thing — a small square dongle that plugged into the headphone jack and turned any iPhone into a card reader. The software was elegant. The pricing was transparent. Square launched in 2010, and within months, people were using it everywhere — at farmers markets, in taxis, at food trucks.

The timing was perfect. The iPhone had created millions of potential point-of-sale terminals. The financial crisis had created millions of people looking for side income, selling things, driving for hire, doing contract work. Square gave them a way to get paid professionally. The company grew explosively.

But the dongle was never the business. The dongle was the hook. The business was the payment processing — taking a small percentage of every transaction. Square's challenge was that payment processing has thin margins and powerful incumbents. The established processors had relationships with banks, economies of scale, and no reason to welcome a new competitor. Square needed to build everything from scratch: fraud detection, compliance systems, relationships with card networks, reserve funds to cover chargebacks.

The early years were financially brutal. Square processed billions in payments but lost money on every transaction. Fraud was rampant — criminals quickly learned they could use stolen cards with Square dongles, and the company had to eat the losses. By 2011, Square was processing $2 billion annually but hemorrhaging cash. Venture capitalists poured in hundreds of millions, betting that scale would eventually bring profitability.

Dorsey's dual role became controversial. He was CEO of Square while also serving as executive chairman of Twitter. Critics questioned whether anyone could run two companies. Inside Square, there were tensions about strategy. The company kept expanding — not just dongles but larger card readers, a full point-of-sale system for restaurants and retail stores, business loans, payroll services. Each new product was elegant and well-designed, but each also complicated the path to profitability.

The breakthrough came from software. Square developed Cash App in 2013, initially as a simple way to send money to friends. It was Venmo-like but simpler, with one crucial difference: Square owned the payment infrastructure underneath. Cash App didn't need to be immediately profitable because it fed into Square's larger ecosystem. People who used Cash App were more likely to become Square merchants. Merchants who used Square were more likely to recommend Cash App to customers.

Then Cash App found an unexpected killer feature: Bitcoin. In 2018, Square added the ability to buy and sell Bitcoin through Cash App. The timing was fortuitous — cryptocurrency was entering a new bull market. Millions of users who had never owned Bitcoin suddenly could buy it with a few taps. Square took a small spread on each transaction, but the volume was enormous. By 2020, Bitcoin trading was generating billions in revenue.

Dorsey had become personally obsessed with Bitcoin. He talked about it constantly, framing it not as an investment but as the native currency of the internet. In 2021, he took the symbolic step of renaming Square to Block, signaling a transformation from payments company to something broader — a builder of tools for economic empowerment, with Bitcoin at the center.

The name change coincided with Dorsey leaving Twitter (for the second time) to focus entirely on Block. The company's strategy became clearer: own the full stack of financial services for individuals and small businesses. Cash App for consumers. Square for merchants. Tidal for music streaming (acquired in 2021). TBD for Bitcoin and decentralized finance infrastructure. Spiral for Bitcoin open-source development.

Critics saw empire-building and distraction. Believers saw an ecosystem. The financial results were mixed. Cash App became hugely profitable, driven by Bitcoin trading and a growing user base that treated it as their primary bank account. The original Square business stabilized, profitable but no longer high-growth. The newer ventures — music streaming, Bitcoin development — were expensive bets.

What started as a simple dongle for accepting credit cards had become a sprawling financial services company with a cryptocurrency philosophy. Block in 2024 processes over $200 billion annually, has over 50 million Cash App users, and holds billions in Bitcoin on its balance sheet. Its stock price fluctuates with Bitcoin sentiment as much as with business fundamentals.

The company Dorsey built solved the problem he set out to solve — any merchant can now accept payments easily. But the path from there led somewhere unexpected. The payment processor became a true believer, convinced that the entire financial system it had learned to navigate would eventually be replaced by something decentralized. Whether that belief is visionary or delusional remains unresolved. Block is placing a very large bet that the future of money looks nothing like its past.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Beyond Meat
entries.descriptor: The mission-driven IPO that discovered Wall Street doesn't care about mission
entries.content:
# Beyond Meat

***The mission-driven IPO that discovered Wall Street doesn't care about mission***

> **Years:** 2009-present

Ethan Brown was not a vegan when he decided to build a company that would replace meat. He was a businessman who had spent years in clean energy and fuel cells, convinced that the biggest climate problem wasn't energy—it was livestock. Animals raised for food generated more greenhouse gases than all transportation combined. But telling people to eat less meat had failed for decades. Brown's insight was simpler: make plant-based meat that carnivores would actually choose.

He founded Beyond Meat in 2009 with backing from Kleiner Perkins and the Obvious Corporation, the investment vehicle of Twitter's founders. Early investors included Bill Gates, who had become obsessed with agricultural emissions. The pitch was compelling—not "eat vegetables" but "eat meat made from vegetables." The technology came from Fu-Hung Hsieh, a University of Missouri food scientist who had figured out how to align plant proteins to mimic muscle fiber. Brown licensed the patents and started building.

The first products were terrible. Beyond Chicken strips launched in 2012 at Whole Foods, marketed as a meat substitute for health-conscious shoppers. They tasted like health food. Sales were modest. The company burned cash developing better formulations while competitors like Impossible Foods raised more money and generated more buzz. For years, Beyond Meat was the also-ran in the fake meat race, the company investors tolerated because of Brown's relentlessness and the mission's appeal.

Everything changed with the Beyond Burger in 2016. Instead of mimicking processed chicken, Brown's team reverse-engineered ground beef—the texture, the browning, the way it cooked. They used beet juice to make it "bleed." And crucially, they sold it in the meat aisle, not the vegetarian section. The product was designed for meat-eaters, positioned as meat, priced like premium meat. Whole Foods put it in 280 stores. It sold out immediately.

The Beyond Burger created a category. Sales went from $16 million in 2016 to $88 million in 2017 to $88 million in just the first quarter of 2019. Restaurant chains started calling. TGI Friday's, Carl's Jr., A&W in Canada—mainstream brands wanted plant-based options, and Beyond Meat had the product that didn't taste like compromise. The company signed a deal with Dunkin' for breakfast sausage. Another with KFC for plant-based chicken. Distribution expanded to Costco, Target, Kroger.

Brown took the company public in May 2019, one of the year's most anticipated IPOs. Beyond Meat priced at $25, a valuation that made venture investors nervous—too high, too early, too much hype. The stock opened at $46 and hit $239 by July, making it the best-performing IPO of the year. The company was worth $14 billion despite annual revenue of just $88 million. Wall Street had decided plant-based meat was the future, and Beyond Meat was going to own it.

Then reality arrived. The stock's peak came before the company could deliver the growth investors had priced in. Competition intensified—Impossible Foods expanded retail distribution, legacy food companies launched their own plant-based lines, and restaurant chains played suppliers against each other for better terms. The pandemic initially boosted Beyond Meat as consumers stocked home kitchens, but restaurant sales collapsed. Revenue grew but not fast enough to justify the valuation.

By 2022, the cracks were obvious. Revenue growth slowed to single digits. The company had expanded internationally and into new products before proving profitability in core markets. Operating losses widened. Retailers cut shelf space as demand softened and competition crowded freezers. The stock that hit $239 fell below $10. The market cap dropped from $14 billion to under $600 million.

The problem wasn't the product—the Beyond Burger worked. The problem was the assumption that creating a good meat alternative would automatically generate a massive, loyal customer base. Beyond Meat discovered that most consumers were curious triers, not committed converts. People would buy plant-based meat occasionally, when convenient or on sale, but rarely as a full replacement. The die-hard vegans and vegetarians were a small base. The flexitarians were fickle. The meat-eaters mostly kept eating meat.

Brown's mission hadn't changed, but the market had stopped caring about mission. Investors wanted profits, not impact metrics about carbon saved or animals spared. The company cut costs, laid off staff, and consolidated product lines. By 2024, Beyond Meat was profitable in some quarters but shrinking overall. It remained the category leader in a category that turned out to be smaller than the hype suggested.

Beyond Meat proved you could build great plant-based meat and still struggle to build a great business. The path from innovation to IPO had worked. The path from IPO to sustainable giant hadn't. It became a case study in what happens when a mission-driven company goes public at a mission-driven valuation and then has to answer to shareholders who just want returns.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Rivian
entries.descriptor: The $100 billion bet on electric adventure
entries.content:
# Rivian

***The $100 billion bet on electric adventure***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In 2009, when RJ Scaringe founded Rivian in his father's basement in Melbourne, Florida, Tesla had yet to ship the Model S and electric vehicles were still curiosities. Scaringe, fresh from an MIT PhD in mechanical engineering, didn't want to build another urban commuter car. He wanted to electrify the open road — pickup trucks and SUVs for people who actually used them.

The timing looked terrible. The auto industry was collapsing. GM and Chrysler were in bankruptcy. Electric startups like Fisker and Better Place would soon become cautionary tales. But Scaringe had studied the cemetery of failed car companies and believed he understood what killed them: they tried to compete with Detroit on Detroit's terms. He would go elsewhere — to adventure vehicles where incumbents were complacent and margins were fat.

For the first eight years, Rivian was invisible. Scaringe raised modest amounts from friends, family, and patient investors. The company worked in silence, building prototypes, developing skateboard platform architecture, recruiting engineers from Tesla and Ford. No flashy unveilings. No viral marketing. Just methodical engineering of battery packs, motors, and software. By 2017, Rivian had burned through nearly $200 million and shipped nothing.

Then came the pivot that changed everything. In November 2018, Rivian unveiled the R1T pickup and R1S SUV at the LA Auto Show. The vehicles were beautiful — rugged but refined, capable but luxurious. They promised 400 miles of range, 0-60 in three seconds, and the ability to wade through three feet of water. The market reaction was explosive. Here was an electric vehicle for people who didn't think of themselves as "EV people."

What happened next was unprecedented for an auto startup. In 2019, Amazon ordered 100,000 electric delivery vans. Ford invested $500 million. By the end of 2020, Rivian had raised $2.85 billion in a single round led by T. Rowe Price. The company was valued at $27.6 billion before delivering a single consumer vehicle.

The Amazon deal was both salvation and burden. It guaranteed revenue and validated Rivian's technology, but it meant building two products simultaneously — the consumer vehicles and a commercial van with different requirements. Rivian built a factory in Normal, Illinois, a former Mitsubishi plant, and hired thousands. Scaringe remained CEO, maintaining unusual founder control as the company scaled.

In November 2021, Rivian went public in the largest IPO of the year. On its second day of trading, the company reached a valuation of $153 billion — more than Ford or GM, despite having delivered roughly 150 vehicles. Wall Street was betting not just on Rivian but on the electrification of the entire automotive industry. Every established automaker was now scrambling to catch up.

Then reality hit. Supply chains snarled. Battery costs soared. Rivian was losing $1.5 billion per quarter while producing vehicles at a fraction of planned capacity. In March 2022, the company tried to raise prices on existing reservations by as much as $20,000. The backlash was immediate and fierce. Scaringe reversed the decision within 48 hours, taking full blame. The stock cratered, losing three-quarters of its value.

The company faced the classic startup dilemma at automotive scale. To reach profitability, Rivian needed volume. To reach volume, it needed capital. To raise capital, it needed to prove it could build efficiently. But learning to manufacture vehicles is the most capital-intensive lesson in business. GM and Ford had spent a century learning it. Rivian was trying to compress that into years.

Throughout 2022 and 2023, Rivian steadily increased production, shipping 24,000 vehicles in 2022 and 57,000 in 2023. The company hit its first quarterly gross profit in late 2023. Reviews remained strong — the R1T won Motor Trend's Truck of the Year. Owners loved the vehicles. But the math was brutal. Each vehicle cost more to build than Rivian charged for it. The path to profitability required redesigning vehicles for manufacturing efficiency, a process that would take years.

Meanwhile, the competitive landscape transformed. Ford launched the F-150 Lightning. GM introduced the Silverado EV. Tesla's Cybertruck finally shipped. Legacy automakers had decided electric pickups mattered, bringing their century of manufacturing expertise and economies of scale. What had been an empty field was now crowded.

Scaringe made a crucial decision: Rivian would focus on its strength — premium adventure vehicles with superior software and vertical integration. The company redesigned the R1T and R1S for lower cost production while developing a smaller, cheaper R2 platform for 2026. Amazon remained committed, ordering more vans. Rivian was building a second factory in Georgia.

By early 2025, Rivian was producing roughly 1,000 vehicles per week, still losing money but with a path to break-even visible. The company had $7 billion in cash but was burning through it. The market cap had stabilized around $12 billion — a fraction of the peak, but still substantial for a money-losing automaker.

The story remains unfinished. Rivian has survived longer and executed better than any electric vehicle startup except Tesla. But survival is not success, and the distance between a beloved product and a profitable company can be infinite. Scaringe built the trucks he envisioned. Whether he can build them profitably enough, at scale enough, fast enough to justify the capital and faith invested — that is the question the next five years will answer. The path from idea to outcome is still being driven.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Okta
entries.descriptor: The identity layer that became infrastructure
entries.content:
# Okta

***The identity layer that became infrastructure***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In 2009, Todd McKinnon was vice president of engineering at Salesforce, watching the company grow into a cloud giant. He had helped build the platform that was replacing on-premise software, but he kept noticing the same problem: every company adopting cloud services faced a nightmare of managing user identities and access across dozens of disconnected systems. IT departments were drowning in password resets, security teams couldn't enforce policies consistently, and employees wasted hours logging into different applications. The old solution—Microsoft Active Directory—worked beautifully for on-premise software but was never designed for a world where everything lived in the cloud.

McKinnon saw the pattern clearly. As more companies moved to cloud services, identity would become critical infrastructure. Someone needed to build the identity layer for the cloud era. He pitched the idea to Frederic Kerrest, a former colleague, and in January 2009 they left Salesforce to start Okta. The timing seemed terrible. The financial crisis had just cratered the economy, venture capital had dried up, and enterprise software was supposed to be dead. Everyone said the cloud was for consumers, not businesses.

They raised a seed round anyway—$750,000 from Floodgate—and built the first version in Kerrest's apartment. The concept was straightforward: a cloud-based service that would manage employee identities and control access to all their applications through single sign-on. One login, one password, access to everything. Behind the scenes, Okta would handle authentication, enforce security policies, and integrate with both cloud apps and on-premise systems. They called it "identity as a service."

The first challenge was the chicken-and-egg problem. Enterprises wouldn't adopt Okta without integrations to the apps they used. App vendors wouldn't prioritize Okta integrations without a large customer base. McKinnon and Kerrest spent the first year building integrations manually, one app at a time. Salesforce, Google Apps, Box, Workday. They created a network of pre-built connections that made Okta immediately useful. By the time they launched publicly in 2010, they had a hundred integrations ready.

Early customers were mostly tech companies already comfortable with cloud services—Salesforce customers who understood the problem Okta solved. The sales cycle was brutal. Selling security software to enterprises meant navigating IT bureaucracies, proving reliability, and overcoming deep skepticism about trusting a startup with something as critical as identity. But every successful deployment became a reference account. Every integration they built made the next sale easier.

The product market fit came faster than expected. By 2011, they had real revenue and momentum. Companies weren't just buying Okta for convenience—they were buying it because cloud adoption was accelerating and they had no other solution. The alternative was cobbling together VPNs, legacy directory services, and manual processes. Okta worked. It scaled. It didn't break.

They raised a Series A in 2011, then a Series B in 2012. McKinnon made a crucial strategic decision: focus exclusively on the enterprise market. No consumer products, no distractions. While competitors tried to be everything to everyone, Okta would be the best identity platform for businesses. They built features enterprises demanded—multi-factor authentication, lifecycle management, detailed audit logs, compliance certifications. They hired enterprise sales teams who understood how to sell to CIOs and security officers.

The competitive landscape was treacherous. Microsoft, with its dominant Active Directory, was the obvious threat. But Microsoft was slow to adapt Active Directory for the cloud, giving Okta years of runway. Smaller competitors like OneLogin and Ping Identity competed directly, but Okta outexecuted them on integrations, user experience, and enterprise features. The key advantage was the network effect—every new integration made Okta more valuable, and every new customer created incentive for more integrations.

By 2015, Okta had grown into a substantial business with thousands of customers and hundreds of millions in revenue. They had become infrastructure—companies depended on Okta to run. The valuation climbed with each funding round. They raised a Series E at a $1.2 billion valuation, joining the unicorn club. McKinnon resisted pressure to sell. He believed Okta could be an independent, enduring company.

The IPO came in April 2017. They priced at $17 per share, raising $187 million and valuing the company at $1.6 billion. The stock jumped 38% on the first day. Wall Street finally understood what McKinnon had seen in 2009: identity was fundamental infrastructure for the cloud era, and Okta had won the race to build it.

Post-IPO, Okta expanded aggressively. They acquired Stormpath in 2017 to add developer tools, then Auth0 in 2021 for $6.5 billion—a massive bet on customer identity management beyond the enterprise. The Auth0 acquisition was controversial. Some saw it as Okta dominating the entire identity market. Others worried the company was overpaying and straying from its core.

The business kept growing. By 2024, Okta served over 18,000 customers including half the Fortune 500. The product had expanded far beyond single sign-on into a comprehensive identity platform managing billions of authentication events daily. The market cap fluctuated with tech valuations but held above $10 billion even through downturns.

What McKinnon and Kerrest built wasn't just a product but a category. "Identity as a service" became standard nomenclature. Every cloud-first company now assumes identity will be managed by a specialized platform, not homegrown tools. Okta made that assumption possible. The company that started in an apartment during a financial crisis became the infrastructure layer enabling modern work—invisible, essential, ubiquitous.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Sea Limited/Shopee
entries.descriptor: From gaming dividends to the Amazon of Southeast Asia
entries.content:
# Sea Limited/Shopee

***From gaming dividends to the Amazon of Southeast Asia***

> **Years:** 2009-present

In 2009, Forrest Li founded Garena in Singapore with a simple insight: Southeast Asia's fragmented gaming market needed a unifying platform. He had worked at Motorola and studied at Stanford, but his advantage wasn't technical genius or industry connections. It was understanding that a region of 600 million people, spread across languages and countries, needed someone to handle localization, payment systems, and internet café distribution networks that Western gaming companies couldn't navigate.

Garena became the exclusive distributor for League of Legends and other games across Southeast Asia. By 2014, it was profitable and dominant — but Li saw the business model's ceiling. Distribution rights could be revoked. The real money was in platforms that owned customer relationships, not just licensed content.

Li looked at e-commerce. In 2015, Southeast Asia's online retail market was primitive compared to China or the US. Logistics infrastructure was terrible. Payment systems fragmented. Alibaba's Lazada had a head start but operated like a Chinese company transplanted — expensive, centralized, uncertain about local nuances. Most observers thought the region was too messy, too poor, too fragmented to support an Amazon-style platform.

Li thought the opposite. He had gaming revenues to fund experimentation. He understood each country's peculiarities. And he saw that the smartphone revolution was coming to Southeast Asia faster than infrastructure could catch up, creating a brief window where a mobile-first platform could leapfrog traditional retail.

Shopee launched in 2015 with a model copied directly from China's playbook — or more precisely, from Taobao's. Free listings. Seller-friendly tools. In-app chat between buyers and sellers. Heavy subsidies to bootstrap supply and demand simultaneously. And gamification everywhere: daily check-in bonuses, in-app games, social features that made shopping feel like entertainment.

The Western tech press barely noticed. Southeast Asia wasn't considered a serious market. But Shopee understood something Lazada didn't: in price-sensitive markets with weak logistics, you win by making shopping fun and social enough that people forgive late deliveries and sketchy quality. You win by being the platform sellers want to use because listing is free and buyers swarm the app daily.

By 2017, Shopee's order volume exceeded Lazada's in several markets. Alibaba, alarmed, doubled down on Lazada with billions in fresh capital. It didn't matter. Lazada was structured like an inventory-heavy retailer. Shopee was structured like a marketplace that treated logistics as a service layer, not a core competency. Lazada focused on electronics and branded goods. Shopee focused on everything — fashion, cosmetics, phone accessories, the long tail of small sellers who would never qualify for Lazada's merchant onboarding.

The company went public in 2017 as Sea Limited, revealing the full strategic architecture. Three businesses: Garena (gaming), Shopee (e-commerce), and SeaMoney (digital financial services). The thesis was vertical integration across the digital economy. Gaming profits funded e-commerce growth. E-commerce transaction data enabled financial services. Financial services reduced friction in e-commerce. Each business fed the others.

Wall Street was skeptical. Sea lost money at staggering rates. Shopee's unit economics looked terrible — heavy subsidies, thin margins, logistics costs that defied traditional retail math. But Li argued that profitability would come after dominance, and dominance required outlasting competitors with weaker capital access or weaker strategic patience.

The pandemic validated everything. E-commerce adoption in Southeast Asia accelerated by years. Shopee's mobile-first design and logistics network — patchy but functional — scaled faster than competitors. By 2021, Sea's market cap hit $200 billion, briefly making it more valuable than any other Southeast Asian company.

Then the music stopped. Interest rates rose. Tech valuations crashed. Investors who had tolerated growth-at-all-costs demanded profitability. Sea's stock fell 80% from its peak. Li cut jobs, withdrew from money-losing markets like India and Europe, and refocused on core Southeast Asian markets. The company that had expanded everywhere pulled back everywhere except home.

But the pullback revealed strength, not weakness. Shopee wasn't Uber or WeWork — a business model held together by subsidies. It was dominant in Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, and increasingly Taiwan. By 2023, Shopee turned profitable. Gaming remained a cash cow. SeaMoney showed traction as digital payments spread.

What saved Sea was the same thing that created it: understanding that emerging markets don't follow Western timelines or logic. You expand when capital is cheap, consolidate when it's expensive, and survive long enough for infrastructure to catch up to your model. Lazada had Alibaba's billions but lost the market. Shopee had inferior resources but better market fit.

Today Sea is neither the Amazon of the East nor the next Alibaba. It's something stranger: a gaming company that became an e-commerce giant that might ultimately be a fintech platform. Its path reveals a truth about building in emerging markets — you don't need to be first or best capitalized. You need to understand local conditions deeply enough to build something that works despite infrastructure gaps, and patient capital that lets you survive until the market matures around you.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Mojang/Minecraft
entries.descriptor: The accidental empire built by a developer who never wanted to be a CEO
entries.content:
# Mojang/Minecraft

***The accidental empire built by a developer who never wanted to be a CEO***

> **Years:** 2009-2014 (acquired by Microsoft)

In May 2009, Markus "Notch" Persson was a Swedish game developer working at King.com, grinding out browser games for casual players. On the side, he coded for himself — experiments, prototypes, weird ideas. He'd been obsessed with a game called Infiniminer, a mining game with destructible block-based worlds. When its developer abandoned it, Notch decided to make his own version. He called it "Cave Game," then "Minecraft."

The first version was primitive: break blocks, place blocks, nothing else. He posted it on the TIGSource forums — an indie game community — on May 17, 2009. People downloaded it. They liked it. Within days, players were sharing screenshots of crude structures they'd built. Within weeks, Notch realized he had something.

He quit King.com. He registered Minecraft.net. He started charging €10 for early access to what he called the "alpha" version — pay now, get all future updates free. This was before "early access" became an industry term. It was just how Notch thought it should work: you support development, you get the game forever.

The game grew through iteration and accident. Notch added survival mode — monsters that appeared at night, health, crafting. He didn't have a grand design document. He added what felt right. Players invented their own purposes: some built elaborate castles, others engineered complex machines with "redstone" circuits, still others just explored the infinite procedurally-generated worlds.

The community became the game's marketing engine. YouTube exploded with Minecraft videos — "Let's Plays" where creators documented their builds and adventures. The videos were hypnotic: low-res blocky graphics, ambient music, infinite possibility. Teenagers watched for hours. The algorithm loved it. Minecraft became YouTube's most-watched game without Mojang spending a dollar on advertising.

By late 2010, Minecraft had sold over 800,000 copies. Notch incorporated Mojang — Swedish for "gadget" — and hired a small team. Jakob Porser, an old colleague, joined as CEO. Notch remained the public face, tweeting constantly, sharing development updates, arguing with players about features. The relationship between creator and community was intimate and volatile.

The game officially launched in November 2011. By then, it had already sold 4 million copies. The launch was ceremonial — the game had been "done" in the sense that mattered for months.
