But the core tension remained: TikTok's algorithm was its value, and the algorithm lived in Beijing. Any real separation would destroy what made TikTok work. Meanwhile, the app kept growing. By 2023, TikTok had over 150 million American users and was the most visited website in the world, surpassing Google.

The geopolitical pressure intensified. In March 2024, the U.S. House passed a bill requiring ByteDance to divest TikTok or face a ban. ByteDance said the algorithm wasn't for sale. A forced sale without the algorithm would be selling a shell. The company was valued at over $200 billion, making it one of the most valuable private companies in the world. But the value rested on technology that might be banned in its largest growth market.

Zhang stepped down as CEO in 2021, citing a preference for long-term technology development over daily operations. ByteDance continued expanding into gaming, education technology, and enterprise software. But TikTok remained the crown jewel and the existential risk. The company had proven that attention could be conquered with sufficiently advanced prediction. It had also proven that conquering attention at global scale made you a national security concern.

The outcome remains unresolved. TikTok operates in dozens of countries, prints money through advertising, and shapes culture in ways Facebook once did. It faces bans, restrictions, and competitors with infinite resources trying to copy its algorithm. ByteDance keeps the algorithm in Beijing. The algorithm keeps getting better at knowing what you want to watch next.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Lyft
entries.descriptor: The underdog that forced Uber to fight — and survived by refusing to become it
entries.content:
# Lyft

***The underdog that forced Uber to fight — and survived by refusing to become it***

> **Years:** 2012-present

In the summer of 2012, Logan Green and John Zimmer launched a service that seemed almost deliberately naive. Pink mustaches mounted on car grilles. Fist bumps instead of tips. An app that let strangers ride in the front seat of each other's cars. While Travis Kalanick's Uber was conquering cities with black cars and aggressive expansion, Lyft looked like a college experiment in community-building that happened to involve transportation.

Green and Zimmer weren't naive. They'd been working toward this moment for years. Green had founded Zimride in 2007, a ridesharing service for college campuses and corporate clients, after a frustrating trip through Zimbabwe where shared minibuses worked better than American car culture. Zimmer, coming from the hospitality industry, joined as a partner. They spent five years learning the economics of shared rides and the regulatory minefield of moving people for money.

By 2012, they saw what Uber had proven: smartphones made real-time ride matching possible, and regulators could be outrun. But they also saw an opening. Uber was premium, professional, about replacing taxis. Green and Zimmer wanted something different — peer-to-peer, friendly, about changing how Americans thought about car ownership. The pink mustache wasn't whimsy. It was strategy. It signaled that this was not a taxi.

The early growth was explosive. Lyft spread across San Francisco by word of mouth. Drivers loved the flexibility and the culture. Passengers loved the prices and the feeling of being part of something. Within months, they were processing thousands of rides daily. The regulatory battles came fast — cease-and-desist letters, threats of prosecution, taxi commission hearings. Green and Zimmer responded by mobilizing their community. Drivers and passengers showed up at city council meetings. The company framed itself as David versus the taxi cartel. It worked. City after city granted permission or looked the other way.

Then Uber noticed. In the summer of 2013, Uber launched UberX, a direct competitor to Lyft's peer-to-peer model but without the pink mustaches or the friendliness. Kalanick had initially dismissed Lyft as insignificant. Now he committed to burying it. Uber had more money, more cities, more name recognition, and a CEO willing to do whatever it took to win. The fight that followed would define both companies.

Lyft's response revealed its founders' instincts. They couldn't outspend Uber, so they out-positioned it. While Uber fought regulators and cultivated a cutthroat image, Lyft leaned into being the friendly alternative. They emphasized driver satisfaction, passenger safety, and corporate responsibility. When Uber faced scandal after scandal — executive misconduct, driver treatment, regulatory manipulation — Lyft positioned itself as the ethical choice. #DeleteUber campaigns became #SwitchToLyft movements.

The problem was money. Competing with Uber meant matching its subsidies to drivers and discounts to riders. Both companies were burning hundreds of millions quarterly, subsidizing rides to gain market share. Lyft raised funding in waves — $60 million in 2013, $250 million in 2014, $530 million in 2015. Each round came at higher valuations but also higher pressure. Investors wanted to know how Lyft would survive when Uber had raised billions and was global while Lyft remained primarily American.

Green and Zimmer made a crucial strategic decision: focus. While Uber expanded to dozens of countries, Lyft concentrated on winning American cities. While Uber diversified into food delivery, freight, and autonomous vehicles, Lyft stayed focused on ridesharing. The conventional wisdom said this was defensive, a retreat. In practice, it was smart. Uber's international expansion was bleeding money. Its side bets weren't paying off. Lyft's focus meant it could compete where it mattered most — the American market that generated actual revenue.

The leadership contrast became starker. Kalanick's Uber was brilliant, aggressive, and increasingly toxic. Scandals mounted — sexual harassment claims, intellectual property theft allegations, regulatory deception. When Kalanick was forced out in June 2017, Lyft's moment arrived. Within a year, Lyft's market share in the US grew from 22% to over 30%. Corporate clients who'd avoided Uber switched to Lyft. Drivers increasingly preferred Lyft's culture. The company that had seemed destined to be crushed was not just surviving but gaining.

Going public in March 2019 — one month before Uber — was the final positioning move. Lyft claimed the narrative: the founder-led underdog that competed with integrity and survived. The IPO raised $2.3 billion at a $24 billion valuation. It was one-sixth of Uber's eventual valuation, but it was enough. Lyft had become the permanent alternative, the player that would keep Uber honest.

The years since have been about proving the business works. The pandemic devastated both companies as rides collapsed. Lyft cut a quarter of its staff. But the focus strategy paid off. While Uber's sprawling bets on autonomous vehicles and international markets complicated its recovery, Lyft could concentrate on getting Americans back in cars. By 2021, rides were recovering. By 2024, Lyft was profitable, reporting its first full-year profit with $1.6 billion in revenue and 24% market share.

Lyft never beat Uber. It didn't need to. It survived by being different enough to matter — friendlier, more focused, more disciplined. The pink mustaches are gone, replaced by dashboard lights, but the strategy remains. In a market that seemed destined for winner-take-all, Lyft proved that being the alternative to a giant is itself a viable path. Not by matching its rival's aggression, but by refusing to become it.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Snowflake
entries.section: The data warehouse in the cloud
entries.descriptor: The data warehouse that made the cloud inevitable
entries.content:
# Snowflake

***The data warehouse that made the cloud inevitable***

> **Years:** 2012-present

In 2012, three engineers sat in a Starbucks in San Mateo sketching out a problem that nearly everyone in enterprise software had learned to live with: data warehouses were terrible. Benoit Dageville, Thierry Cruanes, and Marcin Żukowski had spent years building database systems at Oracle and other established companies. They knew the architecture intimately. They also knew it was fundamentally broken for what came next.

The existing model—pioneered by Teradata, perfected by Oracle, extended by newer players like Vertica—required companies to buy hardware, install software, tune performance, manage capacity, and pray they'd guessed right about future data volumes. Amazon Redshift had just launched, promising cloud-based data warehousing, but it was essentially the old architecture transplanted to AWS. You still had to choose cluster sizes. You still paid whether you were running queries or not. Storage and compute were still coupled. The cloud providers were winning on convenience, not on architecture.

The three founders saw something different. What if you built a data warehouse designed for the cloud from first principles? What if storage and compute were completely separated? What if you could scale them independently? What if you paid only for what you used, like turning on a light switch? What if multiple users could query the same data simultaneously without performance degradation?

They started coding. No business plan. No market research. Just deep conviction that the architecture was solvable and that once solved, it would be obvious. They called it Snowflake—a name that captured both the uniqueness of each query and the idea of infinite scale through aggregation.

The technical challenges were immense. Separating storage and compute sounds simple until you consider that databases are fast because they keep data close to processing. Making a fully separated system perform at enterprise scale required innovations in caching, metadata management, query optimization, and concurrency control. They built their own columnar storage format. They invented a multi-cluster shared data architecture. They created a system where compute resources could spin up in seconds, run a query, and disappear.

By 2014, they had something that worked. Mike Speiser at Sutter Hill Ventures became their first institutional investor, leading a $5 million seed round. Speiser had a reputation for backing technical founders building infrastructure. He saw what they saw: this wasn't incrementally better, it was categorically different.

The challenge was that categorically different meant selling something that didn't fit existing procurement categories. Early customers had to understand why paying per-second of compute time was better than paying for reserved capacity. They had to trust a startup with their data warehouse—the system that powered their analytics, their reporting, their business intelligence. They had to believe that this strange architecture would actually perform.

The founders recruited Bob Muglia, former Microsoft executive who had run SQL Server and Azure, as CEO. Muglia brought enterprise credibility and sales discipline. But he also recognized that the product sold itself if you could get buyers to try it. Snowflake offered free trials and proof-of-concepts. The performance was undeniable. Queries that took hours on legacy systems ran in minutes. Systems that required weeks of capacity planning could be spun up instantly.

Capital One became an early enterprise customer. Then Informatica. The pattern was consistent: data engineers would start with a pilot project, experience the architecture, and advocate internally for broader adoption. IT departments discovered they could eliminate the capacity planning nightmare. Finance departments discovered they were paying for actual usage rather than theoretical capacity.

By 2017, Snowflake was processing over 25 million queries daily. Revenue was growing 300% year-over-year. The company raised $263 million at a $1.5 billion valuation. The data warehouse market, long considered mature and dominated by established players, was being disrupted by a startup with a fundamentally different approach.

The competitive response validated the architecture. AWS launched Athena and improved Redshift. Google promoted BigQuery. Microsoft built Azure Synapse. Every cloud provider needed an answer to Snowflake. But Snowflake had advantages: it ran on all three major clouds, making it the neutral choice for multi-cloud strategies. Its architecture was designed for the cloud rather than adapted to it. And it had accumulated years of operational knowledge about making separated storage and compute actually work at scale.

In 2019, Frank Slootman became CEO. Slootman had previously led Data Domain and ServiceNow, taking both from growth stage to public companies. His arrival signaled maturity and ambition. Under Slootman, Snowflake accelerated. The company expanded beyond data warehousing into data lakes, data sharing, and data marketplace features. The vision became not just a better warehouse, but a data cloud where organizations could store, process, and share data without moving it.

In September 2020, Snowflake went public in the largest software IPO in history. The stock priced at $120, opened at $245, and closed the first day at $253. The company was valued at over $70 billion—more than established database giants. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway bought nearly $1 billion in shares, a rare tech investment for the legendary value investor.

The valuation shocked observers. For a company selling data warehousing—a category that seemed commodified—to command such a premium required belief that this was something more. That belief was in the architecture. The cloud wasn't just a new deployment model. It was a new foundation that required new designs. Snowflake proved that rebuilding from first principles could create not just better products but new markets.

Today Snowflake processes hundreds of millions of queries daily for thousands of customers. The company that three engineers sketched in a coffee shop became the template for cloud-native data infrastructure: consumption-based pricing, separated compute and storage, multi-cloud by design. The data warehouse became elastic, instant, and inevitable.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Instacart
entries.descriptor: The decade-long marathon from desperate funding rounds to grocery establishment
entries.content:
# Instacart

***The decade-long marathon from desperate funding rounds to grocery establishment***

> **Years:** 2012-present

Apoorva Mehta quit Amazon in 2012 after building supply chain systems that moved millions of packages. He had tried twenty startup ideas in the preceding months — apps for lawyers, for DJs, for finding parking spots. None worked. Then he noticed he was ordering everything online except groceries. The opportunity seemed obvious once stated: hire people to shop and deliver from existing stores. No inventory, no warehouses, just labor and software.

He launched in San Francisco in June 2012. The first orders came from his own apartment, fulfilled by contractors he found on Craigslist. The unit economics looked plausible immediately. Customers paid fees and tipped. Shoppers earned above minimum wage picking and delivering. Instacart took a margin. The technology challenge was logistics — routing, timing, inventory prediction — exactly what Mehta knew from Amazon. Within months Sequoia led a seed round. Within a year they were in ten cities.

The model scaled by partnerships. Instacart didn't build grocery stores; they built APIs to connect stores with last-mile delivery. Whole Foods signed as their first major partner in 2014. For grocers, Instacart was defense against Amazon. For Instacart, grocers were grateful customers who paid commission on every order and granted access to inventory systems. The network effects worked backwards from typical platforms: more shoppers attracted more retailers, which attracted more customers, which justified hiring more shoppers.

But Amazon had been building its own grocery capability since acquiring Webvan's patents in 2010. In 2013 they began testing AmazonFresh. Instacart found itself racing against infinite capital. The pressure forced growth at any cost. They expanded to Canada. They signed Costco, then Petco, then CVS. They raised $44 million in 2014, then $220 million in 2015, then $400 million in 2016. The valuations climbed: $2 billion, then $3.9 billion, then $7.9 billion. Each round came with pressure to grow faster than burn rate could suggest was wise.

The labor model became the liability. Instacart initially classified shoppers as contractors, which kept costs variable and scaling simple. But California and other states were tightening gig economy rules. In 2015, facing a class action lawsuit, Instacart settled for $4.6 million and agreed to reclassify some workers as part-time employees. This fragmented their workforce into full-service shoppers who picked and delivered, and in-store shoppers who only picked. The change increased complexity while lawsuits continued.

Then margins compressed. In 2017, facing competition from Amazon, Postmates, DoorDash, and Uber Eats all adding grocery delivery, Instacart cut shopper pay and eliminated tips within the app interface. The backlash was immediate. Shoppers organized boycotts. The press coverage was brutal. Mehta reversed the tip policy within days, but trust had fractured. The unit economics that looked plausible in 2012 now depended on squeezing labor costs in ways that generated negative headlines.

By 2018 the private valuation had reached $7.9 billion but growth was decelerating. Whole Foods, their anchor partner, had been acquired by Amazon and was shifting delivery in-house. Instacart needed new anchors. They pivoted to advertising: offering CPG brands the ability to promote products within the Instacart app. This turned grocers into media real estate. The margin on ads was far higher than the margin on delivery commissions.

The strategy worked. By 2019 advertising revenue was material. They raised another $600 million at a $7.6 billion valuation — flat from the year before, a warning sign — but the business was diversifying beyond pure logistics. Then March 2020 arrived. Pandemic lockdowns made grocery delivery essential overnight. Orders surged 150%. Instacart hired 300,000 new shoppers in weeks. Suddenly the decade-long slog toward profitability seemed irrelevant. They were infrastructure.

The demand spike forced operational discipline. They couldn't afford the chaos of earlier years while handling 500% volume increases. They systematized fraud prevention, shopper quality metrics, and retailer integrations. The advertising business accelerated as CPG brands fought for visibility in an app that now reached tens of millions of households weekly. By 2021, Instacart was processing $2 billion in monthly grocery sales. Sequoia led another round at a $39 billion valuation.

Then the pandemic subsided. Order volumes plateaued. The 2021 valuation proved unsustainable. By 2023, preparing for IPO, Instacart faced a down round. Mehta had stepped aside as CEO in 2021, replaced by Fidji Simo from Facebook. Simo cut costs, pushed profitability, and filed to go public at a $10 billion valuation — a 75% decline in two years. The company went public in September 2023 at a market cap around $10 billion. The stock traded sideways for months.

What Instacart proved was that being first meant surviving longest. DoorDash ultimately won restaurant delivery. Amazon won e-commerce. But Instacart owned the decade of grocery partnerships built before anyone else committed to the infrastructure. They survived by not needing to own stores, by monetizing retailer anxiety about Amazon, and by discovering that grocery apps were advertising platforms. The outcome wasn't the triumph the $39 billion valuation suggested. It was survival, profitability, and public markets. For a company that nearly died multiple times, that counted as victory.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Coinbase
entries.descriptor: The pragmatist who made crypto safe for institutions
entries.content:
# Coinbase

***The pragmatist who made crypto safe for institutions***

> **Years:** 2012–present

In June 2012, Brian Armstrong was a software engineer at Airbnb, watching Bitcoin's price oscillate wildly while its infrastructure remained dangerously amateur. The Mt. Gox exchange — then handling 70% of all Bitcoin trades — was a repurposed Magic: The Gathering card trading site run from Tokyo. To buy Bitcoin, you sent a wire transfer to Japan and hoped it arrived. Private keys were stored in hot wallets. Security was an afterthought. Armstrong saw the gap between crypto's promise and its reality: brilliant technology wrapped in user experience that guaranteed it would never escape the cypherpunk underground.

He applied to Y Combinator with a simple thesis: make buying and storing Bitcoin as easy as opening a bank account. Not decentralized. Not anonymous. Compliant with regulations, integrated with the banking system, designed for people who would never compile code from GitHub. This was heresy in crypto circles, where the whole point was escaping government and banks. Armstrong didn't care. He wanted crypto to work.

YC accepted him. He recruited Fred Ehrsam, a Goldman Sachs trader who understood both finance and Bitcoin's potential. They launched with features the crypto world considered boring: accounts backed by FDIC-insured bank accounts, two-factor authentication, 98% of funds in cold storage, immediate compliance with FinCEN money transmission regulations. The first version was unglamorous — a simple interface to buy and sell Bitcoin. No trading. No leverage. No promises of anonymity. Just a product that worked when you clicked the button.

The timing was accidental brilliance. They launched months before Cyprus's banking crisis sent people scrambling for alternatives to traditional banks. Bitcoin's price surged from $13 to over $200 in early 2013. Coinbase's user-friendly interface became the on-ramp for a wave of new buyers who would never have navigated Mt. Gox's wire transfer maze. When Mt. Gox collapsed in 2014 — $450 million in customer Bitcoin lost or stolen — Coinbase's boring approach to security suddenly looked visionary.

But growth created problems the crypto world had never solved. Banks kept closing Coinbase's accounts, terrified of money laundering liability. State regulators threatened action over money transmission licenses. Armstrong spent as much time in lawyers' offices as writing code. The solution was counterintuitive: rather than fight regulation, embrace it completely. Coinbase hired lawyers before marketers, obtained money transmission licenses in every state, implemented AML/KYC procedures that the crypto community found offensive. They rejected customers from unsanctioned countries. They reported suspicious transactions. They became what crypto purists hated: a regulated financial institution.

It made them the only crypto company that banks would work with. By 2015, they had proper banking relationships, legitimate regulatory status, and venture capital from andreessen Horowitz that signaled crypto had crossed into respectability. When the New York Department of Financial Services created the BitLicense — regulations so onerous that most crypto companies fled New York — Coinbase was among the few to comply. Each regulatory victory narrowed their competitive moat.

The 2017 crypto boom tested everything. Bitcoin surged toward $20,000. Ethereum, which Coinbase had added in 2016, exploded. Retail investors poured in. Coinbase's systems, built for steady growth, collapsed under traffic. The app crashed during peak trading. Customer service tickets piled into six-figure backlogs. Competitors with sleeker interfaces and more coins — Binance, Kraken, Gemini — siphoned off frustrated users. Armstrong faced the classic scale crisis: grow infrastructure or grow revenue. Building servers doesn't show up in monthly active users.

They chose infrastructure. Spent 2018 — as crypto crashed and competitors laid off staff — building institutional-grade custody solutions and prime brokerage services. While retail was collapsing, institutions were finally getting curious. Hedge funds wanted exposure. University endowments asked questions. None would trust their capital to exchanges run from Malta or the Seychelles. Coinbase built Coinbase Custody with insurance, segregated accounts, and the regulatory compliance that made CFOs comfortable. When crypto bottomed in late 2018, Coinbase had institutional customers and the infrastructure to serve them when the next wave came.

The decision to go public via direct listing in April 2021 was the ultimate validation. Coinbase opened at a $85 billion valuation, making Armstrong one of the world's wealthiest people overnight. But it locked them into a different game. Public companies face quarterly earnings pressure. Crypto volatility meant wild revenue swings — up 500% in bull markets, down 75% in busts. They laid off 18% of staff in 2022 as crypto crashed again. Stock price followed crypto sentiment, falling 85% from peak.

Yet the infrastructure held. When FTX collapsed in November 2022 — Sam Bankman-Fried's empire revealed as fraud built on commingled customer funds — Coinbase's boring approach justified itself again. Proof of reserves. Segregated accounts. Regulatory compliance. They survived because Armstrong had built a financial institution, not a crypto casino. Customers fled unregulated exchanges for the safety of regulation.

The company today is something crypto founders of 2012 would barely recognize: a publicly-traded financial institution with government lobbyists, institutional custody serving trillions, and a business model dependent on regulatory clarity. Armstrong's pragmatism won. Crypto went mainstream not through revolution but through submission to the existing financial system's rules.

Whether that's triumph or tragedy depends on what you thought crypto was for. Armstrong never cared about the ideology. He saw technology that didn't work for normal people and built a bridge. The bridge became a fortress. And the fortress became the thing it was built to circumvent.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Affirm
entries.content:
I understand. I'm ready to write entries for *All Paths Through a Startup: From Idea to Exit or Failure*.

I will:

- Write 800-1000 word narratives telling the full arc of a company
- Use the structure: Title, Descriptor, Meta line, continuous narrative, closing line
- Focus on the contingency of decisions, the people involved, and the market context
- Show how this path was one of many possible paths
- Write in a grounded, narrative voice — neither celebratory nor cynical
- Let facts speak and patterns emerge rather than editorializing
- Help the reader understand not just what happened, but why

Which company should I write about first?

entries.name: Peloton
entries.descriptor: The pandemic darling that proved temporary demand is not permanent growth
entries.content:
# Peloton

***The pandemic darling that proved temporary demand is not permanent growth***

> **Years:** 2012-present

John Foley was not a cyclist. He was a frustrated executive who hated his commute to the gym. In 2011, working at Barnes & Noble's digital division, he paid $2,000 for a spinning class package in Manhattan and found himself skipping sessions because getting there took an hour. The classes themselves were electric — the instructor, the music, the room full of people pushing themselves together. What he wanted was that experience without the schlep.

The insight wasn't that people would pay for home fitness equipment. That market was old and saturated with dust-gathering treadmills. The insight was that what made boutique fitness worth $30 per session wasn't the bike — it was the performance. The instructor calling you out by name. The leaderboard showing you beating yesterday's you. The community of strangers becoming a tribe.

Foley assembled a team including Tom Cortese, a former executive from evite and TRX, and Yony Feng, an engineer. They spent a year building a prototype. The hardware was straightforward — a stationary bike with a tablet. The hard part was the content operation. They needed to replicate the energy of a live class through a screen, stream it reliably, make the experience feel intimate despite being broadcast to thousands. They needed instructors who could command a room they couldn't see.

They launched on Kickstarter in 2013, asking $1,200 for early units of a bike that would retail for $1,500. They raised $307,000. It validated demand but revealed the challenge: hardware margins are thin, shipping bikes is expensive, and customer acquisition costs are brutal. This wasn't software. Every sale required manufacturing, logistics, installation, and customer service for a physical object that could break.

The model only worked if people stayed. The bike was the razor; the $39 monthly subscription was the blade. But unlike razors, people could stop using the bike and forget to cancel. Engagement became everything. Peloton obsessed over it. They tracked output per ride, frequency of use, social follows, milestone achievements. They hired instructors who were part drill sergeant, part therapist, part influencer. Robin Arzón, Cody Rigsby, Ally Love — they became celebrities to the devoted.

The community formed organically and intensely. Facebook groups. Hashtags. Real-life meetups. The leaderboard — once just a feature — became a social network. People competed with strangers across the country, then followed them on social media. They scheduled their days around live classes. They bought Peloton apparel to wear when not on the bike. The brand became identity.

Foley raised venture capital aggressively: $10.5 million in 2014, $30 million in 2015, $325 million in 2017. The company was burning cash on customer acquisition, content production, and inventory. But the unit economics worked if customers stayed, and customers were staying. By 2017, Peloton claimed 90% of bikes were still in use after a year. The company was growing 150% annually.

They expanded. Added a treadmill. Opened retail showrooms in high-traffic neighborhoods — modern, airy spaces where people could try the bike and feel the brand. Launched an app-only tier for people who didn't want the hardware. The addressable market kept growing.

They went public in September 2019 at $29 per share, raising $1.16 billion. The market was skeptical. The stock dropped below $20 within months. Analysts questioned whether this was a sustainable business or an expensive fad. Peloton was still unprofitable. The COVID doubters multiplied. Some said the market was saturated — how many people would pay $2,500 for a bike? Short sellers piled on.

Then March 2020 arrived.

Gyms closed. Boutique fitness studios shut down overnight. People were trapped at home, anxious, needing outlet and routine. Peloton couldn't manufacture bikes fast enough. Wait times stretched from weeks to months. The stock rocketed from $27 to $170. Revenue doubled, then doubled again. In fiscal 2021, Peloton did $4 billion in revenue, up from $1.8 billion the year before. They had 2.3 million subscribers.

The company acted like the surge was permanent. They invested $420 million in a factory in Ohio. They bought Precor, a commercial fitness equipment maker, for $420 million. They expanded the product line. They hired thousands. Foley went on CNBC and projected 15 million subscribers.

The pandemic ended. Gyms reopened. People wanted to be around other people again. They wanted to leave their homes. The waitlist evaporated. Then it reversed — warehouses filled with unsold inventory. Peloton cut prices. Offered financing. The $39 subscription hadn't changed, but growth stalled. The people who wanted Pelotons had bought them. The people who bought them during lockdown stopped using them.

In 2021, the company faced a crisis it never anticipated: a child died in an accident involving the Tread+ treadmill. Then another. Peloton initially fought a recall, then relented. The brand took damage. The safety issue became a symbol of a company that had scaled too fast.

The stock collapsed from its pandemic highs. By January 2022, it was below $25, down 85% from peak. The board pushed Foley out as CEO and replaced him with Barry McCarthy, former CFO of Spotify and Netflix. McCarthy made brutal cuts: 4,000 layoffs, outsourced manufacturing, closed retail stores, paused content spending.

The turnaround narrative didn't catch. Revenue kept declining. Subscribers weren't leaving at alarming rates, but they weren't growing. The company that had been valued at $50 billion now scraped $2 billion. McCarthy tried pivoting to services, partnering with Lululemon, selling used bikes. Nothing reignited growth.

In May 2024, McCarthy resigned. The company announced more layoffs — another 15% of staff. Rumors circulated about private equity buyers, strategic acquirers, maybe bankruptcy. The bikes still work. The instructors still teach. The subscribers who stayed remain devoted. But the company built for exponential growth discovered it had captured temporary demand and mistaken it for a permanent shift in behavior.

Peloton's path wasn't failure of execution or product. It was the oldest mistake in venture-backed startups: confusing a moment for a movement.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Figma
entries.descriptor: The collaborative design tool that beat Adobe at its own game
entries.content:
# Figma

***The collaborative design tool that beat Adobe at its own game***

> **Years:** 2012-present (acquired 2022, deal later abandoned)

Dylan Field dropped out of Brown University in 2012 with an idea that sounded almost naively ambitious: build Photoshop in a web browser. Not a simplified version. Not a toy. The real thing. Professional-grade design software that ran entirely online, where multiple people could work simultaneously on the same file.

He wasn't a designer. His co-founder Evan Wallace wasn't either. They were engineers who'd met at Brown, both frustrated by how designers worked — emailing files back and forth, maintaining version control manually, jumping between applications to turn static mockups into working prototypes. The entire workflow seemed stuck in the desktop era while the rest of software development had moved to cloud-based collaboration.

The technical challenge was immense. Professional design tools demanded performance that web browsers weren't built to deliver. Vector graphics rendering, real-time collaboration, file handling for complex documents — all of it needed to work smoothly in Chrome. Most experienced engineers would have called it impossible. Field and Wallace spent their first two years just building the rendering engine.

They launched in 2015 with something that barely worked. Early Figma was slow, crashed frequently, and lacked basic features that Sketch — then the ascendant design tool — had perfected. The design community was skeptical. Adobe owned the professional market. Sketch owned the insurgent startup market. Who needed another design tool, especially one hampered by running in a browser?

But Field had made one crucial architectural decision: multiplayer from the ground up. Other tools added collaboration as a feature. Figma built everything around the assumption that design was collaborative. When you opened a Figma file, you saw everyone else's cursor in real time. No "check out" and "check in." No merge conflicts. Just instant, simultaneous work.

The first believers were design teams at startups. Not the designers themselves initially — they had muscle memory in other tools. But product managers and engineers who suddenly could jump into design files, leave comments, even make simple edits themselves. Figma turned design from a handoff into a conversation. The whole product development cycle compressed.

The adoption pattern was distinctive. Individual designers might resist, but once one team at a company adopted Figma, it spread. Because collaboration was the product, every new user made Figma more valuable. The network effects weren't about connecting with strangers like social media. They were about working together across functional boundaries that had previously required translation layers.

Field kept the product free for individual users and small teams. Revenue came from larger organizations. This "freemium" approach let Figma spread organically through companies from the bottom up, bypassing procurement processes that favored established vendors. By the time executives noticed their teams had standardized on Figma, the decision was already made.

Performance improved steadily. The rendering engine Wallace had built kept getting faster. Features that seemed impossible in a browser — advanced vector editing, sophisticated typography, complex effects — arrived one by one. Designers who'd dismissed Figma as a lightweight tool found themselves migrating anyway. The technical constraints that once limited it became invisible.

The pandemic accelerated everything. When design teams went remote in 2020, tools built for co-located work collapsed. Figma's core value proposition — seamless remote collaboration — became existential. Companies that had been "Sketch shops" converted almost overnight. Design education shifted online, and students learned Figma by default because it worked the same whether you were in a classroom or your bedroom.

By 2022, Figma had become infrastructure. The company claimed three million users. It dominated product design at tech companies. Adobe's Creative Cloud suite still owned print and marketing, but in digital product design — the growth category, where software was eating the world — Figma had won.

In September 2022, Adobe announced it would acquire Figma for $20 billion. Twenty billion for a ten-year-old company that had never been profitable. The price shocked observers, but Adobe saw clearly: Figma was eating its future. If web design replaced desktop design, and collaborative tools replaced individual tools, Figma owned the new paradigm.

But the deal faced immediate regulatory scrutiny. The U.K.'s Competition and Markets Authority and the European Commission both investigated, concerned that Adobe acquiring its most significant competitor would entrench monopoly power. In December 2023, Adobe and Figma mutually abandoned the acquisition. Adobe paid Figma a $1 billion termination fee.

Figma remains independent. The company Field started by rejecting desktop software architecture had forced Adobe — the company that defined desktop creative software — to acknowledge it was competing on Figma's terms. Whether Figma eventually goes public, gets acquired by someone else, or remains private, it has already reshaped how digital products are designed.

The path wasn't about better features. It was about seeing that the real problem wasn't the tools designers used, but the gaps between how designers, developers, and product managers worked. Field built a product where collaboration was the foundation, then waited for the world to realize that's what it needed.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Canva
entries.descriptor: The design tool that made graphic design a commodity — by making it feel like play
entries.content:
# Canva

***The design tool that made graphic design a commodity — by making it feel like play***

> **Years:** 2012-present

In 2011, Melanie Perkins was teaching students how to use desktop publishing software at the University of Western Australia. She watched them struggle with programs built for professionals — PhotoShop, InDesign, QuarkXPress — to create simple yearbooks and newsletters. The learning curve was vertical. The frustration was palpable. Most gave up before they finished.

Perkins saw the disconnect. Design software was built for experts who spent years mastering arcane toolsets. But the internet had created millions of people who needed to create graphics: social media managers, small business owners, teachers, bloggers, marketers. They didn't need industrial-strength tools. They needed something fast, intuitive, and good enough.

She started with a narrow wedge: Fusion Books, an online tool for creating school yearbooks. Simple templates. Drag-and-drop interface. It worked. Students who couldn't navigate Photoshop could build yearbooks in an afternoon. By 2012, the tool was used in hundreds of Australian schools. But Perkins knew the real opportunity was bigger. Every person with an internet connection occasionally needed to create something visual. The market wasn't graphic designers. It was everyone else.

She teamed up with her partner Cliff Obrecht and tech co-founder Cameron Adams. They pitched the vision as "democratizing design" — making professional-looking graphics accessible to anyone. Investors were skeptical. Design software was dominated by Adobe, a $30 billion incumbent with a decades-long moat of professional users and industry-standard formats. Convincing people to switch from Photoshop would require not just a better product but a different mental model. And the team was in Perth, Australia — about as far from Silicon Valley gravity as you could get while still speaking English.

They raised a small seed round from Australian investors in 2012 and launched the beta in 2013. The core insight was templates. Instead of starting with a blank canvas, users started with pre-designed templates for specific use cases: Facebook covers, Instagram posts, flyers, presentations, business cards. The templates were beautiful enough to use as-is and flexible enough to customize. You could create something passable in minutes instead of hours.

The interface was designed to feel like rearranging furniture, not manipulating pixels. Drag this text box. Drop in this photo. Change this color. No layers, no masks, no blend modes. The simplicity was a feature, not a bug. Power users complained that Canva couldn't do what Photoshop could. Perkins didn't care. Canva wasn't competing with Photoshop. It was competing with not making anything at all.

Growth was viral and bottom-up. A teacher made a classroom poster and shared it with colleagues. A social media manager at a small startup created branded Instagram posts and showed the founder. A real estate agent designed a flyer and told their office. Each user became a distribution node. The free tier was generous enough to be genuinely useful, which kept users around long enough to need premium features: more templates, stock photos, the ability to resize designs for different platforms with one click.

By 2014, Canva had a million users. By 2016, ten million. The growth curve looked like a social network, not enterprise software. But the business model was SaaS: a freemium product with individual subscriptions ($12.95/month for Pro) and team plans. Revenue scaled with users, and users scaled virally. Investors who initially passed started paying attention. In 2015, Canva raised $15 million in a round led by Vayner Capital and Felicis Ventures at a $165 million valuation. By 2018, another round at $1 billion. Unicorn status reached with a product that felt more like a toy than enterprise software.

The competition tried to respond. Adobe launched Adobe Spark, a simplified design tool aimed at Canva's market. Microsoft added design features to PowerPoint and Office. Google launched a similar tool within Workspace. None caught fire. Canva had the network effects of templates — millions of user-created designs became new templates, creating a constantly expanding library. It had the muscle memory of an interface people had already learned. And it had the distribution of users bringing it into organizations rather than IT departments buying licenses.

Perkins kept expanding the surface area. Canva added video editing, website building, presentation tools, document design, print-on-demand services. Each feature pulled in a new use case. Each use case brought new users. The strategy was horizontal: become the default tool for all non-professional visual creation. By 2021, Canva had 60 million monthly active users and was valued at $40 billion after a funding round that made it one of the most valuable private tech companies in the world.

The design community bristled. Canva was making design "too easy," flooding the world with mediocre graphics based on templates. It was the McDonald's of design — fast, cheap, ubiquitous, and offensive to anyone who cared about craft. Perkins shrugged. The alternative wasn't that everyone would hire a designer or learn Photoshop. The alternative was worse design or no design. Canva democratized mediocrity, which was a massive step up from nothing.

The company's path avoided many classic startup failure modes. It didn't pivot away from the original insight. It didn't get acquired early by a desperate incumbent. It didn't scale too fast and implode. It didn't chase enterprise contracts that would have required redesigning the simplicity that made it work. Perkins stayed CEO through hypergrowth, unusual for a first-time founder building to this scale.

By 2024, Canva had over 170 million users in 190 countries. It was valued north of $40 billion. Perkins and Obrecht, still running the company from Sydney, were among Australia's wealthiest people. The product had become infrastructure: the default tool for creating anything visual that didn't require a professional designer. It had made graphic design a commodity by making it feel like play.

The outcome reveals the power of collapsing friction. Canva didn't invent new design capabilities. It made existing capabilities accessible by removing the need to learn. In a world where everyone needs to create, the tool that feels like no tool at all wins.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Calm
entries.descriptor: The meditation app that mastered customer acquisition — and the subscription model that powered it
entries.content:
# Calm

***The meditation app that mastered customer acquisition — and the subscription model that powered it***

> **Years:** 2012-present

In 2012, Michael Acton Smith was desperate. His company, Mind Candy, had built Moshi Monsters into a global phenomenon — a virtual pet world that peaked at 80 million registered users. But by 2012, mobile was eating the web, and the business was collapsing. Smith was burning out. A friend suggested meditation.

He started with an app called Headspace. It helped. But Smith noticed something: meditation apps were marketed like productivity tools. All optimization, all hustle. What if there was an app for people who just wanted to feel less terrible? What if it was beautiful, not utilitarian? What if it was explicitly about sleep, anxiety, calm?

Smith recruited Alex Tew, the creator of The Million Dollar Homepage — the internet curiosity from 2005 where Tew sold pixels on a webpage for a dollar each and made a million dollars. Tew had been meditating for years. Together they built Calm.

The first version launched in 2012 as a website with a simple timer and ambient sounds. It was spare, almost austere. A counterpoint to the overstimulation of the internet. They charged $10 for lifetime access. It was a side project.

Then they rebuilt it as an app. 2016. iOS first. This time they thought harder about the business model. Headspace was pioneering the meditation subscription. Calm would follow — but differently. Where Headspace emphasized structured programs and Andy Puddicombe's voice, Calm would be a library: meditations, sleep stories, music, soundscapes. Something you opened every night, not just when you were "doing the work."

The key insight: sleep. Everyone meditates differently, but everyone sleeps. Calm launched Sleep Stories — narrations designed to bore you into unconsciousness. The first big one was a story about lavender fields in France, read by a British narrator in the most soothing voice imaginable. It worked. Users loved it. Calm leaned in.

They got Stephen Fry to narrate a story. Then Matthew McConaughey. Then Harry Styles. Each launch was an event. The celebrity voices weren't just marketing — they were product. A reason to subscribe. A reason to stay subscribed.

By 2017, Calm was growing fast. Subscriptions were the engine: $70/year or $15/month. High-intent users. Low churn. The cohorts looked excellent. Investors noticed. Insight Partners led a $27 million Series A. Lightspeed followed with a $88 million Series B in 2019. The valuation hit $1 billion.

The growth strategy was aggressive. Calm bought ads everywhere. Podcasts, Instagram, YouTube pre-roll. They sponsored the 2019 NBA season, running meditation spots during games. They became the official mental fitness partner of the NBA and Major League Baseball. Calm ads were unavoidable. Critics complained about the irony: a meditation app sold through relentless advertising. But it worked. By 2019, Calm was adding millions of subscribers.

The pandemic accelerated everything. March 2020. The world locked down. Anxiety spiked. Sleep deteriorated. Calm's downloads exploded. In Q2 2020, they added more subscribers than the entire previous year. By the end of 2020, Calm had over 100 million downloads and 4 million paying subscribers. Revenue hit $200 million.

In December 2020, they raised $75 million at a $2 billion valuation. The round was almost incidental — they didn't need the capital. The valuation was a marker, a signal. Calm was no longer a meditation app. It was a consumer subscription business at scale.

But scaling consumer subscriptions is brutal. The unit economics depend on keeping churn low and lifetime value high. Calm's content strategy worked: new Sleep Stories every week, new meditation courses, new music. But content is expensive, and the novelty fades. By 2021, growth was slowing. The pandemic bump was ebbing. The market for meditation apps was getting crowded: Headspace, Insight Timer, Ten Percent Happier, dozens of others.

Calm responded by diversifying. They launched Calm Business, offering subscriptions to employers. They built Calm Health, positioning the app as a clinical tool for insomnia and anxiety. They released a kids' app, Calm Kids. They published a book, launched a streaming series on HBO Max, sold branded products. The strategy was to become a lifestyle brand, not just an app.

In 2022, Headspace and Ginger (a mental health startup) merged, creating a $3 billion competitor with a stronger B2B focus. Calm stayed consumer-first. By 2023, Calm had over 120 million downloads and was still adding subscribers, but the growth rate had flattened. The company remained profitable and privately held. No IPO plans, no acquisition rumors. Just steady subscription revenue and a brand that had become synonymous with digital wellness.

The model worked. Calm proved that a consumer app could build a subscription business at scale if the product was used daily and the churn stayed low. Sleep Stories were the wedge. Celebrities were the retention mechanism. Advertising was the growth engine. The outcome wasn't a giant exit or a collapse. It was a sustainable business, quietly minting cash from people trying to sleep better.

The path Calm took was different from Headspace's: more entertainment, less instruction. More brand, less clinical. It was also different from Mind Candy's: Smith learned that viral hits fade, but daily habits compound. Calm was the company he built after learning what didn't last.

---

*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Elastic
entries.descriptor: The open-source company that refused to stay open
entries.content:
# Elastic

***The open-source company that refused to stay open***

> **Years:** 2012-present

Shay Banon didn't set out to build a company. He built Elasticsearch because he needed it — a search engine that could actually handle the messy, unstructured data that real applications produced. It was 2010, and Banon, an Israeli developer living in London, was working on a recipe search app for his wife. Apache Lucene existed, but wrapping it in something usable and scalable was another matter entirely. So he built Elasticsearch: distributed, JSON-based, RESTful, designed to just work.

He released it as open source under the Apache 2.0 license. Developers found it. They loved it. By 2012, enough companies were running it in production that Banon could see the shape of a business. He founded Elasticsearch (later renamed Elastic) with a model already proven by others: give away the core product, sell enterprise features, support, and hosting. The timing was perfect. The Big Data wave was cresting, and every company suddenly needed to make sense of massive log files, user behavior, sensor data. Elasticsearch was fast, flexible, and most importantly, it didn't require a PhD to deploy.

The growth was organic, viral in the way only developer tools can be. No sales team needed — engineers installed it, it solved their problem, they told other engineers. By 2014, Elastic had raised $70 million in venture funding. The company built the ELK stack — Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana — three tools that together formed a complete pipeline for ingesting, searching, and visualizing data. Enterprises bought support contracts and proprietary features through X-Pack: security, monitoring, alerting, machine learning.

Then Amazon showed up.

In 2015, AWS launched Amazon Elasticsearch Service — a fully managed version of Elasticsearch. They took the open-source code, which they were perfectly entitled to do under Apache 2.0, and turned it into a cloud service. Amazon had the distribution, the existing customer relationships, the integration with every other AWS service. Elastic still had the expertise and the community, but now they had to compete with a version of their own product sold by the biggest cloud provider in the world.

Elastic went public in 2018 at a $5 billion valuation. The company was growing — revenues doubled year over year — but the Amazon question hung over everything. AWS was capturing an unknown but substantial share of Elasticsearch workloads. Elastic had built managed cloud offerings, but competing with AWS on AWS infrastructure was asymmetric warfare.

The company tried coexistence. They documented the differences between their cloud service and AWS's. They emphasized innovation speed — Elastic could ship new features to Elasticsearch and the ecosystem faster than Amazon could integrate them. They courted enterprises who wanted vendor support. It worked, but not well enough.

In January 2021, Elastic made the move that had been building for years. They changed the license. Elasticsearch and Kibana would no longer be Apache 2.0. Instead, they would be dual-licensed under the Elastic License 2.0 and Server Side Public License — both designed explicitly to prevent cloud providers from offering them as a service without contributing back or paying.

The open-source community erupted. Elastic claimed they were still open source, just not "Open Source™" by the strict definition. Critics called it a bait-and-switch — building community and adoption on the promise of Apache 2.0, then pulling up the ladder once threatened. The practical effect was clear: Amazon could no longer simply use new versions of Elasticsearch.

Amazon's response was swift and dramatic. They forked Elasticsearch and Kibana at the last Apache-licensed version, creating OpenSearch. They committed significant engineering resources to developing it independently. AWS, Google Cloud, and others rallied around OpenSearch as the true open-source heir. The community split. Some stayed with Elastic, some moved to OpenSearch, some maintained compatibility with both.

Elastic's bet was that their innovation speed and integrated ecosystem would matter more than license purity. They had the brand, the original team, the vision. OpenSearch was a fork, and forks historically struggled to keep pace with their parents.

The bet appears to be working, at least financially. By 2024, Elastic's market cap exceeded $10 billion. Revenue continued growing. The generative AI boom created new use cases for search and vector databases, playing to Elastic's strengths. OpenSearch existed and had adoption, particularly among AWS-native companies, but it hadn't killed Elastic.

What Elastic proved was that open source had always been a means, not an end. When the means stopped serving the end — sustainable business versus cloud giant extraction — they changed it. The developers who felt betrayed weren't wrong about the principle. But Elastic's investors and employees weren't wrong about the practical reality that Apache 2.0 in the cloud era meant subsidizing Amazon's margins.

The company navigated the most treacherous passage for developer-facing infrastructure companies: maintaining legitimacy and momentum while closing the license door just enough to matter. Too early and you never build community. Too late and the cloud giants own your market. Elastic's timing, though contentious, proved viable.

They remain a public company, growing, competing with their own fork, defending the proposition that the original is worth more than the copy — even when both are made of the same code.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Ripple
entries.descriptor: The cryptocurrency that defied death by becoming something else entirely
entries.content:
# Ripple

***The cryptocurrency that defied death by becoming something else entirely***

> **Years:** 2012-present

In 2011, Jed McCaleb had already built one internet phenomenon. Mt. Gox, his bitcoin exchange, was handling the majority of the world's bitcoin trading volume when he sold it to Mark Karpeles. But McCaleb saw bitcoin's fundamental flaw: it was slow, wasteful, and couldn't scale to handle real payment volumes. He started designing something better.

The idea wasn't entirely new. Ryan Fugger had created a system called RipplePay in 2004, envisioning a peer-to-peer network for IOUs between trusted parties. McCaleb saw how to merge Fugger's vision with cryptocurrency technology — but without bitcoin's energy-hungry mining. He recruited Chris Larsen, who had co-founded the online lender E-LOAN and then Prosper, bringing financial industry credibility and operational experience. In 2012, they launched OpenCoin, later renamed Ripple Labs, with Fugger's blessing to build on his concept.

The Ripple protocol they designed was technically elegant. Instead of proof-of-work mining, transactions would be validated by a consensus of trusted nodes. Settlement would take seconds, not minutes or hours. The network could handle 1,500 transactions per second versus bitcoin's seven. And crucially, it was designed to bridge currencies — dollars to euros to bitcoin to anything else — making it a protocol for the financial system rather than a replacement for it.

They pre-mined 100 billion XRP tokens at inception. Ripple Labs would hold the majority, using them to incentivize adoption and fund operations. This decision would haunt them for years. To bitcoin purists, pre-mining was sacrilege. Cryptocurrency was supposed to be decentralized, mined by anyone with computing power. Ripple looked like a company playing at crypto while maintaining corporate control.

McCaleb departed in 2013 amid disagreements about direction, later founding Stellar as a more decentralized alternative. But Ripple pressed forward with a bet that seemed counterintuitive in cryptocurrency circles: they would court the very banks that crypto was supposedly disrupting.

The pitch to financial institutions was compelling. Cross-border payments were a nightmare of correspondent banking relationships, SWIFT messages, and settlement delays. A transaction from the US to Thailand might pass through five intermediary banks, taking days and losing 6-7% to fees. Ripple offered near-instant settlement at a fraction of the cost. Banks could hold XRP as a bridge currency or use Ripple's technology without the token at all.

Initial traction came from smaller banks and remittance companies willing to experiment. Spain's Santander became an early adopter. American Express partnered with Ripple for business payments to the UK. MoneyGram, the remittance giant, signed on. Each partnership was trumpeted as validation. But adoption remained limited. Banks were interested in the technology, skeptical of the token.

The regulatory hammer fell slowly, then all at once. In December 2020, the Securities and Exchange Commission filed suit against Ripple Labs, Larsen, and current CEO Brad Garlinghouse. The charge: conducting an unregistered securities offering by selling XRP. If XRP was a security, not a currency, then Ripple had violated decades of securities law.

The lawsuit exposed Ripple's central contradiction. They claimed XRP was a decentralized cryptocurrency like bitcoin. But the SEC pointed out that Ripple Labs controlled the vast majority of tokens, funding operations by selling them. They marketed XRP's price appreciation. They were, the SEC argued, selling shares in a common enterprise — the textbook definition of a security.

The case should have killed Ripple. Bitcoin and Ethereum were arguably decentralized enough to avoid securities classification. But projects with corporate parents, pre-mined tokens, and ongoing sales? The SEC seemed to be drawing a line, and Ripple was on the wrong side.

XRP's price crashed. Exchanges delisted the token. Banking partners quietly distanced themselves. The company had raised hundreds of millions but now faced existential legal risk. Larsen and Garlinghouse faced potential personal liability. The smart play was settlement, accepting restrictions, moving on.

Instead, Ripple fought. They spent over $200 million on legal fees, hired former government officials, and mounted an aggressive defense. The bet was that proving XRP wasn't a security would unlock massive value — both for the company's holdings and for the broader crypto industry watching nervously.

In July 2023, Judge Analisa Torres delivered a split decision that felt like victory. She ruled that XRP sales on public exchanges were not securities offerings because purchasers had no reasonable expectation of profit from Ripple's efforts. But institutional sales directly from Ripple to sophisticated investors were securities. It was a nuanced ruling in a space used to binary outcomes.

Ripple declared victory. XRP's price doubled. But the ruling solved less than it seemed. Institutional sales were still restricted. The SEC appealed. Banking adoption remained limited. The company had survived but not yet thrived.

By 2024, Ripple had evolved into something unexpected: a blockchain infrastructure company that happened to hold enormous amounts of a cryptocurrency. They offered enterprise blockchain solutions. They invested in crypto startups. They lobbied aggressively for favorable regulation. XRP existed, sometimes useful as a bridge asset, often just there — a legacy of the original vision, neither dead nor fully alive.

The company endures not because it achieved its original vision of revolutionizing cross-border payments through XRP, but because it had the resources to outlast a regulatory assault and the flexibility to become something else. Ripple proved that a well-funded crypto project could survive by adaptation, political engagement, and sheer attrition. Whether that survival leads to eventual triumph or just延长的 irrelevance remains the question XRP holders keep asking.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Grab
entries.descriptor: From ride-hailing clone to Southeast Asian super-app empire
entries.content:
# Grab

***From ride-hailing clone to Southeast Asian super-app empire***

> **Years:** 2012–present

In 2012, Anthony Tan was a second-generation Malaysian businessman studying at Harvard Business School when he noticed something odd about his family's automotive distribution empire back home. His relatives owned cars, but getting around Kuala Lumpur remained a nightmare. Taxis were unreliable, often refused short trips, and operated like cartels. Meanwhile, halfway around the world, a startup called Uber was beginning to reimagine urban transportation.

Tan's Harvard thesis became a business plan. He pitched the idea of "MyTeksi" — essentially Uber for Malaysia — to his classmates, his professors, and anyone who would listen. Most were skeptical. Southeast Asia seemed too fragmented, too poor, too cash-dependent for a smartphone-based ride-hailing service. But Tan had an advantage: his family's automotive connections and his co-founder Tan Hooi Ling, a Harvard classmate who understood technology. In June 2012, they launched MyTeksi in Malaysia with money from Tan's family and the conviction that what worked in San Francisco could work in Kuala Lumpur.

The early bet paid off faster than anyone expected. Drivers loved having a reliable way to find passengers. Passengers loved knowing a car would actually show up. Within months, MyTeksi was processing thousands of rides daily. Tan and Hooi Ling expanded to Singapore, the Philippines, Thailand. They weren't inventing the model — they were adapting it to terrain Uber didn't yet understand. They integrated cash payments when Uber insisted on credit cards. They recruited motorcycle taxis when cars made no sense in Bangkok traffic. They negotiated with regulators while Uber picked fights.

In 2014, venture capital arrived. SoftBank, Didi Chuxing, and other investors poured money into the renamed "GrabTaxi," valuing the company at hundreds of millions. The message was clear: Southeast Asia's ride-hailing market was up for grabs, and whoever dominated it would control urban mobility across 600 million people. Uber took notice and launched aggressively across the region. For three years, Grab and Uber burned cash in a brutal subsidy war, paying drivers and discounting rides to win market share city by city.

Grab had advantages Uber couldn't replicate. The founders spoke local languages, understood local regulations, and knew which officials mattered in Jakarta versus Manila. They hired local teams who knew that Indonesia required cash, that Thailand required motorcycle taxis, that Singapore required premium service. Uber optimized globally; Grab adapted locally. By 2016, Grab was winning on every metric that mattered: rides, drivers, user growth.

But the subsidy war was unsustainable. Both companies were losing hundreds of millions annually. Uber was simultaneously fighting Didi in China, Ola in India, and established taxi lobbies everywhere. In 2017, Uber sold its China operations to Didi in exchange for equity. The message to Southeast Asia was coming: Uber wanted out of expensive battles it couldn't win.

In March 2018, Uber surrendered. It sold its Southeast Asian operations to Grab for 27.5% equity in the combined company. Overnight, Grab went from battling for market share to owning it — dominant in ride-hailing across eight countries, with minimal competition. The victory was total. Tan had taken a Harvard thesis and turned it into a regional monopoly in six years.

But Grab's ambitions extended beyond rides. Even before acquiring Uber's operations, the company was transforming into a super-app — the WeChat or Alipay of Southeast Asia. GrabPay became a digital wallet. GrabFood delivered meals. GrabExpress couriered packages. The logic was elegant: once you controlled urban mobility, you controlled the daily transaction layer of hundreds of millions of lives. Drivers became delivery workers. Cars became distribution networks. The app became infrastructure.

The super-app strategy unlocked new revenue streams but created new challenges. Grab entered businesses — food delivery, payments, lending — where established players fought back. Regulators grew concerned about monopoly power. The company faced antitrust investigations in multiple countries. Profitability remained elusive as expansion costs mounted. SoftBank kept funding, but each round came with higher expectations and more pressure to justify the valuations.

In 2021, Grab went public via SPAC at a $40 billion valuation — the largest-ever SPAC deal at the time. The stock soared briefly, then collapsed as investors realized the company was nowhere near profitable and the post-pandemic mobility market looked different than projected. By 2022, shares had fallen 75% from their peak. The super-app was real, used by millions daily, but the path to profitability remained unclear.

Today, Grab operates across 500 cities in eight countries. It processes billions in transactions annually across ride-hailing, food delivery, payments, and financial services. The founders succeeded beyond their Harvard thesis: they built the closest thing Southeast Asia has to a digital infrastructure layer. But the outcome remains unfinished. The company that won the ride-hailing war now fights different battles — against unprofitability, against super-app competitors like GoTo in Indonesia, against the gravitational pull of Western investors who expected faster returns.

What looked like total victory in 2018 turned out to be the beginning of a longer, harder path. Grab proved that cloning Silicon Valley's best ideas and adapting them to Southeast Asia could build an empire. Whether that empire ever justifies its cost is still an open question.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Careem
entries.section: The Middle East's Uber, sold to Uber
entries.descriptor: The ride-hailing company that made Uber pay $3.1 billion to win the Middle East
entries.content:
# Careem

***The ride-hailing company that made Uber pay $3.1 billion to win the Middle East***

> **Years:** 2012-2019 (acquired), 2019-present (as Uber subsidiary)

In July 2012, two management consultants in Dubai had a problem that felt trivial but was maddeningly persistent: getting a reliable ride. Mudassir Sheikha and Magnus Olsson, both working at McKinsey, found themselves constantly frustrated by the region's fragmented taxi system. Drivers refused short trips. Dispatchers promised cars that never came. Credit cards weren't accepted. For a region building gleaming skyscrapers and positioning itself as a global business hub, the basic infrastructure of urban mobility was broken.

They started Careem — the name comes from the Arabic word for "generous" or "noble" — not to disrupt an industry but to solve an immediate, personal inconvenience. The initial concept was simple: a call center that would dispatch professional drivers in nice cars. Premium service for business travelers who needed reliability. They launched with a call center, a basic booking system, and a fleet of contracted drivers in Dubai. No app at first. Just phones and spreadsheets.

What they discovered was unexpected. The demand wasn't just from expatriate consultants. It came from everyone. Women who couldn't drive in Saudi Arabia. Families without cars. Young professionals. The middle class across the Middle East had been underserved by transportation for decades, and even a basic booking system felt revolutionary. Within months, they had built an app. Within a year, they were in multiple cities.

The business model seemed straightforward — replicate Uber's playbook in a region Uber hadn't yet prioritized — but the execution required constant adaptation. The Middle East wasn't a single market but a collection of distinct ones, each with different regulations, cultural norms, and infrastructure challenges. In Saudi Arabia, they had to navigate restrictions on women's mobility before the driving ban was lifted. In Egypt, cash payments dominated because credit card penetration was low. In Pakistan, street addresses barely existed; riders would describe landmarks and neighborhoods instead of entering pins on a map.

Careem built for these conditions rather than trying to change them. They accepted cash when Uber insisted on cards. They allowed booking for others — a crucial feature in cultures where men might book rides for female family members. They hired local operations teams who understood not just the language but the social dynamics of each market. When Uber arrived in force in 2013 and 2014, Careem didn't try to out-spend them. They tried to out-local them.

The result was a grinding, city-by-city battle for market share. Uber had effectively infinite capital from Silicon Valley investors. Careem raised aggressively from regional investors — the Saudi sovereign wealth fund, Rakuten, Didi Chuxing — but never matched Uber's war chest. Both companies burned hundreds of millions of dollars on driver incentives and rider discounts. The economics were brutal. Neither company made money. Both were building networks in the hope that one would blink first.

What kept Careem alive was credibility. Drivers trusted them more. Riders perceived them as understanding local needs better. Regulators saw them as partners rather than foreign disruptors. In markets like Egypt and Pakistan, this mattered enormously. But trust doesn't pay the bills, and by 2017, Careem was facing a question that haunts every well-funded startup in a cash-burning war: how long can we keep going?

The answer came in an unexpected form. Rather than fight to the death, Uber made an offer. In March 2019, they announced a $3.1 billion acquisition — the largest tech exit in the Middle East's history. The structure was unusual: Careem would continue to operate under its own brand, with Sheikha staying on as CEO. Uber wasn't buying Careem to shut it down. They were buying it because seven years of competition had proven that winning the Middle East required Careem's approach, not Uber's.

The acquisition was simultaneously a triumph and a capitulation. Careem had built a business covering 100+ cities across 15 countries, with millions of riders and hundreds of thousands of drivers. They had created thousands of jobs and changed how people moved through cities where public transit was sparse and car ownership was expensive. They had proven that a regional champion could compete with a global giant, at least long enough to force a deal.

But they had also proven that in a cash-burning, winner-take-all market, the global giant usually wins — either by outlasting you or by buying you. The $3.1 billion payout was real. It created wealth for founders, employees, and investors. But the independent path ended. Careem didn't IPO. They didn't build a standalone public company. They became a subsidiary.

Since the acquisition, Careem has expanded beyond ride-hailing into payments, food delivery, and quick commerce, operating increasingly as Uber's experimental arm for emerging markets. The brand remains. The team remains. But the story of Careem is the story of a company that fought the right fight, built the right product, and then negotiated the best exit they could when the capital war became unsustainable. They made Uber pay a premium to control the Middle East. That was victory enough.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Pebble
entries.section: The smartwatch that came too early
entries.descriptor: The smartwatch before anyone wanted smartwatches
entries.content:
# Pebble

***The smartwatch before anyone wanted smartwatches***

> **Years:** 2012-2016

Eric Migicovsky had been trying to make a smartwatch since 2008, back when that meant hacking together a BlackBerry dev kit and strapping it to your wrist. He was a student at the University of Waterloo, cycling through the Canadian winter, irritated that checking his phone meant taking off his gloves. By 2011, his company Allerta had built InPulse, a watch that showed BlackBerry notifications. It sold maybe a thousand units. BlackBerry declined to acquire them. The company was nearly dead.

Then the iPhone happened. Not the launch—the dominance. By 2012, BlackBerry was collapsing and iOS was ascendant. Migicovsky saw the opening: rebuild the watch for iPhone users. But there was no money. Y Combinator rejected them twice. Traditional VCs wouldn't touch hardware. In April 2012, Migicovsky uploaded a Kickstarter campaign with a goal of $100,000. He expected it might fail.

It raised $10.3 million in 37 days—the most successful Kickstarter campaign ever at that point. Sixty-nine thousand people pre-ordered a watch that didn't exist yet. The pitch was simple: your phone stays in your pocket, notifications appear on your wrist, the battery lasts a week. The demo video showed Migicovsky on his bike, glancing at his wrist. It felt like the future, delivered.

Manufacturing nearly killed them. Pebble had been five people building prototypes in a Palo Alto garage. Now they owed sixty-nine thousand watches to strangers on the internet. They found a factory in Taiwan, flew engineers back and forth, discovered that injection molding is unforgiving and supply chains are brutal. The first batch had screen tearing issues. The second batch shipped six months late. Backers grew furious. Migicovsky kept posting updates, kept apologizing, kept shipping. By September 2013, they had delivered.

And people loved it. The hardware was chunky, the screen was e-paper, the interface was primitive. But it worked. Cyclists, runners, people in meetings—anyone who wanted to triage notifications without pulling out their phone. App developers started building for it. Pebble released an SDK. Thousands of watch faces appeared. The community felt like early iPhone, scrappy and inventive.

In 2013, Pebble raised $15 million in Series A funding. The narrative was irresistible: Kickstarter success story scales into real company. They hired, expanded, built Pebble Steel—a sleeker version in metal. Then rumors started circulating that Apple was building a watch.

When Apple announced the Apple Watch in September 2014, Pebble's path forked. They could try to compete directly—color screens, touch interfaces, apps—or double down on what made them different: week-long battery, always-on display, simplicity. They chose both. Pebble Time launched on Kickstarter in February 2015, raising $20.3 million, breaking their own record. Color e-paper screen, thinner design, new timeline interface. It looked like validation.

But the Apple Watch launched in April 2015, and the oxygen left the room. Pebble Time shipped to backers in May—a month later than planned. Reviews were kind but tepid. The Apple Watch was slow, expensive, needed daily charging, but it was an Apple product. Retail chains cleared shelf space. Best Buy, Target, AT&T stores—all carried Apple. Pebble had online sales and scattered boutique retailers.

Sales cratered. Pebble had grown to 180 employees expecting millions in revenue. Instead, they sold hundreds of thousands. Migicovsky cut 25% of staff in March 2016. They launched Pebble 2 and Pebble Time 2 on Kickstarter again—raising $12.8 million—but now Kickstarter felt like a lifeline, not a launchpad. The campaigns succeeded; the business was failing.
