Slack owned employee communication. Together, they could own more of the enterprise. But the price revealed something else: independent Slack couldn't outspend Microsoft indefinitely.

Butterfield stayed to run Slack inside Salesforce. The product continued growing, now bundled with Salesforce's offerings. The startup that had accidentally spawned from a failed game, that had grown faster than almost any enterprise company in history, that had genuinely changed how millions of people worked, had found its exit.

The irony was perfect. Butterfield had built two companies from the ruins of games. Flickr had sold for $25 million and slowly withered inside Yahoo. Slack sold for a thousand times more and became part of something larger. Both times, he had created something people loved by trying to build something else.

The pivot had worked. The question was whether the ending was triumph or escape.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Ring
entries.descriptor: The doorbell camera that survived death, a pivot, and Amazon
entries.content:
# Ring

***The doorbell camera that survived death, a pivot, and Amazon***

> **Years:** 2013-2018

Jamie Siminoff stood in his garage in 2011, frustrated. He was building a WiFi-enabled doorbell because he kept missing package deliveries while working. The device would send video to his phone when someone rang. Simple enough. What he didn't know was that this frustration would lead him through a very public rejection, a complete rebrand, and ultimately a sale that would make him a symbol of vindication.

The original product was called DoorBot. Siminoff, a serial entrepreneur who'd sold his previous company, launched it in 2013 after raising money on Christie Street, a crowdfunding platform he himself had co-founded. The irony wasn't lost on him. DoorBot worked — a camera, a speaker, motion detection, cloud recording — but it looked like what it was: a first-generation hardware product built by a software entrepreneur. Bulky. Utilitarian. The kind of thing early adopters forgive but mainstream consumers ignore.

In late 2013, Siminoff did what founders of promising-but-struggling hardware startups do: he applied for Shark Tank. The appearance aired in November 2013. He asked for $700,000 for 10% equity. The Sharks circled. Kevin O'Leary called it "a worthless product." The segment ended with all five Sharks declining to invest. The clip would later be replayed millions of times as a lesson in how wrong investors can be.

But in the moment, it was just rejection on national television. Except for one thing: the exposure worked. DoorBot's website crashed from traffic. Orders flooded in. The product was getting attention even if the Sharks didn't bite. Siminoff realized he had something, but he also realized DoorBot as a name and brand wasn't going to carry it far enough. The product was good. The packaging was wrong.

In 2014, he rebranded. DoorBot became Ring. Same product, better name, cleaner design. The shift was more than cosmetic. Ring positioned itself not as a doorbell replacement but as the first line of defense for your home. Security, not convenience. The market was larger, the value proposition sharper, the customer's willingness to pay higher. This wasn't about missed packages anymore. It was about knowing who's at your door, recording evidence, deterring theft.

The rebrand worked. Ring began growing quickly. Siminoff raised a Series A from DFJ and other investors. Then more rounds followed. Ring expanded the product line: spotlight cameras, floodlight cameras, stick-up cameras, alarm systems. Each product integrated with the Ring app and the growing network of Ring users. The company wasn't just selling cameras; it was building an ecosystem.

The ecosystem had network effects. Ring launched the Neighbors app, which let users share videos of suspicious activity in their area. A theft caught on one Ring camera could alert an entire neighborhood. Police departments began partnering with Ring, requesting footage for investigations. Privacy advocates raised alarms, but the value proposition for homeowners was clear: your camera protects you, but the network protects everyone.

By 2016, Ring had raised over $200 million. Revenue was accelerating. The company was on track to hit $100 million in sales. But hardware is brutal. Margins are thin. Customer acquisition is expensive. Siminoff was burning cash to grow, betting that scale would eventually deliver profitability. The math wasn't guaranteed.

Amazon noticed. In early 2018, Amazon acquired Ring for a reported $1 billion. The deal made sense for both sides. Ring got Amazon's resources, distribution, and integration with Alexa. Amazon got a foothold in home security, a category it didn't dominate, and access to a network of devices and data that fit its ambient computing vision. Siminoff stayed on to run Ring as a subsidiary.

The acquisition made Siminoff a symbol. The Shark Tank rejection became retroactive proof of investor blindness. The clip was re-edited, shared, memed. Siminoff appeared on shows to talk about perseverance. The narrative was irresistible: rejected on TV, vindicated by billion-dollar exit. But the real story was less about vindication and more about iteration. DoorBot failed because it was positioned wrong. Ring succeeded because Siminoff listened, rebranded, expanded, and found the bigger market.

After the acquisition, Ring's growth accelerated further. Amazon integrated Ring devices across its ecosystem. By 2020, Ring was estimated to control more than 40% of the smart doorbell market. Police partnerships expanded. Privacy concerns grew louder. Ring became a case study in surveillance capitalism, neighborhood watch culture, and the tradeoffs between security and privacy.

For Siminoff, the path from garage frustration to billion-dollar exit took seven years. It required a public failure, a rebrand, a pivot from convenience to security, and the luck of timing — entering a market just as home automation was becoming mainstream and privacy concerns hadn't yet hardened into regulation. The doorbell camera wasn't a new idea, but Ring found the frame that made it essential. That's the path that worked.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Juicero
entries.descriptor: The $700 million monument to solving problems that didn't exist
entries.content:
# Juicero

***The $700 million monument to solving problems that didn't exist***

> **Years:** 2013-2017

In 2013, Doug Evans had a vision. A raw food enthusiast who'd built and sold an organic juice company, Evans believed the future of health was fresh-pressed juice in every home. Not juice from a bottle. Not juice from concentrate. Juice pressed from whole fruits and vegetables, extracted by thousands of pounds of force, consumed within moments of pressing. The kind of juice that cost $10 at boutique juice bars, now available at the touch of a button in your kitchen.

The problem, as Evans saw it, was that home juicers were terrible. They oxidized nutrients. They left pulp. They required cleaning. They produced inferior juice. What the world needed was a completely new approach: a machine with the pressing power of commercial juicers, paired with pre-portioned produce packs that eliminated prep work, waste, and variability. A Keurig for juice, but vastly more sophisticated.

Evans was charismatic and convinced. He spoke about Juicero the way Steve Jobs spoke about the iPhone—as inevitable, transformative, necessary. He attracted top talent from Apple and Tesla. He raised $3 million in seed funding, then $70 million in Series A and B rounds from some of Silicon Valley's most respected investors: Kleiner Perkins, Google Ventures, Campbell's Soup. By 2016, Juicero had raised $120 million.

The machine they built was genuinely impressive as an engineering artifact. It contained custom circuit boards, sensors, a QR code scanner, Wi-Fi connectivity, and a pressing mechanism that exerted 8,000 pounds of force. The industrial design was sleek. The tolerances were tight. One early prototype cost $750,000 to build. The first production units, priced at $699, were still sold at a loss.

The produce packs—what Juicero called "Produce Packs"—were equally sophisticated. Pre-chopped fruits and vegetables sealed in proprietary bags with QR codes that connected to the cloud. The machine would scan the code, verify the pack wasn't expired, then press the bag between two metal plates, extracting juice through a built-in filter. The packs cost $5-7 each and produced one 8-ounce glass of juice. They required cold chain logistics and had a week-long shelf life.

In March 2016, Juicero launched in three test markets. The company had lined up retail partnerships and built out subscription infrastructure. The press was initially positive—here was a company trying to revolutionize a category, backed by serious investors, with a founder who spoke passionately about transforming health. Early customers were Silicon Valley true believers, the kind who appreciated precision engineering and were willing to pay premium prices for optimized nutrition.

But the economics were punishing. Each machine cost more to build than the retail price. The produce packs required specialized facilities, cold storage, and rapid distribution. Juicero was manufacturing and distributing fresh produce at scale—essentially running a subset of the grocery business with none of the efficiencies. The company needed massive volume to reach profitability.

The volume never came. At $699 plus $5-7 per serving, Juicero was competing with $10 juice bars, where consumers could see the produce being pressed and drink immediately without a $699 capital outlay. It was competing with $50 home juicers, despite producing a different kind of juice through a vastly more complex process that most consumers couldn't perceive or value. It was competing with $4 cold-pressed juice bottles at Whole Foods.

By April 2017, facing slow sales, Juicero dropped the machine price to $399. It didn't help. The company was burning through capital. Then, in April 2017, Bloomberg published a video demonstration that became viral. Reporters showed that you could squeeze the produce packs by hand—without the $400 machine—and get roughly the same amount of juice in roughly the same time.

The video was devastating. It turned Juicero from an ambitious startup into a punchline overnight. Late-night comedians mocked it. Twitter was merciless. The narrative flipped completely: this wasn't sophisticated engineering solving a real problem; this was over-engineered absurdity, Silicon Valley hubris in physical form, a monument to venture capital's disconnect from consumer reality.

Juicero's defense was technically correct. The machine did extract slightly more juice with less effort and more consistency than hand-squeezing. The QR code scanning prevented using expired packs. The connected features enabled remote troubleshooting. But these arguments were beside the point. The video had revealed the fundamental problem: even if the machine did something marginally better, it was solving a problem—drinking cold-pressed juice at home—that most consumers didn't have or value at that price point.

The company tried to recover. They emphasized health benefits, convenience, the quality of the produce sourcing. They highlighted sustainability efforts. Nothing worked. Consumer perception had crystallized around those hands squeezing the bag. Even supporters couldn't escape the question: why does this need to exist?

Four months later, in September 2017, Juicero shut down. They offered refunds to customers. The company had raised $120 million and spent nearly all of it. Evans, in his final message to customers, maintained that Juicero had been a "remarkable journey" and expressed pride in the innovation.

The investors, collectively, had funded what became a cautionary tale. Not a cautionary tale about technology—the engineering was real—but about market need. Juicero had solved the problem of inconsistent home juicing, but most consumers either didn't juice at home, didn't see inconsistency as a problem worth solving, or were willing to accept inconsistency at a much lower price point. The company had built sophisticated supply chain and hardware solutions for a market that barely existed at the required price point, then scaled before discovering whether anyone wanted it badly enough to sustain the business model.

The produce packs that required a machine to press became the perfect metaphor for product-market misfit: solving a problem that was easier solved without the solution.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Nikola
entries.descriptor: The hydrogen hype that fooled Wall Street
entries.content:
# Nikola

***The hydrogen hype that fooled Wall Street***

> **Years:** 2014-2023

Trevor Milton grew up in Utah, dropped out of college, and bounced through sales jobs before founding his first company — dHybrid Systems — which sold natural gas storage systems for trucks. He sold it in 2014 and walked away with enough money and confidence to try something bigger. Much bigger. He wanted to build the Tesla of heavy trucks, except better than Tesla, cleaner than Tesla, using hydrogen instead of batteries.

In 2014, he founded Nikola Motor Company, named after Nikola Tesla, the inventor Elon Musk's company was also named after. The symmetry was intentional. Milton positioned Nikola as the anti-Tesla — a company that would dominate the one market Tesla couldn't crack: long-haul trucking. Battery-electric semis, Milton argued, would never work for cross-country freight. Too heavy, too slow to charge, too limited in range. Hydrogen fuel cells were the answer. Fill up in minutes, drive 500 miles, zero emissions. He promised a future where Nikola would build both the trucks and the fueling infrastructure — a vertically integrated hydrogen ecosystem.

The first truck design, the Nikola One, was unveiled in 2016 at a Salt Lake City event that felt more like a Steve Jobs keynote than a trucking conference. Sleek renders, dramatic lighting, Milton on stage in jeans and a black shirt, promising a revolution. He showed a prototype. It looked real. Fleet operators took reservations. The company claimed billions in pre-orders. Milton's pitch was magnetic: early Tesla investor without the Tesla valuation, first-mover in hydrogen, savior of the climate and the trucking industry.

But Nikola had no revenue. It had no production facility. It had barely tested the technology. What it had was Milton's charisma, slick marketing, and a vision ambitious enough to suspend disbelief.

The company went public in June 2020 via a SPAC merger — the hot financial instrument of that moment — and the market went wild. Nikola's valuation hit $28 billion within days, briefly surpassing Ford. It had delivered zero trucks. Milton became a billionaire on paper. Institutional investors piled in. General Motors announced a $2 billion partnership. Nikola seemed to be the next Tesla, the next SpaceX, the next Musk-scale disruption.

Then the short-seller report dropped.

In September 2020, Hindenburg Research published "Nikola: How to Parlay an Ocean of Lies into a Partnership with the Largest Auto OEM in America." It was devastating. Hindenburg accused Milton of lying about nearly everything: the technology, the partnerships, the pre-orders, even basic demonstrations. The most damning claim involved a 2018 promotional video showing the Nikola One driving down a highway. Hindenburg alleged the truck wasn't driving — it was rolling downhill. Nikola had towed it to the top of a slope and filmed it coasting.

Nikola issued a rebuttal that didn't help. The company admitted the truck in the video was not self-propelled but insisted it had never claimed otherwise. "In motion" was not the same as "under its own power," they argued. Investors weren't convinced. The stock collapsed. GM renegotiated its partnership into something much smaller. Within two weeks, Milton resigned as executive chairman. He denied all fraud allegations.

Federal prosecutors disagreed. In July 2021, Milton was indicted on three counts of fraud. The indictment painted a picture of systematic deception: false claims about production timelines, fake orders from customers, fabricated demonstrations, misleading statements to investors. The case went to trial in 2022. Prosecutors argued Milton had knowingly lied to inflate the stock price and enrich himself. Milton's defense claimed he was an optimistic entrepreneur, not a criminal — that startup founders exaggerate, and that's not fraud.

In October 2022, a jury convicted him on three counts. He was acquitted on one. In December 2023, he was sentenced to four years in prison.

Meanwhile, Nikola the company continued without him, though barely. It pivoted away from hydrogen and toward battery-electric trucks — the very technology Milton had dismissed. It began delivering small numbers of trucks to customers, real trucks this time, but the company burned cash and struggled to scale. The grand vision of a hydrogen network never materialized. By 2023, Nikola was a penny stock, valued at a fraction of its peak, still not profitable, still not clear it would survive.

The Nikola story became a parable. It had everything: a charismatic founder, a climate-friendly narrative, buzzy technology, institutional validation, SPAC-era exuberance, and then the unraveling. It revealed how much the market wanted to believe in the next Tesla, how powerful storytelling could substitute for substance, and how long a company could survive on narrative alone — until it couldn't.

Milton went to prison. Nikola limped on. The hydrogen revolution in trucking never came. The path that seemed to lead toward Tesla-scale dominance led instead to disgrace, indictment, and collapse — a reminder that hype and hope are not a business model.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Jet.com
entries.descriptor: The price war that became an exit
entries.content:
# Jet.com

***The price war that became an exit***

> **Years:** 2014-2018

Marc Lore had already sold one company to Amazon. In 2010, Quidsi — the parent company of Diapers.com — went to Jeff Bezos for $545 million after Amazon had waged a brutal price war against them. Lore stayed through the earnout, watched Amazon's machine up close, then left. He spent his non-compete period thinking about what he'd learned. The conclusion: you could beat Amazon, but only by being more Amazon than Amazon.

Jet.com launched in July 2015 with the most audacious retail proposition in years. The pitch was simple: real-time dynamic pricing that dropped with every item you added to your cart. Buying more things together? Lower prices. Waiving free returns? Lower prices. Paying with debit instead of credit? Lower prices. The algorithm would show you exactly how much you'd save by making different choices. It was Amazon's efficiency engine turned into a customer-facing game.

The model made sense if you squinted. Amazon had trained customers to expect low prices and free shipping, but those conveniences cost money. Jet would expose the economics and let customers optimize. Buy a case instead of a single unit. Let the system route from a closer warehouse. Accept a slightly longer delivery window. Each choice shaved pennies, and pennies accumulated.

Lore raised $80 million before launch — then another $140 million four months later. The investors included Bain Capital, Accel, and Goldman Sachs. The story was compelling: Lore knew Amazon's playbook, had already built a billion-dollar e-commerce company, and was attacking the giant's core business with better unit economics. Jet claimed it could be 10-15% cheaper than Amazon on identical baskets while maintaining healthier margins.

The launch was massive. Jet spent heavily on customer acquisition — $20 million in the first few months alone. The company offered a $50 membership fee (later dropped) and discounts deep enough that early adopters called it "too good to be true." They were right to be skeptical. Jet was losing money on every order, burning through venture capital to buy market share. But Lore had seen this playbook work. Quidsi had done the same thing. So had Amazon itself in the early days.

The problem was scale and speed. Amazon had fifteen years and infinite capital. Jet needed to reach critical mass before the money ran out. By early 2016, Jet had raised $500 million total — a staggering sum — but was burning $20-25 million per month. The company had maybe eighteen months of runway, and Amazon was watching. More worryingly, the "smart cart" that was supposed to drive Jet's advantage wasn't creating the behavior change the model required. Customers liked the discounts but didn't optimize their carts the way Jet needed them to. They wanted Amazon-level convenience at Jet-level prices, which was economically impossible.

Walmart was also watching. Doug McMillon had become CEO in 2014 with a mandate to fix Walmart's catastrophic digital strategy. Walmart.com was growing but still a rounding error against Amazon. The company needed to move faster, buy better technology, and convince Silicon Valley that Walmart was serious about e-commerce. Jet looked like a solution: proven leadership, modern tech stack, and a direct assault on Amazon's model.

In August 2016, Walmart acquired Jet for $3.3 billion in cash and stock. It was Walmart's largest-ever acquisition and the biggest e-commerce deal in years. Lore would run Walmart's entire U.S. e-commerce operation, and Jet would become the laboratory for Walmart's digital ambitions. The price seemed insane — Jet was barely a year old, unprofitable, and hadn't proven its model worked. But Walmart wasn't buying revenue; it was buying talent, technology, and speed.

The deal made Lore the most successful repeat founder in e-commerce. He'd now sold two companies for nearly $4 billion combined, both to giants trying to compete with Amazon. The investors who'd bet on him eighteen months earlier had just made spectacular returns. For a moment, Jet looked like vindication of the blitzscaling model: raise huge rounds, grow impossibly fast, and exit before unit economics matter.

But Jet's path after acquisition revealed the problem with the strategy. Walmart tried to scale Jet as a standalone brand targeting urban millennials — the demographic Walmart struggled to reach. The dynamic pricing remained, but it never drove the behavior change required. Customers used Jet for deals but didn't change how they shopped. By 2018, Walmart quietly began integrating Jet into Walmart.com. By 2020, Jet.com was effectively shut down, its technology and team absorbed into the parent company.

Lore stayed until 2021, overseeing Walmart's successful e-commerce transformation. Walmart's online sales grew from $13 billion when he joined to over $40 billion when he left. He built the infrastructure, bought the companies (Bonobos, ModCloth, Eloquii), and created a credible challenger to Amazon's retail dominance. But Jet itself — the brand, the model, the original vision — disappeared.

The company existed for six years and as an independent brand for barely two. It never proved its unit economics worked at scale. It never became the Amazon-killer Lore envisioned. But it achieved something more pragmatic: it was the key that unlocked Walmart's digital transformation and made Lore and his investors very rich. Whether that counts as success depends on which story you're telling.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Casper
entries.section: The mattress in a box that deflated
entries.descriptor: The mattress-in-a-box that rode the DTC wave to a $1.1 billion valuation, then discovered that disruption doesn't guarantee profits
entries.content:
# Casper

***The mattress-in-a-box that rode the DTC wave to a $1.1 billion valuation, then discovered that disruption doesn't guarantee profits***

> **Years:** 2014–present

In 2013, five friends sat in a New York apartment discussing a shared frustration: buying a mattress was terrible. The showrooms felt sleazy, the prices seemed arbitrary, the whole experience designed to confuse rather than inform. Philip Krim, Neil Parikh, T. Luke Sherwin, Jeff Chapin, and Gabe Flateman weren't mattress industry veterans. That was precisely the point.

They launched Casper in April 2014 with a single product: a $950 memory foam mattress that arrived compressed in a box. The pitch was elegant in its simplicity. One mattress, universal comfort, 100-night trial, free returns. No showrooms, no commissioned salespeople, no twelve models with meaningless names. Order online, sleep on it, decide.

The timing proved impeccable. Direct-to-consumer brands were having a moment. Warby Parker had shown that you could disrupt sleepy industries by cutting out middlemen and selling online. Venture capital was flooding into companies with similar playbooks. Casper's founders had studied these models carefully. They knew the formula: take a product that hadn't changed in decades, wrap it in millennial-friendly branding, compress the supply chain, and ride Facebook ads to growth.

The mattress-in-a-box concept wasn't entirely new — smaller companies had tried it — but Casper executed with unprecedented polish. The unboxing became an event. Customers filmed themselves cutting open the vacuum-sealed package, watching the mattress expand like a magic trick. Videos went viral. The company encouraged this, understanding that every shared unboxing was free advertising.

Revenue hit $100 million in less than two years. By 2015, Casper had raised $70 million from investors including Leonardo DiCaprio and Tobey Maguire. The Wall Street Journal called it "the Warby Parker of mattresses." The comparison seemed apt: both sold something traditionally purchased in physical stores, both promised better products at lower prices, both built brands that felt less like corporations and more like lifestyle choices.

But the mattress industry noticed. And unlike eyewear, mattresses had low barriers to entry. Within months, competitors emerged: Leesa, Tuft & Needle, Purple, Nectar, dozens more. Each promised the same basic value proposition — comfort, convenience, fair prices. Casper had created a category, but categories are hard to own.

The company responded by expanding. They added pillows, sheets, dog beds, a $1,200 mattress, a $3,500 mattress. They opened retail stores — the very thing they'd claimed to disrupt. In 2017, they launched a sleep-tracking device. The strategy seemed to be: become a sleep company, not just a mattress company. Own the entire nighttime routine.

Customer acquisition costs, meanwhile, climbed steadily. In the early days, Facebook ads were cheap and effective. By 2018, every DTC brand was competing for the same audience. Casper spent $300 million on marketing that year — nearly as much as its revenue. They were buying growth, and the unit economics were brutal.

The venture capital kept flowing anyway. In 2019, Casper raised $100 million at a $1.1 billion valuation. They filed to go public the following February, and the S-1 revealed the reality behind the growth story. The company had never turned a profit. It had lost $73 million in the first nine months of 2019 alone. Revenue growth was slowing. Customer acquisition costs were rising faster than revenue.

The IPO priced at $12 per share, well below the expected range, valuing the company at $476 million — less than half the previous private valuation. On the first day of trading, shares fell further. Investors weren't buying the story anymore. The math didn't work.

Then COVID-19 hit. In a twist no one predicted, lockdowns boosted mattress sales. People stuck at home decided to upgrade their sleep experience. Casper's revenue grew. But so did every competitor's. The pandemic revealed that Casper wasn't winning because of superior execution or sustainable advantages. It was riding category-wide trends.

Target acquired a minority stake in 2020 and began selling Casper products in stores. This was presented as expansion, but it looked like desperation — giving away margin to a retailer after spending years claiming retail was the problem. By 2021, the stock traded around $4 per share.

In November 2021, a private equity firm acquired Casper for $286 million — roughly a quarter of its peak valuation. The founders exited. Early employees who'd joined for equity found their shares worth a fraction of what they'd been promised. Late-stage investors took significant losses.

The company still exists, still sells mattresses, still has the clean website and the clever branding. But the story Casper told about itself — that you could build a massive, profitable business by disrupting distribution and branding — proved incomplete. They disrupted the buying experience. They built a recognizable brand. They grew fast. But they never solved the fundamental problem: mattresses are commodities, and in a commodity business, sustainable profits require either massive scale or meaningful differentiation. Casper achieved neither.

What they did achieve was proof of concept. They showed that people would buy mattresses online, that the category could be reimagined, that the old players were vulnerable. Every competitor that flooded in after them owed their existence to Casper's validation. They built the road everyone else drove on. That may matter more, in the end, than whether Casper itself survived the journey profitably.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: OpenAI
entries.descriptor: The nonprofit that became a capped-profit that became the most valuable AI company in the world
entries.content:
# OpenAI

***The nonprofit that became a capped-profit that became the most valuable AI company in the world***

> **Years:** 2015–present

---

In December 2015, Sam Altman and Elon Musk announced OpenAI as a nonprofit AI research lab with $1 billion in pledged funding. The stated mission: ensure artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity. The implicit mission: prevent Google DeepMind from monopolizing the path to AGI.

The founding team read like a who's who of deep learning: Ilya Sutskever from Google, Greg Brockman from Stripe, Wojciech Zaremba, John Schulman. They set up in San Francisco's Mission District and started publishing everything. Radical transparency was the point. If AGI was coming, its development should happen in the open, not behind corporate walls.

For three years, OpenAI published groundbreaking research and bled money. They made advances in reinforcement learning, trained AI to beat humans at Dota 2, and released clever demos. But publishing everything meant Google and others could incorporate their breakthroughs immediately. The nonprofit structure meant they couldn't offer competitive equity. Top researchers left for DeepMind's bigger paychecks and compute budgets. Musk departed the board in 2018, citing conflicts with Tesla's own AI work.

The existential problem: training cutting-edge AI required hundreds of millions in compute. OpenAI was spending $50 million annually with no revenue and a nonprofit structure that couldn't raise serious capital or properly compensate talent. They had conviction about the AGI path but no sustainable way to walk it.

In 2019, OpenAI created a new structure: a "capped-profit" company, OpenAI LP, controlled by the original nonprofit. Investors could earn returns up to 100x their investment, after which excess profits would flow to the nonprofit. It was a legal architecture that had never been attempted at this scale. Microsoft invested $1 billion for exclusive cloud computing rights and a minority stake.

The Microsoft capital let OpenAI do what mattered: scale. They licensed and built on GPT-2, the language model they'd developed but initially refused to release, claiming it was "too dangerous." When they did release it months later, the catastrophic misuse they'd warned about didn't materialize. The caution looked like marketing.

GPT-3 launched in 2020 as an API, not an open release. This was the full pivot: OpenAI was now a product company with a business model. Developers paid per token to access the model. The waiting list grew to hundreds of thousands. Y Combinator startups built entire companies on top of GPT-3. OpenAI's revenue reached tens of millions.

Internally, the company had become a pressure cooker. The original vision of open research collided with commercial reality. Safety teams clashed with capabilities teams over release decisions. The capped-profit structure meant Microsoft had extraordinary influence. Some early employees left, disillusioned. But the talent they were attracting now was different: engineers who wanted to ship products, not just publish papers.

In November 2022, they released ChatGPT as a research preview. It was GPT-3.5 with a conversational interface and reinforcement learning from human feedback to make it helpful and harder to misuse. The team expected modest interest. Instead, it became the fastest-growing consumer application in history, hitting 100 million users in two months.

The release scrambled the entire technology industry. Google declared a "code red" and rushed to release Bard. Microsoft invested another $10 billion in OpenAI and integrated GPT-4 into Bing. Every major tech company suddenly needed an AI strategy. OpenAI went from research lab to the center of the technology universe in ninety days.

ChatGPT Plus launched at $20/month. Enterprise contracts followed. By mid-2023, OpenAI's revenue run rate exceeded $1 billion. The valuation reached $29 billion, then $80 billion in secondary sales. The capped-profit structure that seemed like a compromise now looked like a masterstroke: investor returns were capped, but the caps were so high and distant that capital flowed freely.

Then in November 2023, the board fired Altman. The stated reason: he had not been "consistently candid" with the board. The real reasons remained opaque. Within 48 hours, most of OpenAI's 700+ employees signed a letter threatening to quit and follow Altman to Microsoft unless the board resigned. Investors revolted. The board capitulated. Altman returned. Several board members were replaced.

The crisis revealed the contradictions the capped-profit structure had papered over. The nonprofit board was supposed to ensure mission alignment, but it had become disconnected from the commercial entity it ostensibly controlled. Employees were loyal to Altman and their equity, not the nonprofit's mission. The AGI safety concerns that justified the original structure had been subordinated to growth.

OpenAI emerged from the crisis more conventional: a heavily funded, rapidly scaling company with founder-CEO control and a board that wouldn't interfere. The nonprofit governance structure remained, but its power was theoretical. What had started as an idealistic attempt to democratize AGI had become the industry's dominant player, with market power to match incumbents it was founded to counterbalance.

By 2024, OpenAI was valued at $150 billion. It had evolved from nonprofit to capped-profit to something approximating a traditional venture-backed company that happened to have a nonprofit parent. The mission language persisted, but the path was unmistakable: this was a company optimizing for commercial AI dominance, not a research collective ensuring AGI benefits everyone.

The outcome wasn't failure or success but transformation. OpenAI had solved the hardest problem in AI—not technical but structural: how to fund AGI research at scale. The answer required abandoning almost every principle it was founded on. Whether that made it the cautionary tale or the template, the verdict remained unwritten.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Revolut
entries.section: The challenger bank with sharp elbows
entries.descriptor: From fintech disruptor to regulated bank: the high-speed, high-pressure path to European dominance
entries.content:
# Revolut

***From fintech disruptor to regulated bank: the high-speed, high-pressure path to European dominance***

> **Years:** 2015-present

In 2015, Nikolay Storonsky was working at Credit Suisse, frustrated every time he traveled. Foreign exchange fees ate into his spending. Currency conversion was opaque, expensive, and controlled by banks that had no incentive to make it better. His co-founder, Vlad Yatsenko, came from Deutsche Bank's technology side. They both saw the same thing: retail banking was ripe for disruption, and mobile technology made it possible to rebuild financial services from scratch.

They started with a simple idea: a prepaid debit card that let you spend abroad at interbank exchange rates, no markup. Launch a mobile app, link it to a card, and let people hold multiple currencies in one account. The target was obvious—young Europeans who traveled frequently, lived across borders, and were comfortable managing money through their phones. The kind of customer who would trade the reassurance of a bank branch for better rates and a slicker interface.

Revolut launched in the UK in July 2015. Within months, they had tens of thousands of users. The product worked. The proposition was clear. But Storonsky wasn't building a travel money app. He was building a bank replacement, and he was building it fast.

The next eighteen months were a blur of feature releases. Stock trading. Cryptocurrency exchange. Budgeting tools. Vaults for savings. Insurance products. Revolut added functionality at a pace that made traditional banks look fossilized. Each feature served two purposes: deepen engagement, and expand the surface area of financial services Revolut could capture. The strategy was Amazon-like—land with a wedge product, then expand horizontally until you own the customer relationship.

Growth was ferocious. By 2017, Revolut had a million users. By 2018, two million. They expanded across Europe, launching in market after market with minimal friction because the core infrastructure was already built. Funding followed the user growth. Series B in 2017 at a $250 million valuation. Series C in 2018 at $1.7 billion. Suddenly Revolut was a unicorn, one of Europe's fastest-growing fintech companies, and Storonsky was being compared to Travis Kalanick—aggressive, relentless, willing to push boundaries.

The comparison wasn't accidental. Inside Revolut, the culture was intense. Employees worked long hours. Targets were aggressive. Performance reviews were blunt. Storonsky ran the company like a trading floor—high pressure, high churn, results above all. Some thrived. Many burned out. By 2019, reports surfaced of toxic work conditions, impossible expectations, and a revolving door of departures. Storonsky's response was unapologetic: this was what it took to build a company at this speed.

But speed created other problems. In 2019, compliance issues surfaced. Revolut had scaled so fast that its anti-money laundering systems struggled to keep pace. Regulators raised concerns. Media scrutiny intensified. The narrative shifted from scrappy disruptor to reckless growth machine. Storonsky had always operated in the grey zones of regulation—launching features before seeking full approval, testing boundaries, moving faster than regulators could respond. It worked until it didn't.

The wake-up call came in the form of banking licenses. Revolut had operated under e-money licenses, which allowed payment services but not full banking capabilities. To truly compete with incumbents, they needed proper banking licenses in key markets. That meant submitting to regulatory oversight, building compliance infrastructure, and slowing down. It was antithetical to everything that had made Revolut successful.

The UK banking license took three years. Revolut applied in 2018 and received conditional approval in 2021. During that time, they rebuilt their compliance systems, hired hundreds of risk and regulatory staff, and accepted that the wild west phase was over. They secured a European banking license in Lithuania in 2018, which helped, but the UK license was symbolic—proof they could operate within the system.

Meanwhile, COVID hit. For a company built on international spending, a global pandemic that grounded travelers should have been catastrophic. Instead, Revolut pivoted. They pushed savings products, stock trading, and cryptocurrency harder. Stuck-at-home users started trading GameStop and Dogecoin through Revolut. The app became a gateway to retail investment mania. User numbers kept climbing.

By 2021, Revolut had 15 million users. Series E funding valued the company at $33 billion, making it the UK's most valuable fintech startup. They expanded into the US, Japan, and Australia. They added business accounts, freelancer tools, and salary advance features. The original vision—a bank replacement—was becoming real.

But the path had costs. Employee churn remained high. Regulatory scrutiny never fully subsided. Profitability was elusive—the company burned cash on customer acquisition, feature development, and geographic expansion. Storonsky remained convinced that scale was everything, that once Revolut reached critical mass, network effects and cross-selling would generate sustainable margins.

In 2024, Revolut finally turned a profit. Annual revenue crossed $2 billion. User numbers exceeded 40 million. The company was no longer a startup but a financial institution—regulated, complex, and operationally mature. The wild early days were gone. What remained was a company that had successfully disrupted retail banking in Europe, built a product millions trusted with their money, and forced incumbents to modernize or die.

Storonsky is still CEO. The company is still private, though IPO rumors circulate constantly. The path from frustrated trader to banking kingpin took a decade of relentless execution, regulatory battles, and cultural controversy. Revolut proved that banking was vulnerable to disruption. Whether it proved that disruption could be done sustainably remains an open question.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Pinduoduo
entries.descriptor: The social commerce insurgent that became China's Walmart
entries.content:
# Pinduoduo

***The social commerce insurgent that became China's Walmart***

> **Years:** 2015-present

---

In 2015, Colin Huang stood at an unusual vantage point. He had studied at Zhejiang University, then Wisconsin and Stanford. He had worked at Google in its early days, interned under the company's legendary team. He had founded and sold an e-commerce company. He understood both American technology and Chinese consumer behavior. And he saw something the giants missed.

Alibaba and JD.com dominated Chinese e-commerce. They had conquered the cities, built the logistics, trained hundreds of millions to shop online. The conventional wisdom said the game was over. New entrants would compete at the margins — luxury, imports, niche categories. No one could challenge the incumbents on their home ground.

Huang saw a different map. China had 600 million people in lower-tier cities and rural areas. They were coming online through smartphones, often skipping desktop internet entirely. They were price-sensitive. They had time but not much money. And they were social in ways that Western e-commerce platforms ignored.

He launched Pinduoduo with a simple mechanic: team purchases. Instead of buying alone, users would share deals with friends and family through WeChat. Get enough people to join your "team" within 24 hours, and everyone got a steep discount. The product you wanted for 50 yuan? Assemble five friends and pay 30.

The mechanism was brilliant in its simplicity. It turned every user into a marketer. Shopping became a social activity, not a solitary transaction. And it worked perfectly on mobile, perfectly on WeChat, perfectly for people who had time to organize group buys and friends who wanted deals.

The early products were unglamorous. Toilet paper. Cooking oil. Fruit. The kind of everyday items where price mattered more than brand. Pinduoduo went directly to manufacturers, cutting out layers of distribution. They could offer prices that Alibaba and JD.com couldn't match because they didn't carry the overhead of those mature platforms.

Users came fast. Within a year, Pinduoduo had tens of millions of monthly active users. Within two years, hundreds of millions. The growth curve was vertical in a way that shocked observers. This was supposed to be a mature market.

The secret was the demographic everyone else had written off. In China's Tier 3, 4, and 5 cities — places most urbanites had never heard of — people were bargain hunting with the intensity of sport. They would spend twenty minutes organizing a team purchase to save three yuan. They would share deals constantly. The gamification worked. The social pressure worked. The prices worked.

Tencent noticed. In 2016, they invested, giving Pinduoduo privileged access to WeChat's ecosystem. This was crucial. WeChat was where China lived — messaging, payments, news, everything. Pinduoduo could spread through WeChat like a virus, with minimal friction.

By 2017, critics were calling it a haven for counterfeits. The platform had exploded so fast that quality control was loose. You could find suspiciously cheap versions of name brands. Huang didn't deny the problem. He called it a "social responsibility" the company was addressing. But he also noted something true: many of his customers weren't looking for authentic Nike shoes. They wanted cheap shoes that looked like Nikes. The market he served was different from the market critics imagined.

In July 2018, three years after founding, Pinduoduo went public on NASDAQ. The company was valued at $24 billion despite never turning a profit. The prospectus revealed the scale: 343 million annual active buyers, more than JD.com. Average revenue per user was low — these were not high-spending customers. But the volume was staggering.

The listing drew immediate comparisons. Some called it China's Groupon. Others said it was more like Wish. Huang rejected both. "We're not a shopping platform," he said. "We're a combination of Costco and Disneyland." The Costco part was the relentless focus on value. The Disneyland part was making shopping entertaining, social, even fun.

Alibaba and JD.com finally responded. They launched their own team-purchase features. They invested in rural logistics. They targeted lower-tier cities with promotions. But they were late and conflicted. Their existing business models — supporting brand merchants, premium experiences, high margins — clashed with Pinduoduo's bare-knuckle approach.

Pinduoduo kept evolving. They added live-streaming, where farmers would broadcast from their fields selling fruit directly to consumers. They launched an agricultural initiative, connecting rural producers to urban buyers. They invested in technology — machine learning to predict demand, optimize logistics, detect fraud. The platform began to look less scrappy and more sophisticated.

The pandemic accelerated everything. With physical retail disrupted, even more people shifted online. Pinduoduo's agricultural supply chains proved resilient. By 2020, the platform had 788 million annual active buyers, surpassing Alibaba's China retail marketplaces. This was the moment everyone realized: this was not a niche player. This was a new giant.

The profitability came later, but when it came, it was dramatic. By 2021, as the platform matured and efficiency improved, margins expanded. The company that critics said would never make money was printing cash.

Today Pinduoduo stands as China's third-largest e-commerce platform, sometimes second depending on how you measure. Its parent company PDD Holdings has expanded internationally with Temu, using similar tactics to disrupt Western e-commerce. The strategy: go where incumbents aren't, serve customers incumbents ignore, and grow so fast that by the time they react, you're entrenched.

Colin Huang stepped down as chairman in 2021, at 41, enormously wealthy and saying he wanted to focus on science and research. The company he built had reshaped Chinese retail. Not through better logistics than Alibaba or faster delivery than JD.com, but by recognizing that e-commerce didn't have to be a solo activity and that hundreds of millions of people would trade their time and social capital for cheaper toilet paper.

---

*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Rappi
entries.descriptor: The company that turned Latin America's chaos into a delivery empire
entries.content:
# Rappi

***The company that turned Latin America's chaos into a delivery empire***

> **Years:** 2015-present

Simón Borrero, Sebastián Mejía, and Felipe Villamarín started Rappi in 2015 in Bogotá, Colombia, with a simple premise: in Latin America, everything takes too long and nothing works reliably. Banks are bureaucratic, formal retail is limited, and logistics infrastructure is decades behind developed markets. What looked like disadvantages to most investors looked like opportunity to them.

The initial idea was narrow: deliver anything in under an hour. Not restaurant food. Not groceries specifically. Anything. A bottle of wine, a phone charger, cigarettes, cash from an ATM, a birthday cake at midnight. They called their couriers "Rappitenderos" and gave them a mandate that bordered on absurd: if a customer requests it and it's legal, figure out how to deliver it.

This wasn't Uber Eats or Instacart with a Latin American skin. Those services assumed existing retail infrastructure — restaurants with digital ordering systems, grocery stores with inventory management, addresses that map software could parse. Rappi assumed none of that existed. They built backward from chaos.

The founders recruited their first Rappitenderos from motorcycle taxi drivers, a gray-market workforce that already knew how to navigate Bogotá's anarchic traffic. They launched with no restaurant partnerships and no retail agreements. A customer would order. A Rappitendero would buy it with a Rappi credit card, photograph the receipt, and deliver it. The unit economics made no sense. The logistics were manual. But it worked.

Within months, they realized something: Latin America's informal economy was massive, unbanked, and hungry for digital tools. Street vendors, small restaurants, corner stores — they all wanted access to customers but had no way to reach them. Rappi became the interface between informal commerce and smartphone users. They weren't just a delivery app. They were infrastructure.

In 2016, they raised $2 million and expanded to Mexico. The playbook was the same: hire local couriers, enable cash payment, deliver anything. By 2017, they had raised $100 million from Andreessen Horowitz and DST Global. Not because the metrics were pretty — they were burning cash at a staggering rate — but because the vision was clear. Rappi was building the operating system for commerce in a region where one didn't exist.

They expanded vertically. RappiBank offered credit and payment services to the unbanked. RappiTravel booked buses and flights. RappiCash let users withdraw money from ATMs via courier. RappiPay became a digital wallet. Every product attacked a different friction point in daily life. Critics called it unfocused. Borrero called it inevitable. In markets where infrastructure is broken, you have to build everything.

The growth was exponential but messy. City launches were chaotic. Couriers sometimes fought with restaurant staff. Fraud was rampant — both from users and Rappitenderos. But Rappi's advantage was speed. They iterated faster than competitors, absorbed losses incumbents couldn't stomach, and treated regulation as a problem to negotiate around rather than a constraint to respect.

By 2018, they were in nine countries and raising a $200 million Series D led by DST Global. Valuation hit $1 billion. Rappi was Latin America's first delivery unicorn, and the narrative shifted. No longer a scrappy Colombian startup, they were the champion that might actually compete with global giants like Uber and Deliveroo expanding into the region.

But the competitive landscape intensified. Uber Eats poured money into Mexico and Brazil. iFood, backed by Naspers, dominated Brazil. DiDi Food entered with Chinese capital and Chinese playbooks. Every market became a subsidy war. Discounts spiraled. Driver wages rose. Margins, already negative, cratered further.

Rappi's response was to go bigger. In 2019, they raised $1 billion in a Series E led by SoftBank's Vision Fund, hitting a $3.5 billion valuation. The capital was a weapon. They could outlast competitors in the burn race. They expanded into convenience stores, dark stores, and ghost kitchens. They launched Turbo, a subscription service for unlimited free delivery. They added pharmacies, pet supplies, and electronics.

The pandemic was a paradox. Demand exploded — delivery became essential overnight. But so did costs. Every competitor saw the same surge. Labor became scarce. Regulations tightened. Governments suddenly cared about gig worker conditions. The gold rush was crowded, and margins stayed brutal.

SoftBank's blessing became a curse when the Vision Fund imploded. Growth-at-all-costs fell out of fashion. Profitability became the metric that mattered. Rappi, built to expand infinitely on cheap capital, had to become a real business. They cut unprofitable cities, reduced discounts, raised prices, and laid off staff.

In 2021, they raised $500 million more at a $5.25 billion valuation — up, but not the hypergrowth trajectory SoftBank deals implied. The message was clear: they were too big to fail but too messy to thrive. By 2022, layoffs hit 6% of corporate staff. Borrero stepped back from day-to-day operations.

The path to exit narrowed. An IPO seemed distant — public markets had soured on unprofitable delivery companies. Acquisition was complicated — potential buyers like Uber or Amazon faced antitrust scrutiny. A slow grind toward profitability while fending off competitors became the likely future.

Today, Rappi operates across nine countries, facilitates billions in transactions, and remains the largest delivery platform in Latin America outside Brazil. It has not exited. It has not failed. It has become the thing it set out to disrupt: essential infrastructure, too embedded to remove, too subscale to dominate, locked in a permanent war of attrition with rivals who won't surrender.

The story of Rappi is the story of seeing chaos as canvas. They built when others waited for infrastructure to arrive. But infrastructure isn't profitable. It's necessary. And necessity doesn't always make you rich.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Convoy
entries.section: The trucking Uber that ran out of road
entries.descriptor: The freight broker that tried to eliminate freight brokers
entries.content:
# Convoy

***The freight broker that tried to eliminate freight brokers***

> **Years:** 2015-2023

In 2015, Dan Lewis and Grant Goodale looked at the $800 billion trucking industry and saw what every tech founder sees in legacy sectors: inefficiency, middlemen, and fragmentation. Lewis had been CEO of Amazon's grocery business. Goodale had built mapping systems at Microsoft. They knew logistics. They knew software. The gap seemed obvious.

American trucking runs on a network of 20,000 freight brokers — intermediaries who match companies that need stuff moved with truckers who have capacity. Brokers work phones and spreadsheets. They take cuts of 15-20%. The system works, but it's expensive, opaque, and slow. Lewis and Goodale believed software could do better.

They raised $2.5 million from Greylock and Y Combinator. The pitch was elegant: build an app that connects shippers directly to truckers. Cut out the middleman. Lower costs. Increase transparency. Use data to optimize routing and pricing. It was Uber for freight — a comparison investors loved and that would prove both clarifying and cursed.

The first version launched in Seattle. Truckers could browse available loads on their phones. Shippers could book capacity with a few taps. No haggling. No broker fees. Just technology matching supply and demand.

Early traction was real. Truckers liked the transparency. Shippers liked the prices. By 2016, Convoy had raised $18 million from Cascade Investment — Bill Gates's personal investment vehicle. By 2017, another $62 million. By 2018, $185 million at a $1 billion valuation. Bono invested. Jeff Bezos invested. The story wrote itself: tech-savvy founders bringing Silicon Valley magic to an unsexy industry.

Convoy grew fast. They expanded beyond the Pacific Northwest into Texas, then the Southeast, then nationwide. They built sophisticated algorithms to predict pricing and demand. They hired hundreds of sales people to sign up both truckers and shippers. Revenue scaled from millions to hundreds of millions.

But the business never quite worked the way the pitch suggested. The vision was a platform — lightweight, software-driven, connecting two sides of a marketplace with minimal overhead. The reality was operations-heavy and margin-thin. Trucking wasn't like ridesharing. A shipper booking a load isn't like someone hailing a ride. The stakes are higher. The reliability requirements are absolute. When a truck doesn't show up or a load gets damaged, you can't just cancel and try again.

Convoy discovered what brokers had always known: the value isn't just matching. It's relationships, trust, problem-solving, and absorbing risk. When weather delays a shipment or a trucker breaks down, someone needs to fix it. That someone was increasingly Convoy's operations team — effectively becoming the brokers they had promised to eliminate.

The unit economics were stubborn. Take rates — the percentage Convoy kept from each transaction — hovered around 15%, roughly the same as traditional brokers. But Convoy had Silicon Valley costs: engineers, data scientists, product managers, venture-scale growth targets. Traditional brokers had sales guys and phones.

The pandemic initially seemed like a validation. Freight volumes surged. Capacity tightened. Spot rates spiked. Convoy's revenue hit $1 billion in 2021. They raised $260 million at a $3.8 billion valuation. But the boom masked the structural challenges. In a hot market, even inefficient models make money.

When freight markets cooled in 2022, the problems surfaced. Shipping volumes fell. Excess trucking capacity returned. Spot rates collapsed. Convoy's growth stalled. The company had 1,500 employees and a cost structure built for hypergrowth in an industry entering a downturn.

Competitors were everywhere. Legacy brokers like C.H. Robinson had digitized. Uber Freight existed. Dozens of venture-backed startups chased the same thesis. The industry hadn't been disrupted — it had gotten more competitive without fundamentally changing.

Convoy tried to adapt. They launched their own trucks — shifting from pure platform to hybrid asset operator. They cut staff. They focused on profitability over growth. But venture math is unforgiving. A company valued at $3.8 billion needs an exit that justifies that valuation. Steady, modest profitability in a cyclical, low-margin business doesn't get there.

By 2023, runway was short. Convoy sought buyers. Flexport, itself struggling, agreed to acquire Convoy — not for the valuation, but for the technology and customer relationships. Then Flexport backed out. In October 2023, Convoy shut down. They laid off 1,000 employees with one week of severance. The assets were sold in pieces. Customers scrambled to rebook loads. Truckers were left unpaid for completed hauls.

Convoy had raised $1 billion across its life. It moved freight for major corporations. It built real technology. But technology alone couldn't overcome the economics of a business where margins are thin, relationships matter, and being right about the future doesn't make you profitable today.

The insight was correct: trucking brokerage was ripe for software. The mistake was believing software could replace the messiness entirely. The path from insight to sustainable business required either lower costs than incumbents or higher value to customers — ideally both. Convoy achieved neither decisively enough. In the end, they became expensive brokers in an industry that already had plenty of cheap ones.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Katerra
entries.descriptor: The unicorn that tried to reinvent construction and collapsed under its own ambition
entries.content:
# Katerra

***The unicorn that tried to reinvent construction and collapsed under its own ambition***

> **Years:** 2015-2021

In 2015, a group of Silicon Valley veterans looked at the construction industry and saw what tech founders always see in old industries: waste, inefficiency, and opportunity. Michael Marks, former CEO of Flextronics, and Fritz Wolff, his longtime colleague, believed they could do to construction what manufacturing had done to electronics — vertically integrate, automate, and optimize the entire supply chain. They called it Katerra.

The pitch was elegant. Construction was fragmented: architects, general contractors, subcontractors, suppliers, each taking a margin, each a source of delay and error. Buildings cost too much and took too long because nobody controlled the whole process. Katerra would own it all — design, manufacturing, materials, assembly. They would prefabricate components in factories, ship them to sites, and assemble buildings like IKEA furniture. Faster, cheaper, better.

It was catnip for investors. SoftBank's Vision Fund, flush with capital and hungry for moonshots, led a $865 million round in 2018. By 2019, Katerra had raised over $2 billion. The valuation hit $4 billion. It was the largest construction technology startup in the world.

The company expanded at breathtaking speed. They acquired firms across the value chain: an architecture practice, an engineering firm, a door manufacturer, a lumber company. They built factories in Phoenix and Spokane to manufacture cross-laminated timber and modular building components. They hired thousands of employees. They signed contracts for apartment buildings, hotels, corporate campuses. They promised to revolutionize how America builds.

The cracks appeared early, but momentum obscured them. Factories opened before manufacturing processes were debugged. Projects launched before designs were finalized. The company took on complex custom buildings when their model worked best for repetitive designs. They bid aggressively to win contracts, often underbidding to gain market share, assuming they would achieve the efficiency gains that would make the numbers work.

Those gains never materialized. Prefabrication sounds simple in theory but is viciously hard in practice. Buildings are not phones. Every site has different soil, different codes, different inspectors. Components manufactured in Phoenix had to work in Seattle and Dallas and Sacramento, each with different regulations. Designs that looked good in CAD software failed when they met reality. Tolerances that worked in a factory didn't survive shipping and field conditions.

Projects fell behind schedule. Costs overran budgets. Clients complained. Some sued. Katerra's reputation among developers — the customers they needed most — began to sour. The vertically integrated model that was supposed to eliminate margins instead multiplied risk. When one piece failed, everything downstream failed with it.

The financial model was brutal. Construction operates on thin margins even when executed well. Katerra was burning cash on factories, acquisitions, and overhead while underpricing projects and struggling to deliver. They needed perfect execution to break even. They were getting chaos.

By 2020, the company was restructuring. Executive turnover accelerated. Projects were cancelled or handed off. The lumber mill shut down. Then COVID hit, disrupting supply chains and halting construction sites. But COVID was catalyst, not cause. Katerra was already in trouble.

SoftBank, dealing with its own crisis after WeWork's collapse and a brutal portfolio writedown, stopped funding Katerra's losses. In early 2021, the company desperately sought a buyer or new investor. None materialized. In June 2021, Katerra shut down. Thousands lost their jobs. Projects were abandoned mid-construction. Suppliers went unpaid. Lawsuits piled up.

The failure revealed a fundamental misunderstanding. Katerra's founders believed construction was inefficient because it lacked vertical integration and technology. The truth was more complex. Construction is fragmented because buildings are heterogeneous, contexts are variable, and risk is easier to manage when distributed. The industry's structure is not an accident of history but an adaptation to reality.

Vertical integration works brilliantly for standardized products at scale. It can work for construction when focused narrowly — modular hotels, cookie-cutter apartments, repeatable designs. But Katerra tried to be everything: custom and standardized, luxury and affordable, residential and commercial. They built the infrastructure for mass production while taking on bespoke projects. They optimized for a future that required discipline they didn't have and a market that didn't yet exist.

The technology was real. The factories could produce quality components. The software worked. But technology alone couldn't overcome the fundamentals: undisciplined expansion, cash burn that required perfection, a business model that multiplied rather than mitigated construction's inherent risks.

Katerra's collapse became a case study in the limits of "tech thinking" applied to physical industries. Not every industry can be disrupted by treating it as a software problem. Not every inefficiency is an opportunity. Sometimes the old way is old because it works, and the new way is just expensive failure with better branding.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Scale AI
entries.section: Labeling data for the AI boom
entries.descriptor: The labeling company that became infrastructure for the AI age
entries.content:
# Scale AI

***The labeling company that became infrastructure for the AI age***

> **Years:** 2016-present

Alexandr Wang was nineteen when he dropped out of MIT in 2016. He had been working on machine learning projects and kept running into the same problem: getting labeled training data was painfully slow and expensive. Every AI researcher faced this bottleneck. You could build elegant neural networks, but they were useless without massive amounts of accurately labeled data — images tagged, text categorized, objects outlined pixel by pixel.

The standard approach was crowdsourcing through platforms like Mechanical Turk, but the quality was inconsistent and the workflow was clunky. Researchers spent more time managing labelers than building models. Wang saw the opening. If AI was going to eat the world, someone needed to solve the data problem first.

He founded Scale AI with Lucy Guo, another young engineer who had left Quora. Their insight was deceptively simple: build an API that handled the entire data labeling pipeline. Developers would send raw data, specify what they needed labeled, and get back structured training data. Scale would handle quality control, workforce management, and the messy logistics underneath.

The first customers were self-driving car companies. Autonomous vehicles needed millions of images with every car, pedestrian, and lane marking precisely annotated. This was tedious human work that required accuracy measured in pixels. Scale built tools that made labelers more productive and quality control more rigorous. They combined human workers with automated checks, catching errors before they corrupted training datasets.

The timing was perfect. In 2016, deep learning was transitioning from research curiosity to industrial necessity. Companies were hiring entire teams just to wrangle training data. Scale offered to handle it all for a price per task. For AI teams, it was obviously worth it — they could focus on models instead of data operations.

Scale raised a $4.5 million seed round in 2016. By 2017, they had expanded beyond autonomous vehicles into any company building computer vision systems — robotics, drones, security cameras. The product was working. Labeling that previously took weeks now took days.

But the real opportunity was broader than computer vision. As AI spread into every industry, every company building machine learning systems hit the same wall: they needed training data. Scale built new products for different data types — text annotation for NLP models, audio transcription, sensor data labeling for robotics. Each vertical required different tools and quality standards, but the core insight held: companies would pay to outsource the grunt work of AI.

The business model scaled beautifully. Scale managed a global workforce of contractors who did the actual labeling. As volume grew, they invested in better tools and automation to make those workers more productive. Some tasks became partially automated — a model would make first-pass annotations, humans would correct them, and those corrections trained better models. The flywheel spun faster.

By 2019, Scale had raised $100 million at a $1 billion valuation. Wang was twenty-two. The company had become essential infrastructure for AI development. Self-driving companies like Cruise and Waymo, tech giants building AI products, and thousands of smaller companies all relied on Scale. When you used an AI product, there was a good chance Scale had labeled some of its training data.

The pandemic accelerated everything. As companies rushed to deploy AI systems — for remote work, automation, predictive analytics — Scale's revenue exploded. They raised $325 million in late 2021 at a $7.3 billion valuation. Wang became the youngest self-made billionaire in America.

Scale had successfully positioned itself at a critical bottleneck. As AI capabilities advanced, the hunger for training data only increased. Larger models needed more data. New modalities — video, 3D environments, medical images — opened new markets. Scale expanded into data used for testing and evaluating AI models, not just training them. They built products for government and defense applications.

The competitive landscape remained surprisingly favorable. Data labeling looked like it should be commoditized — many companies offered similar services. But Scale had built genuine advantages. Their tools were better, making labelers more productive. Their quality control was tighter. Their API was easier to integrate. And they had accumulated expertise in what good training data looked like across dozens of domains. A new competitor couldn't just hire some contractors and match them.

By 2024, Scale was processing billions of data points annually. The company had expanded from pure labeling into broader "data engine" services — helping companies debug their datasets, understand model failures, and continuously improve their training pipelines. As foundation models like GPT became widespread, Scale positioned itself as infrastructure for fine-tuning and customizing them.

The path from MIT dropout to AI infrastructure giant happened faster than anyone expected, Wang included. But it followed a classic pattern: find an unglamorous bottleneck in an exploding market, solve it better than anyone else, and scale before competitors catch up.

The business wasn't intellectually glamorous. Labeling data is tedious work. But that was the point. Every revolutionary technology rests on mundane foundations. Scale built the foundations while others chased the glory. In the AI gold rush, they sold shovels — very sophisticated, API-driven shovels that became harder to replace the more you used them.

Wang remained CEO, unusually young to be running a company of this scale and importance. The outcome was still being written, but the path was clear: Scale had made itself essential infrastructure for the AI age by solving the problem that AI researchers least wanted to think about.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Hugging Face
entries.descriptor: The open-source gambit that made AI infrastructure inevitable
entries.content:
# Hugging Face

***The open-source gambit that made AI infrastructure inevitable***

> **Years:** 2016-present

In 2016, three French engineers created a chatbot app for teenagers. By 2024, their company hosted the infrastructure for the entire open-source AI movement, reached a $4.5 billion valuation, and became as essential to machine learning as GitHub is to code. The path from emoji-laden chat app to AI platform reveals how betting on community and open access can build moats deeper than any proprietary technology.

Clément Delangue, Julien Chaumond, and Thomas Wolf met in the Paris startup scene. Delangue had sold a previous company. Chaumond and Wolf were machine learning researchers who'd grown frustrated with how hard it was to deploy models. Their first product was utterly different from what Hugging Face would become: a chatbot app where teenagers could talk to AI personas about their feelings, their crushes, their fears. The mascot was a yellow hugging face emoji. The app used natural language processing to generate empathetic responses. It was personal AI before anyone called it that.

The app gained users — enough to raise $1.2 million in seed funding — but not enough to justify the ambition. What Delangue and team noticed was something more interesting than their product: the AI models powering the chatbot were impossibly difficult to work with. Every time they wanted to try a new model architecture, it meant weeks of implementation work. The research community was publishing breakthroughs daily, but actually using those breakthroughs required rebuilding everything from scratch. The best models lived in academic papers, not production systems.

In 2018, they pivoted. Instead of building a consumer app, they would build infrastructure for AI developers. They started with a library called Transformers — an open-source Python package that made state-of-the-art natural language processing models accessible with just a few lines of code. Want to use BERT, the breakthrough model from Google? Download it from Hugging Face, load it with three lines of Python, and start using it. No need to read the paper, reimplement the architecture, hunt for pre-trained weights, or debug obscure tensor operations.

The timing was exquisite. Transformer models — the architecture underlying GPT, BERT, and eventually everything in modern AI — were exploding in capability and complexity. Google, Facebook, and OpenAI were publishing new models constantly. But each one required expertise to deploy. Hugging Face became the translation layer between cutting-edge research and working code. Researchers started uploading their models directly to the platform. Companies started building products on top of them.

The Transformers library grew from a tool to an ecosystem. Hugging Face launched the Model Hub, a repository where anyone could upload, share, and download machine learning models. It was GitHub for AI — except instead of code repositories, it hosted trained neural networks. By 2020, thousands of models lived on the platform. By 2024, over a million. The models ranged from Google's most powerful language models to small specialized networks trained to detect hate speech in Portuguese or classify plant diseases from photos.

The business model emerged gradually. The core technology remained open source — the Transformers library is MIT licensed, free forever — but Hugging Face offered paid services around it. Need to fine-tune a model on your company's proprietary data? Pay for compute. Need private model hosting? Subscribe. Want enterprise support? There's a tier for that. The revenue came from making open-source AI easier to use, not from locking it away.

They raised $40 million in 2021, then $100 million in 2022 at a $2 billion valuation. By then, Hugging Face wasn't just a platform; it was infrastructure. When researchers published a new model, they published it on Hugging Face. When companies needed to add AI capabilities, they pulled models from Hugging Face. When Meta released Llama 2, arguably the most important open-source large language model, they released it on Hugging Face. It became the default, the standard, the place where open AI lived.

The company expanded beyond language models. They added support for image generation, audio processing, reinforcement learning. They launched Spaces, a hosting service where developers could deploy machine learning demos and applications directly on the platform. They introduced Datasets, a library for downloading and processing the massive datasets required to train modern AI. Each product extended the ecosystem, made open-source AI more accessible, pulled more users into the platform.

The competition was fierce and existential. OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic were building closed systems — powerful models you could only access through APIs, never download or modify. AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure offered their own model hosting and deployment services. GitHub, owned by Microsoft, launched GitHub Models, directly targeting Hugging Face's territory. But Hugging Face had something the giants couldn't easily replicate: the trust of the open-source community and the network effects of being where everyone already published their work.

By 2024, Hugging Face employed over 400 people, had raised more than $400 million, and was valued at $4.5 billion. The platform hosted over a million models, 300,000 datasets, and 500,000 applications. More than 50,000 companies used it. The teenage chatbot was long forgotten, but the hugging face emoji remained — a reminder that the best products often emerge from abandoned ideas, that infrastructure businesses hide inside failed consumer apps, and that opening the castle gates can build a stronger kingdom than any wall.

The company still faced existential questions. Could they remain independent as Big Tech consolidated AI infrastructure? Would open-source AI survive regulatory pressure and copyright battles? Could they build a sustainable business while keeping the core technology free? The answers would determine not just Hugging Face's fate, but the fate of open AI itself. For now, they had proven something crucial: you can build a giant company by giving everything away, if what you give away makes everyone more powerful.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Bird
entries.descriptor: The scooter boom that became a cautionary tale about unit economics
entries.content:
# Bird

***The scooter boom that became a cautionary tale about unit economics***

> **Years:** 2017-2023

Bird didn't invent the electric scooter, but in September 2017, Travis VanderZanden invented the idea that scooters could be left anywhere, ridden by anyone, and somehow make money.
