He'd been at Uber and Lyft — he understood the religion of growth, the faith that scale solves all problems. He saw cars as too heavy, bikes as too effortful, and scooters as the perfect last-mile solution. The insight was simple: people will pay $1 to unlock and 15 cents per minute to ride something they find on a street corner.

The early days were pure land-grab. Bird dropped scooters in Santa Monica without asking permission. The city responded with cease-and-desist letters. Bird kept dropping scooters. The friction created press. The press created users. Within months, riders were taking 250,000 trips. VanderZanden raised $15 million, then $100 million, then $300 million. Sequoia led. The valuation hit $1 billion nine months after launch — the fastest in startup history at the time. The story wrote itself: micro-mobility would replace car ownership, reduce emissions, remake cities. Investors saw Uber-scale returns. No one wanted to miss it.

The competition arrived instantly. Lime launched weeks after Bird. Skip, Spin, Jump, and dozens of others followed. Each needed to blanket cities before rivals did. Each raised huge rounds. The playbook was identical: flood streets with scooters, subsidize rides, capture territory, worry about profits later. Cities initially banned them, then created permit systems. Getting a permit meant proving you could operate at scale. Operating at scale meant burning capital. The incentives pushed everyone toward the same unsustainable expansion.

The unit economics were broken from the start, though few said so publicly. A scooter cost $300-400. It lasted maybe three months before vandalism, weather, or wear destroyed it. Revenue per scooter was perhaps $100 per month in good markets, less in others. Maintenance, charging, rebalancing, customer support — the costs accumulated. The gig workers who charged scooters overnight earned $5-20 per device. They hunted scooters like bounty. Some hoarded them, some damaged competitors' units. The operational chaos was immense.

Bird kept raising. By 2018, the company had raised $415 million at a $2 billion valuation. The narrative was familiar: they were first, biggest, best-known. They'd achieve density that competitors couldn't match. Density would drive down per-unit costs. Scale would bring leverage with cities, manufacturers, chargers. The economics would flip. Investors who'd seen Amazon lose money for years before dominating retail believed the pattern would repeat.

It didn't. The fundamentals never improved. Scooters remained expensive and fragile. Riders treated them roughly because they owned nothing. Vandalism was constant — scooters thrown in rivers, lit on fire, hoarded in garages. Bird introduced sturdier models, but durability added cost and weight, making scooters less pleasant to ride. The company tried owning its supply chain, then outsourcing it, then partnering with manufacturers. Nothing solved the core problem: the thing being rented wore out faster than it generated profit.

The pandemic nearly killed them. Cities emptied. Scooter trips collapsed. Bird furloughed 30% of staff, then laid off hundreds more. VanderZanden took the company public via SPAC in late 2021 at a $2.3 billion valuation — down from the $3 billion private peak but still remarkable given the losses. The SPAC provided $428 million in capital. The pitch was the same: scale would work. The post-pandemic world would embrace micro-mobility. Profits were coming.

The stock immediately collapsed. By mid-2022, Bird traded below $1. The company was burning $20-30 million per quarter with no path to break-even. Competitors were dying too — Skip sold to Lime, Jump shut down, dozens of smaller players disappeared. Lime survived by raising another billion dollars and finding, somehow, a path to positive cash flow in some markets. Bird couldn't. The company withdrew from dozens of cities, sold off scooters, cut staff again.

In December 2023, Bird filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. The company that nine months after launch was worth $1 billion was now worth essentially nothing. The bankruptcy plan involved selling assets, stiffing creditors, and maybe continuing some operations under new ownership, though the details remained unclear. Shareholders were wiped out. VanderZanden was gone. The scooters, where they remained, looked like artifacts from a brief collective delusion.

The story of Bird is the story of mistaking adoption for viability. Millions of people rode the scooters. The product worked — it moved humans from point A to point B pleasantly enough. But the business never worked. The romantic vision — scooters as climate solution, as liberation from car dependency — collided with the arithmetic. A thing that costs $400 and breaks in three months while earning $300 total cannot be the foundation of a billion-dollar company, no matter how fast you scale it. The path from idea to failure was paved with capital that believed scale would solve physics.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Anduril
entries.section: Defense tech from the Oculus founder
entries.descriptor: The defense insurgent that bet America would rearm
entries.content:
# Anduril

***The defense insurgent that bet America would rearm***

> **Years:** 2017-present

Palmer Luckey was twenty-four when Facebook acquired Oculus for $2 billion, making him one of Silicon Valley's youngest self-made fortunes. Three years later, he was out—fired, he would later say, for his political donations. The Valley that had celebrated his hacker genius now treated him as radioactive. Most twenty-six-year-olds would have retreated. Luckey decided to build weapons.

He founded Anduril Industries in 2017 with a thesis that seemed either prophetic or delusional: that Silicon Valley's greatest competitive advantage—the ability to iterate software faster than incumbents—could shatter the defense industrial base. That sensors, autonomy, and AI could replace Cold War-era weapons systems. That America's military edge was eroding and the Pentagon knew it but couldn't fix itself. And that venture capital, which had spent two decades avoiding defense as morally and commercially toxic, would fund the transformation.

The name came from Tolkien—the sword reforged, the weapon that returns when needed. The founding team combined Oculus veterans, former Palantir engineers, and defense industry apostates. They structured the company around a central conviction: software should control hardware, not the reverse. Build the autonomy stack. Make it modular. Integrate sensors into a mesh network that turns surveillance into persistent awareness. Then wrap it in products the Pentagon might actually buy.

Their first product was Lattice, an AI-powered operating system that fused data from diverse sensors—drones, towers, ground units—into a single real-time picture. The second was a suite of autonomous surveillance systems: sentry towers for the border, counter-drone systems, underwater vehicles. Not glamorous. Not the fighter jets and aircraft carriers that dominate defense budgets. But nimble, software-defined, deployable at a fraction of traditional costs.

The border surveillance towers, deployed along the U.S.-Mexico boundary, became their proof of concept. Traditional border systems cost millions per mile and took years to deploy. Anduril's towers—solar powered, autonomous, requiring no infrastructure—went up in months for a fraction of the price. Customs and Border Protection ordered hundreds. The product worked. Revenue followed.

What made Anduril dangerous to incumbents wasn't just better technology—it was speed. Defense primes like Lockheed and Raytheon operated on decade-long development cycles, cost-plus contracts that rewarded process over results, and Pentagon acquisition rules designed to prevent the last war's scandals. Anduril adopted commercial sales tactics: fixed-price contracts, rapid prototyping, demonstrations that made generals believers. They hired from SpaceX and tech, not from Boeing and Northrop. They moved like a startup because they were one.

The Pentagon, desperate to counter China's military modernization and Russia's aggression, proved surprisingly receptive. Replicator, a DoD initiative to field thousands of autonomous systems within two years, named Anduril a key partner. The Air Force bought their counter-drone systems. The Navy tested their underwater drones. Australia ordered Ghost Shark autonomous submarines—a billion-dollar deal that signaled Anduril had graduated from border security to prime contractor.

Venture capitalists, once allergic to defense, suddenly saw the sector as frontier territory. Anduril raised at escalating valuations: $1 billion in 2019, $4.6 billion in 2022, $14 billion in 2024. Each round validated the thesis that defense would be the next great venture outcome. Founders Fund, Andreessen Horowitz, Thrive Capital—firms that had ignored defense for years—now competed to fund it. Anduril became the proof point, the company that showed defense could scale like SaaS and return like enterprise software.

But success created new constraints. As Anduril grew from insurgent to incumbent-in-waiting, it faced the contradictions that had constrained its predecessors. Pentagon budgets remained finite and political. Protests from employees uncomfortable with weapons work mirrored those at Google and Microsoft. International expansion required navigating arms export regulations more complex than any software compliance regime. And the defense primes weren't conceding—they had decades of relationships, manufacturing scale, and lobbying muscle Anduril couldn't match overnight.

The company's ambitions escalated. Roadrunner, a reusable interceptor drone designed to destroy incoming missiles and return to base, represented a technological leap: autonomous air defense at costs that made mass deployment feasible. Arsenal-1, a hyperscale manufacturing facility under construction in Ohio, signaled intent to build at prime contractor volumes. Anduril wasn't just selling software anymore—it was becoming a vertically integrated defense platform.

Luckey, now thirty-one, styled himself as a contrarian vindicated. His firing from Facebook, once career-ending, became origin story—proof that Silicon Valley's groupthink had blinded it to hard problems and uncomfortable clients. Anduril's rise coincided with a broader shift: venture capital rediscovering "tough tech," Washington awakening to Chinese competition, and a generation of engineers deciding that defense work wasn't morally disqualifying.

By 2024, Anduril had crossed the threshold from promising startup to essential player. The question was no longer whether software could transform defense, but whether Anduril could scale fast enough to matter before geopolitics turned theoretical edge into actual conflict. The company had proven it could win contracts. Whether it could deliver at the speed and scale required—and whether America would pay for the transformation its strategists claimed was necessary—remained unanswered.

The sword had been reforged. Whether it would be drawn in time was the bet Anduril and its backers had placed.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Brex
entries.descriptor: Two Stanford dropouts without credit histories built the corporate card for companies without credit histories
entries.content:
# Brex

***Two Stanford dropouts without credit histories built the corporate card for companies without credit histories***

> **Years:** 2017-present

Henrique Dubugras and Pedro Franceschi had already sold one company before they could legally drink in the United States. They founded Pagar.me, a Brazilian payments processor, as teenagers and sold it for eight figures before moving to California to attend Stanford. The plan was to get degrees and perhaps start something new. They lasted one semester.

The insight came from frustration. As international students building a VR company — their first attempt in the Valley — they couldn't get corporate credit cards. No credit history meant no cards, even though their bank accounts showed they had money. Every founder they talked to had the same problem. Startups flush with venture capital couldn't access basic financial infrastructure because they lacked the traditional metrics banks used to assess risk.

The obvious solution was to build a credit card for startups. The less obvious part was how. Traditional card issuers underwrote based on personal credit scores and years of financial history. Startups had neither. But they had something else: venture funding. Money sitting in bank accounts. Burn rates that were trackable. Investors who had already performed due diligence.

Dubugras and Franceschi spent months meeting with banks, trying to convince them to issue cards against this new model. Every bank said no. The regulatory burden was too high, the risk too uncertain, the market too small. Then they found a partner willing to take the bet: a small bank in Utah that would handle the regulatory infrastructure while Brex built the product and underwrote the risk.

They launched in 2018 with a card that approved companies in minutes based on their cash in the bank and venture funding, rather than personal credit scores. No personal guarantee required. Higher limits than traditional cards. Rewards structured for startup spending — points for rideshares and SaaS subscriptions instead of airline miles. Integration with accounting software so expenses flowed directly into ledgers.

The timing was perfect. Venture funding was flooding into startups. The companies receiving that funding were run by founders who had grown up with smartphone interfaces and expected instant gratification. Applying for a corporate card shouldn't take weeks of paperwork and credit checks. Brex's application took ten minutes.

The viral loop was built into the product. VCs recommended Brex to their portfolio companies. Those companies' employees got cards and later started their own companies and chose Brex. The addressable market expanded as venture funding grew. By the end of 2018, Brex had thousands of customers and had issued hundreds of millions in credit.

But the card was just the entry point. Every time a company swiped a Brex card, Brex learned about their spending patterns, their growth, their financial health. That data became the foundation for everything else: cash management accounts, bill pay, expense management software. Brex wasn't just issuing credit. It was building the financial operating system for startups.

The challenge was the same one every neobank faced: how to make money. Interchange fees — the percentage merchants pay on card transactions — provided revenue, but margins were thin. The real money was in interest on deposits and lending. But in 2019, interest rates were near zero, and lending to startups was exactly the risk traditional banks avoided.

Brex expanded beyond startups. In 2020, they launched products for e-commerce companies and later for life sciences and mid-market businesses. Each vertical required different underwriting models and different product features. E-commerce companies needed higher limits for inventory purchases. Life sciences companies needed longer payment terms for equipment.

The pandemic was simultaneously crisis and opportunity. Startups cut spending, which meant lower interchange revenue. But remote work accelerated digital banking adoption. Companies that might have stuck with legacy banks for inertia were suddenly reconsidering all their vendors. Brex raised money at a $7.4 billion valuation in 2021, at the peak of fintech enthusiasm.

Then the market turned. Venture funding dried up. Startups cut spending or died. The very volatility that made startups underserved by traditional banks also made them risky customers in a downturn. In 2023, Brex laid off 20% of its staff and announced it would no longer serve small startups, focusing instead on companies spending at least $400,000 annually.

The pivot was controversial. The companies Brex was abandoning were the ones it had been founded to serve. But the unit economics didn't work. Small startups generated little interchange revenue and required significant support. Mid-market companies were more stable, spent more, and needed sophisticated treasury management tools that commanded higher margins.

By 2024, Brex had navigated the transition. Revenue was growing. The product had evolved into a comprehensive financial platform — corporate cards, expense management, bill pay, travel booking, reimbursements — all integrated. The company served thousands of businesses, processing billions in annual spending.

What Brex proved was that credit history wasn't the only signal of creditworthiness. Cash in the bank, VC backing, and spending patterns could underwrite risk just as effectively. They didn't invent corporate cards, but they rebuilt them for a generation of companies that banks didn't understand and wouldn't serve.

The company founded by two Stanford dropouts without credit histories had become the bank for companies without credit histories. Until it decided those companies weren't profitable enough.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: IRL
entries.section: The social app that faked its users
entries.descriptor: The app that tried to make spontaneity social — and discovered no one wanted that
entries.content:
# IRL

***The app that tried to make spontaneity social — and discovered no one wanted that***

> **Years:** 2013-2017

Abraham Shafi watched his friends texting each other "what are you doing right now?" and saw inefficiency. It was 2013. Location was ubiquitous, smartphones were everywhere, and yet coordinating spontaneous hangouts still required a flurry of messages. Shafi, a Stanford dropout who had previously founded a failed location app, believed the solution was simple: show people what their friends were doing in real time, and let spontaneity emerge organically.

He founded IRL with co-founders Scott Kurnit and Henry Khachatryan. The pitch was elegant. Open the app, see which friends are available, see what they're up to, and join them. No planning. No coordination overhead. Just frictionless real-world connection. The name itself — IRL, "in real life" — positioned the app as an antidote to the very platform it lived on.

The timing seemed perfect. Snapchat had proven that ephemeral, real-time sharing resonated with young users. Location services had matured. The "fear of missing out" was a recognized cultural phenomenon. And investors were hungry for the next social platform. IRL raised $1.2 million in seed funding in 2014, then $5 million more in 2015.

The app launched with a core feature set: users would post what they were doing or planning to do — "getting coffee at Philz," "watching the game at my place" — and friends could see these activities on a feed and a map. You could express interest in joining, message to coordinate, or just show up. The interface was clean, the interaction model straightforward.

Early traction came from college campuses, particularly around Stanford and Berkeley. Students had dense friend networks, flexible schedules, and a culture of spontaneous socializing. The app made sense in that context. Users posted plans, friends responded, and real-world hangouts materialized. The team celebrated each story of connections made through IRL as validation of the vision.

But as they expanded beyond that initial cohort, patterns emerged that troubled the founders. People would check the app, see what friends were doing, and... do nothing. The conversion from viewing to joining was far lower than projected. Users liked knowing what was happening but rarely acted on it.

The team hypothesized that the problem was critical mass. If more friends were on the app, more activities would be posted, creating more opportunities to join. They pushed hard on growth, running referral programs and campus ambassador initiatives. They added features to reduce friction: one-tap responses, better notifications, integrations with messaging apps.

The user numbers grew. The engagement numbers didn't follow. By 2016, IRL had hundreds of thousands of downloads but anemic daily active usage. People would install it, try it, then let it languish. The app became something users checked occasionally rather than a live feed of possibility.

The company pivoted — or rather, pivoted repeatedly. They tried making IRL more like an event discovery platform, surfacing public activities and local happenings. They experimented with AI-driven suggestions: "Your friend Sarah is free tonight and likes sushi, here are three places you both might go." They added group planning tools, making the app useful for coordinated hangouts rather than just spontaneous ones.

Each pivot brought a small bump in engagement, then a return to flatline. The fundamental problem remained: people didn't actually want to broadcast their availability and whereabouts in real time. The spontaneity the app promised felt invasive to many. Knowing what everyone was doing amplified FOMO rather than solving it. And the friends who might genuinely want to hang out spontaneously were already texting each other directly.

By mid-2016, the company was burning through its runway with no clear path to product-market fit. Shafi and the team tried to raise a Series A, positioning IRL as a platform for real-world social discovery. Investors passed. The social space was too crowded, the engagement metrics too weak, and the fundamental value proposition too narrow. Snapchat, Instagram, and Facebook were all adding features that overlapped with IRL's territory.

The company tried to acquihire itself to larger platforms. Facebook and Snapchat both took meetings but passed. The technology wasn't novel enough, and the team, while talented, wasn't large enough to justify an acquisition. By early 2017, IRL was running on fumes.

Shafi made the decision to shut down in April 2017. The company sent a final notification to its users: "Thank you for being part of IRL. We're shutting down, but we hope you keep hanging out with your friends — even without an app."

The post-mortem was clear to observers, if not immediately to the founders. They had solved a problem that didn't exist. The friction of texting "what are you up to?" wasn't actually friction — it was social signaling, intentionality, the difference between a genuine invitation and ambient availability. People wanted to choose who to reach out to and when, not operate from a shared real-time dashboard of their social lives.

IRL had mistaken information asymmetry for a product opportunity. Knowing what everyone was doing all the time turned out to be uncomfortable rather than useful. The app had optimized for spontaneity in a world where people increasingly valued intentionality. It made social coordination more efficient at the cost of making it less meaningful.

The app that promised to bring people together IRL became a cautionary tale about building solutions for problems users didn't actually have.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Celsius Network
entries.descriptor: The crypto bank that promised safety and delivered catastrophe
entries.content:
# Celsius Network

***The crypto bank that promised safety and delivered catastrophe***

> **Years:** 2017-2022

Alex Mashinsky had sold companies before. VoIP pioneer, serial entrepreneur, veteran of multiple exits. When he turned to cryptocurrency in 2017, he brought the confidence of someone who had played the game and won. His pitch for Celsius Network was seductive in its simplicity: a bank for the crypto age, where you deposited your Bitcoin or Ethereum and earned yields that made traditional savings accounts look like jokes. Eight percent, ten percent, sometimes higher. No fees. Weekly payouts. Your crypto working for you.

The tagline said it all: "Unbank yourself."

Mashinsky positioned Celsius as the antithesis of traditional finance. Banks paid you nothing and charged for everything. Celsius would flip the model — 80% of revenue to depositors, 20% to the company. It was populist, anti-establishment, perfectly pitched for the crypto true believers who saw themselves as revolutionaries dismantling the old order. Mashinsky became a fixture on Twitter and YouTube, preaching the gospel of decentralized finance while building a centralized institution that controlled billions in customer assets.

The model appeared elegant. Customers deposited crypto. Celsius lent it out to institutional borrowers at higher rates, pocketed the spread, and passed most of it back to depositors. Basic banking, dressed in blockchain rhetoric. By 2021, Celsius held over $20 billion in customer assets. Mashinsky's personal wealth soared. The company became one of crypto's most visible brands.

What customers didn't see was the risk accumulating behind the curtain. Celsius wasn't just lending crypto to creditworthy institutions. It was gambling. The company deployed customer funds into DeFi protocols — decentralized finance platforms that promised high returns through algorithmic lending and yield farming. These protocols were experimental, often audited poorly or not at all, and carried risks that traditional finance would never accept. Celsius also made enormous bets on specific tokens, held vast amounts of its own CEL token as collateral, and operated with minimal transparency about where customer funds actually went.

The warning signs emerged early. Customers trying to withdraw large amounts faced delays. Executives quietly departed. Short-sellers and crypto researchers began publishing reports questioning Celsius's solvency, pointing to mismatches between assets and liabilities, to risky bets on illiquid tokens, to the circular logic of using CEL tokens as collateral for loans. Mashinsky dismissed critics as fear-mongers and haters. He appeared on podcasts defending the model, insisting Celsius was rock-solid.

Then came May 2022. Terra, a supposedly stable algorithmic stablecoin ecosystem, collapsed in a death spiral that wiped out $40 billion in value within days. Celsius had exposure. Not catastrophic on its own, but enough to spook depositors. Withdrawal requests surged. Celsius paid them — at first. But the company's assets weren't as liquid as promised. Much of what Celsius held was locked in DeFi protocols, or consisted of tokens that couldn't be sold without crashing their price, or was tied up in the company's own CEL token, which had no real external value.

On June 12, 2022, Celsius froze all withdrawals. Customers woke to find their funds trapped. No warning, no gradual unwinding. One day you could access your crypto, the next you couldn't. The company claimed it needed to "stabilize liquidity" and promised resumption soon. It never came.

The unraveling was swift and brutal. Within weeks, Celsius filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Court filings revealed a $1.2 billion hole in the balance sheet. Customer funds had been used to plug losses, to manipulate the price of CEL tokens, to pay earlier withdrawals in classic Ponzi fashion. Celsius had lent hundreds of millions to Mashinsky himself. The institutional loans that were supposed to be low-risk turned out to be catastrophically bad bets. The DeFi protocols that promised transparency were black boxes that lost everything.

Over 600,000 customers lost access to their funds. Some had life savings in Celsius. Retirees, immigrants who trusted Mashinsky's pitch that this was safer than banks, people who believed the yields were real and the risk was minimal. Bankruptcy proceedings would recover pennies on the dollar. Mashinsky, who had withdrawn millions before the collapse, faced fraud charges from the SEC, CFTC, and Department of Justice.

Celsius was sold as the future of banking: transparent, fair, customer-first. It operated like the worst of banking's past: opaque, reckless, extractive. Mashinsky had promised to unbank his customers. In the end, he simply robbed them.

The company that styled itself as revolution was just a bank run waiting to happen, and when it happened, there was no FDIC, no bailout, no safety net. Just locked accounts and empty promises.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Frank
entries.descriptor: The pivot that became a neobank — before neobanks were cool
entries.content:
# Frank

***The pivot that became a neobank — before neobanks were cool***

> **Years:** 2017-present

Charlie Lee was 27 when he founded Frank in 2017, but he'd already lived the college financing nightmare. At Wharton, he'd watched classmates struggle with the FAFSA — the Free Application for Federal Student Aid — a 108-question labyrinth that determined access to billions in financial aid. The form was so confusing that families paid hundreds of dollars to services that would fill it out for them. Lee saw the absurdity: a free form that required paid help to complete.

Frank's original pitch was simple: make FAFSA easy. The product used AI to auto-populate fields, translated jargon into plain language, and walked students through the process. It took twenty minutes instead of hours. Students loved it. Parents loved it. Lee raised a seed round from Reach Capital and other education-focused VCs. By 2019, Frank claimed 300,000 users.

But there was a problem buried in the model. Frank was free to students. Revenue came from partnerships with colleges that paid to reach Frank's users — students actively seeking financial aid, a valuable audience. The business worked, but it was a marketing channel play dressed up as fintech. Lee was building a lead generation engine, not a financial services company.

The pivot came in 2020. Frank launched banking products — a no-fee checking account, a debit card, savings tools. The thesis: students who trusted Frank with their FAFSA would trust it with their money. The FAFSA tool became the acquisition funnel for a neobank. Lee began talking about Frank as "a financial platform for young people," positioning against Chime and Current but with an education-first brand.

The pivot attracted new capital. In 2021, Frank raised $25 million in a Series B led by Contour Venture Partners. The round valued the company at over $100 million. Lee told the press Frank had five million users. The number appeared in pitch decks, articles, and investor presentations. It was the foundation of Frank's valuation: millions of young customers, high engagement, a path to monetization.

JPMorgan Chase noticed. The bank had been trying to reach younger customers for years, launching digital products that never gained traction. Frank offered a shortcut: a beloved brand, millions of Gen Z users, a founder who spoke their language. In September 2021, JPMorgan announced it would acquire Frank for $175 million.

The deal closed in early 2022. Lee joined JPMorgan as a managing director. Frank's team integrated into the consumer bank. The plan was to use Frank's brand and user base to accelerate JPMorgan's student banking strategy. For Lee, it was vindication — a 27-year-old founder selling to America's largest bank.

But something was wrong with the numbers. After the acquisition closed, JPMorgan began digging into Frank's user data. The claimed five million users — the figure that justified a nine-figure acquisition — didn't match reality. According to JPMorgan's later court filings, fewer than 300,000 customers had opened accounts. The inflated numbers appeared to have been used to secure partnerships, attract investors, and ultimately sell the company.

In 2022, JPMorgan shut down Frank. In January 2023, they sued Charlie Lee and Frank's Chief Growth Officer for fraud, seeking $175 million in damages. The complaint alleged Lee had purchased data on millions of students to create fake accounts, making Frank appear far larger than it was. JPMorgan claimed they'd been deceived at every stage — pitch meetings, due diligence, final negotiations.

Lee denied the allegations. His legal team argued JPMorgan was deflecting from its own failed integration, that the bank hadn't properly understood Frank's metrics, that "users" had been defined broadly to include anyone who'd interacted with Frank's free tools, not just account holders. The case is ongoing.

What remains is a story about the compression of startup lifecycle. Frank went from idea to exit in under five years. It pivoted from fintech-adjacent to actual fintech. It claimed hockey-stick growth in a market desperate for youth engagement. And it sold to an acquirer that wanted the story — the brand, the founder, the user base — without fully interrogating whether the story was true.

The Frank case has become a cautionary tale cited in M&A negotiations and due diligence processes. For founders, it's a reminder that inflated metrics eventually collide with reality. For acquirers, it's evidence that even sophisticated buyers can be blinded by narrative. For the startup ecosystem, it's a data point on what happens when growth-at-all-costs culture meets the actual costs.

The legal battle will determine Lee's culpability. But the path is already clear: a legitimate problem (FAFSA complexity), a reasonable solution (simplification software), a logical pivot (banking for students), inflated metrics, a rushed acquisition, and a spectacular unraveling. The story Frank told worked right up until someone checked if it was true.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Quibi
entries.descriptor: The billion-dollar bet that mobile video would be different
entries.content:
# Quibi

***The billion-dollar bet that mobile video would be different***

> **Years:** 2018-2020

Jeffrey Katzenberg had run Disney's animation renaissance and co-founded DreamWorks. Meg Whitman had led eBay through explosive growth and run for governor of California. When they announced their new venture in 2018, investors lined up. Here were two proven executives with a simple thesis: people wanted premium short-form video designed for mobile phones. They called it Quibi — quick bites — and raised $1.75 billion before launching a single product.

The idea emerged from Katzenberg's belief that Hollywood had missed the mobile revolution. YouTube was amateur. Netflix was for the couch. Instagram was social. But nobody was making professional entertainment specifically engineered for phones — vertical video, episodes under ten minutes, designed to be watched while commuting or waiting in line. Katzenberg saw white space. He had connections to every studio and star in Hollywood. Whitman had the operational expertise to build the platform. The timing seemed perfect.

They spent two years before launch. The budget was staggering — $1.1 billion for content in year one alone. Steven Spielberg developed a horror series that could only be watched after dark. Guillermo del Toro, Idris Elba, Liam Hemsworth, Jennifer Lopez — A-list talent lined up. They shot in "Turnstyle" — a proprietary technology that let viewers rotate their phones between vertical and horizontal, with both versions filmed simultaneously. The shows weren't long-form chopped into pieces. They were architected for mobile from conception.

The business model was equally bold. Quibi would launch with two tiers: $4.99 with ads, $7.99 without. Content would disappear after viewing — no screenshots, no sharing clips, no watching on TV. This wasn't just mobile-first. It was mobile-only. The restrictions were features, not bugs. Quibi would be scarce in an age of infinite content, premium in an age of free.

They launched April 6, 2020.

The timing was catastrophic. COVID-19 had locked down America two weeks earlier. Nobody was commuting. Nobody was waiting in lines. The entire thesis — entertainment for in-between moments — assumed a world that had just stopped existing. People were home, watching Netflix on TVs. Quibi had optimized for a use case that vanished overnight.

But timing wasn't the only problem. The restrictions that seemed like features revealed themselves as liabilities. Users couldn't share clips, which meant no organic growth through social media. They couldn't cast to TVs, which made the vertical video feel gimmicky rather than revolutionary. The content itself, while expensive, wasn't generating buzz. A show about a woman shrunk to miniature size. A Dummy about a sex doll. Nothing broke through. The cultural conversation happened elsewhere.

The download numbers looked promising at first — 1.7 million in the first week. Then they collapsed. By May, the app had fallen out of the top 50 on both iOS and Android. Free trial conversions were dismal. Katzenberg blamed the pandemic in interviews. "If we knew on March 1 what we know now, you would say it's not a good idea to launch," he told the New York Times. But the problems ran deeper than circumstances.

YouTube had trained a generation to expect free. Netflix and Disney+ offered unlimited libraries for the same price or less. TikTok had proven that mobile video could be massive — but through user-generated content and algorithmic feeds, not Hollywood production values and curated releases. Quibi was premium in form but entered a market where premium was measured by volume, not quality per minute.

The content spend accelerated anyway. By summer they'd announced a second year of programming. But the users weren't coming. By June, only 72,000 people were paying subscribers. The company had spent over $1.75 billion and captured a rounding error of the streaming market. Advisors suggested pivoting — allow TV casting, enable sharing, drop the mobile-only restriction. Each suggestion undermined the original thesis. If Quibi became just another streaming service, what was the point?

October 21, 2020 — six months after launch — Katzenberg and Whitman announced Quibi was shutting down. "We feel that we've exhausted all our options," Whitman wrote to employees. They returned $350 million to investors — a remarkable outcome in startup failure, possible only because they'd barely spent half the war chest. The content library was sold piecemeal. Roku bought the technology and shows for an undisclosed sum reported to be under $100 million.

The post-mortems were immediate. Some blamed COVID-19. Some blamed the restrictions. Some blamed the content. But the deeper failure was conceptual. Katzenberg and Whitman had built for a user behavior they believed should exist rather than observing what did exist. They'd raised too much money, which removed the pressure to learn and adjust. They'd hired Hollywood royalty, which reinforced their assumptions rather than challenging them. And they'd launched into a market where the answer to "what should I watch on my phone?" was already answered — by TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, and every other streaming service that worked on mobile as well as it worked anywhere else.

Quibi proved that pedigree and capital can't manufacture a market. Sometimes the gap you see is a gap nobody wants filled.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Runway
entries.descriptor: The AI video company that raised a billion dollars before making a product anyone could use
entries.content:
# Runway

***The AI video company that raised a billion dollars before making a product anyone could use***

> **Years:** 2018-present

In 2018, three young artists — Cristóbal Valenzuela, Anastasis Germanidis, and Alejandro Matamala — met at NYU's Interactive Telecommunications Program. They were not engineers trying to build a business. They were creative people frustrated by creative tools. Photoshop hadn't fundamentally changed in years. Video editing was still frame-by-frame tedium. The gap between imagination and execution felt unnecessarily wide.

They had been experimenting with machine learning models — the early generative systems coming out of academic labs. What if these tools could be wrapped in interfaces artists could actually use? What if you didn't need a PhD to apply a neural style transfer or remove a background? They called the project Runway, after the fashion show. A place where ideas walked.

The first version, released in 2019, was a desktop app that packaged open-source AI models into a usable interface. Green screen removal. Object detection. Style transfer. Nothing proprietary. Just access. It found an audience immediately among motion designers, indie filmmakers, TikTok creators — people who wanted the magic without the command line. It was Photoshop-like, but weirder. And it hinted at something larger.

OpenAI had just released GPT-2. DeepMind was making headlines with protein folding. The AI moment was beginning. Valenzuela and his co-founders realized they were sitting at the intersection of two waves: generative AI and creator tools. They raised a $2 million seed round in 2019.

The insight was correct. The timing was early. Generative models in 2019 were impressive in labs and bad in production. Image synthesis was blurry. Video was basically impossible. The infrastructure didn't exist. GPUs were expensive. Latency was fatal. Runway spent two years building incremental features while waiting for the models to catch up.

Then, in 2021, everything accelerated. OpenAI released CLIP and DALL-E. Stability AI open-sourced Stable Diffusion. Suddenly text-to-image wasn't science fiction. It was shipping. Runway pivoted hard. They stopped thinking of themselves as a tool company. They started thinking of themselves as a model company.

In 2022, they released Gen-1, a text-to-video model trained in partnership with academic researchers. It was rough — short clips, low resolution, occasional incoherence — but it worked. You typed a prompt. You got video. No other consumer product could do that. They raised $50 million.

Six months later, they released Gen-2. Better quality. Longer clips. More control. The demos were stunning. A teddy bear walking through Times Square. A drone shot over a forest that never existed. The creative community went wild. Every AI-curious filmmaker and motion designer wanted access. Runway's waitlist exploded.

But the product was not ready. Generation times were slow. Results were inconsistent. Most prompts produced garbage. The good outputs required dozens of iterations. The system consumed enormous compute. Each video cost Runway real money to generate. They were giving it away for free to build hype while bleeding cash on infrastructure.

In 2023, they raised $141 million at a $1.5 billion valuation. The pitch was clear: Runway would be the foundational model company for video, the way OpenAI was for text and Stability was for images. They would train their own models. They would build the infrastructure. They would own the stack.

Except video is orders of magnitude harder than images. A single frame of video is an image. A second of video is 24-30 images that need to be temporally coherent. A minute of video is 1,440-1,800 frames that need to follow physics, maintain object permanence, and respect narrative logic. The compute required is staggering. The training data is scarcer and messier. The model architectures are unsolved problems.

Runway kept shipping. Gen-2 got better. They added motion brush tools, camera controls, image-to-video. The interface became more sophisticated. But the core problem remained: generating good video was still expensive, slow, and unreliable. Professional filmmakers tested it and went back to After Effects. Hobbyists made viral clips and hit paywalls. The product was a demo, not a tool.

Meanwhile, competition emerged. Stability AI released Stable Video Diffusion. Pika Labs launched with better generation speed. Google had its own internal video models. Meta was researching video. The window of differentiation was closing.

By 2024, Runway had raised over $230 million and was reportedly raising more at a $4 billion valuation. They had built a team of world-class researchers. They had released multiple model versions. They had hundreds of thousands of users. But they still hadn't shipped a product that professionals would pay meaningful money to use at scale.

The founders had been right about the wave. They had been early in a way that mattered. They had built a brand and a community. But they had also made a bet: that being first in generative video would create a durable moat. That raising at escalating valuations would attract talent and compute. That the models would improve fast enough to justify the hype.

Now they are running a race. Behind them, larger companies with more resources are catching up. Ahead of them, the technical problems are not obviously solvable on any timeline that matches their valuation. Around them, the burn rate is high and the revenue model is unclear.

Runway is not dead. It may yet win. Generative video may become the platform they imagined. But the path from here to there is narrow, expensive, and crowded. They bet everything on being the company that turned imagination into video. Whether that bet pays off depends on models, markets, and time — and time, in AI, moves faster than any roadmap.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: FTX
entries.descriptor: The crypto empire built on customer money
entries.content:
# FTX

***The crypto empire built on customer money***

> **Years:** 2019-2022

Sam Bankman-Fried started his career as a trader at Jane Street Capital, where he learned arbitrage and risk management in traditional markets. In 2017, he left to start Alameda Research, a quantitative crypto trading firm. The insight was simple: crypto markets were inefficient. Bitcoin traded at different prices on different exchanges. Sometimes the spread was 10%, 20%, even 30%. You could buy on one exchange, sell on another, and pocket the difference.

Alameda printed money. By 2019, Bankman-Fried — known universally as SBF — had identified a different opportunity. Crypto traders needed better infrastructure. The existing exchanges were slow, crashed during volatile periods, and lacked sophisticated products. He recruited Gary Wang, an MIT classmate and Google engineer, and Nishad Singh, a Facebook engineer. Together they built FTX: a cryptocurrency exchange designed by traders for traders.

FTX launched in May 2019 with a focus on derivatives — futures, options, leveraged tokens. The interface was clean. The matching engine was fast. It offered products professional traders wanted but couldn't find elsewhere. Within months, FTX was processing billions in daily volume. The growth was extraordinary even by crypto standards.

SBF had a story investors loved. He was an effective altruist who planned to earn billions and give it all away. He lived simply, drove a Corolla, slept on a beanbag. He spoke the language of rationalism and expected value maximization. In 2021, FTX raised $900 million at an $18 billion valuation. Six months later, another $400 million at $32 billion. The cap table included Sequoia, SoftBank, BlackRock, Tiger Global.

FTX moved fast. It acquired smaller exchanges and trading platforms. It bought naming rights to the Miami Heat arena for $135 million. It hired Tom Brady and Gisele Bündchen as brand ambassadors. It ran Super Bowl ads. SBF became the face of crypto regulation, testifying before Congress, meeting with regulators, arguing that clear rules would legitimize the industry.

Behind the growth was a structure that would prove fatal. Alameda Research and FTX were separate companies in theory. In practice, they were intertwined. Alameda was FTX's largest trading client. It had special privileges on the platform — exemptions from liquidation protocols, the ability to maintain a negative balance. This was justified as market-making necessity. Major trading firms need special arrangements. Nothing unusual.

What was unusual: Alameda was borrowing customer funds from FTX. Not as formal loans. The customer deposits sat in accounts controlled by Alameda. When Alameda needed capital for trading, it used FTX customer money. Billions of dollars of customer assets were lent to a trading firm that was supposed to be independent. The separation was fiction.

For a while, it worked. Crypto was booming. Assets were appreciating. Alameda's bets were winning more than losing. FTX kept growing. The system seemed to function. But the structure was inherently unstable. It depended on continued growth, continued confidence, continued market conditions that made the borrowing invisible.

In 2022, the crypto market collapsed. Bitcoin fell from $69,000 to under $20,000. Ethereum, Solana, and everything else fell harder. Alameda had enormous positions that were suddenly deeply underwater. The collateral securing its borrowing lost value. FTX faced a math problem: customer deposits that should have been available weren't. They were locked in Alameda's losing positions.

In November 2022, CoinDesk published a report on Alameda's balance sheet. It showed that most of Alameda's assets were FTT — FTX's own token. This meant Alameda was backed by value FTX itself had created, a circular arrangement. Binance's CEO, Changpeng Zhao — known as CZ — announced he would sell Binance's FTT holdings. FTT price dropped. Customers grew nervous.

They began withdrawing. On November 6, FTX saw $5 billion in withdrawal requests. It couldn't meet them. The money wasn't there. SBF scrambled to raise emergency funding. He convinced CZ to consider acquiring FTX. CZ did initial due diligence and backed out within 24 hours. The hole was too large.

On November 11, FTX filed for bankruptcy. The company's assets were missing. Somewhere between $8 billion and $10 billion in customer funds had disappeared — lent to Alameda, lost in bad trades, or in some cases simply stolen. SBF had transferred hundreds of millions to personal accounts. FTX's new CEO, brought in to manage the bankruptcy, called it the worst failure of corporate controls he had seen in his career.

The unraveling was fast. SBF was arrested in the Bahamas in December 2022 and extradited to the United States. At trial in 2023, Caroline Ellison, who ran Alameda and had been SBF's girlfriend, testified that he directed her to use FTX customer funds. Gary Wang and Nishad Singh, the co-founders who built the technology, also pleaded guilty and testified. The defense was that SBF was disorganized, not fraudulent — that he made mistakes but didn't intend to steal. The jury deliberated for four hours. Guilty on all seven counts.

FTX had seemed like the future — fast, sophisticated, well-funded, politically connected. It collapsed in a week. The story revealed that the gap between appearing successful and being legitimate can persist until the moment it can't. In markets built on confidence, that moment comes fast.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Stability AI
entries.descriptor: The open-source insurgent that couldn't stay stable
entries.content:
# Stability AI

***The open-source insurgent that couldn't stay stable***

> **Years:** 2019-present

Emad Mostaque didn't look like a typical AI founder when he took control of Stability AI in 2020. He was a hedge fund analyst turned entrepreneur with a philosophy degree from Oxford, more interested in democratizing technology than hoarding it behind API gates. The company had been founded in 2019 as a collective working on open-source machine learning, but Mostaque saw something bigger: a chance to challenge the emerging AI oligarchy before it consolidated power.

His thesis was simple and radical. While OpenAI, Google, and others were building increasingly closed systems, Stability would release powerful AI models freely. Anyone could download them, modify them, run them locally. No usage fees, no content filters controlled by distant corporations, no gatekeepers deciding which use cases were acceptable. In a world racing toward AI as a service, Stability would offer AI as a tool.

The vehicle for this vision arrived in August 2022: Stable Diffusion. The image generation model wasn't the first of its kind — DALL-E 2 and Midjourney existed — but it was the first anyone could truly own. Within weeks of release, millions had downloaded it. Artists used it. Researchers built on it. Pornographers and propagandists exploited it. This was precisely Mostaque's point: technology shouldn't come with a corporate morality layer.

The market responded enthusiastically to the vision if not yet the business model. Stability raised $101 million in October 2022 at a $1 billion valuation. Coatue and Lightspeed led. The pitch was compelling: become the Red Hat of AI, offering open models with commercial support and services around them. Let others charge per token while Stability built the infrastructure layer everyone needed.

But creating revolutionary technology and building a sustainable company are different problems. Stable Diffusion was generating more controversy than revenue. Artists claimed it was trained on their work without permission, filing class-action lawsuits. Getty Images sued for copyright infringement over its training data. Meanwhile, the model was free — thousands ran it on their own hardware, paying Stability nothing.

The financial reality was brutal. Training large models cost tens of millions in compute. Stability was burning roughly $8 million monthly by mid-2023 while generating minimal revenue. The team had grown to over 200 people across multiple continents, an unfocused sprawl working on audio models, video generation, language models, and various other projects. There was innovation but no coherence.

Mostaque's leadership style accelerated the chaos. He was a visionary who inspired loyalty but struggled with execution. He made grand promises about capabilities and timelines that engineering teams couldn't meet. He positioned himself as the face of open-source AI in countless interviews and podcasts while competitors quietly built businesses. Former employees would later describe a company where strategy shifted constantly, where resources were scattered across too many initiatives, where the CEO's priorities seemed more philosophical than practical.

By early 2024, the finances were catastrophic. Stability had burned through its funding and needed more. But the fundamentals looked terrible to investors. Despite Stable Diffusion's massive adoption, monetization remained elusive. Competition had intensified — Meta released its own open-source models, Midjourney dominated the commercial image generation market, and well-funded players were entering every category Stability touched.

In March 2024, Mostaque resigned as CEO. The stated reason was to pursue decentralized AI. The reality was investor pressure and a board that had lost confidence. He'd failed to build a business commensurate with the technology's impact. Stable Diffusion had been downloaded over 100 million times, influencing an entire generation of AI development, yet the company was nearly insolvent.

The new leadership, including former Weta Digital CTO Prem Akkaraju as CEO, pivoted toward enterprise deals and commercial licensing. They cut staff, focused resources, and tried to build actual revenue streams around the technology. But the window might have closed. Open-source alternatives existed for nearly everything Stability offered. The company that had democratized AI generation now had to compete in markets it had helped create but never dominated commercially.

The lawsuits continued. In July 2024, a consortium of music publishers sued over alleged copyright infringement in training data for audio models. The legal bills mounted while revenue stayed modest. By late 2024, Stability had raised additional funding but on terms suggesting desperation rather than strength. Valuation details remained undisclosed, generally a bad sign.

Stability AI had changed the AI landscape. By proving that powerful generative models could be open-sourced, it forced every major player to reconsider their strategies. Meta's Llama models exist partly because Stability demonstrated the viability of open distribution. The creative tools landscape was entirely reshaped by what Stability released.

But impact isn't sustainability. The company that championed accessible AI couldn't make the model accessible and profitable simultaneously. Mostaque's vision was correct about where technology should go and wrong about how companies get there. You can build in public or build a business, but doing both simultaneously requires execution discipline Stability never achieved. The revolution they sparked would be monetized by others — some who kept their models closed, others who open-sourced with clearer business models.

By 2025, Stability existed in an awkward middle space: too important to ignore, too troubled to bet on, too chaotic to stabilize. The name had become unintentionally ironic — a company built on the idea of democratizing AI, destabilized by the economics of doing so.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Cohere
entries.descriptor: The enterprise AI bet that stayed course while others chased consumers
entries.content:
# Cohere

***The enterprise AI bet that stayed course while others chased consumers***

> **Years:** 2019-present

In 2019, three researchers from Google Brain — Aidan Gomez, Ivan Zhang, and Nick Frosst — left to start Cohere. They had worked on the transformer architecture that would power the generative AI revolution, but they saw a different opportunity than most of their peers. While others would chase the spectacular — chatbots that could fool humans, image generators that could make art — Cohere aimed for the prosaic: helping businesses actually use large language models.

The founding insight was tactical. Gomez had co-authored "Attention Is All You Need," the 2017 paper that introduced transformers. He understood the technology's power. But he also understood its friction. Companies couldn't easily deploy these models. They were too large, too expensive, too complex. OpenAI and Anthropic would build towards artificial general intelligence. Cohere would build towards enterprise adoption.

They raised $40 million from Index Ventures in their Series A before they had a product. The pitch was credible because the founders were credible. Gomez was 23 but had the paper that mattered. The timing seemed perfect: GPT-3 had just demonstrated what was possible, but there was no clear path for enterprises to use it.

The early product was an API for language generation and understanding. Developers could fine-tune models for specific tasks without the infrastructure burden. The first customers were unglamorous — customer service platforms, content moderation systems, search functions. This was intentional. Cohere wanted repeatable use cases, not moonshots.

In 2021, they raised a $125 million Series B led by Tiger Global, at a reported $700 million valuation. The market was heating up. GPT-3 was now widely available through OpenAI's API. Competitors were emerging. But Cohere differentiated on deployment model: they offered models that could run on a company's own infrastructure, addressing data privacy concerns that OpenAI's cloud-only approach couldn't.

The strategy was "models as a service, but make it enterprise." They built smaller, more efficient models optimized for specific tasks. While OpenAI pursued scale — bigger models, more parameters — Cohere pursued efficiency. A 52-billion parameter model that performed like a larger one. Multilingual models that actually worked. Fine-tuning that didn't require a machine learning team.

Then ChatGPT launched in November 2022, and the market exploded. Suddenly every company needed an AI strategy. Cohere's enterprise focus, which had seemed conservative, looked prescient. They had relationships with companies that were now desperate to deploy LLMs safely. They had infrastructure for private deployment. They had fine-tuning tools that worked.

In June 2023, they raised $270 million in a Series C co-led by Inovia and Oracle, valuing the company at $2.2 billion. Oracle's involvement was telling — they integrated Cohere into their cloud platform. This was the distribution strategy: partner with enterprise infrastructure providers rather than compete with them.

But the competition intensified. OpenAI had launched an enterprise tier. Anthropic had Claude. Google had Bard. Every major cloud provider was offering foundation models. The question became: would enterprises want a specialized AI company, or would they default to their existing vendors?

Cohere's answer was differentiation through deployment flexibility and domain specificity. They launched Command, optimized for business use cases. They offered models that could run entirely on-premises. They built industry-specific solutions — legal document analysis, financial services, healthcare.

By 2024, they had hundreds of enterprise customers, including McKinsey, Salesforce, and LivePerson. They weren't growing as explosively as OpenAI, but they were growing profitably, with contracts measured in years rather than API calls. Revenue was reportedly in the hundreds of millions.

The path revealed a pattern: in a winner-take-all market, there might be room for enterprise specialists. While OpenAI captured headlines and consumer imagination, Cohere captured procurement departments and IT security teams. The technology was similar. The go-to-market was entirely different.

There were criticisms. Some argued their models lagged behind OpenAI and Anthropic in raw capability. Others said the enterprise focus limited their ceiling — they'd never be the default AI like Google became the default search. The counterargument was survival: many consumer AI companies were burning billions with no revenue model. Cohere had revenue, margins, and a sustainable business.

The company continued to raise. In 2024, reports suggested they were raising at a $5 billion valuation. The growth was steady rather than exponential. They weren't the story of AI — OpenAI owned that. But they were a story of how to build an AI company that enterprises would actually pay for.

What made Cohere's path distinct was its consistency. They never chased the consumer dream. They never tried to build AGI. They identified a wedge — enterprise deployment of LLMs — and drove it deeper. When the market validated generative AI, they had the relationships and infrastructure to capture enterprise spend.

The outcome remains unwritten. They could become the enterprise standard, acquired by a cloud giant or sustaining independently. They could be commoditized as every platform offers similar capabilities. They could break out with a killer application that transforms how businesses operate. But the path they chose — enterprise-first, deployment-flexible, efficiency-focused — was different from the path that captured the world's attention. And in a market where most AI companies would fail or be acquihired, different might be what matters.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Fast
entries.descriptor: The grocery app that burned a billion dollars in nine months
entries.content:
# Fast

***The grocery app that burned a billion dollars in nine months***

> **Years:** 2021-2022

Fast promised to eliminate checkout friction across the internet with a single click. It raised $102 million, burned through it in less than a year, and collapsed so quickly that its story became a cautionary tale about venture excess in the everything bubble.

Domm Holland founded Fast in 2019 after growing frustrated with online checkout experiences. The pitch was elegant: users create one Fast account, and any merchant using Fast's button lets them complete purchases instantly—no forms, no saved cards, no friction. Amazon had one-click for its own stores; Fast would bring it everywhere.

Holland was Australian, charismatic, and good at raising money. He had previously founded two companies in Australia with modest exits. Fast was his shot at Silicon Valley scale. He relocated to San Francisco, hired aggressively, and positioned Fast as infrastructure for the future of e-commerce. Stripe Capital and Index Ventures led the rounds.

The product launched in early 2020. Merchants could integrate Fast's checkout button with a few lines of code. Users signed up, saved payment methods, and could check out on any Fast-enabled site with one click. The demo was smooth. The technology worked. The question was whether anyone would use it.

They didn't. Not in meaningful numbers.

E-commerce checkout conversion rates matter enormously—a few percentage points separate profit from loss. Fast claimed its button improved conversion, but many merchants saw negligible impact. Amazon's one-click worked because customers already had Amazon accounts and trusted Amazon with their payment information. Fast was asking users to create yet another account and trust a startup they'd never heard of. The value proposition—saving thirty seconds on checkout—wasn't compelling enough to drive adoption.

By mid-2021, Fast had raised $102 million across two rounds at a $580 million valuation. It had fewer than 10,000 active users. Revenue was minimal—some reports suggested under $1 million annually. Burn rate was extraordinary.

Holland spent like Fast had already won. The company moved into expensive office space in San Francisco. It sponsored an Australian rugby team for $3 million. It ran Super Bowl ads—a $10 million commitment—in February 2021 when the company had barely any users. The ad showed people clicking Fast's checkout button. It generated mockery, not customers.

Inside Fast, morale was strange. Employees described a culture of optimism disconnected from metrics. Holland was convinced Fast would become ubiquitous. He spoke about IPOs and billion-dollar outcomes while daily active users remained in the low thousands. Engineering was strong; the product was technically sound. But no one wanted it enough to matter.

Holland raised a $20 million extension round in late 2021, bringing total funding to $120 million. But by then, reality was unavoidable. Burn rate was over $10 million per month. Revenue remained negligible. Growth was flat. The Super Bowl ad had been a spectacular waste. Venture investors who had been excited about Fast's potential started asking uncomfortable questions about unit economics and customer acquisition costs.

Fast tried pivoting. It explored Fast Checkout for crypto. It considered becoming payment infrastructure for Web3. Nothing gained traction. The problem wasn't the technology—it was that solving checkout friction required either network effects Fast couldn't build or integration depth it couldn't achieve without becoming a payment processor itself, competing with Stripe and PayPal.

In early 2022, Fast began talks with potential acquirers. No one wanted it. The product had users but not enough. The technology was competent but not proprietary. The brand was damaged by the Super Bowl debacle. Stripe, the obvious buyer, wasn't interested—they already had Stripe Checkout and far more merchant relationships.

By March 2022, Fast was out of money. Holland tried raising again. No one bit. Investors who had poured $120 million into Fast were unwilling to put in more. The math didn't work, and belief was gone.

Fast shut down on April 5, 2022. It announced the closure via blog post and Twitter. Holland posted a selfie with the caption "you either win or you learn." Employees were laid off with minimal severance. Vendors went unpaid. The company had existed for less than three years, spent over $120 million, and generated perhaps $600,000 in total revenue.

The collapse was dissected across tech media as emblematic of late-stage venture bubbles. Here was a company that raised nine figures for a product with no meaningful demand, spent recklessly on brand before achieving product-market fit, and collapsed when the money stopped. Competitors like Bolt—another one-click checkout startup—watched nervously, aware Fast's failure would taint the entire category.

Domm Holland eventually started another company. Investors moved on. The Fast domain went dark. What remains is the story—an almost perfect case study in mistaking capital for validation, spending for progress, and belief for evidence.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Wirecard
entries.descriptor: The fraud that fooled everyone until it couldn't
entries.content:
# Wirecard

***The fraud that fooled everyone until it couldn't***

> **Years:** 1999-2020

Wirecard began in 1999 as a payment processor for online gambling and pornography — the kinds of businesses that traditional banks didn't want to touch. Markus Braun, an Austrian consultant with a PhD in information systems, took control in 2002 and saw an opportunity. Europe's payment infrastructure was fragmented. E-commerce was exploding. Wirecard could be the platform that connected merchants to customers across borders, handling the messy work of reconciling currencies, regulations, and risk.

The pitch was compelling: Wirecard wasn't just processing payments, it was providing the entire technology stack. While competitors like PayPal focused on consumer-facing products, Wirecard sold itself as the behind-the-scenes infrastructure for enterprise clients. By the mid-2000s, it had moved beyond its sketchy origins to sign legitimate retailers. The company went public in 2005.

Braun was methodical, reserved, technical. He spoke the language of digital transformation before it became a cliché. Germany's business establishment embraced him. Here was a genuine tech company emerging from Munich — not another industrial manufacturer, but a payments platform that could compete with Silicon Valley. By 2018, Wirecard joined the DAX 30, replacing Commerzbank. It was worth more than Deutsche Bank. Braun became the first tech CEO in the DAX.

The growth story was extraordinary. Revenue grew from €40 million in 2005 to €2 billion by 2017. The stock price climbed relentlessly. Wirecard announced partnerships with Softbank, signed deals with major airlines, and launched a mobile banking app. It was expanding into Asia, where Braun claimed the real growth would come. The company projected it would handle €1 trillion in payment volume by 2025.

But the story had problems. As early as 2008, there were questions. A report by a forensic analyst named Matthew Earl raised concerns about accounting irregularities. The Financial Times began investigating in 2015, publishing articles that questioned Wirecard's revenue in Asia, alleged round-tripping of funds, and suggested the company was inflating profits. Braun dismissed the reports as attacks from short sellers trying to manipulate the stock. German regulators investigated — the Financial Times journalists, not Wirecard. BaFin, Germany's financial regulator, banned short-selling of Wirecard shares. Ernst & Young, the auditor, signed off on the books year after year.

The scale of the deception became clear in June 2020. Wirecard announced that €1.9 billion in cash — representing nearly a quarter of its balance sheet — didn't exist. The money was supposedly held in escrow accounts at two Philippine banks for the benefit of Wirecard's merchant acquiring business. The banks denied ever holding the funds. EY refused to sign off on the 2019 audit. Within days, Wirecard admitted the cash "probably doesn't exist."

Braun resigned and was arrested. The company filed for insolvency. The stock, which had been worth €104 billion at its peak, became worthless. As prosecutors dug into the wreckage, the picture that emerged was staggering. The fraud wasn't a recent desperation move — it appeared to have been running for years, possibly since 2015 or earlier. Revenue from Wirecard's Asian operations, the supposed engine of growth, had been fabricated. A network of shell companies with vague business purposes had circulated money to create the appearance of legitimate transactions. Internal documents suggested senior executives knew. The company had been booking revenue from partners that didn't exist and customers who had never signed contracts.

Jan Marsalek, the company's COO and Braun's deputy, disappeared. Intelligence agencies later reported he had fled to Belarus and then Russia. Before his disappearance, there were allegations he had connections to Russian intelligence services, that Wirecard had been conducting espionage on journalists investigating the company, and that parts of the operation functioned less as a tech company and more as a sophisticated money-laundering network.

The questions multiplied. How did EY miss it during annual audits? Why did BaFin defend Wirecard so aggressively? Why did German politicians embrace Braun so enthusiastically? The answers pointed to a system that wanted to believe. Germany wanted a tech champion. The financial establishment wanted to believe the growth story. Auditors relied on documents that looked legitimate. Regulators trusted a DAX-listed company more than suspicious journalists.

Braun went to trial in 2022, maintaining he was deceived by Marsalek and others. The prosecution argued he orchestrated the fraud from the beginning. As of 2024, the trial continues, with over 200 hours of testimony. Marsalek remains at large. Investors lost billions. The insolvency administrators recovered a fraction of the company's claimed assets. 

Wirecard's technology platform, the actual operational business that processed real payments, was sold off in pieces. Some of it still functions, stripped of the brand and the fiction, processing transactions for merchants who need payment infrastructure. The legitimate business existed. It just wasn't worth €24 billion. It was never the revolutionary platform Braun described. It was a mid-tier payment processor that its CEO wrapped in a story too compelling to question — until the story demanded cash that didn't exist.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Enron
entries.section: The smartest guys in the room
entries.descriptor: The accounting fraud that became synonymous with corporate collapse
entries.content:
# Enron

***The accounting fraud that became synonymous with corporate collapse***

> **Years:** 1985–2001

In 1985, Kenneth Lay merged two natural gas pipeline companies to form Enron. The timing seemed perfect: deregulation was sweeping through American energy markets, and Lay, a former federal energy regulator, knew how to work the system. The company would transport natural gas through thousands of miles of pipeline. Steady, predictable, boring—the kind of infrastructure business that pays reliable dividends and puts executives to sleep.

Jeffrey Skilling had other ideas. A McKinsey consultant who studied Enron's business, Skilling saw pipelines as primitive. The real opportunity was creating markets. In 1990, Lay brought him in to launch Enron Finance Corp, which would trade natural gas like commodities traders moved pork bellies and wheat. Instead of owning assets, Enron would be an intermediary, matching buyers and sellers, taking spreads, creating liquidity where none existed.

Skilling convinced Lay to adopt mark-to-market accounting—a technique banks used where you book the projected future profits of a deal immediately, rather than recognizing revenue as it actually comes in. Sign a twenty-year gas supply contract, run your model, and record the net present value of all those future profits today. For a company built on long-term contracts, it transformed financial statements overnight. Revenues exploded. Wall Street loved it. Skilling became president and COO.

The business seemed brilliant. Enron would create markets in anything: electricity, bandwidth, weather derivatives, advertising time. You had surplus capacity in anything? Enron would build a trading platform. They launched Enron Online in 1999, an electronic marketplace that became one of the world's largest e-commerce sites. At its peak, Enron traded $2.5 billion in commodities daily.

The culture reflected Skilling's philosophy. He instituted rank-and-yank: employees rated on a forced curve, bottom 15% fired annually. Traders made fortunes. The Houston headquarters became a monument to ambition—traders on trading floors, glass and steel architecture, contemporary art in the lobbies. Fortune named Enron "America's Most Innovative Company" six years straight.

But mark-to-market created a perverse incentive: deals mattered more than their actual performance. Book the revenue now, worry about delivery later. Traders learned to game their own models. Assume high future prices. Assume low future costs. Assume the deal performs perfectly for twenty years. The accounting department became a profit center.

Andrew Fastow, the CFO, took it further. He created hundreds of special purpose entities—off-balance-sheet partnerships that existed in accounting limbo. These partnerships would buy Enron assets, often at inflated prices, removing debt from Enron's balance sheet while booking gains. Fastow named them after Star Wars characters: Chewbacca, Jedi, Raptor. He personally managed some, earning millions in management fees while employed as Enron's CFO—an obvious conflict the board approved.

The SPEs became a shell game. Enron would transfer underperforming assets to them, booking profits. The partnerships needed equity to remain off-balance-sheet, so Enron guaranteed them with its own stock. As long as Enron's stock price rose, the scheme held. Fastow created partnerships to hedge other partnerships. The whole structure was collateralized by Enron stock and faith in Enron's future.

The California energy crisis of 2000–2001 revealed the culture's rot. Enron traders manipulated electricity markets, creating artificial shortages, driving up prices. Internal memos later showed them joking about "stealing money from California grandmothers." They created strategies with names like "Fat Boy" and "Death Star." Rolling blackouts hit the state while Enron posted record profits.

In early 2001, Skilling became CEO. Lay moved to chairman. But cracks appeared. An executive named Sherron Watkins sent Lay an anonymous memo warning that Enron would "implode in a wave of accounting scandals." Analysts started asking questions about the SPEs on quarterly calls. Skilling grew defensive, famously calling one "an asshole" on a conference call.

In August 2001, Skilling abruptly resigned, citing personal reasons. The stock, which had hit $90, was trading in the $40s. Lay reassured investors, bought stock himself. But in October, Enron disclosed a $638 million third-quarter loss and a $1.2 billion reduction in shareholder equity related to the partnerships. The SEC opened an inquiry.

The credit rating agencies, suddenly attentive, began downgrading Enron's debt. This triggered clauses in the SPE agreements requiring Enron to post more collateral or issue more stock. Which lowered the stock price. Which triggered more collateral requirements. The death spiral accelerated.

Banks stopped lending. Trading partners stopped trading—Enron's business depended on counterparty confidence. In November, Dynegy agreed to acquire Enron for $8 billion, then backed out after examining the books. On December 2, 2001, Enron filed for bankruptcy—the largest in American history at the time.
