The stock, once $90, was pennies.

Twenty thousand employees lost jobs. Many had retirement accounts heavily invested in Enron stock, encouraged by management even as executives sold their own shares. Arthur Andersen, Enron's accounting firm, was convicted of obstruction for shredding documents and effectively dissolved. Congress passed Sarbanes-Oxley, reshaping corporate governance and accounting oversight.

Skilling got twenty-four years (later reduced). Fastow got six. Lay was convicted but died before sentencing. The company that revolutionized energy trading became a case study in fraud.

What killed Enron wasn't bad luck or market forces. It was a culture that rewarded appearance over substance, that confused projections with reality, that turned accounting into storytelling. Mark-to-market let them book future profits. The SPEs let them hide present losses. As long as the stock price rose, reality could be deferred. When it fell, there was nothing underneath.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: WorldCom
entries.section: The accounting fraud that dwarfed Enron
entries.descriptor: The fraud that became too big to hide
entries.content:
# WorldCom

***The fraud that became too big to hide***

> **Years:** 1983-2002

In 1983, Bernie Ebbers was a motel manager in Mississippi. He had no background in telecommunications, no technical expertise, no venture capital connections. What he had was timing and appetite. When AT&T was broken up that year, the long-distance market opened to competition. Ebbers and a group of small investors pooled $650,000 to start Long Distance Discount Services, later renamed WorldCom.

The strategy was simple: buy capacity wholesale from AT&T's network, resell it at a discount, undercut the big carriers on price. It worked. Small businesses that had paid inflated rates to AT&T switched eagerly. Within two years, WorldCom was profitable. Within five, it was growing fast enough to go public.

But Ebbers didn't want to build a company. He wanted to build an empire.

The telecommunications industry in the 1990s was consolidating rapidly. Ebbers saw that the winners would be those who controlled the most fiber, the most network capacity, the most customers. So he started acquiring. Between 1991 and 1997, WorldCom bought more than sixty companies. Some were small regional carriers. Others were significant players. The deals were financed through stock swaps and debt — WorldCom's rising share price made it currency for expansion.

The largest deal came in 1997: MFS Communications, which owned UUNET, the backbone provider carrying much of the internet's traffic. At $14 billion, it was the largest merger in tech history to that point. Suddenly WorldCom wasn't just a discount long-distance reseller. It owned critical internet infrastructure. Wall Street loved the story. The stock soared.

Then came the deal that made WorldCom a giant: MCI, the second-largest long-distance carrier in America. MCI had been negotiating with British Telecom when Ebbers swooped in with a $37 billion bid. It was hostile, audacious, and successful. Overnight, WorldCom became the second-largest telecommunications company in the world, carrying half of all internet traffic and serving millions of business customers.

Ebbers was celebrated as a visionary. He appeared on magazine covers. Analysts praised his strategy of "roll-up growth" — buying competitors, cutting costs, capturing economies of scale. The company's headquarters remained in Clinton, Mississippi, a small town where Ebbers still lived. He drove a pickup truck. He owned a ranch. He seemed like the anti-corporate CEO, building an empire without losing touch.

But the growth concealed a problem. The acquisitions were expensive. The debt was mounting. And the telecom market was changing. The dot-com boom had led to massive overinvestment in fiber optic capacity. By 2000, there was far more bandwidth than demand. Prices for long-distance and data services collapsed. Every major carrier saw revenues stagnate or decline.

WorldCom had always grown by acquiring revenue. Now revenue was flat, and the stock price was falling. Wall Street expected growth. Ebbers needed to show growth. So he began manufacturing it.

The fraud started small. In 1999, under pressure to meet earnings targets, WorldCom's CFO Scott Sullivan began reclassifying operating expenses as capital expenditures. The distinction is simple: operating expenses reduce profits immediately; capital expenditures are spread over time. By moving costs from one category to the other, WorldCom could report higher profits without any actual improvement in performance.

At first, the amounts were modest — millions, not billions. But as the telecom market worsened, the gap between reality and expectations widened. So the fraud escalated. By 2001, WorldCom was booking $3.8 billion in false profits. Quarterly earnings reports showed steady growth while the underlying business hemorrhaged cash.

Ebbers didn't invent the fraud, but he created the pressure that made it necessary. He borrowed personally against his WorldCom stock to finance other ventures — timber, hotels, a yacht-building company. As the stock fell, margin calls forced him to borrow more. The board extended him over $400 million in loans. He needed the stock price to recover. Sullivan needed to make the numbers work. Auditors asked questions, but Sullivan had answers. The fraud continued.

In March 2002, the SEC began investigating WorldCom's accounting. Ebbers resigned the next month, though he maintained ignorance of any wrongdoing. Internal auditors, suspicious of irregularities, began their own investigation. On June 25, 2002, Cynthia Cooper, WorldCom's head of internal audit, informed the board that the company had fraudulently inflated profits by $3.8 billion.

The number would eventually reach $11 billion — the largest accounting fraud in U.S. history to that point.

WorldCom filed for bankruptcy on July 21, 2002. It was the largest bankruptcy in American history, displacing Enron, which had collapsed just seven months earlier. Seventeen thousand employees lost their jobs. Investors lost $180 billion in market value. Pension funds that had trusted WorldCom's blue-chip status were devastated.

Ebbers was convicted of fraud, conspiracy, and filing false statements in 2005. He was sentenced to twenty-five years in prison. Sullivan received five years after cooperating with prosecutors. The company that Ebbers built from $650,000 in a Mississippi motel room had become too big to survive its own lies.

WorldCom emerged from bankruptcy in 2004 as MCI Inc., a smaller, cleaner company. Verizon acquired it in 2006. The brand disappeared entirely. What remained was a cautionary tale: growth at any cost becomes cost at any price.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Lehman Brothers
entries.section: The bank that broke the world
entries.descriptor: The firm that survived wars and depressions but couldn't survive its own appetite
entries.content:
# Lehman Brothers

***The firm that survived wars and depressions but couldn't survive its own appetite***

> **Years:** 1850-2008

In 1844, a Bavarian immigrant named Henry Lehman opened a dry goods store in Montgomery, Alabama. His brothers Emanuel and Mayer joined him. They accepted cotton as payment from farmers, then sold it to textile mills. By the time the Civil War ended, they were cotton brokers. By 1870, they were in New York, trading commodities. By 1906, they were underwriting securities. The American dream, compounding across generations.

For 150 years, Lehman Brothers survived everything. The panic of 1873. The crash of 1929. The Depression. World War II. The stagflation of the 1970s. Every crisis that killed competitors made Lehman stronger, more careful, more aware of risk. The firm had institutional memory. Partners who remembered losses kept the next generation cautious.

Then came 1994. The partnership structure dissolved. Lehman became a public company. Suddenly, partners weren't risking their own capital. They were managing other people's money. The incentives changed. The culture changed. The memory faded.

Richard Fuld became CEO in 1994. He was a trader, not a banker. He'd spent his career at Lehman, surviving the near-collapse of 1984 when American Express almost let them die. He was loyal, intense, and obsessed with never being vulnerable again. Under Fuld, Lehman would become bigger, more profitable, more aggressive than any competitor. No one would push them around. No one would acquire them. They would be the predator.

The strategy was simple: leverage. Lehman borrowed money to buy mortgage-backed securities and other assets. The more leverage, the higher the returns. In 2003, Lehman's leverage ratio was 24:1 — for every dollar of equity, they controlled twenty-four dollars of assets. By 2007, it reached 31:1. They were a $700 billion firm on $25 billion of equity.

The bet was on housing. Lehman didn't just invest in mortgages — they originated them, securitized them, traded them, and held them. They owned subprime lenders. They created collateralized debt obligations. They financed other firms doing the same. When housing prices rose, this flywheel printed money. Lehman's profits quadrupled between 2003 and 2007. Fuld became a billionaire. The stock hit $86.

The culture became brutal. Fuld was called "the Gorilla." Executives who questioned the mortgage bet were sidelined or fired. Mike Gelband, the head of fixed income, warned in 2006 that the mortgage market was overheated. He was pushed out. Madelyn Antoncic, the chief risk officer, raised concerns about leverage. She was marginalized, then left. The dissidents were gone by 2007, just as the market turned.

The first cracks appeared in June 2007. Two Bear Stearns hedge funds collapsed under mortgage losses. Lehman's stock fell but recovered. Fuld insisted this was a buying opportunity. Lehman increased its mortgage holdings. By November 2007, they held $85 billion in mortgage-related assets — more than their entire equity base.

Then housing prices began to fall. Not slowly. Not predictably. The entire market seized. Mortgage-backed securities couldn't be sold at any price. Lehman's assets became illiquid. Their leverage, which magnified gains, now magnified losses. In Q1 2008, they reported a $2.8 billion loss — their first since going public. Fuld called it a blip.

Bear Stearns collapsed in March 2008. JPMorgan acquired them with government backing. Lehman's stock plummeted. Fuld began seeking buyers. Korea Development Bank, HSBC, Bank of America — all looked, all walked away. The mortgage portfolio was too toxic. The leverage too extreme. No one could price the risk.

By summer 2008, Lehman was dying. They tried to raise capital. They tried to sell assets. They moved $50 billion of commercial real estate into a separate entity called SpinCo, hoping someone would buy it. No one did. The stock fell from $60 in January to $15 in August to $4 in September.

On September 10, 2008, Lehman reported a $3.9 billion loss. The stock fell to $3. Counterparties stopped trading with them. Credit default swaps — insurance against Lehman's failure — went vertical. The market was pricing in death.

That weekend, the government tried to broker a rescue. Bank of America and Barclays looked at the books. Both declined without government guarantees. Treasury Secretary Hank Paulson refused. Two weeks earlier, the government had rescued Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. A week earlier, they'd saved AIG. But Lehman? Paulson wanted a lesson taught. Moral hazard needed consequences.

On Monday, September 15, 2008, Lehman Brothers filed for bankruptcy. The largest bankruptcy in American history. $639 billion in assets. $619 billion in debt. Ten thousand employees lost their jobs that day. Fuld's billion-dollar stake became worthless.

The cascading effects were immediate. Money market funds held Lehman debt and "broke the buck." Credit markets froze. The global financial system seized. Within days, the government reversed course and bailed out AIG, then the entire banking system with TARP. But Lehman was already gone.

The post-mortems revealed the rot. Lehman had used accounting tricks called "Repo 105" to temporarily move $50 billion off their balance sheet before quarterly reports. They'd hidden losses. They'd lied to investors, regulators, and themselves. The institutional memory that once saved them had been replaced by quarterly bonuses and stock options.

Fuld blamed everyone — short sellers, the government, the media. He never acknowledged the leverage, the concentration, the warnings ignored. In Congressional testimony, he seemed genuinely confused about what had gone wrong. A 150-year-old firm had died in 18 months, and its CEO couldn't explain why.

The bankruptcy took years to unwind. Creditors recovered about 40 cents on the dollar. Barclays bought the North American operations for $1.75 billion — a rounding error on what Lehman was worth two years earlier. The London office became Nomura. The rest disappeared.

Lehman's collapse didn't cause the financial crisis, but it transformed it from a mortgage problem into a systemic panic. The government's decision to let them fail became the most analyzed choice of the era. Some say it was necessary. Some say it was catastrophic. Either way, it revealed that no institution, no matter how old or how connected, was immune to its own hubris.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Long-Term Capital Management
entries.descriptor: When the smartest guys in the room built a machine that couldn't fail — until it did
entries.content:
# Long-Term Capital Management

***When the smartest guys in the room built a machine that couldn't fail — until it did***

> **Years:** 1994-1998

In 1994, John Meriwether walked out of Salomon Brothers with a plan to build the most sophisticated hedge fund the world had ever seen. He had been forced out after the Treasury bond scandal, but his reputation as a master bond trader remained intact. More importantly, he had relationships with the kind of people who could turn finance into physics.

He assembled a partnership that read like a Nobel Prize committee. Myron Scholes and Robert Merton — who would win the Nobel in Economics in 1997 for their work on derivatives pricing. David Mullins, former vice chairman of the Federal Reserve. A team of PhD mathematicians and computer scientists from the Salomon arbitrage group. They weren't just traders. They were building a machine to extract inefficiency from global markets.

The pitch was elegant: markets occasionally misprice nearly identical securities. A German government bond and an Italian government bond with similar maturities should trade at predictable spreads. When they don't, you short the expensive one, buy the cheap one, wait for convergence, and collect the difference. It's arbitrage — theoretically risk-free profit from temporary dislocations.

But the spreads were tiny. To make real money, you needed massive leverage. Long-Term Capital Management started with $1.25 billion in investor capital — an enormous raise for a new fund. They immediately borrowed $125 billion more. The leverage ratio climbed from there.

The first three years were spectacular. Returns of 21%, then 43%, then 41%. By early 1998, LTCM managed $4.8 billion in equity with positions worth over $1 trillion in notional value. They had positions in every major market: U.S. Treasuries, European government bonds, Japanese stocks, emerging market debt, mortgage-backed securities. The computer models hummed. The partners collected hundreds of millions in management fees.

The models assumed that markets were rational, that price dislocations would converge, that historical correlations would hold, that liquidity would remain constant. The models were built on decades of data. They accounted for multiple standard deviations of risk. They were mathematically sound.

In May 1998, Russia began defaulting on its domestic debt. This should have been containable — LTCM's direct Russia exposure was modest. But the Russian default triggered something the models hadn't anticipated: a global flight to quality. Investors everywhere abandoned anything risky and piled into U.S. Treasuries.

Every one of LTCM's positions was a bet on convergence — that spreads would narrow, that correlations would normalize, that markets would be rational. Instead, everything moved in the same direction at once. Spreads widened. Positions that were supposed to be uncorrelated became perfectly correlated. The Italian bonds they were long fell. The German bonds they were short rose. Mortgage-backed securities collapsed. Emerging market debt cratered. Every bet lost simultaneously.

Worse: they couldn't exit. LTCM's positions were so large that attempting to unwind them would move markets against them. Other traders knew this. The fund was being picked apart by competitors who could see their forced selling. The leverage that amplified gains on the way up accelerated the destruction on the way down.

By August, LTCM had lost 44% of its capital. The partners injected their own money — $100 million from Meriwether alone. It didn't matter. The losses continued. $500 million in a single day. Then $700 million. By September, the fund was down to $600 million in equity supporting positions worth more than $100 billion. The leverage ratio exceeded 150 to 1.

The Federal Reserve convened an emergency meeting. Not to bail out LTCM directly — this wasn't a bank. But LTCM's collapse would force liquidation of massive positions across global markets. The shock could trigger a systemic crisis. Fourteen major banks were organized into a consortium. They injected $3.65 billion in exchange for 90% of the fund. It was a takeover, not a rescue.

The positions were unwound over months. By the time it was finished, the original investors had lost 90% of their capital. The partners lost nearly everything. Scholes and Merton's Nobel Prize became a symbol of hubris — awarded in October 1997, just as the fund was beginning its death spiral.

The aftermath revealed what the models had missed: that risk cannot be fully quantified, that rare events happen more often than statistics predict, that leverage transforms temporary losses into permanent catastrophe, that correlation becomes one in a crisis. LTCM had built a machine that worked perfectly — until it encountered a world that didn't conform to its assumptions.

Years later, Meriwether started another hedge fund. Several LTCM partners went on to prestigious roles in finance. The models got more sophisticated. The leverage remained.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Solyndra
entries.section: The green energy poster child that wasn't
entries.descriptor: The spectacular failure that became a political weapon
entries.content:
# Solyndra

***The spectacular failure that became a political weapon***

> **Years:** 2005-2011

In 2005, Chris Gronet saw an opportunity in solar's biggest constraint. Traditional solar panels — flat rectangles of crystalline silicon — could only capture light hitting them directly. They required expensive mounting systems, careful angling, optimal positioning. Gronet, a semiconductor industry veteran who had helped build Applied Materials into a manufacturing giant, thought he could do better.

His insight: cylindrical solar cells. Tubes instead of panels. They would capture sunlight from all angles throughout the day, requiring no tracking systems. They could be laid flat on commercial rooftops without the heavy mounting racks traditional panels demanded. Installation would be faster, cheaper, simpler. The tubes would use copper indium gallium selenide — CIGS — a thin-film semiconductor that required far less silicon than conventional cells.

This last point mattered enormously. In 2005, a global silicon shortage had sent prices soaring. Silicon-based solar companies were scrambling for supply. Gronet's tubes would sidestep that constraint entirely. He founded Solyndra with backing from CMEA Capital and U.S. Venture Partners.

The technology worked. The company built a working prototype. The cylindrical cells did capture light from multiple angles. The installation story made sense — commercial rooftops were indeed a massive market, and building managers cared more about installation cost than theoretical maximum efficiency. Solyndra raised $78 million in Series B funding in 2007.

Then came 2008. The financial crisis froze capital markets. Solyndra had been planning an IPO. Instead, the company needed a rescue. The Obama administration, elected on a platform including clean energy investment, had just received authority to issue loan guarantees under a program created during the Bush administration. Solyndra applied.

What happened next would become the most politically contentious loan guarantee in American history. In March 2009, Solyndra received a $535 million loan guarantee — the first issued under the program. Vice President Biden attended a September 2009 groundbreaking. President Obama visited the new factory in May 2010, holding up Solyndra as proof that clean energy meant American jobs and manufacturing leadership.

But the market was shifting beneath them. Chinese manufacturers, backed by massive government subsidies and access to cheap capital, were flooding the market with conventional silicon panels. Prices were falling faster than anyone had predicted. The silicon shortage that had been Solyndra's strategic opportunity disappeared. By late 2009, silicon was cheap again.

Solyndra's technology still worked. Their cylindrical cells still captured light from all angles. But the economic logic had reversed. When silicon was expensive, avoiding it made sense. When silicon was cheap, Solyndra's more complex manufacturing process became a liability rather than an advantage. Their cost per watt couldn't match Chinese panels on a free-falling price curve.

The company burned through cash trying to scale manufacturing fast enough to reach cost competitiveness. They raised more private capital — $535 million more between 2009 and 2011, mostly from existing investors including Argonaut Ventures, the investment arm of the Walton family. They restructured the government loan to allow private investors first claim on assets if the company failed. They built a second factory.

None of it mattered. The Chinese price decline was too steep, too fast. By summer 2011, Solyndra was selling panels for less than it cost to make them, with no path to profitability visible. On August 31, 2011, the company ceased operations. On September 6, it filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. 1,100 workers lost their jobs immediately.

The political explosion was instant and massive. Congressional Republicans had been skeptical of the loan guarantee from the start. Now they had a scandal. FBI agents raided Solyndra's headquarters. Congressional hearings began. Emails between White House officials and the Office of Management and Budget became front-page news. Had the administration rushed the loan for political reasons? Had they ignored warnings about Solyndra's viability?

The investigations found no evidence of criminal wrongdoing. What they revealed was a government loan program moving with unusual speed, a company caught by unpredictable market changes, and a clean energy sector experiencing cost declines faster than any forecasting model had predicted. Those declines — devastating to Solyndra — would eventually make solar power cost-competitive with fossil fuels, one of the great technology victories of the decade.

For Gronet and Solyndra, it didn't matter. The bankruptcy proceedings dragged on. The government loan guarantee program, which would ultimately turn a profit across its entire portfolio, was politically toxic. Solyndra became a shorthand for government waste, crony capitalism, picking winners and losers.

The technology itself was acquired by SQN Capital Management for $7.5 million — a fraction of the $2 billion invested. The manufacturing equipment was auctioned. The factory sat empty. The story became a cautionary tale about government intervention in markets, about the dangers of betting on specific technologies, about the perils of being too early or too confident in a market about to undergo massive disruption.

What the story actually revealed was simpler and more brutal: in a commodity market experiencing radical price decline, technical elegance cannot overcome economic physics. Solyndra's cylinders captured more light — but when panels became cheap enough to just install more of them, elegance became irrelevant.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Better Place
entries.section: The electric car infrastructure that wasn't
entries.descriptor: The billion-dollar bet on swappable batteries that couldn't outrun the charging curve
entries.content:
# Better Place

***The billion-dollar bet on swappable batteries that couldn't outrun the charging curve***

> **Years:** 2007-2013

Shai Agassi had the kind of résumé that made investors listen. By forty, he'd risen to the top ranks of SAP, in line to become CEO of one of Europe's largest software companies. But in 2007, he walked away to pursue an idea that seemed both audacious and inevitable: he would solve electric vehicles by solving the battery problem.

The insight was elegant. Electric cars weren't taking off because batteries were expensive, had limited range, and took hours to charge. What if you didn't own the battery? What if you could swap it out in minutes, like exchanging a propane tank? Build a network of battery-swapping stations, sell the car cheap, charge subscription fees for the battery service. The model worked for mobile phones — why not vehicles?

Agassi had charisma, connections, and a pitch that landed with the force of revelation. He raised $200 million before Better Place had built anything. Investors included HSBC, Morgan Stanley, Israel Corporation, and VantagePoint Capital Partners. Shimon Peres, Israel's president, became a vocal supporter. By 2009, Better Place had raised nearly $750 million, one of the largest cleantech venture rounds in history.

Israel became the test market. Small country, short distances, a government eager to reduce oil dependence. Better Place announced partnerships with Renault-Nissan, which would build the cars. The Fluence Z.E. sedan would be the vehicle, modified to accommodate Better Place's swappable battery system. Denmark signed on as a second market.

The technology was real. Better Place built battery-swap stations that could exchange a depleted battery for a charged one in under five minutes. Robotic arms lifted the car, removed the battery from beneath, and installed a fresh one. It worked. Videos of the swap in action looked like science fiction made manifest.

But the model required density to function. A driver needed confidence that a swap station would be nearby. That meant building hundreds of stations before selling many cars. Better Place began construction across Israel. Each station cost over $500,000. The company also installed thousands of public charging points as backup. The infrastructure came first, burning cash with no revenue to offset it.

Then the car arrived. The Fluence Z.E. wasn't what Agassi had promised. It was a converted sedan, not a vehicle designed from the ground up for electric propulsion. It was expensive even with the battery subscription model. It was, frankly, boring. Early adopters wanted Teslas, not rebranded Renaults.

Tesla, meanwhile, was proving a different thesis. Elon Musk bet that battery technology would improve fast enough to make swapping unnecessary. The Model S, launched in 2012, offered over 200 miles of range and access to a growing Supercharger network. Charging times were dropping. The technology curve was steeper than Agassi had anticipated.

Better Place had locked itself into Renault. No other manufacturer wanted to redesign vehicles around Better Place's proprietary swapping system. That meant customers had one car option. Tesla owners could choose from a growing array of electric vehicles. Better Place offered a single model few people wanted.

The economics never closed. Better Place needed scale to spread infrastructure costs, but couldn't achieve scale without more cars, and couldn't offer more cars without more manufacturers, and couldn't attract manufacturers without scale. The subscription model assumed high utilization, but customers in Israel didn't drive enough miles to generate sufficient revenue. Batteries degraded slower than projected, meaning fewer swaps.

Internal tensions mounted. Agassi was a visionary CEO, not an operational one. Executives clashed over strategy. Should they expand to new markets or fix Israel first? Should they pivot to charging instead of swapping? Agassi resisted changes that would invalidate the original vision. The board forced him out in October 2012.

His replacement, Evan Thornley, inherited a company burning through $10 million a month with no path to breakeven. Better Place had sold fewer than 1,400 cars in Israel. Denmark had failed to gain traction. The company went back to investors for more capital. No one wanted in. By early 2013, Better Place had burned through nearly $850 million.

In May 2013, Better Place filed for bankruptcy. The assets sold for $12 million to a scrap dealer. The battery-swap stations were dismantled. Customers who'd bought Fluence Z.E. vehicles were left with cars dependent on an infrastructure that no longer existed.

Agassi's fundamental error was betting against the technology curve. Battery swapping made sense if batteries remained expensive, low-range, and slow to charge. But all three constraints were loosening faster than Better Place could build network effects. By the time Better Place collapsed, Tesla was selling thousands of Model S sedans that charged faster, drove farther, and didn't require proprietary infrastructure.

The model assumed the battery problem was permanent. It wasn't. It was temporary, and Better Place built a billion-dollar solution to a problem that was already disappearing. The charging curve caught them.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Jawbone
entries.descriptor: The design darling that chased too many futures
entries.content:
# Jawbone

***The design darling that chased too many futures***

> **Years:** 1999-2017

In 1999, Alexander Asseily and Hosain Rahman founded Aliph in San Francisco with a single elegant idea: use military-grade noise cancellation technology to make phone calls clearer. Rahman, a Stanford engineering graduate, had watched his father struggle with hearing aids and saw opportunity in signal processing. Asseily brought capital and connections from his family's fortune. Their timing seemed perfect — mobile phones were everywhere, but call quality was terrible.

The first Jawbone Bluetooth headset launched in 2006 after seven years of development. It was beautiful. Industrial designer Yves Béhar gave it organic curves that looked nothing like the Star Trek cosplay of competing headsets. More importantly, it worked. The noise cancellation could filter out a jet engine while preserving voice. Early adopters loved it. Revenue hit $60 million in the first year.

But the consumer electronics market they'd entered was brutal. Bluetooth headsets were commoditizing fast. Chinese manufacturers flooded the market with $20 alternatives. Jawbone's products cost $120. They needed differentiation beyond design, so they added features — HD audio, motion sensors, voice commands. The headsets got better and more expensive. The mass market bought cheaper.

In 2009, Rahman made the first pivot: speakers. The Jambox launched in 2010 as a premium portable Bluetooth speaker with the same design DNA. It was a sensation. Colorful, small enough for a bag, loud enough for a party. Revenue exploded to $300 million by 2011. Suddenly Jawbone looked prescient. The tech press called Rahman a visionary. Sequoia Capital led a $49 million round at a $500 million valuation.

Rahman saw a bigger prize. Nike had launched the FuelBand in 2012. Fitbit was growing fast. Wearable fitness tracking looked like the next major platform. Jawbone had sensors, Bluetooth expertise, design credibility, and a brand associated with personal technology. They launched the UP fitness band in 2011.

The first version was a disaster. No display, no wireless syncing — users had to plug it into their phone's headphone jack. Worse, the bands broke. Constantly. Jawbone recalled the entire product line after four months. They redesigned, relaunched, iterated. UP 2, UP 3, UP 4. Each generation got better. Each absorbed more capital.

The problem was the competition. Fitbit had gone all-in on fitness tracking. They had better battery life, more reliable hardware, and a two-year head start building a social platform. Apple launched the Apple Watch in 2015 with infinite resources and integration throughout iOS. Jawbone's bands were prettier than Fitbit's and cheaper than Apple's, but caught between giants in a market that might only support one or two winners.

Rahman kept raising money. $93 million in 2013. $147 million in 2014. The valuations climbed past $1 billion, then $3 billion. Jawbone became one of the most valuable private hardware companies in Silicon Valley. But the revenue never matched the hype. The speaker business declined as Amazon Echo and Google Home redefined the category. The fitness tracker business bled cash competing with Fitbit's scale and Apple's ecosystem.

The unit economics never closed. Each UP band cost nearly as much to make and market as Jawbone sold it for. The company burned through $900 million in funding trying to reach a scale that would make the math work. They never got there. By 2015, Jawbone was in crisis. Layoffs began. The speaker business shut down. They bet everything on UP — now rebranded as health tracking, not just fitness.

In 2016, Rahman tried one more pivot. He started a stealth company called Jawbone Health Hub, focused on using sensor data and AI for medical applications. This was the real vision, he told investors: consumer hardware was just a data-gathering exercise for healthcare AI. Some investors bought it. Others walked.

The end came in 2017. Jawbone stopped paying vendors. A lawsuit from a manufacturing partner exposed the finances: the company had $164 million in debt and was insolvent. They liquidated the assets. Fitbit bought some patents and hired some engineers. Jawbone Health Hub continued briefly under the name Jawbone Health, but it was over.

The story of Jawbone is the story of being too good at too many things that mattered less than the one thing that mattered most: finding a sustainable business model before the money ran out. They had world-class design, genuine technical innovation, and strong brand recognition. What they didn't have was focus. Each pivot made sense in isolation. Each represented a rational response to market conditions. Together they added up to a company that never became essential enough in any single category to survive the competition.

Rahman had recognized three different waves — Bluetooth audio, portable speakers, wearable sensors — before the rest of the market. But recognizing waves and riding them to sustainable scale are different skills. Jawbone caught every wave and drowned anyway. The design awards and the press coverage and the sky-high valuations couldn't change the unit economics. In hardware, being cool and innovative isn't enough. You also have to be profitable. Or at least, you have to be profitable before you run out of runway.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Olive AI
entries.descriptor: The healthcare AI that raised $400 million before realizing it had built the wrong thing
entries.content:
# Olive AI

***The healthcare AI that raised $400 million before realizing it had built the wrong thing***

> **Years:** 2012-2023

In 2012, Sean Lane was working at a healthcare technology company when he noticed something obvious: hospitals were drowning in administrative work. Prior authorizations, claims processing, eligibility verification — an endless stream of repetitive tasks that consumed enormous resources and delayed patient care. The processes were standardized enough to automate but too complex for simple software. Lane saw an opportunity for artificial intelligence before AI was fashionable.

He founded Olive with a specific thesis: healthcare's back office could be transformed by intelligent automation. Not patient diagnosis or treatment — the unglamorous work of moving information between systems, checking insurance eligibility, processing referrals. The kind of work that employed thousands of people doing essentially the same tasks slightly differently at every hospital in America.

The timing seemed perfect. Healthcare costs were ballooning. The Affordable Care Act had added complexity. Hospitals were desperate for efficiency. And crucially, the technology was finally ready. Machine learning could handle the pattern recognition. Robotic process automation could execute the tasks. Olive would be the "AI workforce" that healthcare administrators had been waiting for.

The early customers validated the concept. Hospitals saw immediate value in automating prior authorizations. Olive's software could watch human workers, learn their processes, then replicate them faster and more accurately. The company called it a "digital workforce" and gave the AI a friendly personality. Installation took weeks instead of months. ROI was measurable. Customers were happy.

Venture capitalists noticed. In an industry notorious for slow sales cycles and entrenched vendors, Olive was growing quickly. The company raised $32.8 million in 2018, then $51 million in 2019. Lane articulated a compelling vision: Olive would become the operating system for healthcare's back office, deployed across thousands of hospitals, handling billions of administrative transactions.

By 2020, momentum was accelerating. The COVID-19 pandemic stressed healthcare systems to breaking points and made automation urgent rather than optional. Hospitals needed to do more with less. Olive raised $225.5 million at a $1.5 billion valuation. The company had 500 employees and over 500 hospital customers. Healthcare publications wrote glowing profiles. Industry analysts predicted dominance.

But something wasn't working. Beneath the growth metrics and funding rounds, Olive was struggling with a fundamental problem: the very thing that made healthcare administration so ripe for automation also made it nearly impossible to automate at scale. Every hospital had different systems, different workflows, different exceptions. What worked at one site often broke at another. Olive's AI had to be constantly retrained and adjusted. The "digital workforce" required a large human workforce to maintain it.

The economics were troubling. Implementations were taking longer than projected. Customer success required more resources than planned. The software that was supposed to scale effortlessly needed constant customization. Olive was selling automation but delivering semi-automation with expensive human oversight.

Still, the company pushed forward. In 2021, it raised another $400 million at a $4 billion valuation. The funding was meant to accelerate growth and expand capabilities. Instead, it bought time to confront an uncomfortable truth: Olive had built technology that worked in demos and pilots but struggled at production scale. The AI was impressive but not autonomous enough. The market was real but the solution was incomplete.

Lane tried to pivot. In 2022, Olive acquired several companies to fill gaps in its platform and broaden its capabilities. It pursued an IPO. But the market had shifted. Tech valuations were collapsing. Customers were scrutinizing ROI more carefully. Competitors had emerged with simpler, cheaper solutions. The promised revolution in healthcare automation was happening, but not the way Olive had imagined.

By mid-2023, Olive was in trouble. Revenue wasn't meeting projections. The company announced layoffs — first hundreds, then more. The IPO was abandoned. In November 2023, Olive sold its core business units to other companies for undisclosed sums and shut down operations. Four billion dollars in peak valuation, gone. Four hundred million in total funding, largely vaporized.

The autopsy was straightforward: Olive had identified a real problem and raised enough capital to appear to be solving it, but never achieved the technical breakthrough required to make the economics work at scale. The company had confused customer interest with product-market fit, growth with sustainability, and funding with validation. Healthcare's back office still needed automation. Olive's AI still wasn't quite intelligent enough to deliver it profitably.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Epic Games
entries.descriptor: From game engine to platform power — and a fight with Apple that redefined the war for digital distribution
entries.content:
# Epic Games

***From game engine to platform power — and a fight with Apple that redefined the war for digital distribution***

> **Years:** 1991–present

Tim Sweeney started Epic Games in his parents' basement in Potomac, Maryland, in 1991, teaching himself to code by reading assembly language manuals. His first game, ZZT, was a shareware success — simple ASCII graphics, but it came with an editor that let players create their own worlds. That editor was the signal of everything to come. Sweeney wasn't just building games. He was building tools.

Through the nineties, Epic released a string of successful titles — Jazz Jackrabbit, Unreal. But Unreal was different. The game itself was a solid first-person shooter, but the real innovation was the Unreal Engine, the technology powering it. Sweeney had built a modular, licensable game engine that other developers could use. While id Software kept its tech proprietary, Epic sold access. By 1998, studios were licensing Unreal Engine to build their own games. Epic had found a business model that didn't depend on hit after hit. It could profit from everyone else's hits.

The engine business scaled. Unreal Engine 2 powered hits like Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell and BioShock. Unreal Engine 3 became the industry standard for the Xbox 360 and PlayStation 3 era. Epic wasn't the biggest game company, but it was the infrastructure. Developers paid upfront licensing fees plus royalties on sales. The incentives aligned: Epic succeeded when its customers succeeded.

In 2006, Epic released Gears of War, a franchise that became a tentpole for Microsoft's Xbox. The company was profitable, independent, growing. Sweeney maintained majority control — rare for a company of Epic's scale. Tencent invested $330 million in 2012 for a 40% stake, valuing Epic at over $800 million, but Sweeney kept decision-making power. The capital gave Epic room to take risks without answering to a board demanding quarterly growth.

The first big risk was Fortnite. Originally conceived as a cooperative survival game, Fortnite languished in development for years. It launched in July 2017 to decent but not exceptional reception. Then PUBG exploded. The battle royale format — 100 players, last one standing — was suddenly everywhere. Epic saw the opening. In two months, a small team inside Epic built Fortnite Battle Royale. It launched in September 2017 as a free-to-play mode.

It was a cultural phenomenon. By mid-2018, Fortnite had 125 million players. It wasn't just big — it was ubiquitous. Kids played it, celebrities played it, athletes did Fortnite dances in end zones. Epic's monetization was brilliant: the game was free, but players paid for cosmetic items — skins, emotes, pickaxes. No pay-to-win, just digital fashion. Fortnite generated $5.4 billion in 2018 alone.

The Fortnite windfall gave Epic leverage to make a move on distribution. For years, Valve's Steam had been the dominant PC game store, taking a 30% cut of every sale. Epic launched the Epic Games Store in December 2018, offering developers an 88/12 split. They paid tens of millions for timed exclusives, undercutting Steam with better terms and guaranteed revenue for developers. It was a direct assault on Valve's monopoly.

Then Epic went after mobile. Apple's App Store and Google's Play Store both took 30% of in-app purchases. For years, developers grumbled but complied — there was no alternative. In August 2020, Epic pushed an update to Fortnite on iOS and Android that let players buy V-Bucks (in-game currency) directly from Epic at a discount, bypassing Apple and Google's payment systems. Within hours, both companies removed Fortnite from their stores.

Epic immediately filed lawsuits and released a video parodying Apple's famous 1984 ad, casting Apple as Big Brother. This wasn't an accident. Epic had planned the confrontation, built a coalition of developers frustrated with app store fees, and framed the fight as a battle for the open internet. Sweeney positioned Epic as the underdog fighting monopolistic gatekeepers, even though Epic was worth over $15 billion.

The legal battle dragged on. In 2021, a judge ruled that Apple didn't have an illegal monopoly on mobile gaming but ordered Apple to allow developers to link to external payment methods. Both sides appealed. Epic lost on most counts but won a symbolic crack in Apple's armor. More importantly, the case put regulatory pressure on Apple and Google worldwide. South Korea passed a law banning app store payment monopolies. The EU's Digital Markets Act forced Apple to allow third-party app stores.

Epic didn't win the lawsuit outright, but it moved the window. The fight made app store fees a political issue, not just a business gripe. Sweeney was willing to burn billions in Fortnite revenue to wage the war. Fortnite remained banned on iOS — the most lucrative mobile platform — for years. Epic absorbed the loss.

By 2023, Epic was valued at $31.5 billion. Unreal Engine 5 had become the standard for next-gen games and was expanding into film, architecture, and virtual production. Fortnite remained a cash machine despite the iOS ban. The Epic Games Store was still unprofitable but growing, sustained by Fortnite money. Epic had acquired Bandcamp, Sketchfab, and ArtStation — infrastructure for creators. The strategy was clear: build the tools, control the platforms, own the distribution.

Epic's path wasn't about hitting a single jackpot. It was a decades-long accumulation of leverage: game success funded engine development, engine revenue funded platform experiments, Fortnite's windfall funded a war on app stores. Sweeney never cashed out, never went public, never let investors dilute his control. He built a company that could afford to fight multi-year legal battles because it owned the infrastructure everyone else depended on. Epic didn't just make games. It made the battlefield and then chose its wars.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Activision Blizzard
entries.descriptor: When the merger of giants became a study in cultural collision
entries.content:
# Activision Blizzard

***When the merger of giants became a study in cultural collision***

> **Years:** 2008-2023

In July 2008, two titans of gaming merged to create what should have been the industry's unassailable leader. Activision, the scrappy American publisher behind *Call of Duty* and *Guitar Hero*, combined with Vivendi Games, which owned Blizzard Entertainment — the legendary maker of *World of Warcraft*, *StarCraft*, and *Diablo*. The $18.9 billion deal created Activision Blizzard, the world's largest gaming company by revenue. Wall Street loved it. Gamers were skeptical. Both would be proven right.

The merger was Bobby Kotick's masterwork. He'd been running Activision since 1991, transforming it from a struggling publisher into a hit-making machine through ruthless focus on profitability and franchise potential. Kotick saw games as products, not art. He cut unprofitable studios, killed underperforming titles mid-development, and built an empire on annualized releases. His philosophy: find what works, exploit it relentlessly, minimize risk.

Blizzard was the opposite. Founded in 1991 as Silicon & Synapse by UCLA graduates Michael Morhaime, Frank Pearce, and Allen Adham, it had built a reputation on one principle: "it's done when it's done." They delayed games for years to get them right. *StarCraft* took three years. *World of Warcraft* took five. Players didn't just buy Blizzard games; they lived in them. *WoW* peaked at 12 million subscribers, generating over $1 billion annually. Quality at Blizzard wasn't marketing — it was religion.

The merger terms seemed to recognize this. Blizzard would remain autonomous, keeping its campus in Irvine, its culture intact, Morhaime as president. Activision got the financial power of *WoW*'s subscription revenue. Blizzard got resources and protection from Vivendi's corporate interference. The combined company would dominate both casual and hardcore gaming.

For a few years, the formula worked. *Call of Duty* became the best-selling franchise in North America. *World of Warcraft* prints money. New releases like *Overwatch* and *Hearthstone* expanded Blizzard's portfolio. Revenue climbed past $4 billion, then $6 billion, then $7 billion. Activision Blizzard became more valuable than Electronic Arts.

But beneath the earnings reports, something was fracturing. Kotick's Activision operated on annual cycles and franchise thinking. Blizzard's pace — years between releases, constant iteration, delayed launches — drove him mad. The pressure began subtly: gentle questions about timelines, suggestions about monetization, encouragement to explore mobile. Blizzard resisted. They'd always resisted.

The cracks widened after 2013. *World of Warcraft* subscriptions began their long decline from the peak. Mobile gaming exploded in Asia. Activision watched competitors like Tencent and Supercell mint billions from smartphones while Blizzard remained focused on PC and console. The message from corporate grew less gentle. Why wasn't Blizzard capitalizing on its IP in mobile? Why were development cycles so long? Where was the next *WoW*?

*Overwatch* launched in 2016 to massive success — but took six years to develop, emerging from the ashes of the canceled *Titan* MMO. It was classic Blizzard: delayed, expensive, brilliant. It was everything Kotick's model wasn't. The tension became structural.

In 2018, Blizzard made its most catastrophic announcement. At BlizzCon, their annual fan convention, they unveiled *Diablo Immortal* — a mobile game developed with Chinese partner NetEase. No new PC *Diablo*. No *Diablo IV* teaser. Just a mobile game for a franchise that defined PC gaming. The audience booed. One fan asked if it was an "out-of-season April Fools' joke." The moment became a meme, a symbol of Blizzard's perceived betrayal of its core audience for Chinese mobile money.

Months later, the cultural collision exploded into public view. In October 2018, Activision Blizzard announced record revenue and profit — and immediately laid off 800 employees, almost 8% of staff. Kotick received a $28 million compensation package the same year. The juxtaposition was brutal. The gaming community raged. Blizzard developers leaked internal frustrations. The autonomy was revealed as illusion.

Morhaime stepped down as Blizzard president in October 2018, replaced by J. Allen Brack, who'd spent 15 years at the company but was more corporate, less founder. The old guard was departing. In 2019, Blizzard banned a *Hearthstone* player for supporting Hong Kong protests, then reversed course under pressure — pleasing no one, revealing a company paralyzed between Chinese market access and Western values. The Blizzard that once moved slowly because it cared seemed to be moving slowly because it was lost.

Meanwhile, workplace rot festered. In July 2021, California sued Activision Blizzard for systematic sexual harassment and discrimination. The two-year investigation uncovered a "frat boy" culture of abuse, unequal pay, and retaliation. Employees walked out. Sponsors dropped partnerships. Kotick's knowledge of incidents and his own alleged misconduct became public. The company that made games about heroes was run by villains.

*Diablo IV* and *Overwatch 2* finally launched in 2023 — both good, neither transcendent. The Blizzard magic felt diminished, processed, corporatized. The delays were no longer about perfection but dysfunction.

In January 2022, Microsoft announced it would acquire Activision Blizzard for $68.7 billion — the largest acquisition in gaming history. After regulatory battles spanning 20 months, the deal closed in October 2023. Kotick departed with a $400 million exit package.

The merger that created Activision Blizzard promised the best of both worlds: Activision's commercial discipline and Blizzard's creative excellence. Instead, it revealed they were incompatible. You cannot systematize genius. You cannot annualize art. The attempt destroyed what made Blizzard special while failing to extract its full commercial potential. The culture that made *World of Warcraft* couldn't survive inside the machine that made *Call of Duty*. Microsoft inherited both the IPs and the wreckage.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Anthropic
entries.descriptor: The safety-first AI lab that became OpenAI's rival
entries.content:
# Anthropic

***The safety-first AI lab that became OpenAI's rival***

> **Years:** 2021-present

In May 2021, seven researchers left OpenAI. The departure wasn't quiet. Dario Amodei had been OpenAI's Vice President of Research. His sister Daniela had been VP of Operations. They'd watched their former employer transform from nonprofit research lab to something harder to define—a capped-profit entity with a $1 billion investment from Microsoft, building products, chasing scale, and in their view, moving too fast.

They founded Anthropic with a specific thesis: AI safety couldn't be bolted on after the fact. It had to be foundational. The company would build large language models, but differently. The approach was called "Constitutional AI"—training models with explicit values and constraints from the start, making them more predictable, more aligned, safer.

The timing mattered. GPT-3 had demonstrated the power of large language models in 2020, but the technology still lived mostly in research papers and developer APIs. The public didn't yet understand what was coming. That gave Anthropic a window.

The founding team was formidable. Beyond the Amodei siblings, it included Chris Olah, a legendary figure in AI interpretability research, and several others who'd worked on GPT-3. They knew how to build these systems. They'd done it before. Now they wanted to do it right.

Funding came quickly. By 2022, Anthropic had raised $704 million, led by Sam Bankman-Fried's FTX and Alameda Research, with participation from Google, Spark Capital, and others. The SBF connection would later become awkward when FTX collapsed, but at the time it signaled something: serious money believed safety-focused AI could compete.

The technical approach was genuinely different. Constitutional AI meant training Claude—their model—with a two-step process. First, a model learns to critique its own outputs against a set of principles (the "constitution"). Then it's trained to prefer outputs that better satisfy those principles. The goal was models that were helpful, harmless, and honest by design, not just by filtering.

They published. A lot. Papers on interpretability, on constitutional AI, on scaling laws, on red-teaming. This wasn't a research lab hiding behind trade secrets. It was more like DeepMind's playbook—serious academic rigor, but building products too.

But OpenAI moved first and faster. ChatGPT launched in November 2022 and became the fastest-growing consumer application in history. Suddenly everyone understood what LLMs could do. The window closed. Anthropic was still in research mode when the race began.

They responded by March 2023 with Claude. Not a chatbot interface—that came later—but an API. The pitch was explicit: Claude was safer, more reliable, better at following instructions, less likely to hallucinate or say harmful things. For enterprises nervous about AI risk, that mattered.

Google invested again—heavily. By late 2023, they'd committed up to $2 billion, with an initial $500 million and future tranches tied to usage of Google Cloud. Amazon followed with up to $4 billion, with similar cloud commitments. Anthropic was suddenly playing the same game as OpenAI—raising massive capital from cloud providers who wanted the model layer locked to their infrastructure.

The product strategy evolved. Claude 2 in July 2023 brought a 100,000-token context window—far larger than GPT-4's at launch. That meant Claude could process entire codebases, long documents, whole books. For certain use cases, it was simply better. Then Claude Pro, a consumer subscription. Then Claude for Enterprise.

The positioning sharpened: Anthropic was the responsible choice. While OpenAI's Sam Altman testified to Congress about existential risk and called for AI regulation, Anthropic was actually building the safety features regulators would eventually require. They hired policy experts, published model cards, created systems for monitoring misuse.

Competition intensified. OpenAI, Google's Gemini, Meta's Llama, Mistral, Cohere—the foundation model space was suddenly crowded. But Anthropic had carved a niche. Enterprises that needed AI but feared reputational risk chose Claude. So did AI safety researchers. So did companies in regulated industries.

By 2024, the technical lead narrowed. Claude 3 launched in March with three variants—Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku—matching or exceeding GPT-4 on benchmarks. The context window hit 200,000 tokens. The safety features worked. The company had actual revenue—hundreds of millions—from API usage and enterprise contracts.

But the cost structure was brutal. Training runs cost hundreds of millions. Inference was expensive. Anthropic was burning through capital, though at a slower rate than some competitors because they hadn't yet bet everything on a consumer play. The Amazon and Google deals provided runway, but also dependence.

The market position became clearer: Anthropic wasn't going to win by being biggest. They'd win by being trusted. Perplexity, Notion, DuckDuckGo, and others built on Claude. Enterprises like Bridgewater, Lonely Planet, and the Boston Consulting Group chose it. The safety brand mattered.

The broader question remained unanswered: could a safety-first approach actually compete at the frontier? Or would the companies willing to move fastest, push hardest, and worry about safety later win the race? OpenAI had first-mover advantage. Google had distribution. Meta was open-sourcing. Anthropic had principles and performance.

By early 2025, Anthropic was neither the largest AI lab nor the fastest-growing. But it was one of the few that might survive the shakeout ahead. The Amodei siblings had made a bet: that as AI became more powerful, safety wouldn't be optional. That regulators would intervene. That enterprises would demand it. That the constitutional approach would scale.

The outcome wasn't yet written. But Anthropic had proven that leaving OpenAI to build the alternative wasn't hubris. It was viable. Whether viable meant successful, or just not-failed-yet, depended on what happened next in the race they'd helped define.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Midjourney
entries.section: AI art from a small team
entries.descriptor: The art machine that arrived quietly and changed everything
entries.content:
# Midjourney

***The art machine that arrived quietly and changed everything***

> **Years:** 2021-present

David Holz had already built something extraordinary once. At Leap Motion, he'd spent nearly a decade trying to make gesture control the future of computing, raising over $90 million, shipping hardware that could track your fingers with impossible precision. The technology worked beautifully. The product never found its market. By 2019, the company had been acqui-hired by Ultrahaptics for what insiders understood was far less than the capital raised.

Holz emerged from that experience with a particular set of convictions. He'd built complex hardware requiring perfect execution across supply chains, retail partnerships, developer adoption. He'd learned that technical excellence doesn't guarantee product-market fit, that the most sophisticated technology can still be a solution searching for a problem.

So when he started exploring generative AI in 2021, he made different choices.

Midjourney began as an independent research lab with fewer than a dozen people. No venture capital. No elaborate go-to-market strategy. Just a small team exploring what was possible as transformer models began generating images from text. Holz had resources from the Leap Motion exit and a conviction that the coming wave of AI should be accessible to everyone, not locked behind corporate APIs.

The technical approach was unorthodox. While OpenAI and Google built massive models requiring supercomputer infrastructure, Midjourney optimized for efficiency. They used a mix of custom and open-source models, aggressively optimized inference, and made architectural choices that prioritized beauty and artistic coherence over photorealism. The goal wasn't to recreate reality—it was to create something that felt right.

In July 2021, they began a private beta. No press releases. No launch event. Just invitations to artists, designers, and creative technologists to experiment in a Discord server. The choice of Discord—a gaming chat platform—seemed eccentric. But it turned out to be quietly brilliant. Every image generated was visible to the community. People could see prompts, remix ideas, learn techniques by watching. The product was social by design, not as a growth hack but as a fundamental feature.

By early 2022, something unexpected was happening. The images coming out of Midjourney had a distinctive aesthetic—painterly, dreamlike, often surreal. They weren't trying to fool anyone into thinking they were photographs. They looked like art. And artists noticed.

While Stability AI raced to release Stable Diffusion as open source and DALL-E 2 generated headlines for OpenAI, Midjourney quietly accumulated users. By summer 2022, they had hundreds of thousands of people paying $10-$60 per month for subscriptions. No free tier. No advertising. Just a simple proposition: pay monthly, generate unlimited images at standard resolution.

The business model was almost accidentally perfect. Subscription revenue scaled linearly with engaged users. No complicated pricing tiers or compute-credit systems. The Discord community became both customer support and marketing—experienced users helped newcomers, shared techniques, celebrated each other's work. Moderation challenges that would have crushed a venture-backed company trying to scale were managed by the community itself.

Then the controversies arrived. Artists discovered their styles being mimicked. Illustrators protested that the model had been trained on their copyrighted work without permission or compensation. Questions emerged about the ethics of synthetic media, the future of creative work, the rights of those whose art trained the machines.

Midjourney's response was neither defiant nor apologetic. They acknowledged concerns, implemented opt-out systems for artists, banned certain celebrity likenesses. But they didn't pretend the technology could be uninvented. Holz argued that Midjourney was a tool—like Photoshop or the camera before it—that would change creative work but not replace human creativity.

The product kept improving. Version 3, then 4, then 5. Each release brought leap-forward improvements in coherence, detail, and control. By 2023, Midjourney images were winning art competitions, appearing in magazines, filling creative briefs. Professional illustrators who initially protested found themselves using it in their workflows—for concepts, for exploration, for producing finished work faster.

What made Midjourney different wasn't just the technology. It was that Holz had learned from Leap Motion. Don't build what's technically impressive—build what people want to use. Don't optimize for venture metrics—optimize for sustainability. Don't chase enterprise contracts—serve the people who actually care.

By 2024, Midjourney was doing something almost unheard of in Silicon Valley: running profitably with no outside funding. Revenue reportedly exceeded $200 million annually from pure subscription fees. The team remained small—under 40 people—yet they served millions of users. No sales team. No growth hackers. Just engineers and researchers making the product better.

The company operated more like a game studio than a traditional startup. Holz shared update notes in Discord the way game developers talk to players. The community felt like participants in an ongoing experiment rather than customers of a service. When competitors raised hundreds of millions to catch up, Midjourney just kept shipping.

This path wasn't available to everyone. Holz had the resources and credibility from a previous exit. The timing was perfect—launching just as generative AI became viable but before the market was crowded. The technology was complex enough to be defensible but simple enough to ship quickly.

But the choices were deliberate. Stay small. Stay independent. Build for people who care. Ignore conventional wisdom about how AI companies should operate. In an industry obsessed with winner-take-all dynamics and massive rounds, Midjourney proved that another path existed.

The art machine that arrived quietly in a Discord server had changed not just what images could be created, but how an AI company could be built.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Perplexity
entries.descriptor: The search engine that thought it could skip Google's decades
entries.content:
# Perplexity

***The search engine that thought it could skip Google's decades***

> **Years:** 2022-present

In August 2022, when Aravind Srinivas and three co-founders launched Perplexity, the graveyard of Google competitors stretched back two decades. Cuil, Powerset, Blekko, Neeva — each had tried to dethrone search and failed. The pattern was clear: better technology wasn't enough. Google had the index, the infrastructure, the habit, and the ad machinery that printed money. You couldn't beat them by being incrementally better. You had to make search feel obsolete.

Srinivas had been at OpenAI, watching GPT-3 reshape how people interacted with information. His co-founders — Denis Yarats, Johnny Ho, and Andy Konwinski — came from DeepMind, Meta, and Databricks. They'd seen the AI wave building. They saw something Google couldn't see from inside its own castle: the search box was dying. Not because people stopped asking questions, but because they wanted answers, not ten blue links. They wanted conversation, not keyword matching. They wanted synthesis, not a scavenger hunt.

The first version of Perplexity was deceptively simple. You typed a question. It gave you an answer with citations. That was it. No ads. No SEO spam. No "People also ask" boxes filled with tangentially related garbage. Just the answer, sourced, clean. It felt like the future because it was what search should have been all along.

The timing was perfect and terrible. Perfect because ChatGPT launched three months later and suddenly everyone understood what conversational AI could do. Terrible because ChatGPT had OpenAI's brand, Microsoft's billions, and a product that felt magical even when it hallucinated. Perplexity had to compete in a space that went from empty to crowded overnight.

But Perplexity had something ChatGPT didn't: citations. Every answer was grounded in sources. You could verify. You could trace. This mattered to people who wanted truth, not plausible-sounding fiction. While ChatGPT users discovered their AI confidently made things up, Perplexity users got answers they could trust — or at least check.

The growth was explosive by normal startup standards, glacial by AI-hype standards. By early 2023, they had millions of queries per month. By late 2023, tens of millions. The product felt fast, accurate, useful. Power users — researchers, journalists, developers — started switching. Not abandoning Google entirely, but adding Perplexity to their workflow for the questions that mattered.

The money followed. NEA led a $26 million Series A in March 2023. By January 2024, IVP and NEA put in $73.6 million at a $520 million valuation. By April 2024, another $63 million at over $1 billion. By summer, whispers of $250 million more at $3 billion. In eighteen months, Perplexity went from zero to unicorn. The investors weren't betting on revenue — they were betting on displacement. If AI search could take even 10% of Google's market, it was worth tens of billions.

Then came the complications. In June 2024, Forbes and Wired accused Perplexity of plagiarism. The AI was scraping their content, summarizing it, and serving answers that kept users from ever visiting the original sites. Perplexity's defense — that it cited sources — rang hollow to publishers who saw their traffic evaporate. By October, News Corp sent a cease-and-desist. The New York Times was reportedly preparing legal action. Perplexity launched a "Publishers Program" offering revenue share, but most major outlets refused. They'd seen this movie before with Google News. They weren't interested in crumbs.

The deeper problem was structural. Perplexity's value proposition — get the answer without leaving — was existential threat to the web's economic model. Publishers made money from ads and subscriptions on their sites. If AI gave you the answer directly, why click through? Perplexity could cite sources all day, but if citations didn't drive traffic, they were just fig leaves. The open web had survived Google because Google still sent traffic. AI search threatened to break that bargain.

Meanwhile, Google wasn't standing still. In May 2024, they launched AI Overviews — answers at the top of search results, powered by Gemini. It was clunky at first, telling users to put glue on pizza and eat rocks. But Google had infinite resources to improve it. They also had the index, the distribution, the Chrome default, the Android integration. Perplexity was fast and clean, but Google was everywhere.

By late 2024, Perplexity had 100 million weekly queries and 15 million monthly active users. Impressive for a two-year-old startup. A rounding error for Google. They launched Perplexity Pages, letting users create AI-generated research reports. They added voice search, image search, shopping. They were expanding fast, chasing product-market fit in multiple directions at once.

The question wasn't whether Perplexity worked. It did. The question was whether working was enough. Search is a natural monopoly. The best search engine gets more users, more data, better results, more users. Google spent twenty years building that flywheel. Perplexity thought AI could reset the game, create a new kind of search where the old rules didn't apply.

But the old rules still applied to publishers. They still applied to regulation — the FTC was circling. They still applied to unit economics — AI inference was expensive, search needed scale. And they still applied to distribution. Perplexity could be better, but Google was default.

Srinivas talks about building "the world's best answer engine." It's a telling phrase. Not search engine — answer engine. The bet is that the future isn't about finding information, it's about synthesizing it. That Google's core product — the ten blue links — is already dead, people just don't know it yet.

Maybe he's right. Maybe in five years, search means conversation and synthesis, and Google's index is just raw material for AI. Maybe Perplexity captures that future. Or maybe search is harder to disrupt than it looks. Maybe the entrenched player adapts fast enough. Maybe the legal and economic problems are insurmountable. Maybe another startup with more funding and better distribution takes the space.

Perplexity is live ammunition in the battle to reinvent search. Whether it becomes the weapon that wins the war or just another casualty depends on questions without easy answers: Can you kill Google without killing the web? Can you scale AI inference to Google's query volume? Can you become a habit when the competition is already muscle memory? The company is two years old, worth billions on paper, and still figuring out what it wants to be when it grows up. The only certainty is that staying still means death.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Lynda.com
entries.section: Online learning before it was cool
entries.descriptor: The patient build that became an acquisition
entries.content:
# Lynda.com

***The patient build that became an acquisition***

> **Years:** 1995-2015

In 1995, when most people thought of online education as correspondence courses delivered by email, Lynda Weinman was teaching web design in her living room. She had written a book on HTML and discovered that people would pay to learn these new internet skills in person. Her husband, Bruce Heavin, suggested they videotape the classes. They could sell the tapes to people who couldn't attend in person.

The insight was simple: software was changing faster than traditional education could keep up. Universities moved slowly. Publishers moved slowly. But professionals needed to learn Photoshop, Dreamweaver, Flash — and they needed to learn now. Lynda and Bruce built a company around that urgency.

They called it Lynda.com, launched in 1995 as an online repository of web design tutorials. The business model was straightforward: charge for access to video courses taught by expert practitioners. No degrees. No credits. Just skills.

The internet was young. Streaming video was painful. Bandwidth was expensive. Most educational content online was text-based because video files were too large, too slow, too impractical. Lynda.com sold CD-ROMs alongside the website because downloading a single course could take hours.

But Lynda Weinman understood something crucial: her audience wasn't traditional students. They were working professionals who needed to learn specific tools to do their jobs. They didn't want theory. They wanted a screencast showing them exactly where to click. They would tolerate poor streaming quality if the content was immediately useful.

The company grew slowly. Bootstrapped at first, then with modest outside investment. They expanded beyond web design into photography, video editing, business software. Each new subject area followed the same pattern: hire an expert practitioner, have them record detailed software tutorials, package them into courses. The library grew course by course, subject by subject.

By the early 2000s, Lynda.com had established a pattern that would define its path: steady, incremental growth without drama. No viral moments. No pivot that saved the company. No near-death experiences. Just consistent execution of a model that worked. Monthly subscriptions gave users access to the entire library. More subscribers meant more revenue to fund more courses. More courses attracted more subscribers.

The technology improved as bandwidth got cheaper. Streaming became viable. The CD-ROMs disappeared. The website became the product. But the core model never changed: professionals paying monthly fees to access a library of software training taught by practitioners.

Competition emerged. Other companies tried to replicate the model. YouTube filled with free tutorials. Universities launched online course platforms. MOOCs promised to revolutionize education. Lynda.com kept growing. The free YouTube tutorials were inconsistent in quality and hard to navigate. The MOOCs focused on academic subjects, not vocational skills. The universities moved slowly. Lynda.com had a decade-long head start and a library of thousands of courses that grew every month.

By 2010, the company had over 100 employees and millions of subscribers. It was profitable. It had never taken substantial venture capital. Lynda Weinman and Bruce Heavin still owned most of it. The pattern held: slow, steady, profitable growth in a market that kept expanding as software became central to more industries.

Then the market shifted around them. LinkedIn was building an empire in professional networking. It had hundreds of millions of users, massive engagement, and a problem: how do you monetize professional identity beyond recruiting and advertising? LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner saw Lynda.com as the answer. Professional development. Skills. A way to keep users engaged beyond job searching.

The courtship was quiet. LinkedIn approached Lynda.com in 2014. The numbers were staggering: $1.5 billion in cash and stock. For a company that had bootstrapped its way to profitability, that had never raised significant venture capital, that had grown organically for nearly two decades, it was an exit that rewarded patience.

Lynda Weinman and Bruce Heavin sold in April 2015. At the time, some observers questioned whether LinkedIn had overpaid. Lynda.com had revenue around $200 million annually. The multiple seemed rich. But Weiner understood what he was buying: a decade of video content, a trusted brand in professional education, and most importantly, a business model that aligned perfectly with LinkedIn's professional network.

The integration was smooth. Lynda.com became LinkedIn Learning. The courses appeared in LinkedIn profiles. The recommendation engine connected skills gaps to relevant courses. Users who searched for jobs got prompted to learn skills for those positions. The library kept growing.

For Lynda Weinman, the story ended with validation. She had started teaching web design in her living room because she saw a need. She had built a company around meeting that need consistently, year after year, without shortcuts or drama. She had resisted the pressure to raise huge rounds, to grow faster, to pivot into something trendier. She had stayed patient.

When Microsoft acquired LinkedIn for $26.2 billion in 2016, Lynda.com came along as part of the package. The company she started with videotapes of her classes had become a piece of one of the largest tech acquisitions in history. Not because it disrupted anything. Because it built something useful and kept building it for twenty years.

---
*Part of All Paths Through a Startup at alexandria.press*

entries.name: Descript
entries.descriptor: The audio editor that became a video company by making the timeline obsolete
entries.content:
# Descript

***The audio editor that became a video company by making the timeline obsolete***

> **Years:** 2017-present

Andrew Mason had already lived one complete startup arc.
